He enjoyed watching the dance’s turmoil, reading the agitation in the faces of the Countess and Martial, and wondering about the connection between Soulanges’ gloomy looks and the fair unknown’s expression, which alternated between hope and dread. Montcornet stood like the king of the feast, receiving inviting smiles from many beautiful women. As a Colonel of the Imperial Guard, he was one of the best matches in the army.

It was nearly midnight. The conversation, gambling, dancing, flirtations, interests, rivalries, and scheming had reached a fervor that made a young man exclaim, “A fine ball!”

“My sweet little angel,” Madame de Lansac said to the Countess, “you are now at an age when I made many mistakes. Seeing you are enduring a thousand deaths, I thought I might give you some charitable advice. To go wrong at two-and-twenty means spoiling your future; it is like tearing the gown you must wear. My dear, it is not much later that we learn to go about in it without crumpling it. Go on, sweetheart, making clever enemies and friends without sense of conduct, and you will see what a pleasant life you will lead!”

“Oh, madame, it is very hard for a woman to be happy, do you not think?” the Countess eagerly exclaimed.

“My child, at your age, you must learn to choose between pleasure and happiness. You want to marry Martial, who is not wise enough to be a good husband, nor passionate enough to remain a lover. He is in debt, my dear; he is the man to squander your fortune; still, that would be nothing if he could make you happy. Do you not see how aged he is? He must have been ill; he is making the most of what is left him. In three years, he will be a wreck. Then he will be ambitious; perhaps he may succeed. I do not think so. What is he? A man of intrigue, who may have business acumen and be able to gossip agreeably, but he is too presumptuous to have any real merit; he will not go far. Besides—look at him. Is it not written on his brow that, at this very moment, what he sees in you is not a young and pretty woman, but the two million francs you possess? He does not love you, my dear; he is calculating you as if you were an investment. If you are bent on marrying, find an older man with an assured position who is halfway in his career. A widow’s marriage should not be a trivial love affair. Is a mouse to be caught a second time in the same trap? A new alliance should be a good speculation on your part, and in marrying again, you ought at least to hope to be addressed as Madame la Marechale!”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
As she spoke, both women naturally focused on Colonel Montcornet’s handsome face.

“If you prefer to flirt rather than marry again,” the Duchess said bluntly, “you, better than any woman, know how to stir up trouble and then calm it. But please, don’t disturb families or ruin wives’ happiness. I’ve played that dangerous game. For a moment of vanity, a virtuous woman can be destroyed—there are truly virtuous women, dear—and we can become mortally hated. I learned too late that, as the Duc d’Albe said, one salmon is worth a thousand frogs! Genuine affection brings far more happiness than fleeting passion. I came to preach to you; you are the reason for my visit to this lower-class house. Have I not just seen actors here? We used to receive them in our boudoir, but never in the drawing-room! Why are you looking at me in amazement? If you want to play with men, toy only with those whose lives are unsettled and who have no duties. The others won’t forgive us for the errors that make them happy. Take this advice from my long experience. Look at Soulanges, for instance, whom you’ve made lovesick for the past fifteen months. Do you realize what you’ve done? You’ve affected his entire life. He’s been married two years and is adored by a charming wife whom he loves but neglects; she lives in tears. Soulanges has felt remorse far worse than any pleasure you’ve given him. And you, you little minx, have abandoned him. Come see the result of your actions.”

The old lady took Madame de Vaudremont’s hand, and they rose.

“There,” said Madame de Lansac, pointing to a pale, trembling figure under the candlelight, “that is my grandniece, the Comtesse de Soulanges. Today she finally yielded to my persuasion and left the sorrowful room where her child brings her little consolation. You think her charming? Imagine how she must have looked when happiness and love illuminated that now-blighted face.”

The Countess looked away, lost in sad thought.

The Duchess led her to the card-room door, then, scanning the room, said, “And there is Soulanges!”

The Countess shuddered at the sight of Soulanges, pale and rigid in an easy chair. His indifference and the tension in his brow revealed his suffering. The players passed him by, ignoring him as if he were dead. The image of the tearful wife and the morose husband, separated amidst festivity, struck the Countess with a sense of foreboding about her own future. Though her heart was not entirely hardened, she pressed the Duchess’s hand, thanking her with a childlike smile.

“My dear child,” the old lady whispered, “remember that we can repel a man’s attentions just as easily as we can attract them.”

“She is yours if you’re not a fool,” Madame de Lansac whispered to Colonel Montcornet while the Countess remained absorbed in her compassion for Soulanges, still loving him enough to wish to restore his happiness.

“Oh! I will talk to him!” she declared to Madame de Lansac.

“Do nothing of the sort!” the old lady exclaimed as she returned to her chair. “Choose a good husband and shut your door to my nephew. A wife cannot accept her husband’s heart as a gift from another woman; she is far happier believing she has won it back. By bringing my niece here, I believe I’ve given her a chance to regain her husband’s affection. All I need from you is to play the Colonel.” She pointed to Montcornet, and the Countess smiled.

“Well, madame, do you finally know the name of the unknown?” Martial asked the Countess when they were alone.

“Yes,” replied Madame de Vaudremont, her expression playful.

Her smile and bright eyes were like a will-o’-the-wisp leading travelers astray. Martial, believing she still loved him, adopted a charming demeanor. “Will you be annoyed if I seem to care about that name?”

“Will you be annoyed if a remnant of affection prevents me from telling you? And if I forbid you to approach that young lady? It could be dangerous.”

“To lose your favor would be worse than losing my life.”

“Martial,” the Countess said sternly, “she is Madame de Soulanges. Her husband would kill you if he knew.”

“Ha! So the Colonel can leave the man in peace who has taken his love, yet would fight for his wife! What a reversal of principles! I beg you to let me dance with her. You’ll see how little love that icy heart could feel for you; if the Colonel allows me to dance with his wife after letting me—”

“But she loves her husband.”

“An even greater obstacle I shall enjoy overcoming.”

“But she is married.”

“A whimsical objection!”

“Ah!” the Countess said with a bitter smile, “you punish us for both our faults and our repentance!”

“Do not be angry!” Martial exclaimed eagerly. “Forgive me, I beseech you. I will think no more of Madame de Soulanges.”

“You deserve that I should send you to her.”

“I’m off then,” he laughed, “and I’ll return more devoted to you than ever. You’ll see that the prettiest woman cannot capture the heart that is yours.”

“Meaning you want to win Colonel Montcornet’s horse?”

“Ah! Traitor!” he said, playfully threatening his friend. The Colonel smiled and joined them; the Baron gave him a seat near the Countess, saying with a sardonic tone, “Here, madame, is a man who boasted he could win your favor in one evening.”

He left, thinking he had cleverly piqued the Countess’ pride and done Montcornet a disservice, unaware of the irony in Madame de Vaudremont’s words, as both were unconsciously moving toward each other.

At that moment, the lawyer approached Madame de Soulanges, who sat pale and timid, seemingly alive only in her eyes. Her husband entered the ballroom, his eyes flashing with anger. The Duchess, ever watchful, rushed to her nephew, asking for his arm to find her carriage, feigning boredom to prevent a scene. Before leaving, she shot a knowing glance at her niece, signaling her to take revenge.

Madame de Vaudremont noticed the exchange and felt a sudden realization; she feared being the target of the Duchess’s cunning.

“That duplicitous Duchess,” she thought, “may be preaching morality while plotting against me.”

This thought stirred her pride more than her curiosity to unravel the intrigue. In her distraction, she lost control of herself. The Colonel, misinterpreting her embarrassment, became more ardent. The old diplomats, entertained by the unfolding drama, had never witnessed so many simultaneous intrigues. The passions of the two couples were evident in the crowded rooms, reflected in the faces around them, amplifying their own feelings of impotence.

Finally, the Baron found a seat beside Madame de Soulanges. He admired her beauty up close, captivated by her fresh neck and graceful figure. At that time, women wore sashes tied under the bosom, a style that flattered those with perfect figures. As he stole glances at the Countess, Martial was enchanted by her perfection.

“You haven’t danced once this evening, madame,” he said in soft, flattering tones. “Not for lack of a partner, I presume?”

“I never go to parties; I’m quite unknown,” Madame de Soulanges replied coldly, unaware of her aunt’s signal to attract the Baron.

Martial, adjusting the diamond on his hand, felt a spark of hope as he caught the Countess’s eye. She blushed and looked at him with an indefinable expression.

“Do you enjoy dancing?” he asked to continue the conversation.

“Yes, very much, monsieur.”

Their eyes met, and the earnestness in her voice stirred something in him. “Then, am I too bold in offering to be your partner for the next quadrille?”

Artless confusion colored her cheeks.

“But, monsieur, I have already refused one partner—a military man—”

“Was it that tall cavalry colonel over there?”

“Precisely.”

“Oh! He’s a friend of mine; don’t worry. Will you grant me the favor I hope for?”

“Yes, monsieur.”

Her tone revealed a new and deep emotion that touched Martial’s world-weary heart. Overcome with shyness, he lost his confidence, and his southern charm ignited. He tried to speak, but his words felt clumsy compared to Madame de Soulanges’ bright replies. Fortunately, the quadrille was forming, and standing beside her eased his nerves. For many men, dancing is a way to express themselves, believing they can win a woman’s heart through their physical grace. Martial likely intended to showcase all his charms, judging by his pretentious movements and gestures.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
He led his conquest to the quadrille, where the most brilliant women prized dancing above all. As the orchestra sounded the introductory bars, the Baron felt proud surveying the ladies forming the square. Madame de Soulanges’ dress rivaled Madame de Vaudremont’s, who stood opposite him by the lady in blue. Eyes turned to Madame de Soulanges; a flattering murmur showed she was the topic of conversation. The admiration and envy made her blush, enhancing her charm. When she raised her eyes she looked at her partner, as if to transfer the glory of admiration to him, showing she valued his opinion most. She blended innocence and vanity, showing a budding affection typical of youth. As she danced, onlookers might have thought she displayed her grace solely for Martial; though modest and inexperienced in ballroom arts, she knew how to glance at him at just the right moments.

When a new figure, invented by a dancer named Trenis, brought Martial face to face with the Colonel, he joked, “I have won your horse.” Montcornet retorted, “Yes, but you have lost eighty thousand francs a year!” Martial dismissed the concern: “What do I care? Madame de Soulanges is worth millions!”

After the quadrille whispers circulated. Less attractive women made moral comments about Martial and the Comtesse de Soulanges, while the more beautiful admired her apparent ease in surrendering to him. The men were baffled by the Baron’s luck, not seeing him as particularly charming. A few sympathetic women argued against condemning the Countess, suggesting that young wives would be in a dire position if a mere glance or graceful dance could compromise a woman’s reputation.

Only Martial understood the depth of his happiness. During the last figure, as the ladies formed the moulinet, he clasped the Countess’s hand, feeling her response through the thin fabric of her gloves. “Madame,” he said as the quadrille ended, “do not return to that corner where you’ve been hiding. Is admiration the only benefit you gain from your jewels? Come and enjoy the scene and yourself.”

Madame de Soulanges yielded to his charm, and they walked through crowded rooms. She hesitated at every door, peering in before entering, calmed only by his assurance, “He is not here.” They reached a grand picture gallery, where a lavish collation awaited three hundred guests. As supper approached, Martial led the Countess to an oval boudoir overlooking the garden, with fragrant flowers and blue hangings. The noise of the festivities faded. At first startled, the Countess resisted but finally sat on an ottoman after glancing in a mirror, perhaps reassured they might be seen.

“This room is charming,” she said, admiring the decor. “All here is love and delight!” Martial, moved, noticed her face in the dim light—unease, modesty, and eagerness that captivated him. She smiled, calming her struggle. Playfully she took his left hand and removed his ring, exclaiming, “What a fine diamond!”

Martial, intoxicated by her touch, looked at her with eyes bright as the gem. “Wear it,” he said, “in memory of this hour, and for the love of—” He trailed off, caught by her rapture. She asked in surprise, “You give it to me?” “I wish I had the whole world to offer you!” he replied. “Will you accept only my diamond?” She pressed, “You will never take it back?” “Never,” he assured.

She placed the ring on her finger, and as Martial prepared to embrace her, she suddenly stood and said clearly, “I accept the diamond, monsieur, with less scruple because it belongs to me.” The Baron was taken aback. “Monsieur de Soulanges took it from my dressing-table and told me he had lost it.”

“You are mistaken, madame,” Martial said, irritated. “It was given to me by Madame de Vaudremont.” “Precisely,” she replied with a smile. “My husband borrowed it, gave it to her, and she gifted it to you. This ring has made a little journey, that is all. It may reveal secrets to me.” She touched a spring in the ring, revealing a lock of M. de Soulanges’ hair, then fled into the crowded rooms, leaving Martial confounded. He noticed the Colonel and Madame de Vaudremont laughing nearby, and the Colonel teased, “Will you have my horse to chase after your prize?” Martial took their banter in stride, grateful for their silence about the evening’s events, as he exchanged his horse for a young wife.

As the Comtesse de Soulanges drove across Paris to her home in the Faubourg Saint-Germain, anxiety consumed her. Before leaving the Hotel Gondreville she searched for her aunt and husband, who had departed without her. Disturbing suspicions plagued her. She had silently witnessed her husband’s torment since Madame de Vaudremont ensnared him and had hoped for his repentance. Now she feared she had made a grave mistake in agreeing to her aunt’s scheme.

The evening had darkened her innocent spirit. First alarmed by her husband’s suffering, she grew more distressed seeing her rival’s beauty and feeling the weight of societal corruption. As she crossed the Pont Royal she discarded the hair from the diamond, a token of pure affection. Tears flowed as she recalled long-standing grief, and she shuddered at the thought that a woman seeking peace must bury her pain without complaint.

“Alas!” she thought, “What can women do when they do not love? What sustains their devotion? I cannot believe, as my aunt claims, that reason alone is enough.”

Still sighing, she arrived home and rushed upstairs, startled to find her husband by the fire. “How long has it been since you attended balls without telling me?” he asked, his voice strained. “You know a woman is out of place without her husband. You compromised yourself by hiding in the dark corner.”

“Oh, my dear Leon,” she replied coaxingly, “I couldn’t resist the joy of seeing you without your knowing. My aunt took me to the ball, and I was very happy there!”

Her words softened the Count’s sternness; he had feared she would learn of his infidelity and had tried to be the first to criticize her. Delighted to see him smile and grateful for his presence, the Countess looked at him tenderly, blushing and lowering her eyes. Her kindness further charmed Soulanges, especially after the misery he had endured at the ball. He took her hand and kissed it gratefully, for gratitude often accompanies love.

“Hortense, what is that on your finger that hurt my lip?” he asked, laughing. “It’s my diamond, which you said you lost, and which I have found.”

General Montcornet did not marry Madame de Vaudremont, despite their brief understanding; she became one of the victims of the tragic fire that marred the ball hosted by the Austrian ambassador for Napoleon’s marriage to the daughter of Emperor Joseph II.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

In September 1835, Mademoiselle du Rouvre, the only daughter of the Marquis du Rouvre, married Comte Adam Mitgislas Laginski, a Polish exile. The Marquis had squandered much of his fortune, but Clementine remained a great heiress through her mother’s family. Despite her father's mismanagement, settlements by relatives secured her a steady income when she married.

Comte Adam, though an exile, was not a burden on the French government. He came from an illustrious Polish family but encountered prejudice in Paris, where Polish nobility was not highly regarded. At first he seemed a student enjoying Parisian life after losing political prospects. The Polish community in France was split between republican and noble exiles, creating a complex social scene. Adam became the subject of Parisian humor, with some questioning his wealth and status.

In 1834 Adam bought a house in the rue de la Pépinière, and by 1836 he was established in Parisian society, having fallen in love with Clementine. Their marriage raised his standing, and he was soon seen as a fashionable figure. The couple's home was a luxurious creation, blending various architectural styles and filled with exquisite decor, reflecting the wealth and tastes of the new bourgeoisie.

Clementine, now a countess, began to notice the presence of Captain Paz, a factotum who managed their affairs but remained largely unseen. She questioned Adam about Paz, who was constant in their lives, handling everything from finances to horse care. Adam explained that Paz was a nobleman from a poor branch of the Paz family, who had fought valiantly for Poland and had become a close friend through shared hardships.

Paz's loyalty and dedication were evident; he ran the household with remarkable efficiency and discretion. Adam admired Paz's character, saying their friendship rested on mutual respect and understanding. Despite social hierarchy, Adam regarded Paz as an equal, their bond strengthened by shared experiences and challenges.

Clementine expressed admiration for Paz, recognizing his noble spirit and simplicity. Adam recounted offering Paz a financial gift, which Paz declined, underscoring their deep friendship and mutual commitment. Their relationship transcended typical social boundaries, with both men valuing their connection over societal expectations.

As the couple prepared for a day out, Clementine's affection for both Adam and Paz grew, highlighting the unique dynamics of their relationships. The narrative emphasizes loyalty, friendship, and the complexities of social status in 19th-century Paris, showing how personal connections can defy societal norms.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Paz lived a reclusive life, largely unnoticed by Parisian fashion, until the Comtesse Laginska was seen riding with him in the Bois de Boulogne. During their outing Clementine insisted Thaddeus dine with them, so he dressed for the occasion. At dinner he behaved like a subordinate, listening to Clementine and answering little, which frustrated Adam, who urged him to be more lively. Despite his dull demeanor, Thaddeus was conscious of his feelings for Clementine, which he struggled to hide.

After dinner Thaddeus agreed to go to the Opera, where he remained detached and seemingly uninterested. Clementine, feeling slighted, remarked on his lack of engagement, while Adam defended him, suggesting Thaddeus was being disagreeable on purpose. Thaddeus, however, was grappling with unrequited love for Clementine, which deepened on her return from a trip. He felt a bittersweet joy in being essential to their household, certain their life would falter without his management.

Clementine, now more beautiful and assured in her marriage, cared mainly about her social standing in Paris and gave little thought to Thaddeus. Still, one day she noticed him while driving home and invited him to join them, which flustered him. Their interactions were charged with unspoken emotion; Thaddeus felt a mix of gratitude and longing.

As days passed, Thaddeus returned to routine, feeling overlooked by Clementine, consumed by social obligations. He found solace in his role within their household, but his feelings for her remained an inner turmoil. He reflected on his love’s depth and the pain of being unrewarded.

One day Clementine hosted a reception and Thaddeus’s absence went unnoticed. He resumed his solitary habits, content in silent devotion, yet aware his love was both joy and suffering. He admired her from afar, cherishing moments when he could witness her happiness, even as she battled for attention, grew distracted by rivals, and often neglected her relationship with Adam. Thaddeus stayed a steadfast presence, quietly managing their affairs while wrestling with his feelings.

In May, as Clementine prepared to leave for her uncle's estate, she spotted Thaddeus; their brief exchange rekindled her curiosity. She insisted he join them for dinner, and though he hesitated, he complied. During the meal Thaddeus tried to engage more, but shyness and duty kept him from fully expressing himself. As the evening progressed Clementine’s interest in him grew and she began to see him differently. Their conversation revealed a deeper connection, and Thaddeus felt momentarily emboldened. Yet his loyalty to Adam restrained him from openly pursuing his feelings.

After dinner Clementine expressed dissatisfaction with Adam’s behavior and confessed a desire for a more passionate connection. Thaddeus, torn between love for her and friendship with Adam, struggled with the complexity of their relationships. He felt the urge to confess but hesitated. Clementine questioned the nature of love and loyalty; Thaddeus, sensing her vulnerability, felt hope yet remained cautious. Their talk deepened, and the silence between them brimmed with unspoken emotion.

To mask his true feelings Thaddeus fabricated a story about being in love with a circus performer named Malaga, describing her as captivating and agile, a contrast to Parisian society’s expectations. The tale let him express longing for a simpler, more genuine connection without revealing his attachment to Clementine.

Thaddeus’s visits to Malaga became an escape from unrequited love. He provided for her so she lived comfortably but never developed a true romance. His feelings for Clementine continued to haunt him, leaving him torn between loyalty to Adam and desire for Clementine. As Malaga’s fortunes shifted, she began to doubt Thaddeus’s intentions, causing misunderstandings and tension. Despite this, he remained her benefactor, committed even as he struggled with his own contradictions.

Eventually Thaddeus received a heartfelt letter from Malaga, expressing despair over their separation and longing for his companionship. The emotional plea struck a chord, reminding him of the depth of his devotion to Clementine. He realized his commitment to her was unwavering, even as he navigated love, loyalty, and sacrifice—torn between silent adoration, practical duty, and the complex demands of the lives around him.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Clementine returned home, and Paz saw her again, more beautiful than ever. After dinner the count and countess discussed Thaddeus, who had left a letter from Malaga with Adam. Adam lamented Thaddeus's misfortune, fearing he would lose everything because of his involvement with a circus performer. Clementine expressed disdain for such women, while Adam defended Thaddeus, noting his financial sacrifices for Malaga.

Paz, who had asked Adam for money to help Malaga, was viewed by the countess as a potential ruin for Adam. Adam, however, believed Thaddeus would eventually leave Malaga if it served his best interests; the countess remained skeptical.

Days later Paz noticed a change in Clementine’s behavior toward him and felt her contempt, which plunged him into despair. Believing he had caused her to despise him, he nevertheless found solace in silently admiring her at social events and dedicating himself to her well-being and the household. His love was profound, yet he felt unworthy of her respect. He fell ill from his emotional turmoil but eventually recovered.

Meanwhile Adam lost a significant sum gambling and asked Thaddeus to conceal it by attributing the loss to Malaga. Thaddeus reluctantly agreed, burdened by obligation. Clementine discovered Thaddeus’s financial generosity toward Paz and expressed frustration, questioning his loyalty. Thaddeus felt the weight of her expectations and resolved to manage her affairs more effectively, taking it upon himself to teach her household management so she could handle matters independently.

As spring approached Adam fell seriously ill, and Thaddeus devoted himself to nursing him. Clementine, aware of Adam’s precarious condition, sought the best medical care. Thaddeus’s dedication was unwavering; through attentive care he managed to keep the count alive. After weeks of nursing, Adam’s condition improved and Thaddeus received praise, yet he was tormented by his feelings for Clementine and the certainty that he could never have her. He decided to leave for the Caucasus, believing it the only escape from unrequited love.

Before departing Thaddeus wrote a heartfelt letter to Clementine, expressing his deep feelings and the sacrifices he had made for her happiness. He acknowledged that remaining in Paris would only prolong his pain. Clementine, on receiving the letter, reflected on her feelings and the loss of a devoted friend. Adam, unaware of the full extent of Thaddeus’s sacrifices, lamented the loss, believing their bond strong enough to withstand trials.

In the years that followed Clementine often thought of Thaddeus, wondering about his fate. She remained unaware of the depth of his devotion, while he continued to live in the shadows, forever changed by his love. Over time Clementine became involved in a new romantic entanglement, but the memory of Thaddeus lingered as a bittersweet reminder of a love that could never be.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

The Marquise de Listomere embodies the Restoration spirit, balancing religious devotion with worldly elegance. Married seven years to the unremarkable Marquis de Listomere, she maintains a virtuous facade to support his political ambitions. Some women reserve judgment until he becomes a peer, but the marquise’s conduct is virtuous and consistent with her principles.

The marquis is bland, notable for his lack of qualities rather than merit. A silent deputy who votes correctly but lacks charisma, he is nicknamed “dull weather.” His unremarkable nature is seen as a safe match for a virtuous woman, as he is incapable of folly.

Despite her reserve, the marquise commands respect and scorn from men who attempt to flirt with her; she shows only contempt. She converses with agreeable men without arousing gossip, unlike others who pursue ulterior motives. She does not play coy; she is genuinely principled.

The narrator, who admires her, describes her physical presence: not plain nor conventionally pretty, she has a captivating air. Her animated eyes reveal a deeper soul, and she charms without seeking it. This quality appears in her recent encounter with Eugène de Rastignac, a clever young man who combines modesty with liveliness.

Rastignac, skilled in social navigation, speaks with the marquise at a ball on topics from opera to women’s duties. Their exchange shows mutual interest, though he abruptly leaves afterward. The next morning Rastignac reflects, finding himself preoccupied with thoughts of her.

Writing in a comfortable setting, he becomes increasingly absorbed in feelings for the marquise. His thoughts drift to the pleasures of romance before he is interrupted by his friend Horace, with whom he discusses matters including a possible expedition to Morea.

Meanwhile, the marquise receives a letter from Rastignac while dressing. It begins with affectionate terms, provoking curiosity. As she reads, her initial impulse to discard it becomes a desire to understand his sentiments. The letter’s conclusion leaves her fatigued; she reflects on Rastignac’s impertinence and the implications of his words.

This episode captures the tension between her virtuous exterior and the stirrings of emotion within, setting up the complexities of her character and the dynamics of her relationships.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Madame de Listomere forbade her porter to admit Monsieur de Rastignac and resolved to treat him with more than disdain; his recent insolence surpassed previous offenses she had tolerated. She had considered keeping a letter he sent but ultimately burned it.

"Madame received a fine love-letter," Caroline remarked to the housemaid, who was surprised.

That evening, Madame de Listomere went to a party at the Marquis de Beauseant, expecting Rastignac. By two in the morning she realized he would not come, despite intending to confront him with coldness. Stendhal described this mental process as "crystallization."

Four days later Rastignac scolded his valet, Joseph, for misplacing letters he had been given. Joseph, distracted, finally remembered one was for Madame de Listomere and the other for Rastignac's lawyer. When Rastignac learned the letter was not for Madame de Nucingen, he laughed, realizing his heart had wandered.

Eugene's errors began when he planned to amuse Madame de Listomere by discussing the letter's blunder. He worsened matters by staying away for several days, letting her resentment harden.

When he finally called, the porter said Madame de Listomere was out. The Marquis de Listomere, unaware of tensions, invited him in. As they climbed the stairs, Rastignac noticed several social faux pas.

On seeing Eugene, Madame de Listomere blushed, which he observed. He thought, "What! that fortress, too?" and adjusted his cravat. Monsieur de Listomere, absorbed in his newspaper, missed the undercurrent.

Madame de Listomere quickly composed herself. When Eugene asked about her health, she assured him she was well. He suggested his visit might be unwelcome and blamed the marquis's introduction. She replied that her husband did not know all her secrets, hinting at the complexity of their relationship.

Eugene tried to lighten the mood, suggesting silence as his best excuse. Madame de Listomere promised to forget, maintaining dignity. When he mentioned the letter, she feigned offense but could not hide amusement.

Eugene insisted he meant no offense and that the letter was not for her. Madame de Listomere, while pretending to be hurt, said she could laugh at the affair but warned him against more mistakes.

Monsieur de Listomere, still engrossed in his paper, interrupted to announce a mutual acquaintance's death. The ensuing discussion revealed the marquise's irritation with Eugene's earlier remarks. He naively proposed she might be trying to extract a secret from him, which only increased her curiosity.

When Eugene named Madame de Nucingen, Madame de Listomere's demeanor shifted. She rang for wood, signaling it was time for him to leave, and coldly confronted him about the letter, asking how his pen could have written her name. Discomfited, Eugene stammered before departing.

Days later Madame de Listomere confirmed Eugene's claim about the letter's true recipient. She withdrew from society; the marquis explained her absence as a stomach ailment, while her physician diagnosed a nervous condition she used to justify seclusion.

The narrative highlights the complexities of social interaction and misunderstanding, particularly in romantic contexts, illustrating characters' emotional reactions and social roles.

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############

######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########

In Paris, balls often have two parts: an official party of superficial pleasantries and a more intimate follow-up where genuine conversation and laughter flourish. The latter, often hosted by women like Madame d’Espard and Mademoiselle des Touches, preserves the old spirit of French wit and allows free-flowing ideas and camaraderie.

One evening at Mademoiselle des Touches' salon gathered notable figures, including the Minister de Marsay, for lively discourse. The atmosphere, charged with wit and camaraderie, showcased the conversational style Paris is known for. The evening's charm lay in the ease of exchange as anecdotes and confessions flowed, creating a delightful tapestry of interaction.

As the night progressed, conversation turned to de Marsay's early love, notably a passionate affair with a woman six years his senior. He recalled youthful infatuation marked by intense devotion and secrecy. To shield their relationship from prying eyes, he kept a façade of indifference.

He described their love as a careful dance of appearances and emotions. He rode past her carriage, exchanging silent signals through the flowers she carried. Their bond thrived on mutual admiration and the thrill of secrecy, while he remained blind to betrayal.

A turning point came when he fell ill and sent a note canceling a meeting. Overcome with jealousy, he nevertheless visited her despite his condition. He saw her with another man, igniting a storm of emotion. Yet her calm demeanor and tender concern momentarily soothed his doubts.

His narrative darkened as he grappled with betrayal. He felt a profound internal shift, an end to his innocence and the start of a more cynical view of love. He reflected on revenge and the complexities of passion, eventually asserting that true love should transcend the desire for vengeance.

His story resonated with the audience, prompting reflection on love, jealousy, and the social expectations that shape relationships. It served as a poignant reminder of trust's fragility and love's transformative power, leaving a lasting impression.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Cured of my cold and my pure love, I embarked on an adventure with a charming heroine, whose beauty contrasted sharply with that of my deceptive angel. I avoided quarreling with this clever woman, suspecting that true love might not equal the pleasures of her skilled deceit. Such refined hypocrisy can be as good as virtue.

I sought a skilled artist in the Rue Boucher to make a keepsake from my hair for my new angel. He had a monopoly on hair mementos and showed me work surpassing fairy tales, explaining the latest trends, including marking linen with hair. When I recognized a handkerchief he had worked on, my doubts about the authenticity of women’s affections were confirmed. I left the shop still believing in pleasure but skeptical about love.

Two months later I was in the boudoir of this ethereal being, holding her hand as we explored sentiment. In a tender moment I seized the chance to elicit her most delightful lies. Charlotte professed her love, claiming I was the only man in her world and that she had been seeking ways to bind me to her eternally.

The women listening to de Marsay seemed offended by his portrayal of their airs and attitudes. Just as I might have believed her sweet falsehoods, I asked, “When are you to marry the Duke?” The directness startled her; her reaction revealed guilt. She feigned astonishment, but I pressed on, suggesting she should marry him, since he was rich and devout.

Charlotte fell to her knees, insisting she loved no one but me. I urged truthfulness; she swore before God. I confessed my doubts about her fidelity, which she quickly turned against me, claiming I no longer loved her. She sought to escape, but curiosity made her ask what I had seen. I revealed that I had noticed her attending mass with the Duke several times, which she dismissed as jealousy.

I argued her marriage to the Duke would elevate her socially and advised her to embrace the role; the Duke was virtuous and would expect a prude. Charlotte, in tears, lamented that if I had spoken sooner we could have been happy. I maintained it was too late; she had gone too far with the Duke.

She worried about the Duke’s suspicions; I reassured her he likely had none, though she needed to prove her devotion. As she paced the boudoir her demeanor shifted: she held out her hand, called me noble and charming, and vowed never to forget me.

Her tactics were admirable, and I played the distressed lover. She then asked what would become of me, and I revealed advances to another woman. This startled Charlotte, who accused me of unfaithfulness; I countered that she had too much wit for her heart to suffer.

Charlotte declared disillusionment with love, claiming women could never be truly loved. I acknowledged her anger but argued that taking love seriously in society was like romanticizing an actress. She accused me of betrayal; I feigned submission and asked if she would remember her grievances once she became a duchess.

Her bitterness confirmed she still felt wronged, and I realized I had successfully distanced myself. I had studied the lives of successful men with women and believed I had mastered the art of retreat.

The women in the room reacted with sympathy for Charlotte, while de Marsay wore a slight smile. The conversation shifted to the decline of social distinctions. Lady Dudley noted women often did not view their marriages as acts of inconstancy. De Marsay lamented the erosion of the aristocracy, saying duchesses and marquises were disappearing and the Civil Code had leveled hierarchies.

Blondet remarked that aristocracy now began with viscountesses, while de Marsay thought countesses might survive in some form. He reflected on how the last great ladies had vanished with the splendor of the previous century, and the Code had inadvertently created a new class of women.

The Comte de Vandenesse observed that the current structure allowed anyone to claim nobility; Lord Dudley added that property had not been organized, leading to the aristocracy’s decline.

Joseph Bridau said the beauty of womanhood had diminished and the great lady’s fan was broken. Daniel d’Arthez noted the aristocracy had retreated, leaving behind women who might once have shaped society.

The conversation turned to the modern “perfect lady,” a product of the era: she embodied good taste and grace but lacked the grandeur of past great ladies. Mademoiselle des Touches questioned the notion of progress, while Madame de Nucingen argued a woman’s worth was no longer tied to lineage but to character and presentation.

Count Adam Laginski asked what constituted a “perfect lady.” Émile Blondet described her as one who exudes elegance and refinement, effortlessly commanding respect and admiration—graceful and poised in various social settings.

The discussion highlighted the complexities of modern femininity, where the “perfect lady” navigates societal expectations with skill and charm, blending beauty and intellect to captivate those around her.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Now, you will not talk for half an hour with a bourgeoise without her mentioning her husband,” Blondet remarked gravely. “In contrast, even if you know a lady is married, she will skillfully conceal her husband, making it a challenge to discover him. Often, you might only notice her gazing at a middle-aged man with a decoration who bows and leaves. She has ordered her carriage and departs.

“You are not the rose, but you have been with the rose, and you go to bed under a golden canopy of a delightful dream, which may linger even after Sleep has opened the ivory gates of dreams.

“At home, the lady sees no one before four; she is clever enough to keep you waiting. Her house is tastefully arranged; her luxury is for daily use and regularly refreshed. You will find no dusty glass shades or remnants of wrappings. The staircase is warm, and flowers abound—her only accepted gift, and only from select individuals, as they symbolize a promise. Fashionable trinkets are scattered about, but not in a way that resembles a museum. She will be seated by the fire in a low chair, not rising to greet you. Her conversation at home differs from that at the ball; there, she was our creditor; here, she owes you the pleasure of her wit. These are the nuances of which she is a master. What she seeks in you is a man to expand her circle, an object for her attentions. To draw you into her drawing-room, she will be enchantingly charming. This highlights how isolated women are today and why they desire a little world of their own, where they can shine like a constellation. Conversation often relies on generalities.

“Yes,” de Marsay agreed, “you have pinpointed the flaw of our age. The epigram—a volume in a word—no longer strikes at people or things but at sordid events, fading quickly.”

“Hence,” Blondet continued, “a lady’s intelligence, if she possesses any, lies in casting doubt on everything. Herein lies the difference between two women; the townswoman is virtuous, while the lady hesitates and struggles with her virtue. This uncertainty is one of the last graces left to her by our dreadful times. She rarely attends church but will discuss religion; if you express free thought, she will attempt to convert you, leading to the familiar phrases and gestures understood by these women: ‘For shame! I thought you had too much sense to attack religion. Society is tottering, and you deprive it of its support. Religion means you and me; it is property and the future of our children! Let us not be selfish! Individualism is the disease of the age, and religion is the only remedy; it unites families which your laws put asunder,’ and so forth. She then delves into a neo-Christian discourse laced with political notions that is neither Catholic nor Protestant—but moral? Oh, very moral!—a blend of modern doctrines at odds with one another.”

The women laughed at Blondet’s satirical portrayal.

“This explanation, dear Count Adam,” Blondet said to the Pole, “will show you that the ‘perfect lady’ embodies both intellectual and political muddle, surrounded by the flashy yet fleeting products of an industry that aims to destroy its work to replace it with something new. When you leave her, you think: She certainly has superior ideas! You believe it because she has delicately probed your heart and asked for your secrets; she feigns ignorance to learn everything. There are things she never knows, even when she does. You alone will feel uneasy, knowing nothing of her heart’s state. The great ladies of old flaunted their affairs, while today’s lady has her passions neatly arranged like a musical score, with its notes and rests, its pauses and sharps. A mere weak woman, she is anxious not to compromise her love, her husband, or her children’s future. Name, position, and fortune no longer shield her from scrutiny. The aristocracy no longer stands united to protect her. Unlike the great lady of the past, she lacks the demeanor of lofty antagonism; she can crush nothing underfoot; it is she who would be crushed. Thus, she is adept at Jesuitical compromises, navigating between propriety and anonymous passions. She fears her servants as an Englishwoman fears a divorce trial. This woman—so free at a ball, so charming while walking—is a slave at home; she is never independent except in perfect privacy or theoretically. Her task is to maintain her position as a lady.

“For in our day, a woman repudiated by her husband, reduced to a meager allowance, without a carriage, luxury, or opera box, is no longer a wife, maid, or townswoman; she is adrift, becoming a chattel. The Carmelites will not accept a married woman; it would be bigamy. Would her lover still want her? That is the question. Thus, your perfect lady may give rise to calumny, but never to slander.”

“It is all so horribly true,” said the Princesse de Cadignan.

“And so,” Blondet continued, “our ‘perfect lady’ exists between English hypocrisy and the frankness of the eighteenth century—a bastard system, symptomatic of an age where nothing that emerges resembles what has vanished, where transition leads nowhere, and everything is a matter of degree; all great figures fade into the background, and distinction becomes purely personal. I am convinced that it is impossible for a woman, even if born close to a throne, to acquire before the age of twenty-five the encyclopedic knowledge of trifles, the practice of maneuvering, the important small things, the musical tones and harmony of coloring, the angelic mischief and innocent cunning, the speech and silence, the seriousness and banter, the wit and obtuseness, the diplomacy and ignorance that make up the perfect lady.”

“And where, according to your sketch,” Mademoiselle des Touches asked Blondet, “would you classify the female author? Is she a perfect lady, a woman _comme il faut?_”

“When she has no genius, she is a woman _comme il n’en faut pas_,” Blondet replied, emphasizing the words with a glance that could be read as praise for Camille Maupin. “This epigram is not mine, but Napoleon’s,” he added.

“You need not hold a grudge against Napoleon for that,” Canalis interjected emphatically. “He was weak in being jealous of literary genius—he had his flaws. Who can ever explain, depict, or understand Napoleon? A man with arms folded, who did everything, the greatest force known, the most concentrated, the most caustic; a singular genius who spread armed civilization without fixing it anywhere; a man who could do everything because he willed everything; a prodigious phenomenon of will, conquering illness through battle, yet destined to die of disease in bed after living amid balls and bullets; a man with a code and a sword in his brain, word and deed; a clear-sighted spirit that foresaw everything but his own fall; a capricious politician who risked men by handfuls for economy, yet spared three heads—those of Talleyrand, Pozzo de Borgo, and Metternich, diplomats whose deaths would have saved the French Empire, and who seemed to him of greater weight than thousands of soldiers; a man to whom nature, as a rare privilege, gave a heart in a bronze frame; mirthful and kind at midnight among women, and the next morning manipulating Europe as a young girl might amuse herself by splashing water in her bath! Hypocritical and generous; loving tawdriness and simplicity; devoid of taste, yet protecting the arts; and despite these contradictions, truly great in everything by instinct or temperament; Caesar at twenty-five, Cromwell at thirty; and then, like my grocer buried in Père Lachaise, a good husband and father. In short, he improvised public works, empires, kings, codes, verses, a romance—and all with more range than precision. Did he not aim to make all Europe France? And after making us weigh on the earth in such a way as to change the laws of gravitation, he left us poorer than on the day he first laid hands on us; while he, who had taken an empire by his name, lost his name on the frontier of his empire in a sea of blood and soldiers. A man of thought and action, who understood Desaix and Fouché.”

“All despotism and all justice at the right moments. The true king!” de Marsay declared.

“Ah! what a pleasure it is to digest what you talk about,” Baron de Nucingen remarked.

“But do you think the treat we are giving you is a common one?” Joseph Bridau asked. “If you had to pay for the charms of conversation as you do for those of dancing or music, your fortune would be inadequate! There is no second performance of the same flash of wit.”

“And are we really so much diminished as these gentlemen think?” the Princesse de Cadignan asked, smiling skeptically at the women. “Because, in these days, under a regime that makes everything small, you prefer small dishes, small rooms, small pictures, small articles, small newspapers, small books, does that prove that women have grown smaller? Why should the human heart change because you change your coat? In all ages, passions remain the same. I know of beautiful devotion and sublime sufferings that lack the publicity—the glory, if you will—that once gave luster to the errors of some women. But even if one has not saved a King of France, one is not less an Agnès Sorel. Do you believe our dear Marquise d’Espard is not the equal of Madame Doublet or Madame du Deffant, in whose rooms so much evil was spoken and done? Is not Taglioni a match for Camargo? Or Malibran the equal of Saint-Huberti? Are not our poets superior to those of the eighteenth century? If at this moment, due to the Grocers who govern us, we lack a style of our own, did not the Empire have its distinguishing stamp as the age of Louis XV. did, and was not its splendor fabulous? Have the sciences lost anything?”

“I completely agree, madame; the women of this age are truly great,” the Comte de Vandenesse replied. “When posterity looks back, will not Madame Récamier appear in proportions as fine as those of the most beautiful women of the past? We have made so much history that historians will be lacking. The age of Louis XIV. had but one Madame de Sévigné; we have a thousand now in Paris who certainly write better than she did and do not publish their letters. Whether the Frenchwoman is called ‘perfect lady’ or great lady, she will always be the woman among women.

“Émile Blondet has painted a picture of the fascinations of a woman of the day; but, when needed, this creature who bristles or shows off, who echoes the ideas of Mr. This and Mr. That, could be heroic. And it must be said, your faults, mesdames, are all the more poetic because they are always surrounded by greater perils. I have seen much of the world, perhaps too late; but in cases where the illegality of your feelings might be excused, I have always observed the effects of what you may call Providence, inevitably overwhelming those we consider light women.”

“I hope,” Madame de Vandenesse said, “that we can be great in other ways——”

“Oh, let the Comte de Vandenesse preach to us!” Madame de Serizy exclaimed.

“With all the more reason because he has preached a great deal by example,” the Baronne de Nucingen added.

“On my honor!” General de Montriveau said, “in all the dramas—a word you are very fond of,” he said, looking at Blondet—“in which the finger of God has been visible, the most frightful I ever knew was very near being by my act——”

“Well, tell us all about it!” Lady Barimore cried. “I love to shudder!”

“It is the taste of a virtuous woman,” de Marsay replied, glancing at Lord Dudley’s lovely daughter.

“During the campaign of 1812,” General de Montriveau began, “I was the involuntary cause of a terrible disaster which may be of use to you, Doctor Bianchon,” turning to me, “since, while you devote yourself to the human body, you concern yourself a good deal with the mind; it may help solve some problems of the will.

“I was going through my second campaign; I enjoyed danger and laughed at everything, like the young and foolish lieutenant of artillery that I was. When we reached the Beresina, the army had lost all discipline and forgotten military obedience. It was a medley of men of all nations, instinctively making their way from north to south. Soldiers would drive a general in rags and barefoot away from their fire if he brought neither wood nor food. After crossing this famous river, disorder did not diminish. I had come quietly and alone, without food, out of the marshes of Zembin, wandering in search of a house where I might be taken in. Finding none or being driven away from those I encountered, I finally spotted a wretched little Polish farm, which you can only imagine if you have seen the wooden houses of Lower Normandy or the poorest farm buildings of la Beauce. These dwellings consist of a single room, with one end divided off by a wooden partition, the smaller division serving as a store-room for forage.

“In the twilight, I could see faint smoke rising above this house. Hoping to find comrades more compassionate than those I had previously encountered, I boldly approached the farm. Inside, I found the table laid. Several officers, along with a woman—a common sight—were eating potatoes, some horseflesh broiled over charcoal, and frozen beetroots. I recognized a few artillery captains from my first regiment. I was welcomed with a shout of acclamation, which would have amazed me on the other side of the Beresina; but at this moment, the cold was less intense; my fellow-officers were warm, they had food, and the room, strewn with straw, promised a delightful night. We did not ask for much in those days. My comrades could be philanthropists for free—one of the commonest ways of being philanthropic. I sat down to eat on one of the straw bundles.

“At the end of the table, by the door leading to the smaller room full of straw, sat my old colonel, one of the most extraordinary men I ever met among the mixed collection of men I encountered. He was Italian. Whenever human nature is truly fine in the South, it is sublime. I do not know if you have noticed the extreme fairness of Italians when they are fair. It is exquisite, especially under artificial light. When I read the fantastical portrait of Colonel Oudet sketched by Charles Nodier, I found my own sensations in every one of his elegant phrases. Italian, as were most of the officers of his regiment, which had been borrowed by the Emperor from Eugene’s army, my colonel was tall, eight or nine inches above average, admirably proportioned—perhaps a little stout, but prodigiously powerful, active, and clean-limbed like a greyhound. His abundant black curls contrasted with his complexion, as white as a woman’s; he had small hands, a shapely foot, a pleasant mouth, and a delicately formed aquiline nose, which would pinch and turn white when he was angry, which happened often. His irascibility was beyond belief; I will not elaborate. No one could remain calm in his presence. I alone, perhaps, was not afraid of him; he had taken such a singular fancy to me that he thought everything I did was right. When he was angry, his brow would knit, and the muscles of his forehead would set in a delta, or, to be more explicit, in Redgauntlet’s horseshoe. This mark was perhaps even more terrifying than the magnetic flashes of his blue eyes. His whole frame quivered, and his strength, great as it was in his normal state, became almost unbounded.

“He spoke with a strong guttural roll. His voice, at least as powerful as that of Charles Nodier’s Oudet, conveyed an incredible fullness of tone into the syllable or consonant in which this burr was sounded. Though this faulty pronunciation could sometimes be a grace, when commanding his men or when excited, you cannot imagine, unless you had heard it, the force expressed by this accent, which is so common in Paris. When the Colonel was calm, his blue eyes were angelically sweet, and his smooth brow had a charming expression. On parade, or with the army of Italy, no man could compare with him. Indeed, d’Orsay himself, the handsome d’Orsay, was eclipsed by our colonel during the last review held by Napoleon before the invasion of Russia.

“Everything about this exceptional man was in contrast. Passion thrives on contrast. Thus, you need not ask whether he exerted over women the irresistible influences to which our nature yields”—and the general looked at the Princesse de Cadignan—“as vitreous matter is molded under the pipe of the glass-blower; still, by a singular fatality—an observer might explain the phenomenon—the Colonel was not a lady-killer or indifferent to such successes.

“To illustrate his violence, I will recount a brief incident. We were dragging our guns up a narrow road bordered by a high slope on one side and thickets on the other. Halfway up, we encountered another regiment of artillery, its colonel marching at the head. This colonel wanted our captain to back down, but the captain refused. The other colonel ordered his foremost battery to advance, and despite the driver’s care to keep among the scrub, the wheel of the first gun struck our captain’s right leg, breaking it and throwing him over on the near side of his horse. All this happened in an instant. Our Colonel, who was nearby, sensed a quarrel; he galloped up, riding among the guns at the risk of falling with his horse’s four feet in the air, and reached the spot just as the captain fell, calling out ‘Help!’ No, our Italian colonel was no longer human! Foam like champagne rose to his lips; he roared inarticulately like a lion. Incapable of uttering a word or even a cry, he made a terrifying signal to his opponent, pointing to the wood and drawing his sword. The two colonels stepped aside. In two seconds, we saw our Colonel’s opponent stretched on the ground, his skull split in two. The soldiers of his regiment backed away—yes, by heaven, and quickly too.

“The captain, who had nearly been crushed and lay yelping in the puddle where the gun carriage had thrown him, had an Italian wife, a beautiful Sicilian from Messina, who was not indifferent to our Colonel. This circumstance aggravated his rage. He was pledged to protect the husband, bound to defend him as he would have defended the woman herself.

“Now, in the hovel beyond Zembin, where I was so well received, this captain was sitting opposite me, and his wife was at the other end of the table, facing the Colonel. This Sicilian was a little woman named Rosina, very dark, but with all the fire of the Southern sun in her black almond-shaped eyes. At this moment, she was terribly thin; her face was covered with dust, like fruit exposed to the drought of a highroad. Scarcely clothed in rags, exhausted by marches, her hair disheveled and clinging together under a piece of shawl tied over her head, she still possessed the graces of a woman; her movements were engaging, her small rose mouth and white teeth, the outline of her features and figure, charms which misery, cold, and neglect had not entirely defaced, still suggested love to any man who could think of a woman. Rosina had one of those frames that appear fragile but are wiry and full of spring. Her husband, a gentleman from Piedmont, had a face expressive of ironical simplicity, if it is permissible to combine those two words. Brave and well-informed, he seemed unaware of the connections that had existed between his wife and the Colonel for three years. I attributed this unconcern to Italian manners or some domestic secret; yet there was one feature in the man’s countenance that filled me with involuntary distrust. His thin, restless under lip turned down at the corners instead of up, which I thought betrayed a streak of cruelty in a character that seemed phlegmatic and indolent.

“As you can imagine, the conversation was not very lively when I arrived. My weary comrades ate in silence; they asked me some questions, and we shared our misadventures, mingled with reflections on the campaign, the generals, their mistakes, the Russians, and the cold. A minute after my arrival, the colonel, having finished his meager meal, wiped his mustache, bid us goodnight, shot a black look at the Italian woman, saying, ‘Rosina?’ and then, without waiting for a reply, went into the little barn full of hay to sleep. The meaning of the Colonel’s utterance was clear. The young wife responded with an indescribable gesture, expressing all the annoyance she could not feel at seeing her thralldom flaunted without human decency, and the offense to her dignity as a woman and to her husband. But there was also, in the rigid setting of her features and the tight knitting of her brows, a sort of presentiment; perhaps she foresaw her fate. Rosina remained quietly in her place.

“A minute later, and apparently when the Colonel was snug in his straw or hay bed, he repeated, ‘Rosina?’

“The tone of this second call was even more brutally demanding than the first. Rosina turned pale, but she rose, passed behind us, and went to the Colonel.

“All the party sat in utter silence; I, unfortunately, after looking at them all, began to laugh, and then they all laughed too. ‘Tu ridi?—you laugh?’ said the husband.

“‘On my honor, old comrade,’ I replied, becoming serious again, ‘I confess that I was wrong; I ask your pardon a thousand times, and if you are not satisfied with my apologies, I am ready to give you satisfaction.’

“‘Oh! it is not you who are wrong, it is I!’ he replied coldly.

“Thereupon we all lay down in the room, and before long, all were sound asleep.

“Next morning, each one, without waking his neighbor or seeking companionship, set out again on his way, with that selfishness which made our rout one of the most horrible dramas of self-seeking, melancholy, and horror ever enacted under heaven. Nevertheless, about seven or eight hundred paces from our shelter, we most of us met again and walked on together, like geese led in flocks by a child’s wilful tyranny. The same necessity urged us all.

“Having reached a knoll where we could still see the farmhouse where we had spent the night, we heard sounds resembling the roar of lions in the desert, the bellowing of bulls—no, it was a noise that can be compared to no known cry. Yet, mingling with this horrible and ominous roar, we could hear a woman’s feeble scream. We all looked around, seized by an impulse of terror; we no longer saw the house, but a huge bonfire. The farmhouse had been barricaded and was in flames. Swirls of smoke carried hoarse cries and an indescribable pungent smell. A few yards behind, the captain was quietly approaching to join our caravan; we gazed at him in silence, for no one dared question him; but he, understanding our curiosity, pointed to his breast with the forefinger of his right hand, and, waving his left in the direction of the fire, said, ‘_Son’io_.’

“We all walked on without saying a word to him.”

“There is nothing more terrible than the revolt of a sheep,” de Marsay remarked.

“It would be frightful to let us leave with this horrible picture in our memory,” Madame de Montcornet said. “I shall dream of it——”

“And what was the punishment of Monsieur de Marsay’s ‘First’?” Lord Dudley asked, smiling.

“When the English jest, their foils have the buttons on,” Blondet replied.

“Monsieur Bianchon can tell us, for he saw her dying,” de Marsay said, turning to me.

“Yes,” I replied; “and her end was one of the most beautiful I ever witnessed. The Duke and I had spent the night by the dying woman’s pillow; pulmonary consumption, in its last stage, left no hope; she had taken the sacrament the day before. The Duke had fallen asleep. The Duchess, waking around four in the morning, signed to me in the most touching way, with a friendly smile, to leave him to rest, while she was about to die. She had become incredibly thin, but her face retained its sublime outline and features. Her pallor made her skin look like porcelain with a light within. Her bright eyes and color contrasted with this languidly elegant complexion, and her countenance was full of expressive calm. She seemed to pity the Duke, and this feeling stemmed from a lofty tenderness that, as death approached, seemed boundless. The silence was absolute. The room, softly lit by a lamp, resembled every sickroom at the hour of death.

“At this moment, the clock struck. The Duke awoke, despairing at having fallen asleep. I did not see the gesture of impatience he made, expressing regret for having lost sight of his wife for a few of the last moments granted to him; but it is certain that anyone but the dying woman might have misunderstood it. A busy statesman, always thinking of France’s interests, the Duke had a thousand odd ways on the surface, which often lead a genius to be mistaken for a madman, and whose explanation lies in the exacting needs of their intellect. He came to sit in an armchair by his wife’s side and looked fixedly at her. The dying woman reached out her hand, took her husband’s, and clasped it feebly; in a low but agitated voice, she said, ‘My poor dear, who is left to understand you now?’ Then she died, looking at him.”

“The stories the doctor tells us,” the Comte de Vandenesse remarked, “always leave a deep impression.”

“But a sweet one,” Mademoiselle des Touches said, rising.

PARIS, June 1839-42.

######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

“Ah! madame,” replied the doctor, “I have some appalling stories in my collection. But each has its proper hour in a conversation—you know Chamfort’s jest to the Duc de Fronsac: ‘Between your sally and the present moment lie ten bottles of champagne.’”

“But it is two in the morning, and the story of Rosina has prepared us,” said the mistress of the house.

“Tell us, Monsieur Bianchon!” cried everyone.

The obliging doctor bowed, and silence reigned.

“At about a hundred paces from Vendôme, on the banks of the Loir,” he began, “stands an old brown house, crowned with very high roofs, so isolated that there is nothing near it, not even a fetid tannery or a squalid tavern. In front is a garden down to the river, where box shrubs once clipped to edge the walks now straggle at will. A few willows rooted in the stream have grown up quickly like an enclosing fence, half hiding the house. Wild plants have clothed the bank; the fruit trees, neglected for ten years, no longer bear, and their suckers form a thicket. The espaliers resemble a copse. The paths, once graveled, are overgrown—there is no trace of a path.

“From the hilltop where the ruins of the old castle of the Dukes of Vendôme cling, one can see this spot must once have been the joy of a country gentleman devoted to horticulture, especially to choice fruit. An arbor, or rather its wreck, with a table still standing, remains. The aspect of this garden evokes the peaceful life of the provinces, like reading a worthy tradesman’s epitaph. To complete the mournful impression, one wall bears a sundial graced with the motto, ‘Ultimam cogita.’

“The roof is dreadfully dilapidated; the shutters are always closed; the balconies are hung with swallows’ nests; the doors forever shut. Straggling grasses outline flagstones; the ironwork is rusty. Moon and sun, winter, summer, and snow have eaten into the wood, warped the boards, and peeled off the paint. The dreary silence is broken only by birds and cats, polecats, rats, and mice, free to scamper around. An invisible hand has written over it all: ‘Mystery.’

“If you look at this house from the street, you will see a large gate with a round-arched top, full of holes made by children. I later learned that this door had been blocked for ten years. Through these breaches you see the courtyard side in perfect harmony with the garden side: the same ruin. Weeds outline the paving-stones; the walls are scored by enormous cracks; the blackened coping is laced with pellitory. The stone steps are disjointed; the bell-cord is rotten; the gutter-spouts broken. What fire from heaven could have fallen there? By what decree has salt been sown on this dwelling? Has God been mocked here? Or was France betrayed? These are the questions we ask ourselves. Reptiles crawl over it, but give no reply. This empty, deserted house is a vast enigma.

“It was formerly a little domain, held in fief, known as La Grande Bretèche. During my stay at Vendôme, where Despleins had left me in charge of a rich patient, the sight of this strange dwelling became one of my keenest pleasures. Was it not better than a ruin? A ruin keeps memories; this house, still standing though slowly destroyed by an avenging hand, contained a secret, an unrevealed thought. More than once in the evening I climbed the wild hedge surrounding the enclosure, braving scratches to enter this ownerless garden, lingering for hours in its disorder. I would not have asked a single question of any gossiping native for the price of the story to which this strange scene was due. There I wove romances and indulged in melancholy that enchanted me. Had I known the reason for the neglect, I would have lost the unwritten poetry that intoxicated me. To me, this refuge represented phases of human life shadowed by misfortune; sometimes the peace of a graveyard without the dead, who speak in the language of epitaphs; one day I saw it as the home of lepers; another, the house of the Atridae; but above all, I found there provincial life, contemplative and hour-glass-like. I often wept there; I never laughed.

“More than once, involuntary terrors seized me as I heard overhead the dull hum of some hurrying wood-pigeon. The earth is dank; you must watch for lizards, vipers, and frogs wandering about with wild freedom; above all, you must have no fear of cold, for in a few moments you feel an icy cloak settle on your shoulders, like the Commendatore’s hand on Don Giovanni’s neck.

“One evening I felt a shudder; the wind had turned an old rusty weathercock, and the creaking sounded like a cry from the house, just as I was finishing a gloomy drama to account for this monumental embodiment of woe. I returned to my inn, lost in gloomy thoughts. After supper, the hostess came into my room with an air of mystery, saying, ‘Monsieur, here is Monsieur Regnault.’

“‘Who is Monsieur Regnault?’

“‘What, sir, do you not know Monsieur Regnault?—Well, that’s odd,’ she said, leaving the room.

“Suddenly a tall, slim man dressed in black appeared, hat in hand, entering like a ram. He had a receding forehead, a small pointed head, and a colorless face like dirty water. You would have taken him for an usher. His coat was old, worn at the seams, but he had a diamond in his shirt frill and gold rings in his ears.

“‘Monsieur,’ I said, ‘whom have I the honor of addressing?’ He took a chair, placed himself in front of my fire, put his hat on my table, and answered while rubbing his hands: ‘Dear me, it is very cold.—Monsieur, I am Monsieur Regnault.’

“I encouraged myself with the thought: Seek!

“‘I am,’ he continued, ‘notary at Vendôme.’

“‘I am delighted to hear it, monsieur,’ I exclaimed. ‘But I am not in a position to make a will for reasons best known to myself.’

“‘One moment!’ he said, holding up his hand as if to gain silence. ‘Allow me, monsieur! I am informed that you sometimes walk in the garden of la Grande Bretèche.’

“‘Yes, monsieur.’

“‘One moment! That constitutes a misdemeanor. Monsieur, as executor under the will of the late Comtesse de Merret, I come in her name to beg you to discontinue the practice. One moment! I am not a Turk and do not wish to make a crime of it. Besides, you are free to be ignorant of the circumstances which compel me to leave the finest mansion in Vendôme to fall into ruin. Nevertheless, you must be a man of education, and you should know that the laws forbid, under heavy penalties, any trespass on enclosed property. A hedge is the same as a wall. But the state in which the place is left may excuse your curiosity. For my part, I should be quite content to make you free to come and go in the house; but being bound to respect the will of the testatrix, I have the honor, monsieur, to beg that you will go into the garden no more. I myself, monsieur, since the will was read, have never set foot in the house, which, as I had the honor of informing you, is part of the estate of the late Madame de Merret. We have done nothing there but verify the number of doors and windows to assess the taxes I have to pay annually out of the funds left for that purpose by the late Madame de Merret. Ah! my dear sir, her will made a great commotion in the town.’

“The good man paused to blow his nose. I respected his volubility, understanding that the administration of Madame de Merret’s estate had been the most important event of his life, his reputation, his glory, his Restoration. As I was forced to bid farewell to my beautiful reveries and romances, I was to reject learning the truth on official authority.

“‘Monsieur,’ I said, ‘would it be indiscreet if I were to ask you the reasons for such eccentricity?’

“At these words, an expression of pleasure spread across the lawyer’s countenance. He pulled up his collar, took out his snuffbox, opened it, and offered me a pinch; on my refusing, he took a large one. He was happy! A man without a hobby does not know all the good to be had in life. At that moment I understood Sterne’s charming passion and had a perfect idea of the delight with which my uncle Toby, encouraged by Trim, bestrode his hobby-horse.

“‘Monsieur,’ said Monsieur Regnault, ‘I was head-clerk in Monsieur Roguin’s office in Paris. A first-rate house, which you may have heard of? No! An unfortunate bankruptcy made it famous. Not having enough money to purchase a practice in Paris at the inflated prices of 1816, I came here and bought my predecessor’s business. I had relations in Vendôme; among others, a wealthy aunt who allowed me to marry her daughter. Three months after being licensed by the Keeper of the Seals, one evening, as I was going to bed—it was before my marriage—I was sent for by Madame la Comtesse de Merret to her Château of Merret. Her maid, a good girl who is now a servant in this inn, was waiting at my door with the Countess’ own carriage. Ah! one moment! I ought to tell you that Monsieur le Comte de Merret had gone to Paris to die two months before I came here. He came to a miserable end, flinging himself into every kind of dissipation. You understand?

“‘On the day when he left, Madame la Comtesse had quitted la Grande Bretèche, having dismantled it. Some people even say she burnt all the furniture, the hangings—in short, everything used in furnishing the premises now let by the said M.—In short, that she burnt everything in the meadow at Merret. Have you been to Merret, monsieur?—No,’ he answered himself, ‘Ah, it is a very fine place.’

“‘For about three months previously,’ he continued with a jerk of his head, ‘the Count and Countess had lived in an eccentric way; they admitted no visitors; Madame lived on the ground floor, and Monsieur on the first floor. When the Countess was left alone she was never seen except at church. Subsequently at the château she refused to see friends who went to call on her. She was already very much altered when she left la Grande Bretèche to go to Merret. That dear lady—I say dear, for it was she who gave me this diamond, but indeed I saw her but once—was very ill; she had, no doubt, given up all hope, for she died without choosing to send for a doctor; indeed, many fancied she was not quite right in her head. Well, sir, my curiosity was strangely excited by hearing that Madame de Merret had need of my services. Nor was I the only person who took an interest in the affair. That very night, though it was already late, all the town knew that I was going to Merret.

“‘The waiting-woman replied vaguely to my questions on the way; nevertheless, she told me that her mistress had received the Sacrament in the course of the day at the hands of the Curé of Merret and seemed unlikely to live through the night. It was about eleven when I reached the château. I went up the great staircase. After crossing some large, lofty, dark rooms, diabolically cold and damp, I reached the state bedroom where the Countess lay. From the rumors current concerning this lady (monsieur, I should never end if I were to repeat all the tales that were told about her), I had imagined her a coquette. Imagine, then, that I had great difficulty in seeing her in the great bed where she was lying. To light this enormous room, with old-fashioned heavy cornices, and so thick with dust that merely to see it was enough to make you sneeze, she had only an old Argand lamp. Ah! but you have not been to Merret. Well, the bed is one of those old world beds, with a high tester hung with flowered chintz. A small table stood by the bed, on which I saw an ‘Imitation of Christ,’ which, by the way, I bought for my wife, as well as the lamp. There were also a deep armchair for her confidential maid and two small chairs. There was no fire. That was all the furniture, not enough to fill ten lines in an inventory.

“‘My dear sir, if you had seen, as I then saw, that vast room, papered and hung with brown, you would have felt yourself transported into a scene of a romance. It was icy, nay more, funereal,’ and he lifted his hand with a theatrical gesture and paused.

“‘By dint of seeking, as I approached the bed, at last I saw Madame de Merret, under the glimmer of the lamp, which fell on the pillows. Her face was as yellow as wax, and as narrow as two folded hands. The Countess had a lace cap showing her abundant hair, but as white as linen thread. She was sitting up in bed, and seemed to keep upright with great difficulty.
