Her large black eyes, dimmed by fever no doubt, and half-dead already, hardly moved under the bony arch of her eyebrows.—There,’ he added, pointing to his own brow. ‘Her forehead was clammy; her fleshless hands were like bones covered with soft skin; the veins and muscles were perfectly visible. She must have been very handsome; but at this moment I was startled into an indescribable emotion at the sight. Never, said those who wrapped her in her shroud, had any living creature been so emaciated and lived. In short, it was awful to behold! Sickness so consumed that woman, that she was no more than a phantom. Her lips, pale violet, seemed not to move when she spoke to me.

“‘Though my profession has familiarized me with such spectacles, by calling me not infrequently to the bedside of the dying, I confess that families in tears and the agonies I have seen were as nothing in comparison with this lonely and silent woman in her vast château. I heard not the least sound; I did not perceive the movement which the sufferer’s breathing ought to have given to the sheets that covered her, and I stood motionless, absorbed in looking at her in a sort of stupor. In fancy, I am there still. At last her large eyes moved; she tried to raise her right hand, but it fell back on the bed, and she uttered these words, which came like a breath, for her voice was no longer a voice: “I have waited for you with the greatest impatience.” A bright flush rose to her cheeks. It was a great effort to her to speak.

“‘“Madame,” I began.’ She signed to me to be silent. At that moment, the old housekeeper rose and said in my ear, “Do not speak; Madame la Comtesse is not in a state to bear the slightest noise, and what you say might agitate her.”

“‘I sat down. A few instants later, Madame de Merret collected all her remaining strength to move her right hand and slipped it, not without infinite difficulty, under the bolster; she then paused a moment. With a last effort, she withdrew her hand; and when she brought out a sealed paper, drops of perspiration rolled from her brow. “I place my will in your hands—Oh! God! Oh!” and that was all. She clutched a crucifix that lay on the bed, lifted it hastily to her lips, and died.

“‘The expression of her eyes still makes me shudder as I think of it. She must have suffered much! There was joy in her last glance, and it remained stamped on her dead eyes.

“‘I brought away the will, and when it was opened, I found that Madame de Merret had appointed me her executor. She left the whole of her property to the hospital at Vendôme except for a few legacies. But these were her instructions regarding la Grande Bretèche: She ordered me to leave the place, for fifty years from the day of her death, in the state it was at the time of her death, forbidding anyone, whoever he might be, to enter the apartments, prohibiting any repairs whatever, and even settling a salary to pay watchmen if it were necessary to secure the absolute fulfillment of her intentions. At the expiration of that term, if the will of the testatrix has been duly carried out, the house is to become the property of my heirs, for, as you know, a notary cannot take a bequest. Otherwise, la Grande Bretèche reverts to the heirs-at-law, but on condition of fulfilling certain conditions set forth in a codicil to the will, which is not to be opened until the expiration of the said term of fifty years. The will has not been disputed, so——’ And without finishing his sentence, the lanky notary looked at me with an air of triumph; I made him quite happy by offering him my congratulations.

“‘Monsieur,’ I said in conclusion, ‘you have so vividly impressed me that I fancy I see the dying woman whiter than her sheets; her glittering eyes frighten me; I shall dream of her tonight.—But you must have formed some idea as to the instructions contained in that extraordinary will.’

“‘Monsieur,’ said he, with comical reticence, ‘I never allow myself to criticize the conduct of a person who honors me with the gift of a diamond.’

“However, I soon loosened the tongue of the discreet notary of Vendôme, who communicated to me, not without long digressions, the opinions of the deep politicians of both sexes whose judgments are law in Vendôme. But these opinions were so contradictory and diffuse that I was near falling asleep despite my interest in this authentic history. The notary’s ponderous voice and monotonous accent, accustomed no doubt to listen to himself and to make himself listened to by his clients or fellow-townsmen, were too much for my curiosity. Happily, he soon went away.

“‘Ah, ha, monsieur,’ said he on the stairs, ‘a good many persons would be glad to live five-and-forty years longer; but—one moment!’ and he laid the first finger of his right hand to his nostril with a cunning look, as much as to say, ‘Mark my words!—To last as long as that—as long as that,’ said he, ‘you must not be past sixty now.’

“I closed my door, having been roused from my apathy by this last speech, which the notary thought very funny; then I sat down in my armchair, with my feet on the fire-dogs. I had lost myself in a romance à la Radcliffe, constructed on the juridical base given me by Monsieur Regnault, when the door, opened by a woman’s cautious hand, turned on the hinges. I saw my landlady come in, a buxom, florid dame, always good-humored, who had missed her calling in life. She was a Fleming, who ought to have seen the light in a picture by Teniers.

“‘Well, monsieur,’ said she, ‘Monsieur Regnault has no doubt been giving you his history of la Grande Bretèche?’

“‘Yes, Madame Lepas.’

“‘And what did he tell you?’

“I repeated in a few words the creepy and sinister story of Madame de Merret. At each sentence my hostess put her head forward, looking at me with an innkeeper’s keen scrutiny, a happy compromise between the instinct of a police constable, the astuteness of a spy, and the cunning of a dealer.

“‘My good Madame Lepas,’ said I as I ended, ‘you seem to know more about it. Heh? If not, why have you come up to me?’

“‘On my word, as an honest woman——’

“‘Do not swear; your eyes are big with a secret. You knew Monsieur de Merret; what sort of man was he?’

“‘Monsieur de Merret—well, you see he was a man you never could see the top of, he was so tall! A very good gentleman from Picardy, who had, as we say, his head close to his cap. He paid for everything down, so as never to have difficulties with anyone. He was hot-tempered, you see! All our ladies liked him very much.’

“‘Because he was hot-tempered?’ I asked.

“‘Well, maybe,’ she said; ‘and you may suppose, sir, that a man had to have something to show for a figurehead before he could marry Madame de Merret, who, without any reflection on others, was the handsomest and richest heiress in our parts. She had about twenty thousand francs a year. All the town was at the wedding; the bride was pretty and sweet-looking, quite a gem of a woman. Oh, they were a handsome couple in their day!’

“‘And were they happy together?’

“‘Hm, hm! so-so—so far as can be guessed, for, as you may suppose, we of the common sort were not hail-fellow-well-met with them.—Madame de Merret was a kind woman and very pleasant, who had no doubt sometimes to put up with her husband’s tantrums. But though he was rather haughty, we were fond of him. After all, it was his place to behave so. When a man is a born nobleman, you see——’

“‘Still, there must have been some catastrophe for Monsieur and Madame de Merret to part so violently?’

“‘I did not say there was any catastrophe, sir. I know nothing about it.’

“‘Indeed. Well, now, I am sure you know everything.’

“‘Well, sir, I will tell you the whole story.—When I saw Monsieur Regnault go up to see you, it struck me that he would speak to you about Madame de Merret as having to do with la Grande Bretèche. That put it into my head to ask your advice, sir, seeming to me that you are a man of good judgment and incapable of playing a poor woman like me false—for I never did anyone a wrong, and yet I am tormented by my conscience. Up to now, I have never dared to say a word to the people of these parts; they are all chatter-mags, with tongues like knives. And never till now, sir, have I had any traveler here who stayed so long in the inn as you have, and to whom I could tell the history of the fifteen thousand francs——’

“‘My dear Madame Lepas, if there is anything in your story of a nature to compromise me,’ I said, interrupting her flow of words, ‘I would not hear it for all the world.’

“‘You need have no fears,’ said she; ‘you will see.’

“Her eagerness made me suspect that I was not the only person to whom my worthy landlady had communicated the secret of which I was to be the sole possessor, but I listened.

“‘Monsieur,’ said she, ‘when the Emperor sent the Spaniards here, prisoners of war and others, I was required to lodge at the charge of the Government a young Spaniard sent to Vendôme on parole. Notwithstanding his parole, he had to show himself every day to the sub-prefect. He was a Spanish grandee—neither more nor less. He had a name in os and dia, something like Bagos de Férédia. I wrote his name down in my books, and you may see it if you like. Ah! he was a handsome young fellow for a Spaniard, who are all ugly they say. He was not more than five feet two or three in height, but so well made; and he had little hands that he kept so beautifully! Ah! you should have seen them. He had as many brushes for his hands as a woman has for her toilet. He had thick, black hair, a flame in his eye, a somewhat coppery complexion, but which I admired all the same. He wore the finest linen I have ever seen, though I have had princesses to lodge here, and, among others, General Bertrand, the Duc and Duchesse d’Abrantes, Monsieur Descazes, and the King of Spain. He did not eat much, but he had such polite and amiable ways that it was impossible to owe him a grudge for that. Oh! I was very fond of him, though he did not say four words to me in a day, and it was impossible to have the least bit of talk with him; if he was spoken to, he did not answer; it is a way, a mania they all have, it would seem.

“‘He read his breviary like a priest and went to mass and all the services quite regularly. And where did he post himself?—we found this out later.—Within two yards of Madame de Merret’s chapel. As he took that place the very first time he entered the church, no one imagined that there was any purpose in it. Besides, he never raised his nose above his book, poor young man! And then, monsieur, of an evening he went for a walk on the hill among the ruins of the old castle. It was his only amusement, poor man; it reminded him of his native land. They say that Spain is all hills!
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“One evening, shortly after he arrived, he stayed out late. I was uneasy when he didn’t return until midnight, but we were used to his whims; he took the key and we didn’t wait for him. He lived in a house we owned on Rue des Casernes. One of our stable-boys mentioned he thought he saw the Spanish Grandee swimming in the river. When he returned, I warned him about the weeds, and he seemed annoyed at being seen.

“Eventually, one morning, we found he hadn’t returned. Searching his belongings, I discovered a note in his drawer, along with fifty doubloons worth about five thousand francs and a sealed box containing diamonds worth ten thousand francs. The note stated that if he didn’t return, he left us this money and the diamonds to fund masses in gratitude for his escape.

“At that time, my husband went searching for him. Interestingly, he returned with the Spaniard’s clothes, found under a stone on a breakwater near la Grande Bretèche. He went early, so no one saw him. After reading the letter, he burned the clothes and, following Count Férédia’s wishes, we announced he had escaped.

“The sub-prefect sent the constabulary after him, but he was never caught. Lepas believed the Spaniard drowned himself. I, however, think he was involved in the matter concerning Madame de Merret, especially since Rosalie told me that the crucifix her mistress cherished and was buried with was made of ebony and silver; early in his stay, Monsieur Férédia had a similar crucifix, which I never saw again. Now, monsieur, do you think I should feel remorse about the Spaniard’s fifteen thousand francs? Aren’t they truly mine?

“Certainly. But have you ever questioned Rosalie?” I asked.

“Oh, I have, sir. But what can be done? That girl is like a wall. She knows something, but it’s impossible to make her talk.”

After a brief chat, my hostess left me with vague, sinister thoughts, romantic curiosity, and a religious dread, like entering a dark church at night and seeing a faint light under a high vault. La Grande Bretèche, with its overgrown grass, shuttered windows, rusty ironwork, and deserted rooms, came vividly to mind. I felt compelled to explore the mysterious dwelling to uncover the heart of this solemn story, this drama that had claimed three lives.

Rosalie became the most intriguing person in Vendôme to me. Observing her, I sensed an inner thought beneath her blooming health. There was an element of sorrow or hope in her soul; her demeanor suggested a secret, like devout souls who pray excessively or a girl haunted by a child’s last cry. Yet she was simple and clumsy, her vacant smile devoid of criminality; one would deem her innocent seeing the large checked kerchief over her sturdy figure and lilac-and-white-striped gown. No, I resolved, I will not leave Vendôme without knowing the full story of la Grande Bretèche. To achieve this, I would make advances toward Rosalie if necessary.

“Rosalie!” I called one evening.

“Your servant, sir?”

“You’re not married?” She flinched slightly.

“Oh! There’s no shortage of men if I ever fancy being miserable!” she laughed, quickly regaining composure; every woman, from the highest lady to the inn-servant, has a natural presence of mind.

“Yes; you’re attractive enough to never lack suitors! But tell me, Rosalie, why did you become an inn-servant after leaving Madame de Merret? Didn’t she leave you a small annuity?”

“Oh yes, sir. But my position here is the best in all Vendôme.”

Her answer seemed evasive. Rosalie appeared central to this affair; she seemed tied to its core. It wasn’t ordinary courtship material; this girl contained the final chapter of a romance, and from that moment, all my attention was on Rosalie. Studying her, I noticed many admirable qualities: she was clean, neat, and undeniably attractive, soon embodying every charm desire can bestow on a woman, regardless of station. A fortnight after the notary’s visit, one early morning, I said to Rosalie:

“Come, tell me all you know about Madame de Merret.”

“Oh!” she replied, “I will tell you, but keep it a secret.”

“Of course, my child; I will guard your secrets with a thief’s honor, the most loyal known.”

“If it’s all the same to you,” she said, “I’d prefer it be with your own.”

She adjusted her head-kerchief and settled in to tell the tale; a particular attitude of confidence is necessary for storytelling. The best tales are told at a certain hour—just as we are all here at table. No one tells a story well standing or fasting.

If I were to reproduce Rosalie’s diffuse eloquence, a whole volume would scarcely suffice. Now, as the event she recounted stands between the notary’s gossip and Madame Lepas’s account, I will summarize it concisely.

The room at la Grande Bretèche where Madame de Merret slept was on the ground floor; a small cupboard in the wall, about four feet deep, served as her wardrobe. Three months before the evening in question, Madame de Merret had been seriously ill, so much so that her husband had taken to sleeping on the first floor. By chance, he returned home two hours later than usual from the club, where he had been reading the papers and discussing politics. His wife assumed he was in bed and asleep. However, the evening’s animated discussions and his losses at billiards had delayed him.

For some time, Monsieur de Merret had relied on Rosalie to tell him if his wife was in bed; her affirmative responses led him to his room, trusting her completely. But that evening, he decided to check on Madame de Merret to share his misfortunes and perhaps seek comfort. He had noticed her looking particularly lovely at dinner and thought her convalescence had enhanced her beauty, a realization that often comes too late for husbands.

Upon entering, he thought he heard the closet door shut, but when he entered, Madame de Merret stood alone by the fireplace. His instincts were piqued by a nagging doubt, and he noticed an anxious expression in her eyes.

“You’re very late,” she said, her voice slightly husky.

Monsieur de Merret remained silent as Rosalie entered, which startled him. He began pacing the room, arms crossed.

“Have you received bad news, or are you unwell?” his wife asked timidly while Rosalie helped her undress. He didn’t respond.

“You can go, Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said; “I can manage my curl-papers myself.” She sensed disaster in her husband’s demeanor and wanted to be alone with him. Once Rosalie left, or was thought to have left, for she lingered in the passage, Monsieur de Merret confronted his wife, stating coldly, “Madame, there’s someone in your cupboard!” She calmly replied, “No, monsieur.”

This “No” pierced Monsieur de Merret’s heart; he didn’t believe it, yet his wife appeared more saintly than ever. He moved to open the closet door, but Madame de Merret took his hand, stopping him, and said with strange emotion, “Remember, if you find no one there, everything must end between us.”

The extraordinary dignity of her stance filled him with deep respect and inspired a resolve that could become legendary on a grander stage.

“No, Josephine,” he said, “I won’t open it. Regardless of the outcome, we would be forever parted. I know your soul’s purity; you wouldn’t commit a deadly sin to save your life.” At these words, Madame de Merret looked at him with a haggard expression. “Here is your crucifix,” he continued. “Swear to me before God that there’s no one in that closet; I will believe you—I will never open that door.”

Madame de Merret took the crucifix and swore, “I swear it.”

“Louder,” he insisted; “repeat: ‘I swear before God that there is nobody in that closet.’” She repeated the words without flinching.

“That will do,” said Monsieur de Merret coldly. After a moment, he remarked, “You have a fine piece of work there, which I’ve never seen before,” examining the ebony and silver crucifix.

“I found it at Duvivier’s; last year, when that troop of Spanish prisoners passed through Vendôme, he bought it from a Spanish monk.”

“Indeed,” said Monsieur de Merret, hanging the crucifix back on its nail, and he rang for Rosalie.

He waited for her to arrive. “Go to bed, all of you,” he instructed, beckoning Jean, his coachman and confidential servant, to come close. He whispered, “When they’re all asleep—mind, asleep—you understand?—come down and tell me.”

Monsieur de Merret kept his gaze on his wife while giving orders, returning to her at the fireside to discuss the billiards game and the club’s discussions. When Rosalie returned, she found them conversing amiably.

Not long before, Monsieur de Merret had had new ceilings installed in all the reception rooms on the ground floor. Plaster was scarce in Vendôme, and he had ordered a considerable quantity, knowing he could sell any excess. This circumstance inspired his plan.

“Gorenflot is here, sir,” Rosalie whispered.

“Tell him to come in,” he replied aloud.

Madame de Merret paled at the sight of the mason.

“Gorenflot,” said her husband, “go fetch some bricks from the coach-house; bring enough to wall up the cupboard door; you can use the leftover plaster as cement.” Then, pulling Rosalie and the mason close, he whispered, “Listen, Gorenflot, you’ll sleep here tonight; tomorrow morning, I’ll provide you a passport to take you abroad to a place I’ll specify. I’ll give you six thousand francs for your journey. You must live in that town for ten years; if you don’t like it, you may settle elsewhere, but it must be in the same country. Go through Paris and wait there until I join you. I’ll give you an agreement for six thousand francs more, to be paid upon your return, provided you fulfill the conditions of the bargain. In return, you must keep silent about what you do tonight. Rosalie, I’ll secure ten thousand francs for you, which won’t be paid until your wedding day, contingent on your marrying Gorenflot; but to marry, you must remain silent. If not, no wedding gift!”

“Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said, “come and brush my hair.”

Her husband quietly paced the room, keeping an eye on the door, the mason, and his wife, without displaying any suspicion. Gorenflot made some noise. Madame de Merret seized a moment when he was unloading bricks to say to Rosalie: “My dear child, I’ll give you a thousand francs a year if you tell Gorenflot to leave a crack at the bottom.” Then she added casually, “You’d better help him.”

Monsieur and Madame de Merret remained silent while Gorenflot walled up the door. This silence was intentional on the husband’s part; he didn’t want to give his wife the chance to say anything ambiguous. On Madame de Merret’s side, it was either pride or prudence. When the wall was half built, the cunning mason took advantage of his master’s back being turned to break one of the two panes in the top of the door with his pick. Madame de Merret understood that Rosalie had spoken to Gorenflot. They all three then saw the face of a dark, gloomy-looking man with black hair and fiery eyes.

Before her husband turned around, the poor woman nodded to the stranger, signaling “Hope.”

At four o’clock, as dawn broke in September, the work was completed. The mason was placed in charge of Jean, and Monsieur de Merret slept in his wife’s room.

The next morning, he casually mentioned, “Oh, by the way, I must go to the Maire for the passport.” He donned his hat, took a few steps toward the door, paused, and grabbed the crucifix. His wife trembled with joy.

“He will go to Duvivier’s,” she thought.

As soon as he left, Madame de Merret rang for Rosalie and cried in a terrible voice, “The pick! Bring the pick! We must work. I saw how Gorenflot did it yesterday; we have time to make a gap and build it up again.”

In an instant, Rosalie brought her mistress a cleaver; with an intensity beyond words, she began demolishing the wall. She had removed a few bricks when, turning to deliver a stronger blow, she saw Monsieur de Merret behind her. She fainted.

“Lay madame on her bed,” he said coldly.

Foreseeing what would happen in his absence, he had set this trap for his wife; he had merely written to the Maire and summoned Duvivier. The jeweler arrived just as the disorder in the room was being repaired.

“Duvivier,” Monsieur de Merret asked, “didn’t you buy any crucifixes from the Spaniards who passed through town?”

“No, monsieur.”

“Very well; thank you,” he said, casting a fierce glare at his wife. “Jean,” he added, turning to his valet, “you can serve my meals here in Madame de Merret’s room. She is ill, and I won’t leave her until she recovers.”

The cruel man remained in his wife’s room for twenty days. During the early days, when there was some noise in the closet, and Josephine wanted to intercede for the dying man, he said, without allowing her to speak, “You swore on the Cross that there was no one there.”

After this story, all the ladies rose from the table, breaking the spell Bianchon had cast over them. Some among them had shivered at the last words.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

One of the few drawing-rooms where the Archbishop of Besançon was seen during the Restoration was that of the Baronne de Watteville, closely associated with him because of her devout nature.

Monsieur de Watteville, descended from the infamous Watteville, was gentle and unassuming compared to his ancestor. After living quietly in La Franche-Comté, he married the heiress of the Rupt family, Mademoiselle de Rupt, bringing a substantial income. Their union, arranged in 1802, was formalized in 1815 after the second Restoration. After her grandparents' death, Madame de Watteville grew more devout, leading a pious life that influenced Besançon’s upper circles.

Monsieur de Watteville, thin and unremarkable, spent his days as a turner and collector of local geological specimens, especially fossils. Some saw a noble soul: realizing he would never dominate his wife, he immersed himself in work and hobbies.

The Rupt residence was grand, reflecting both families’ status. It featured elegant furnishings and fine food, often served with ceremony, with Monsieur de Watteville often acting as his own butler. Madame de Watteville’s wealth was considerable, while her husband’s estate was modest. Their close ties with the Archbishop attracted several notable abbés to their home.

At a ceremonial dinner in September 1834, guests eagerly welcomed Monsieur l’Abbé de Grancey, who announced a favorable verdict in a lawsuit concerning family property. The Vicar-General said the victory was unexpected given the political climate and credited a young lawyer, Monsieur Savaron.

Curious about Savaron, Madame de Watteville asked his background. The Vicar-General said he lived nearby but his precise origins were unknown, which heightened the guests’ interest. Amedee de Soulas, present, would later prove significant to the unfolding story.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In all ages France and England have exchanged cultural influences, especially in language and fashion, often bypassing customs. The label "English" in Paris is "French" in London, reflecting mutual influence. For example, the English national anthem, "God Save the King," was composed by Lulli for a French play, and hoop skirts, once ridiculed, became dominant in Europe. By 1815 English long waists were mocked in Paris, but French styles soon evolved.

England contributed terms to French usage, such as "dandy" and "lion." The latter, popularized by Alfred de Musset’s poetry, inspired a vogue for stylish young men in Paris that spread to provincial towns. In 1834 Besançon had its own "lion" in Amedee-Sylvain de Soulas, a young man of Spanish descent who felt out of place in the dull military town. He lived modestly in a sparsely furnished apartment but maintained a lavish appearance, employing a young servant, Babylas, as his "tiger." Amedee dressed Babylas fashionably and spent extravagantly on his wardrobe while surviving on limited income.

Though seen as a spendthrift, Amedee was skilled at budgeting, often dining out to save on household costs. His expensive grooming and clothes were a façade to impress the local aristocracy and win the favor of Mademoiselle Rosalie de Watteville, a wealthy heiress. His travels and knowledge of Parisian culture earned him a reputation as well-informed despite financial strain.

Besançon was conservative and resistant to change; the local aristocracy—called "the Colony"—maintained strict hierarchies. Amedee’s distinctive fashion, like trouser-straps, set him apart and reinforced his status as a "lion." The town’s aristocrats were eager to appear modern to visiting Parisians, and Amedee provided that cosmopolitan touch.

Amedee’s ambitions included marrying Rosalie, who had been raised in a strict religious household with little exposure to the wider world. At eighteen she was modest and unassuming, educated under her mother’s watchful eye, trained in domestic skills rather than social graces, and sheltered from contemporary influences.

Despite a passive demeanor, Rosalie possessed a strong spirit inherited from her family. Her mother, Madame de Watteville, was fiercely protective and controlling, convinced her daughter’s honor was at stake. This dynamic produced tension as Amedee courted Rosalie: the Baroness’s strictness caused Rosalie to suppress feelings, creating a complex relationship marked by maternal jealousy and the daughter’s uncertainty.

As Amedee pursued Rosalie he navigated Besançon’s social intricacies, aiming for a favorable marriage while preserving his reputation. The town’s elite found him charming, and talk of an engagement circulated among the nobility. Yet Rosalie’s upbringing and her mother’s influence complicated his efforts, since she remained largely unaware of the romantic expectations around her.

Amedee de Soulas’s life in Besançon thus exemplifies the interplay of social ambition, cultural exchange, and provincial constraints as he strives to win Rosalie de Watteville amid rigid social structures.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Madame,” said Monsieur de Soulas to the Baroness, as he waited for his soup to cool, “one fine morning, a gentleman from Paris arrived at the Hotel National and chose to stay on the first floor of Mademoiselle Galard’s house on Rue du Perron. He registered as a resident at the Mairie and had his name, ‘ALBERT SAVARON,’ entered on the list of barristers at the Court, leaving his card with local officials.”

“The name Savaron is well-known,” Mademoiselle de Watteville interjected, “as one of the oldest and richest families in Belgium.”

“He’s a Frenchman and no man’s son,” Amedee de Soulas replied. “If he wishes to claim the Savaron arms, he must add a bar-sinister. The only remaining member of the Brabant family is a rich heiress, Mademoiselle de Savarus.”

“The bar-sinister indicates a bastard, but a bastard of a Comte de Savarus is still noble,” Rosalie added.

“Enough, that will do, mademoiselle!” the Baroness interjected.

“You insisted on her learning heraldry,” Monsieur de Watteville remarked, “and she knows it well.”

“Please continue, Monsieur de Soulas.”

“In a town like Besancon, where everything is classified and known, Albert Savaron was received without hesitation by the local lawyers. They wondered who had sent him and what he hoped to achieve, especially since he sent his card to the judges instead of calling in person. Three days later, he was forgotten. He hired Jerome, the late Monsieur Galard’s servant, who could cook a little. Savaron was overlooked because no one had seen him.”

“Does he not go to mass?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.

“He attends the early service at Saint-Pierre on Sundays. He rises between one and two in the morning, works until eight, has breakfast, then continues working. He walks in his garden, circling fifty or sixty times, dines, and goes to bed between six and seven.”

“How do you know all this?” Madame de Chavoncourt inquired.

“I live on Rue Neuve, overlooking his house, and my servant communicates with Jerome.”

“And you gossip with Babylas?”

“What else is there to do while riding?”

“How did you come to hire a stranger for your defense?” the Baroness asked, directing the conversation to the Vicar-General.

“The President of the Court assigned him to defend a half-witted peasant accused of forgery. Savaron proved the man’s innocence and exposed the real culprits, leading to their arrest. His defense impressed the Court and jury. A merchant subsequently entrusted him with a difficult case, which he won. Given our situation, Monsieur Berryer advised us to employ Savaron, predicting our success. Upon meeting him, I felt confident in him, and I was right.”

“Is he truly extraordinary?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.

“Certainly,” the Vicar-General replied.

“Tell us more,” urged Madame de Watteville.

“The first time I met him,” the Abbe de Grancey recounted, “he received me in his outer room, which he had decorated like old oak and lined with law books. The room was simply furnished with an old writing table, six armchairs, gray curtains, and a green carpet. As I waited, I did not expect my advocate to be a young man. The setting matched his appearance; he wore a black dressing gown with a red cord, red slippers, a red waistcoat, and a red smoking cap.”

“The devil’s colors!” exclaimed Madame de Watteville.

“Yes,” the Abbe continued, “but he has a magnificent head. His black hair is streaked with gray, thick and curly, with a round white throat, a splendid forehead marked by deep lines of thought, an olive complexion, a square nose, and fiery eyes. Despite his passionate nature, he has a calm demeanor and a sweet voice that can be thunderous when needed.”

“Monsieur Albert Savaron is of average height, neither stout nor thin, with hands like a prelate.”

“The second time I visited, he received me in his bedroom, which was quite shabby. The third time, he was having a frugal breakfast in his library, and I observed him closely. He is no ordinary man; there’s much behind that terrible yet gentle face. He seems to carry a heavy burden.”

“Why would such an eloquent man leave Paris for Besancon?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked. “Doesn’t he know how little chance a stranger has here?”

“After studying his fine head,” the Abbe replied, “and hearing him respond to a prominent Parisian lawyer, I believe he will eventually make a significant impact.”

“Why discuss him? You’ve won your case and paid him,” Madame de Watteville remarked, noting her daughter’s rapt attention.

The conversation shifted, and Albert Savaron was no longer mentioned. The Vicar-General’s portrait of Savaron captivated Rosalie, who found herself drawn to the romance of his character. For the first time, she encountered someone exceptional, contrasting sharply with the mundane Count who had previously courted her. Albert Savaron presented many enigmas to unravel.

“Albert Savaron de Savarus,” she mused, longing to see him. She replayed the Abbe’s words in her mind, fixating on his description. “What a fine forehead!” she thought, scrutinizing the men at the table, finding none that compared.

“Rosalie, why aren’t you eating?” her mother asked.

“I’m not hungry, mamma,” she replied, lost in thought about the prelate’s hands.

As she pondered, she recalled a lighted window she had seen from her bed, realizing it belonged to him. “I must see him!” she decided.

“Monsieur de Grancey, is the Chapter’s lawsuit settled?” Rosalie asked during a lull in conversation.

“What does that matter to you?” her mother replied, sensing her daughter’s unusual interest.

“It might go to the Court of Appeal, but our adversaries will hesitate,” the Abbe answered.

“I never thought Rosalie would think about a lawsuit during dinner,” Madame de Watteville remarked.

“Nor I,” Rosalie said dreamily, prompting laughter. “But Monsieur de Grancey was so engrossed that I became interested.”

After dinner, Rosalie listened for any mention of Albert Savaron, but aside from congratulations to the Abbe for winning his suit, he was not discussed. Eager for bedtime, she planned to wake between two and three to glimpse Albert’s windows. When the hour arrived, she felt a thrill watching the light from his candles through the trees. She saw him writing and imagined the color of his furniture.

“While the world sleeps, he is awake—like God!” she thought.

The education of girls poses serious challenges, as the future of a nation rests on mothers. The University of France has long avoided this issue: Should girls be informed about everything? Should their minds be restrained? The religious system often imposes such restraint. Enlightening them too much can lead to premature maturity, while suppressing their thoughts can result in explosive outbursts, as seen in Moliere’s character Agnes. This crisis was triggered in Rosalie by the Abbe’s portrait of Savaron.

The next morning, while dressing, Rosalie looked out to see Albert walking in the garden next to the Hotel de Rupt. “What if he lived elsewhere? At least I can see him here. What is he thinking?” she wondered.

Having seen this extraordinary man, Rosalie was determined to enter his house, uncover the mystery surrounding him, hear his eloquent voice, and catch a glimpse of his eyes. She resolved to achieve this.

Throughout the day, she embroidered with the concentration of a girl who appears to think of nothing but is deeply reflective. She decided to go to confession. The next morning, after Mass, she arranged to confess at half-past seven, just before the eight o’clock service, hoping to encounter Albert.

She visited her father in his workshop, suggesting he turn larger pieces and construct a grotto in the garden to showcase his work. As he delighted in the idea, she cautioned him not to tell her mother, fearing a scolding.

“Don’t worry!” he replied, sharing her frustration with his wife.

Rosalie envisioned a charming observatory from which she could see into Albert’s private room. Young girls often go to great lengths for the sake of a man, even if he remains unaware.

The eagerly awaited Sunday arrived, and Rosalie dressed with such care that her maid Mariette smiled.

“It’s the first time I’ve seen you so fidgety,” Mariette remarked.

“Perhaps you too are more particular on certain days,” Rosalie replied, blushing.

As she crossed the courtyard, her heart raced with anticipation. She had never walked the streets like this before, feeling as if her mother could read her thoughts and would forbid her confession. She felt invigorated, as if walking on fire. She arrived at church before Mass, praying briefly before checking if the Abbe Giroud was in his confessional, positioning herself to see Albert as he entered.

No man could seem unattractive to Rosalie in her curious state. Albert Savaron, strikingly handsome, made a profound impression on her. His solemn demeanor suggested he bore a heavy burden, and his deep gaze conveyed intense thought. Rosalie understood the Vicar-General’s words fully now. Those eagle-like eyes revealed a hidden ardor. In a moment of boldness, she stood in his path to exchange glances, igniting a rush of warmth within her.

After Albert took a seat, Rosalie found a spot to observe him during her confession. When Mariette announced the arrival of Monsieur Giroud, it felt as if her time in the confessional had been fleeting. By the time she emerged, Mass had ended, and Albert had left.

“The Vicar-General was right,” she thought. “He is unhappy. Why has this eagle come to Besancon? I must know everything! But how?”

With this new desire, Rosalie focused intently on her embroidery, masking her thoughts with an innocent demeanor to deceive her mother.

From that Sunday, after their glance, Rosalie eagerly supported the plan for the Belvedere.

“Mamma,” she said one day, “my father has a unique idea for a Belvedere he wants to build in the garden. Do you approve?”

“I approve of everything your father does,” Madame de Watteville replied dryly. “Why should I object to something that amuses him?”

“Because from there we’ll see into Monsieur de Soulas’ rooms, and he’ll see us. Perhaps remarks will be made—”

“Do you presume to guide your parents, Rosalie, thinking you know more about life and propriety than we do?”

“I’ll say no more, mamma. Besides, my father mentioned a cool room in the grotto where we can take coffee.”

“Your father has had an excellent idea,” Madame de Watteville said, going to inspect the columns.

She fully approved of the design, choosing a location in the garden that could not be seen from Monsieur de Soulas’ windows but would allow them to see into Albert Savaron’s rooms. A builder was commissioned to construct a grotto with a path lined with periwinkles, clematis, ivy, honeysuckle, and Virginia creeper. The interior was to be rustic, with a mirror, an ottoman, and a table of inlaid bark. Monsieur de Soulas suggested an asphalt floor, while Rosalie proposed a rustic chandelier.

“The Wattevilles are creating something lovely in their garden,” rumors spread in Besancon.

“They’re wealthy and can afford such whims—”

“A thousand crowns!” Madame de Chavoncourt exclaimed.

“Yes, a thousand crowns,” young Monsieur de Soulas replied. “A man from Paris is coming to rusticate the interior, and it will be very pretty. Monsieur de Watteville is carving the chandelier himself.”

“Berquet is making a cellar beneath it,” said an Abbe.

“No,” young Monsieur de Soulas corrected, “he’s raising the kiosk on a concrete foundation to prevent dampness.”

“You know the least about what happens in that house,” Madame de Chavoncourt remarked sourly, glancing at her daughter, who had been waiting to marry for a year.

Mademoiselle de Watteville felt a flush of pride at the success of her Belvedere, sensing her superiority over others. No one suspected that a girl thought to be a witless goose had simply devised a plan to get a closer look at Albert Savaron’s private study.

Albert Savaron’s brilliant defense of the Cathedral Chapter was soon forgotten, as envy among other lawyers grew. He remained secluded, avoiding social gatherings, which increased the likelihood of being overlooked as a stranger in Besancon. Nevertheless, he successfully argued three complex cases at the Commercial Tribunal, gaining four prominent merchants as clients who recognized his legal acumen.

On the day the Watteville family inaugurated the Belvedere, Savaron was also establishing a new venture. Through connections he had quietly formed among local merchants, he was launching a fortnightly paper called the Eastern Review, funded by forty shares of five hundred francs each from his first ten clients, who were convinced of the need to promote Besancon’s interests as a trade hub.

To compete with Strasbourg, Besancon needed to become a center of enlightenment and commerce. Albert’s vision was to challenge the literary dominance of Strasbourg and Dijon, bringing light to Eastern France. His ideas resonated with the merchants, who believed they were their own.

Savaron wisely kept his name out of the spotlight, allowing his chief client, Monsieur Boucher, to handle finances while he retained editorial control and a share of the profits. The Review appealed to various regions, and with a low subscription price, it quickly garnered five hundred subscribers.

To avoid offending local pride, Albert invited the Boucher family’s eager son, Alfred, to manage the literary side of the Review. Albert maintained control, subtly guiding Alfred’s contributions while allowing him to believe he was gaining from their association. The commercial community, pleased with the Review’s success, only had to pay a fraction of their shares. With two hundred more subscribers, the publication would yield dividends for shareholders, while the editor remained unpaid.

After the third issue, the Review gained recognition among all French publications, which Albert began reading at home. The third number featured a tale attributed to him, which sparked discussion in Madame de Chavoncourt’s drawing room, despite the higher circles of Besancon dismissing it for its liberal views.

“Papa,” Rosalie said, “there’s a Review published in Besancon; you should subscribe and keep it in your room, as mamma wouldn’t let me read it, but you can lend it to me.”

Monsieur de Watteville, eager to please Rosalie, who had shown him much affection, went to subscribe to the Eastern Review and lent her the four issues already published. That night, Rosalie devoured the tale—the first she had ever read. Given her limited experience, the impact of this work on her cannot be judged by ordinary standards. She had hoped to find in it the sentiments and perhaps the life of Albert. From the first pages, she became convinced that it reflected his experiences, as critics noted that Albert had drawn from his own life in this confession.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In 1823 two young men left Lucerne for a holiday in Switzerland, intending to tour the Lake of the Four Cantons. The journey offered striking mountain, river, and meadow views. Passing the hamlet of Gersau, one friend, Rodolphe, was captivated by a newly built house and a woman at the window and decided to stay; his friend Leopold continued the trip.

Rodolphe and Leopold had studied law together: Leopold was bound for a notary’s post in Paris, while Rodolphe, the illegitimate son of a nobleman, had a more unsettled background. His mother had sacrificed everything to secure his education, hoping to improve his prospects. Passionate and sensitive, Rodolphe was often led by desire, a contrast to Leopold’s calm demeanor.

In Gersau Rodolphe lodged with a couple who took him in. He learned the woman at the window was Fanny Lovelace, who had moved there with her elderly father for his health. They were modestly off, and Fanny supported her father by translating and writing. Rodolphe was struck by her beauty and musical talent.

The next day he sought permission to visit the Bergmanns’ gardens; the Bergmanns rented land to the Lovelaces and were initially hesitant but allowed him to explore. There he noticed a deaf-mute girl, Gina, who suggested a distinctive past. Eager to meet Fanny, Rodolphe encountered resistance from the protective Bergmanns.

That evening he heard Fanny singing and was enchanted. He later met Fanny and Gina in person, but their conversation was cut short when an unknown assailant attacked and wounded him. Fanny and Gina aided him and he was taken to safety.

While recovering, Rodolphe received devastating news: his mother had died. The loss plunged him into deep depression, but he found solace in Fanny’s company. They bonded over shared interests, and Rodolphe confessed his feelings, only to learn Fanny was married to an older man, Emilio. Despite this, Fanny’s affection for Rodolphe grew and they shared intimate moments.

Fanny disclosed that her husband was not as old as he appeared and that she had married him under duress. Rodolphe was torn between his love for her and the reality of her marriage. Fanny encouraged him to pursue his ambitions, believing in his potential.

Their relationship deepened, yet Rodolphe struggled with jealousy and insecurity, particularly when news arrived from Tito, a young man from Fanny’s past. Tito’s appearance provoked rivalry in Rodolphe, who feared losing her affection.

Despite these challenges, Rodolphe and Fanny’s connection remained strong. They shared dreams of freedom and a future together, but the complexities of their circumstances—Rodolphe’s ambition and Fanny’s loyalty to her husband—created persistent tension. As they navigated their feelings, Rodolphe wrestled with the demands of love, ambition, and the sacrifices required for happiness. The beauty of their surroundings mirrored the intensity of their emotions, yet the realities of their lives threatened to overshadow their dreams.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
For twenty days Rodolphe stayed at the Bergmanns’, watching Francesca. He saw in her the youthful imprudence and unbroken spirit of a woman who at times resisted love and at times yielded to it. The old man treated her like a father; her gratitude stirred a native nobility that intrigued Rodolphe and made her an enigma.

Those days mixed secret joys and melancholy, with quarrels as delightful as their reconciliations. Rodolphe grew increasingly captivated by Francesca’s steady tenderness, often jealously exacting over trifles.

One evening, when Francesca said she wanted to leave Gersau because it lacked luxury, Rodolphe asked if she cared for luxury. She answered passionately, equating her fondness for luxury with her love of art and beauty, insisting she had never complained through hardships. Playfully she argued that it was natural for ordinary people to aspire to greatness, revealing her charming character.

She then insisted Rodolphe not come with them to Geneva, citing town gossip and her wish to protect her husband’s reputation. She affirmed her love for Rodolphe but demanded it remain concealed. She kissed him and left him astonished.

The next day the Bergmanns’ lodgers departed early, and restless, Rodolphe went to Vevay and then to Geneva by the end of October, taking a longer route. To avoid the town’s discomforts he rented a room in Eaux-Vives. His first inquiry was after Italian refugees, especially a bookseller named Lamporani, but the landlord said the only new arrivals were Prince and Princess Colonna and Prince and Princess Gandolphini.

Rodolphe’s first walk took him to the Villa Diodati, where the beauty of the place struck him. Approaching, he saw Francesca in an elegant carriage with an old lady and was dizzy with surprise. Learning they were at the Colonna residence, a veil seemed to lift: the implications of their past interactions became clear.

He feared he had been a mere whim for Francesca, struggling with the reality of her noble standing. He sent his card to Prince Gandolphini and was received by the false Lamporani, who invited him to a concert that evening. Though curious, Rodolphe refrained from requesting an audience with the Princess and returned to Eaux-Vives with mingled anticipation and anxiety.

As evening approached his passion deepened and he felt compelled to elevate his social position to match hers. When he finally saw her at the concert she was dazzling; their eyes met with immediate recognition. Francesca’s beauty and grace captivated him and he felt a profound connection, convinced she had remained faithful to their past despite her rank.

After the concert Rodolphe was introduced to the Colonnas and to Francesca, who playfully styled herself “Princess Rodolphini.” The jest revived memories of their time in Gersau; he was overwhelmed by the beauty of her voice and the intimacy of their proximity.

In the ensuing days Francesca perceived Rodolphe’s deep love, which for her felt natural. She said their affection should be faithful and enduring, whatever the distance. Rodolphe, however, was driven by ambition; he wanted to achieve greatness so Francesca could be proud of him.

Francesca was a remarkable blend of gaiety and tenderness. Once destined for a cloister, family misfortunes had compelled her to marry Prince Gandolphini to preserve her family’s standing. Yet despite her noble birth she had acquired a taste for independence and liberal ideas, inclining her toward revolutionary sentiments.

Rodolphe spent the winter in Geneva where time flew in the company of the intelligent and beautiful Francesca. But with spring came inner turmoil: Francesca’s calmness contrasted with his emotional unrest. He wished she would show more feeling, believing Italian women to be more expressive.

One day on the terrace he voiced a bitter complaint about the happiness of others, singling out a woman of noble lineage; his words wounded Francesca, and she withdrew. Rodolphe followed and found her in tears. In a moment of passion he kissed her, but she repelled him and asserted her dignity.

Francesca then ordered him to leave, declaring they would not meet again until Naples. Her command was firm; Rodolphe, recognizing the strength of her will, complied.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
On his return to Paris, Rodolphe discovered a portrait of Princess Gandolphini by Schinner, an artist who had refused many women. Rodolphe doubted the Prince could persuade him, but Francesca charmed Schinner into painting an original for Rodolphe and a duplicate for Emilio. She announced it in a delightful letter, beginning a regular correspondence between Rodolphe and Francesca—their sole indulgence.

Ambitious and fueled by love, Rodolphe invested all he had in a venture that failed through deceit, costing him three years of struggle. The Villele ministry fell just as Rodolphe faced ruin. Seeking solace, he turned to politics, but first went to Naples to heal at the Villa Gandolphini, where the Prince and Princess had been restored to their positions. He went to heal his wounds and regain courage. The three-month respite filled him with hope and restored his spirits.

Rodolphe then tried to rebuild his fortune. His talents were noticed, and a high post was promised for his dedication and past efforts. But the July 1830 storm dashed his ambitions again.

Only Francesca and God witnessed the brave efforts and daring attempts of this young man, endowed with fine qualities but unlucky in fortune. Despite setbacks, sustained by love, he returned to new challenges, guided by a faithful presence and a devoted heart.

Lovers! Pray for him!
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
As Mademoiselle de Watteville concluded her narrative, Rosalie's cheeks burned with rage and tears flowed. The novel, in the popular style of the time, was her first encounter with a story of love told through a man's experiences, which stirred deep emotion. Her agitation sprang from jealousy of Francesca Colonna, whom she believed was Albert's beloved.

Rosalie was consumed by curiosity and a fierce desire to fight for Albert, despite insecurities about her looks and lack of musical talent. She feared he would never love her, which intensified her determination to learn the truth. That night her imagination ran wild with schemes typical of a young woman raised under strict rules: sneaking into Albert's garden, writing to him, or visiting her father's land dispute to draw him into her drawing-room.

Watching Albert's window, she saw something that might reveal his secrets: Jerome, Albert's servant, being helped over the wall by Mariette, the maid. Rosalie noted Mariette's plainness but also her steadiness and means, which explained Jerome's interest. The incident taught Rosalie a dangerous lesson: her mother's rigid upbringing could be undermined by a servant's choices.

The next morning Rosalie accompanied Mariette to Mass and seized the chance to question her about Jerome. She confronted Mariette about their secret meetings and, to her surprise, offered support in return for access to the letters Albert sent through Jerome. Relieved, Mariette agreed.

Rosalie's obsession deepened as she read a letter from Albert to his friend Leopold that revealed his noble lineage and past struggles. She became enthralled by his ambitions and the complexity of his character, which intensified her feelings. The letter portrayed a man striving for success while devoted to the woman he loved—Francesca.

As she continued to read Albert's correspondence, Rosalie became more aware of her own inadequacy and the moral implications of spying. She wrestled with guilt over invading his privacy but rationalized it as proof of love. Her passion slid into dangerous infatuation, prompting penances and self-imposed suffering meant to demonstrate her devotion.

One evening Mariette carried another letter intended for Francesca, plunging Rosalie into jealousy and doubt. She hesitated but ultimately yielded to the urge to read it. The letter confirmed Albert's enduring love for Francesca, outlining his ambitions and hopes for a shared future.

Rosalie's heart ached as she absorbed its contents and grasped the depth of his feeling for another. Though she had once resolved to respect their relationship, she found herself torn between admiration for their fidelity and her own longing. In a moment of clarity she chose to step back, seeing the nobility of their bond and acknowledging her shortcomings.

Preparing to return the letter with Mariette, Rosalie reflected on her feelings and the nature of love. She felt sorrow and resignation, recognizing that true love often requires sacrifice. In her heart she longed for the devotion Albert and Francesca shared, while coming to terms with the reality of her unrequited affection.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Rosalie spent the winter of 1834–35 in turmoil, but by April, at nineteen, she fantasized about triumphing over the Duchesse d’Argaiolo. In solitude that struggle fueled her dark passion and led her to devise numerous plans. Many young women like Rosalie exist; her story is a cautionary tale.

During that winter Albert de Savarus made significant strides in Besançon, eagerly anticipating the Chamber’s dissolution. He’d gained the backing of a wealthy contractor influential in the moderate party. The Romans had invested heavily in town water supplies, and Besançon drew water from Arcier, a distant hill. Restoring an aqueduct to drink the Romans’ water seemed absurd in a town already served by the Doubs, but convincing citizens would generate substantial expenses benefiting the contractor.

Savarus argued the river water was unfit and promoted Arcier water. Articles in the local Review echoed this, and townspeople, across political lines, agreed on the desirability of drinking like the Romans. The issue became a focal point, driven by hidden interests. Opponents were dismissed as old-fashioned, while Savarus’s projects dominated discussion. After eighteen months he had stirred the stagnant town and gained influence without popularity.

Throughout the winter he won several lawsuits for local priests, and his ambition consumed him. His apparent indifference to financial gain masked a form of moral usury: he anticipated a far greater reward.

In October 1834 he helped a struggling merchant with funds from Léopold Hannequin, which also gave him a qualification for election. The Abbé de Grancey, who understood Savarus’s ambitions, remarked on his remarkable nature, suggesting he’d taken a wrong turn in life.

Meanwhile Rosalie resolved to introduce Savarus to the society of the Hôtel de Rupt, initially content to observe him from afar. The Wattevilles’ estate, les Rouxey, yielded ten thousand francs a year, but its management was neglected by the Baron, who left it to an old servant named Modinier. The Baron’s indifference sprang from his wife’s substantial income, and they often retreated to les Rouxey, a picturesque spot created by the famous Watteville.

Watteville had constructed a dam and lake, enhancing the area’s beauty, but his heirs maintained a questionable claim over the village of Riceys, which had long enjoyed rights to the land. The Baron’s death left a legacy of usurpation, and his descendants continued to assert control over the village.

Rosalie’s efforts were complex and subtle. In May 1835 she left Besançon with her father for les Rouxey, where the lake’s beauty captivated her and she associated it with love. She suggested a boat trip to explore the lake, during which her father confided about legal troubles with Riceys over land rights. Rosalie urged him to hire Savarus as their lawyer, stressing the importance of securing the Dent de Vilard for the estate.

She argued that a lawsuit would enliven their lives and insisted on bringing Savarus to les Rouxey. Her determination to secure her future there was evident: she envisioned creating beautiful gardens.

Four days later the Abbé de Grancey visited Savarus to persuade him to represent the Wattevilles. Savarus hesitated, fearing such an alignment would compromise his neutrality. The Abbé urged him to consider the political advantages of supporting the Wattevilles, especially given potential votes.

Rosalie, meanwhile, was determined to keep Savarus in Besançon and prevent his election. She wrote to the préfet, revealing Savarus’s ambitions, but the préfet disregarded her. Rosalie understood the political landscape and sought to manipulate it to her advantage.

As the election approached, a meeting of influential men in Besançon decided to nominate Savarus as a candidate, much to his surprise. He was initially unaware of the political machinations at play. The Abbé, who had been watching, warned Savarus of potential betrayals and advised him to be transparent about his past.

Savarus delivered a passionate speech outlining his vision and ambitions, captivating his audience. However, he faced challenges as the préfet worked to undermine his support, and his voter base began to decline. Despite his efforts, the political landscape shifted against him, and he struggled to maintain his position.

On the day of the first voting the results were disappointing for Savarus, who felt the weight of his ambitions and the pressure of the election. He returned home in despair, reflecting on his love for Francesca and the uncertainty of his future.

Just as hope faded, the Abbé de Grancey arrived with encouraging news. The priest had been working behind the scenes to secure support for Savarus, urging him to visit the Wattevilles and promising their case would be resolved in time for the election.

The Abbé revealed that winning the Rouxey lawsuit could secure crucial votes and encouraged Savarus to pursue a marriage with Sidonie de Chavoncourt to solidify his political standing. Savarus was initially resistant but realized the potential benefits of such a union.

The Abbé’s guidance proved invaluable, and Savarus prepared to navigate the complexities of both love and politics, understanding that his future depended on choices he would have to make in the coming days.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
By nine o’clock the following evening Madame la Baronne de Watteville’s rooms were filled with Besançon’s aristocracy, gathered over the unusual decision to go to the polls for the daughter of the Rupts. The former Master of Appeals, a secretary to a loyal minister of the Elder Branch, was to be presented. Madame de Chavoncourt attended with her younger daughter Sidonie, elegantly dressed; her elder sister, confident in her relationship, had made no effort with her appearance—an omission noted in small towns. The Abbe de Grancey moved among the groups, listening and offering incisive comments that shaped the talk.

“If the Elder Branch returns,” he asked an elderly statesman, “what politicians would they find?” The statesman replied that Berryer, isolated on his bench, was uncertain and might disrupt the Government if supported. They discussed the Duc de Fitz-James in Toulouse and how voting for Monsieur Savarus could align Republicans with them instead of the Moderates.

By nine-thirty Albert had not arrived, and Madame de Watteville found his lateness disrespectful. Madame de Chavoncourt counseled her not to let trifles affect serious issues, suggesting Albert might be delayed by a consultation. Rosalie, watching her mother and Madame de Chavoncourt, noted her leniency toward Albert, hinting at a possible marriage between Sidonie and him.

At ten the absent Albert had provoked growing frustration; some gentlemen began playing cards. The Abbe de Grancey, perplexed, went to a window where Rosalie stood and exclaimed, “He must be dead!” The Vicar-General, Monsieur de Watteville and Rosalie went into the garden to check Albert’s rooms, which were dark and silent.

“Jerome!” Rosalie called to the servant. When he confirmed Albert had left in a post-chaise, the Abbe cried, “He is ruined! Or he is happy!” Rosalie’s expression betrayed joy, which the Vicar-General noticed but pretended to ignore, wondering about her involvement.

Back in the drawing-room, Monsieur de Watteville announced Albert’s unexplained departure. By half-past eleven only fifteen guests remained: Madame de Chavoncourt, the Abbe de Godenars, the two Chavoncourt girls, Monsieur de Vauchelles, the Abbe de Grancey, Rosalie, Amedee de Soulas, a retired magistrate who had supported Albert’s election, and others. The Abbe de Grancey stationed himself near the Baroness to observe Rosalie, whose usually pale face now showed agitation.

“What could have happened to Monsieur de Savarus?” Madame de Chavoncourt wondered aloud. At that moment a servant brought a letter for the Abbe de Grancey on a silver tray. The Baroness urged him to read it. As he did he noticed Rosalie turn pale and recognized the handwriting. He folded the letter and discreetly pocketed it, having exchanged glances with Rosalie that hinted at her feeling.

“She is in love with Albert Savarus!” he concluded. As he prepared to leave, Rosalie intercepted him. “Monsieur de Grancey, it was from Albert!” she cried. When he asked how she recognized the writing, her passionate reply revealed her feelings: “I love him! What is the matter?” He told her that Albert was withdrawing from the election. Rosalie, stunned, begged him to keep the news secret, realizing that if the election ended so too would the prospect of her sister’s marriage to Albert.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In the morning, on her way to Mass, Mademoiselle de Watteville learned from Mariette about Albert’s sudden disappearance. An elderly gentleman from Paris had arrived at the Hotel National and called on Monsieur Savaron. The lawyer turned pale at the gentleman’s card; they met for over an hour. Afterwards the gentleman left with a large package, and Savaron was distraught. He went to the Prefecture, bought an old carriage, ordered post-horses, left his keys, and told his servant to seek other employment.

The next day Savaron’s flight caused a stir in Besançon; the Prefect quickly granted him a passport. The affair fed local prejudice against outsiders, and Albert was soon forgotten by most — except Girardet, the Vicar-General, and Rosalie, who alone knew the truth about the Duc d’Argaiolo’s death.

By April 1836 there was still no news of Albert. The Baroness de Watteville urged Rosalie to marry, noting how much she had changed. The Baron agreed to give Rosalie the family estate, les Rouxey, but the Baroness fretted over the finances. She and the Baron arranged a match with Monsieur de Soulas, which Rosalie firmly rejected; she insisted she was content as she was.

The conflict between Rosalie and her mother escalated, and a summer at les Rouxey made Rosalie’s refusal public. After Mariette and Jerome married, the Baron improved the estate, provoking the Baroness’s resentment. As tensions rose, the Vicar-General tried to mediate.

One day Rosalie confessed to the Vicar-General that she had interfered in Albert’s relationship with Francesca, the Duchess d’Argaiolo: she had intercepted letters and caused their separation. The Vicar-General was horrified, stressing the moral gravity of her deception. He urged her to seek forgiveness and to recognize the consequences she had set in motion.

Rosalie’s guilt deepened when she learned more of Albert’s fate. A Paris clerk representing him came to sell his effects, revealing that Albert had renounced the world and entered a monastery. The Vicar-General wrote to the monastery and received a letter from Albert expressing complete forgiveness for Rosalie and his wish to remain in solitude.

When the Vicar-General shared this, he urged reconciliation with her mother and acceptance of Monsieur de Soulas. Rosalie remained defiant, unwilling to sacrifice her mother’s happiness. The Vicar-General’s death left a void; soon after, Madame de Watteville married Monsieur de Soulas, believing Rosalie’s support.

Now legally of age, Rosalie encouraged the marriage and thus secured her independence. With a substantial fortune she entered Parisian society intent on confronting the Duchess d’Argaiolo. At a ball she publicly revealed her role in Albert’s downfall, leaving the Duchess shaken.

Rosalie returned to Besançon, having exposed the truth, yet the emotional toll of her actions weighed heavily. The tale of love, betrayal, and the consequences of ambition unfolded, leaving a lasting impact on all involved.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Mademoiselle de Watteville lived alone on her estate of les Rouxey, riding, hunting, and improving the land, making her well known in the Eastern provinces. Madame de Soulas, who has two children, appears younger; her husband, Monsieur de Soulas, has grown old. He remarked to young Chavoncourt, “My fortune has cost me dear. To know a bigot, it is unfortunately necessary to marry her!”

Mademoiselle de Watteville was famed for her eccentric vagaries. Each year she visited the walls of the Grande Chartreuse, perhaps following a grand-uncle who once broke through monastery walls to seek freedom. In 1841 she left Besançon, said to be for marriage, though the true reason was unclear. She returned after a tragic Loire steamboat accident: a boiler explosion so grievously injured her she lost her right arm and left leg and suffered disfiguring facial scars. Her health declined; in constant pain, she now leads a life of religious observance at the Chartreuse of les Rouxey.

PARIS, May 1842.

ADDENDUM

######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########

Produced by John Bickers, and Dagny

THE MARRIAGE CONTRACT

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley

DEDICATION

To Rossini.

THE MARRIAGE CONTRACT

CHAPTER I. PRO AND CON

Monsieur de Manerville was a worthy Norman gentleman, known to the Marechal de Richelieu, who married him to a wealthy Bordeaux heiress when the old duke governed Guienne. The Norman sold his Bessin estate and became a Gascon, drawn by the chateau de Lanstrac, his wife’s property. Toward the close of Louis XV’s reign he bought the post of major of the Gate Guards and, having escaped the Revolution’s dangers, lived until 1813.

In late 1790 he went to Martinique, where his wife had interests, leaving his Gascogne property to Mathias, a notary’s clerk open to, if not adopting, the new ideas. On his return, the Comte de Manerville found his estates intact and well managed—the Gascon fruitfully grafted onto the Norman.

Madame de Manerville died in 1810. Through youthful dissipations, the old man had learned to value wealth highly; he became increasingly economical, miserly, and sordid, and allowed almost nothing to his only son.

Paul de Manerville returned from the college of Vendôme in 1810 and lived under strict paternal discipline for three years. The tyranny of the old man of seventy shaped a young heart and character still unformed. Paul, naturally courageous in the Gascon way, dared not struggle against his father and lost the faculty of resistance that begets moral courage. His thwarted feelings were driven inward; later, when they conflicted with the world’s maxims, he could think rightly but act wrongly. He could fight for a word or look yet trembled at dismissing a servant—his timidity showing in contests requiring steady will. Capable of great deeds to escape persecution, he would not have prevented it by steady opposition. Timid in thought, bold in action, he preserved a simplicity that made him a dupe and a voluntary victim of things against which other souls revolt. Imprisoned by his father’s mansion and lacking money to consort with peers, he envied their pleasures without sharing them.

Every evening his father took him in a carriage with ill-harnessed horses and ill-dressed servants to royalist houses, meeting a society of the relics of parliamentary and martial nobility. These, crushed by the vast fortunes of maritime cities, coalesced into a landed aristocracy that disdained the sumptuous displays of commerce and administration. Too young to understand such social distinctions, Paul was bored by these ancients, unaware that such early connections would later secure the preeminence Frenchmen desire.

He found some compensation in the manual exercises his father insisted upon. The old gentleman believed that fencing, arms, riding, tennis, and good manners—frivolous accomplishments of old nobility—made a modern young man a gentleman. Thus Paul took fencing lessons, rode, practiced at a pistol gallery, and spent the rest of his time reading novels, his father forbidding the more abstruse studies now considered necessary.

So monotonous a life would have killed him had not his father’s death delivered him when it became intolerable. Paul then found himself possessor of considerable capital accumulated by paternal avarice, with well-kept estates. But he hated Bordeaux; Lanstrac, where his father had summered hunting, did not attract him.

Once the estate was settled, the young heir, eager for enjoyment, bought consols with his capital, left estate management to Mathias, and spent six years away: attached to the French embassy at Naples, secretary at Madrid, then in London—making a tour of Europe.

After seeing the world, losing illusions, and dissipating the capital his father had amassed, Paul had to draw upon the territorial revenues Mathias had been saving. Seized by a so-called virtuous impulse, he determined to leave Paris, return to Bordeaux, regulate his affairs, live as a country gentleman at Lanstrac, improve his property, marry, and eventually become a deputy.

Paul was a count; nobility had matrimonial value again; he could and ought to make a good marriage. In exchange for seven hundred thousand francs squandered in six years he had gained that possession which cannot be bought—experience: knowledge derived from social apprenticeship, love affairs, duels, lost bets, disillusions, toils, and pleasures. He had become what is called elegant. Despite extravagance he had not become a mere man of fashion. In the burlesque army of men of the world, the man of elegance ranks above the mere fashionable man. Paul enjoyed this reputation and knew how to sustain it. His servants were well dressed, his equipages cited, his suppers were in vogue; his bachelor establishment compared with the finest houses in Paris.

He had not debased any woman; he gambled without shame, was upright enough not to mislead, left no billets-doux about, and kept no coffer of love-letters. Not willing to dip into his Guienne property, he lacked the bold extravagance that attracts attention. He did not borrow, but foolishly lent to friends who deserted him. He seemed to regulate his dissipations methodically. The secret of his character lay in his father’s tyranny, which had made him, as it were, a social mongrel.

One morning he told his friend de Marsay, who later became celebrated:—

“My dear fellow, life has a meaning.”

“You must be twenty-seven to find it out,” replied de Marsay, laughing.

“Well, I am twenty-seven; and because I am twenty-seven I mean to live the life of a country gentleman at Lanstrac. I’ll move my belongings to Bordeaux, and spend three months a year in Paris in this house I shall keep.”

“Will you marry?”

“I will marry.”

“I’m your friend, as you know,” said de Marsay after a pause, “and I say to you: settle down into a worthy father and husband and you’ll be ridiculous for the rest of your days. If you could be happy and ridiculous, it might be thought of; but you will not be happy. You don’t have a strong wrist to manage a household. I’ll do you justice and say you are a perfect horseman; no one knows how to make a horse prance and sit firm. But marriage is another thing. I see you, led along by Madame la Comtesse de Manerville, going where you would not, oftener at a gallop than a trot, and presently unhorsed—unhorsed into a ditch with your legs broken. Listen. You still have some forty-odd thousand francs a year from your Gironde property. Good. Take your horses and servants and furnish your house in Bordeaux; you can be king of Bordeaux, promulgate the edicts we put forth in Paris; be the correspondent of our stupidities. Play the rake in the provinces; commit follies; follies may win you celebrity. But don’t marry. Who marries nowadays? Merchants for capital, peasants for children to work, brokers and notaries for wives’ dots, miserable kings to continue dynasties. We are exempt from the pack, and you want to shoulder it! Why marry? If you marry an heiress as rich as yourself, eighty thousand francs a year for two is not the same as forty thousand for one; two soon become three or four with children. You haven’t love for that silly race of Manerville that would only hamper you? Do you know what a father and mother must be? Marriage, my old Paul, is the silliest social immolation; our children alone profit and don’t know its price until their horses nibble the flowers on our grave. Do you regret your father, that old tyrant who made your first years wretched? How can you be sure your children will love you? The very care you take of their education and the necessary sternness will lessen their affection. Children love a weak or prodigal father, whom they will despise later. You’ll live between fear and contempt. No man is a good head of a family merely by willing it. Look among all our friends and name one you would like for a son. We have known many who dishonor their names. Children, my dear Paul, are the most difficult merchandise to keep. Yours, you think, will be angels; well, so be it! Have you sounded the gulf between bachelor and married life? As a bachelor you can say: ‘I shall never exhibit more than a certain amount of the ridiculous; the public will think of me as I choose.’ Married, you’ll drop into the infinitude of the ridiculous. As a bachelor you make your own happiness; you enjoy some today and do without tomorrow; married, you must take it as it comes, and the day you want it you’ll go without it. Marry and you’ll grow a blockhead; you’ll calculate dowries; you’ll talk morality, civic and religious; you’ll consider young men immoral; in short, you’ll become a social academician. It’s pitiable! The old bachelor hoarding his spoon is blessed compared with a married man. I speak not merely of the annoyances, bores, irritations, coercions, tyrannies and narcotizations of marriage, that struggle of two beings always together who thought they suited; to tell you those things would repeat Boileau. Still, I’ll forgive your absurd idea if you promise to marry ‘en grand seigneur’: entail your property; have two legitimate children; give your wife a house and household distinct from yours; meet her only in society, never return from a journey without sending her a courier to announce it. Two hundred thousand francs a year will suffice, and your antecedents will enable you to marry some rich Englishwoman hungry for a title. That’s an aristocratic life thoroughly French; it retains respect and friendship of a woman and distinguishes a man from the crowd. Thus the Comte de Manerville may advise his epoch, place himself above the world, and be a minister or ambassador. Ridicule can never touch him; he gains social advantages of marriage while keeping bachelor privileges.”
