“My dear friend,” he said, “I am not only the most unfortunate man in the world, but I am also the most ridiculous; and I come to you to get me out of the hornet’s nest into which I have run myself. Modeste is a demon; she sees my difficulty and she laughs at it; she has just spoken to me of a fragment of a letter of Madame de Chaulieu, which I had the folly to give her; if she shows it I can never make my peace with Eleonore. Therefore, will you at once ask Modeste to send me back that paper, and tell her, from me, that I make no pretensions to her hand. Say I count upon her delicacy, upon her propriety as a young girl, to behave to me as if we had never known each other. I beg her not to speak to me; I implore her to treat me harshly—though I hardly dare ask her to feign a jealous anger, which would help my interests amazingly. Go, I will wait here for an answer.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
On re-entering the salon, Ernest de La Briere found the Vicomte de Serizy, a young officer of the Havre guard who had come from Rosny to announce that Madame must be present at the opening of the Chambers. The importance of this constitutional solemnity—when Charles X delivered his speech surrounded by the royal family, Madame la Dauphine and Madame in their gallery—made the choice of emissary a mark of attention to Diane, adored by the charming Vicomte, son of a minister, gentleman in ordinary, and sole heir to an immense fortune. The Duchesse de Maufrigneuse tolerated his attentions mainly to highlight his mother Madame de Serizy’s age; whispered chronicles said she had once deprived Lucien de Rubempre of his heart.

“You will do us the pleasure, I hope, to remain at Rosembray,” said the severe duchess to the young officer.

While listening to scandal, the devout lady shut her eyes to the duke’s carefully chosen guests; these excellent women tolerated much under the pretense of reclaiming lost sheep. “We reckoned without our constitutional government,” said the grand equerry; “and Rosembray, Madame la duchesse, will lose a great honor.”

“We shall be more at our ease,” said a tall thin old man, about seventy-five, dressed in blue cloth and wearing his hunting-cap by permission of the ladies. Resembling the Duc de Bourbon, he was the Prince de Cadignan, Master of the Hunt, one of the last great French lords. Just as La Briere tried to slip behind the sofa to speak with Modeste, a man of thirty-eight, short, fat, and common in appearance, entered the room.

“My son, the Prince de Loudon,” said the Duchesse de Verneuil to Modeste, who could not restrain her amazement at seeing the bearer of a historic name turned into such a commonplace figure.

“Gaspard,” called the duchess; he came at once, and she motioned to Modeste, “Mademoiselle de La Bastie, my friend.”

The heir presumptive, whose marriage with Desplein’s only daughter had lately been arranged, bowed to the young girl without seeming struck, as his father had been, by her beauty. Modeste could thus compare today’s youth with the old age of a past epoch; the old Prince de Cadignan had already shown a reverence that made her feel he rendered homage to womanhood as much as to royalty. The Duc de Rhetore, the eldest son of the Duchesse de Chaulieu, chiefly remarkable for impertinent manners, bowed rather cavalierly. The contrast between fathers and sons suggested that young men no longer felt themselves great beings and dispensed with the duties of greatness. The fathers retained the politeness of their vanished grandeur, like mountain-tops still gilded by the sun when all is twilight in the valley.

Ernest at last slipped a word into Modeste’s ear, and she rose immediately.

“My dear,” said the duchesse, thinking she was going to dress, and pulling a bell-rope, “they shall show you your apartment.”

Ernest accompanied Modeste to the foot of the grand staircase, presenting the request of the luckless poet and trying to touch her feelings by describing Melchior’s agony.

“You see, he loves—he is a captive who thought he could break his chain.”

“Love in such a rapid seeker after fortune!” retorted Modeste.

“Mademoiselle, you are at the entrance of life; you do not know its defiles. The inconsistencies of a man who falls under the dominion of a woman much older than himself should be forgiven; he is really not accountable. Think how many sacrifices Canalis has made to her. He has sown too much seed of that kind to resign the harvest; the duchess represents to him ten years of devotion and happiness. You made him forget all that, and unfortunately, he has more vanity than pride; he did not reflect on what he was losing until he met Madame Chaulieu here today. If you really understood him, you would help him. He is a child, always mismanaging his life. You call him a seeker after fortune, but he seeks very badly; like all poets, he is a victim of sensations—childish, easily dazzled by anything that shines, and pursuing its glitter. He used to love horses and pictures, and he craved fame—well, he sold his pictures to buy armor and old Renaissance and Louis XV furniture; just now he is seeking political power. Admit that his hobbies are noble things.”

“You have said enough,” replied Modeste; “come,” she added, seeing her father, whom she called with a motion of her head to give her his arm; “come with me, and I will give you that scrap of paper; you shall carry it to the great man and assure him of my condescension to his wishes, but on one condition—you must thank him in my name for the pleasure I have taken in seeing one of the finest German plays performed in my honor. I have learned that Goethe’s masterpiece is neither Faust nor Egmont—” and, seeing Ernest’s puzzled look, she added, “It is Torquato Tasso! Tell Monsieur de Canalis to re-read it.” She smiled. “Repeat to your friend word for word what I say; it is not an epigram, it is the justification of his conduct—with this difference, that he will, I trust, become more and more reasonable, thanks to the folly of his Eleonore.”

The duchess’s head-woman conducted Modeste and her father to their apartment, where Francoise Cochet had already put everything in order; the colonel was astounded when he heard there were thirty other apartments in the chateau decorated with the same taste.

“This is what I call a proper country-house,” said Modeste.

“The Comte de La Bastie must build you one like it,” replied her father.

“Here, monsieur,” said Modeste, giving the bit of paper to Ernest; “carry it to our friend and put him out of his misery.”

The word our friend struck the young man’s heart. He looked at Modeste to see if there was anything real in the community of interests she seemed to admit, and she, understanding perfectly what his look meant, added, “Come, go at once, your friend is waiting.”

La Briere colored excessively, and left the room in a state of doubt and anxiety less endurable than despair. For the true lover the path that approaches happiness is like the narrow way Catholic poetry calls the entrance to Paradise—a dark passage echoing with the last cries of earthly anguish.

An hour later this illustrious company was assembled in the salon; some played whist, others conversed; the women had their embroideries, and all awaited the announcement of dinner. The Prince de Cadignan was drawing Monsieur Mignon out upon China and his campaigns under the empire, making him talk about the Portendueres, the L’Estorades, and the Maucombes, Provencal families; he blamed him for not seeking service and assured him nothing would be easier than to restore him to his rank as colonel of the Guard.

“A man of your birth and your fortune ought not to belong to the present Opposition,” said the prince, smiling.

This society of distinguished persons not only pleased Modeste but enabled her, during her stay, to acquire a perfection of manners she might otherwise have lacked. Seeing these women was an education; Modeste had the instinct to appropriate the distinctive qualities of Madame de Maufrigneuse and Madame de Chaulieu. Whereas a bourgeois would have ridiculed their ways or made them absurd by clumsy imitation, a well-born, well-educated, right-minded young woman like Modeste fell naturally into connection with these people and saw at once the differences separating the aristocratic world from the bourgeois world, the provinces from the faubourg Saint-Germain. She caught almost imperceptible shadings and perceived the grace of the grande dame without doubting she could acquire it. She noticed also that her father and La Briere appeared infinitely better in this Olympus than Canalis. The great poet, abdicating his real power—the mind—became nothing more than a courtier seeking a ministry, intriguing for an order, flattering the galaxy. Ernest de La Briere, without ambitions, could be himself; while Melchior became, to use a vulgar expression, a mere toady, courting the Prince de Loudon, the Duc de Rhetore, the Vicomte de Serizy, or the Duc de Maufrigneuse, like a man not free to assert himself, as Colonel Mignon did, proud of his campaigns and of Napoleon’s confidence. Modeste noted the strained efforts of the man of real talent seeking witticisms or compliments to please these grand personages. In a word, to Modeste’s eyes the peacock plucked out his tail-feathers.

Toward the middle of the evening the young girl sat down with the grand equerry in a corner of the salon. She led him there purposely to end a suit she could no longer encourage if she wished to retain her self-respect.

“Monsieur le duc, if you really knew me,” she said, “you would understand how deeply I am touched by your attentions. It is because of the profound respect I feel for your character, and the friendship which a soul like yours inspires in mine, that I cannot endure to wound your self-love. Before your arrival in Havre I loved sincerely, deeply, and forever one who is worthy of being loved, and my affection for whom is still a secret; but I wish you to know—and in saying this I am more sincere than most young girls—that had I not already formed this voluntary attachment, you would have been my choice, for I recognize your noble and beautiful qualities. A few words your aunt and sister have said to me as to your intentions lead me to make this frank avowal. If you think it desirable, a letter from my mother shall recall me, on pretense of her illness, tomorrow morning before the hunt begins. Without your consent I do not choose to be present at a fete I owe to your kindness, and where, if my secret should escape me, you might feel hurt and defrauded. You will ask why I have come here at all. I could not withstand the invitation. Be generous enough not to reproach me for what was almost a necessary curiosity. But this is not the chief, not the most delicate thing I have to say you. You have firm friends in my father and myself—more so than perhaps you realize; and as my fortune was the first cause that brought you to me, I wish to say—but without intending to use it as a sedative to calm the grief gallantry requires you to testify—that my father has thought over the affair of the marshes, his friend Dumay thinks your project feasible, and they have already taken steps to form a company. Gobenheim, Dumay, and my father have subscribed fifteen hundred thousand francs, and undertake to get the rest from capitalists who will feel it in their interest to take up the matter. If I have not the honor of becoming the Duchesse d’Herouville, I have almost the certainty of enabling you to choose her, free from all trammels in your choice, and in a higher sphere than mine. Oh! let me finish,” she cried, at a gesture from the duke.

“Judging by my nephew’s emotion,” whispered Mademoiselle d’Herouville to her niece, “it is easy to see you have a sister.”

“Monsieur le duc, all this was settled in my mind the day of our first ride, when I heard you deplore your situation. This is what I have wished to say to you. That day determined my future life. Though you did not make the conquest of a woman, you have at least gained faithful friends at Ingouville—if you will deign to accord us that title.”

This little discourse, which Modeste had carefully thought over, was said with so much charm of soul that tears came to the grand equerry’s eyes; he seized her hand and kissed it.

“Stay during the hunt,” he said; “my want of merit has accustomed me to these refusals; but while accepting your friendship and that of the colonel, you must let me satisfy myself by the judgment of competent scientific men, that the draining of those marshes will be no risk to the company you speak of, before I agree to the generous offer of your friends. You are a noble girl, and though my heart aches to think I can only be your friend, I will glory in that title, and prove it to you at all times and in all seasons.”

“In that case, Monsieur le duc, let us keep our secret. My choice will not be known, at least I think not, until after my mother’s complete recovery. I should like our first blessing to come from her eyes.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Ladies,” said the Prince de Cadignan, as the guests prepared to separate for the night, “I know that several of you propose to follow the hounds with us tomorrow, and it becomes my duty to tell you that if you will be Dianas you must rise, like Diana, with the dawn. The meet is for half-past eight o’clock. I have seen many women display greater courage than men, but for a few seconds only; and you will need a strong dose of resolution to keep you on horseback the whole day, except for a halt for breakfast, which we shall take, like true hunters and huntresses, on the nail. Are you still determined to show yourselves trained horse-women?”

“Prince, it is necessary for me to do so,” said Modeste, adroitly.

“I answer for myself,” said the Duchesse de Chaulieu.

“And I for my daughter Diane; she is worthy of her name,” added the prince. “So, then, you all persist in your intentions? However, I shall arrange, for the sake of Madame and Mademoiselle de Verneuil and others who stay at home, to drive the stag to the further end of the pond.”

“Make yourself quite easy, mesdames,” said the Prince de Loudon, when the Royal Huntsman had left the room; “that breakfast ‘on the nail’ will take place under a comfortable tent.”

The next day, at dawn, the weather promised a fine hunt: the sky veiled with light gray vapor showed patches of blue the northwest wind would likely clear. As the party left the chateau, the Master of the Hunt, the Duc de Rhetore, and the Prince de Loudon rode in front, noting the chateau’s white mass against autumn foliage.

“The ladies are fortunate in their weather,” remarked the Duc de Rhetore.

“Oh, in spite of all their boasting,” replied the Prince de Cadignan, “I think they will let us hunt without them!”

“So they might, if each had not a squire,” said the duke.

Their attention was drawn by a loud altercation as they reached the open at the forest of Rosembray, the meet. The cause was plain: the Prince de Loudon, faddish for English ways, had brought his own British hunting establishment and placed it under the Master of the Hunt. One of his men, a small fair Englishman—insolent, phlegmatic, neatly dressed—had posted himself on one side. John Barry wore a scarlet frock with buttons bearing the De Verneuil arms, white breeches, top-boots, a striped waistcoat, and carried a small hunting-whip and brass horn. He was first whipper-in to fifteen horses and sixty English hounds, which had cost the Duc de Verneuil much.

John found himself forestalled by three other whippers-in in charge of two royal packs brought in carts. They were the three best huntsmen of the Prince de Cadignan, a marked contrast to the Englishman in character and costume. Each wore a broad three-cornered hat above a swarthy, wrinkled face; lean, sinewy, vigorous—men for whom sport was a passion. They bore immense horns of Dampierre, wound with green worsted, but chiefly controlled their hounds by eye and voice. Those noble animals, marked white, black, or liver, had distinct looks; some young ones yelped impatiently, the old scarred hounds lay quiet with ears to the earth.

As the Englishman came up, the royal dogs and huntsmen looked at each other as though to say, “If we cannot hunt by ourselves his Majesty’s service is insulted.”

What began as jests grew fiercer between Monsieur Jacquin La Roulie, the old French whipper-in, and John Barry. The two princes guessed the subject from afar, and the Master of the Hunt, setting spurs to his horse, brought it to an end with authority.

“Who drew the wood?”

“I, monseigneur,” said the Englishman.

“Very good,” said the Prince de Cadignan, proceeding to take Barry’s report.

Dogs and men became silent and respectful before the Royal Huntsman, as all recognized his dignity. The prince laid out the day’s work; a hunt is like a battle, and the Master of Charles X.’s hounds was their Napoleon. Thanks to the admirable system he had introduced into French venery, he could concentrate on the science and strategy of it. He assigned driving the stag to water to the Prince de Loudon’s establishment, expecting the royal hounds to send the stag into the Crown forest on the horizon before the chateau. He soothed his old huntsmen’s pride by giving them the most arduous task, while employing the Englishman at his specialty, letting the two systems face each other and show their strengths.

“Does monseigneur wish us to wait any longer?” said La Roulie.

“I know what you mean, old friend,” said the prince. “It is late, but—”

“Here come the ladies,” said the second whipper-in.

At that moment sixteen riders approached, four ladies at the front with green veils. Modeste, accompanied by her father the grand equerry and La Briere, rode in advance beside the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, escorted by the Vicomte de Serizy. Behind them rode the Duchesse de Chaulieu, flanked by Canalis, on whom she smiled without rancor. When they reached the open where the huntsmen in red coats and brass bugles, surrounded by hounds, made a tableau, the Duchesse de Chaulieu, despite her embonpoint, rode up to Modeste, deeming it more dignified than avoiding the young woman she'd ignored the evening before.

When the Master of the Hunt finished his compliments on their punctuality, Eleonore observed the magnificent whip glittering in Modeste’s little hand and graciously asked to see it.

“I have never seen anything more beautiful,” she said, showing it to Diane de Maufrigneuse. “It suits its possessor,” she added, returning it.

“You must admit, Madame la duchesse,” answered Mademoiselle de La Bastie, with a tender malicious glance at La Briere, “that it is a rather strange gift from a future husband.”

“I should take it,” said Madame de Maufrigneuse, “as a declaration of my rights, in remembrance of Louis XIV.”

La Briere’s eyes filled; for a moment he dropped his reins; a second glance from Modeste bade him not betray his happiness. The hunt began.

The Duc d’Herouville said in a low voice to his fortunate rival, “Monsieur, I hope you will make your wife happy; if I can be of use, command me—I should be glad to contribute to the happiness of so charming a pair.”

This great day, on which such vast interests of heart and fortune were decided, caused but one anxiety to the Master of the Hunt—whether the stag would cross the pond and be killed on the lawn before the house; huntsmen of his calibre are chess-players who can foresee checkmate. The happy old man attained his wish; the run was magnificent, and the ladies were freed from his attendance for the hunt two days later—which, however, proved rainy.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The Duc de Verneuil’s guests stayed five days at Rosembray. On the last day the Gazette de France announced the Baron de Canalis’s appointment as commander of the Legion of Honor and minister at Carlsruhe.

Early in December, after Desplein’s operation, Madame de La Bastie regained her sight; seeing Ernest de La Briere, she pressed Modeste’s hand and whispered, “I should have chosen him myself.”

By late February Latournelle had signed the deeds for the Provence estates, and the La Bastie family obtained the king’s signature to the marriage contract and the ordinance transmitting their title and arms to La Briere, who thereupon took the name La Briere‑La Bastie. Letters‑patent entailed the La Bastie estate at the end of April. La Briere’s witnesses at his marriage were Canalis and the minister he had served five years as secretary; the bride’s were the Duc d’Herouville and Desplein, whom the Mignons remembered with gratitude after showing their regard.

Later in this history we shall again meet Monsieur and Madame de La Briere‑La Bastie; readers will see how sweet and easy the marriage yoke with an educated, intelligent woman can be. Modeste, avoiding the follies of pedantry, is the pride and happiness of her husband, her family, and all who surround her.

BEATRIX

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley

NOTE

Balzac, who examined character and life's small circumstances, was rarely accused of portraying actual people in the thousands of portraits he drew. In “The Great Man of the Provinces in Paris” some likenesses were guessed: Jules Janin in Etienne Lousteau, Armand Carrel in Michel Chrestien, perhaps Berryer in Daniel d’Arthez. But in this volume, “Beatrix,” he used traits of certain persons, acknowledged at publication. Mademoiselle des Touches (Camille Maupin) is George Sand in character; the personal description sometimes applied to Mademoiselle Georges is recognizable from Couture’s drawing. Beatrix, Conti, and Claude Vignon sketch the Comtesse d’Agoult, Liszt, and the critic Gustave Planche.

The opening scene, showing manners and customs of an old Breton family and the transition of the vieille roche into present ideas, is among Balzac’s notable pictures in the “Comedy of Human Life.”

K.P.W.

BEATRIX

I. A BRETON TOWN AND MANSION

France, especially Brittany, still holds towns outside the movement shaping the nineteenth century. Poor roads and irregular communication with Paris make new civilization a spectacle to be watched rather than embraced. Whether fearing or scoffing, these places remain faithful to old manners. A moral archaeologist observing people rather than stones would find images of Louis XV’s time in many Provençal villages, Louis XIV’s in Poitou, and older types in Brittany. Most such towns have faded from splendors not noted by history, which cares more for facts than for manners. Tradition preserves that splendor in Brittany, where natives forget nothing that concerns their land. Many towns were once capitals of small feudal states—counties or duchies conquered by the crown or divided when male lines failed. Disinherited from active life, these heads became arms; arms without nourishment wither.

In the last thirty years these relics have started to fade. Modern industry, working for masses, destroys creations of old art, which were once as personal to consumer as artisan. Today we have products, not works. Public buildings remain in retrospection; monuments of modern industry are quarries, saltpetre mines, cotton factories. Soon even these old cities will be transformed and seen only in iconography.

One town that preserves the feudal type most accurately is Guerande. The name evokes memories for painters, artists, and thinkers who have visited its slopes—this splendid jewel of feudality commands tides and dunes, the summit of a triangle with Croisic and Batz. Few towns, except Vitre and Avignon, preserve a medieval type so intact.

Guerande is still circled with stout walls; moats are full, battlements entire, loopholes free of vegetation; even ivy has never mantled the towers. The town has three gates with portcullis rings; it is entered by an iron-clamped drawbridge, no longer raised but which could be. The mayoralty was blamed in 1820 for planting poplars along the moat to shade the promenade, excusing it by noting the fortifications’ esplanade had been converted a century earlier into a mall where inhabitants strolled beneath elms.

The houses have not changed their frontages; none have added stories or been plastered by an architect. Some rest on wooden columns forming arcades beneath which pedestrians circulate, floor planks bending but never breaking. Merchant houses are small and low, fronts veneered with slate. Decaying wood appears in carved window-casings and pillars, with grotesque faces and fantastic beasts—relics that resist change and present dusky tones and half-blurred features painters prize.

The streets are as they were four hundred years ago, with one exception: population no longer swarms. Social movement is so dead that a traveler may walk alone, sadly, through a deserted street where mullioned windows are boarded up to avoid the window-tax. This street ends at a postern flanked with a masonry wall, beyond which rises a bouquet of trees planted by Breton nature, one of the most luxuriant vegetations in France. A painter or poet might sit under the postern’s arch, where no voice comes from the city’s life and the landscape is seen through casemate loop-holes once occupied by halberdiers and archers—like sashes of some belvedere arranged for a point of view.

Every step recalls habits and usages of long-past times; the very stones tell of them. Medieval ideas remain with their superstitions. If a gendarme passes with his silver-laced hat, his presence is an anachronism; but modern objects are rare. Contemporary clothing is scarce and adapted to immutable customs and fixed physiognomies. The public square is filled with Breton costumes, which artists flock to draw; these stand out: the white linen of paludiers (salt-gatherers) contrasts with peasants’ blues and browns and the sacredly preserved jewelry of the women. These classes and sailors with jerkins and varnished leather caps are as distinct as castes and still recognize their distance from bourgeoisie, nobility, and clergy. Lines are clearly marked; the revolutionary ideal found the masses too rugged to plane. The immutability attributed by science to zoological species is found in Breton human nature. Even after 1830, Guerande remained apart, essentially Breton, fervently Catholic, silent and self-contained—a place where modern ideas make little headway.

Its geography explains this. The town overlooks a salt‑marsh producing Guerande salt, to which many Bretons attribute the excellence of their butter and sardines. It is connected by two roads only: one from Savenay, stopping at Saint-Nazaire; a second from Vannes linking with Morbihan. The arrondissement road establishes land communication, and from Saint-Nazaire by water with Nantes. The land road is used mainly by government; more frequent travel is by water from Saint-Nazaire. Between this village and Guerande are eighteen miles the mail-coach does not serve; not three coach passengers a year would pass over it.

These obstacles discourage travelers. Government moves slowly; inhabitants readily endure difficulties that separate them from the rest of France. Guerande, at the continent’s extreme end, leads nowhere, and no one comes there. Glad to be ignored, she thinks and cares for herself. The immense product of her salt-marshes, which pays a tax of not less than a million to the Treasury, is chiefly managed at Croisic, a peninsula communicating with Guerande over quicksands that erase tracks at night, and by boats crossing the arm of the sea that makes Croisic’s port.

This town is the Herculaneum of feudality, less its lava. It is afoot but not living; it survives because it has not been demolished. If you reach Guerande from Croisic after crossing dreary salt-marshes, you will feel a strong sensation at sight of that vast fortification. From Saint-Nazaire its picturesque position and naive grace please no less. The country about it is ravishing: hedges full of flowers, honeysuckles, roses, box, and enchanting plants. It is like an English garden designed by a great architect. This rich, coy nature, untrodden with violets or lilies in a forest glade, is framed by an African-like desert banked by the ocean—a desert without tree, herb, or bird; where on sunny days laboring paludiers, clothed in white and scattered among melancholy swamps, recall Arabs in burrows.

Thus Guerande bears no resemblance to any other French place. The town produces upon the mind the effect of a sleeping‑draught. It is silent as Venice. There is no public conveyance beyond the springless wagon of a carrier who carries travelers, merchandise, and occasionally letters from Saint-Nazaire to Guerande and back. Bernus, the carrier, was, in 1829, the factotum of this large community. He went and came when he pleased; all the country knew him; he ran errands for all. The arrival of a carriage in Guerande—perhaps a lady or an invalid going to Croisic for sea-bathing thought more efficacious among those rocks than at Boulogne or Dieppe—is still an immense event. Peasants come on horseback, many with commodities for barter in sacks, induced by the need to purchase jewels distinctive of their caste given to Breton brides, and white linen or cloth for their clothing.

For ten miles round, Guerande is always GUERANDE—the town where the famous treaty was signed in 1365, the coast’s key, which may boast, like Batz, of a splendor lost in time. The jewels, linen, cloth, ribbon, and hats are made elsewhere, but to buyers they are from Guerande. Artists and even certain bourgeois who come feel, as at Venice, a desire (soon forgotten) to end their days amid its peace, walking the beautiful mall that surrounds the town from gate to gate toward the sea. Sometimes this image rises in memory’s temple; she reappears crowned with towers, clasped by her girdle; her flower-strewn robe floats, the golden mantle of her dunes enfolds her, and the fragrant breath of her paths exhales at every step; she fills the mind like an enchanting woman whose presence lingers in a fold of the heart.

Near the church stands a mansion to the town what the town is to the region—an image of the past, the symbol of a great thing destroyed—a poem. This mansion belongs to the noblest local family, the du Guaisnics, who in du Guesclin’s days were as superior in antiquity and fortune as Trojans to Romans. The Guaisnics (du Glaicquin), from which du Guesclin comes, are old as Brittany’s granite. They are neither Frenchmen nor Gauls—they are Bretons, Celts. Formerly they must have been Druids, gathering mistletoe in sacred forests and sacrificing on dolmens. Whatever their origin, today this pure race, equal to the Rohans without having become princes before Hugues Capet’s ancestors, possesses two thousand francs a year, its mansion in Guerande, and the little castle of Guaisnic. All lands belonging to the barony of Guaisnic, the first in Brittany, are pledged to farmers and bring in sixty thousand francs a year despite ignorant culture. The du Guaisnics remain nominal owners although they receive none of the revenues, because for two hundred years they have been unable to pay off advances. They are in the position of the crown towards its engagistes before 1789. Where and when could the barons obtain the million their farmers advanced? Before 1789 the fiefs under Guaisnic were worth fifty thousand francs a year; but a vote of the National Assembly suppressed seigneurs’ dues on inheritance.

In such a situation this family—of little account in France and laughed at in Paris—was in Guerande the whole of Brittany. In Guerande the Baron du Guaisnic is one of the great barons of France, a man above whom there is but one—the King. Today the name du Guaisnic, full of Breton significance (roots explained in “The Chouans”), has been altered as other names have been; the tax-gatherer now writes du Guenic.

At the end of a silent, damp lane stands an arch of a gate wide enough for a horseman—proof that when built carriages did not exist. The granite arch, supported by two jambs, frames an oak gate rugged as bark, studded with enormous nails in geometric figures. The semicircular arch is carved with the Guaisnics’ arms as clean-cut as though the sculptor had just laid down his chisel. This escutcheon delights lovers of heraldry by a simplicity proving the family’s pride and antiquity. It is as in crusaders’ days when the Christian world invented symbols to recognize one another; the Guaisnics have never quartered it. Here it reads: Gules, a hand proper gonfaloned ermine, with a sword argent in pale, and the motto, FAC. Is not that noble? A baronial coronet surmounts this simple escutcheon. The artist has given a proud, chivalrous turn to the hand. With what vigor it holds the sword that recently served the family!

If you go to Guerande after reading this history you cannot fail to quiver at that blazon. Even the most confirmed republican would be moved by the fidelity, nobleness, and grandeur in that dark lane. The du Guaisnics did well yesterday, and are ready to do well tomorrow. To DO is chivalry’s motto. “You did well in the battle” was the praise of the Connetable par excellence, the great du Guesclin who drove the English for a time from France. The depth of carving, sheltered from weather by the arch, matches the depth of the motto in the family’s soul. To those who know the Guaisnics this fact is touching.

The gate, when open, gives a view into a somewhat vast courtyard, on the right the stables, on the left the kitchen and offices. The house is freestone from cellar to garret. The facade on the court-yard has a portico with a double range of steps, its wall covered with carved vestiges now effaced, but an antiquary can still make out the Hand bearing the sword in the center. Granite steps are disjointed, grasses and moss force their way through fissures, yet solidity remains. The door must have had a charming character. From remnants of the designs one judges it was by an artist of the great Venetian school of the thirteenth century. A mixture appears of Byzantine and Saracenic. It is crowned with a circular pediment now wreathed in vegetation—a bouquet rose, brown, yellow, or blue by season. The oak, nail-studded door opens to a noble hall, at the end of which is another door opening to a portico that leads to the garden.

This hall is marvelously well preserved. Panelled wainscot three feet high is chestnut. A magnificent Spanish leather with figures in relief, gilding now peeled or reddened, covers the walls. The ceiling is wooden boards artistically joined and painted; the gold is faint; a few red flowers and green foliage remain. Perhaps cleaning might reveal paintings like those discovered on Tristan’s house at Tours, proving the planks date or were restored in Louis XI’s reign. The chimney-piece is enormous carved stone; within are giant wrought-iron andirons, precious workmanship. It could hold a cart-load of wood. Furniture is wholly oak, each article bearing the family arms. Three English guns fit for chase or war, three sabres, two game-bags and fishermen’s utensils hang from nails on the wall.

On one side is a dining-room connecting with the kitchen by a door cut through a corner tower. This tower corresponds on the facade toward the court-yard with another tower at the opposite corner, where a spiral staircase leads to the two upper stories.

The dining-room hangs with fourteenth-century tapestries; the style and orthography of banderols beneath each figure prove their age, but in the naive language of the fabliaux they are impossible to transcribe here. These tapestries, preserved where light has scarcely penetrated, are framed in bands of oak black as ebony. The ceiling has projecting rafters enriched with varied foliage; spaces between are painted blue where garlands of golden flowers twine. Two old buffers face each other; on shelves rubbed by Mariotte the cook, can be seen as in 1200, four old goblets, an ancient embossed soup-tureen, two silver salt-cellars; many pewter plates and pitchers of gray and blue pottery with arabesque designs and the du Guaisnics’ arms, covered by hinged pewter lids. The chimney-piece is modernized—its state proves the family has lived in this room for the last century. It is of carved stone in Louis XV style, ornamented with a mirror let in the back with gilt beaded moulding. This anachronism, to which the family is indifferent, would grieve a poet. On the mantel-shelf, covered with red velvet, stands a tall tortoise-shell clock inlaid with brass, flanked by silver candelabra of singular design. A large square table with solid legs fills the center; the chairs are turned wood covered with tapestry. On a round table supported by a single leg shaped like a vine-shoot before a window into the garden is an odd lamp: a common glass globe about the size of an ostrich egg fastened to a candlestick by a glass tube; a wick issues through a hole at the top and passes through a brass reed drawing nut-oil coiled within like a tape-worm in a surgeon’s phial. Windows into the garden and court-yard are mullioned with hexagonal panes, draped by curtains with heavy valances of ancient crimson silk with gold reflections, once called brocatelle or small brocade.

Each of the two upper stories has but two rooms: the head of the family’s bedroom and the children’s. Guests lodged in chambers beneath the roof; servants above kitchens and stables. The pointed roof, protected with lead at angles and edges, has noble pointed windows on each side, one looking down on the court-yard, the other on the garden. These windows rise almost to the roof and have slender casings whose carvings have crumbled under salty vapors. Above each arch with stone crossbars grinds the vane of a noble.

Let us note a precious detail, full of naïveté, valuable to an archaeologist. The spiral-stair tower is placed at the corner of a great gable wall with no window. The staircase descends to a little arched door opening on a gravelly yard between house and stables. This tower is repeated on the garden side by another of five sides ending in a cupola containing a bell-turret, instead of being roofed like the sister-tower with a pepper-pot. Architects varied skylines thus. These towers connect on the first floor by a stone gallery supported by brackets each ending in a grotesque human head. The gallery has an exquisite balustrade. From the gable hangs a stone dais like those crowning saints’ statues at church portals. Can you not see a woman walking on this balcony and gazing over Guerande at sunshine gilding the sands and shimmering on Ocean? Admire the gable wall flanked by varied towers. The opposite gable of the Guaisnic mansion adjoins the next house. Harmony carefully sought by architects of those days is maintained on the court-yard facade by the tower communicating between dining-room and kitchen, the same as the staircase tower except it stops at the first upper story and its summit is a small open dome beneath which stands a now blackened statue of Saint Calyste.

The garden is magnificent for so old a place. It covers half an acre, its walls espaliered, the space divided into vegetable squares bordered with cordons of fruit-trees that the man-of-all-work, Gasselin, tends between grooming horses. At the farther end is a grotto with a seat; in the middle, a sun-dial; the paths are gravelled. The garden facade has no towers corresponding to those on the court-yard; but a slender spiral column rises from ground to roof that once bore the family banner—an iron socket at its summit where a few weak plants straggle. This detail, with vestiges of sculpture, proves to a practiced eye that a Venetian architect built the mansion. The graceful staff is a signature revealing Venice, chivalry, and thirteenth-century delicacy. If any doubt remained, the ornamentation’s trefoils with four leaves instead of three would dispel it. This difference plainly indicates the Venetian school depraved by commerce with the East, where semi-Saracenic architects, careless of Catholic thought, give four leaves to clover, while Christian art remained faithful to the Trinity. Here Venetian art becomes heretical.

If this ancient dwelling attracts your imagination, you may ask why such miracles are not renewed. Because today mansions are sold, pulled down, and their grounds turned into streets. No one is sure the next generation will possess the paternal dwelling; homes are no more than inns; whereas in former times when a dwelling was built, men worked, or thought they worked, for a family in perpetuity. Hence the grandeur of these houses. Faith in self, as well as faith in God, performed prodigies.

As for the arrangement of the upper rooms, they may be imagined after this ground-floor description and an account of the family’s manners, customs, and physiognomy. For the last fifty years the du Guaisnics have received friends in the two rooms described, in which, as in the court-yard and external accessories, the spirit, grace, and candor of old noble Brittany still survive. Without the town’s topography and the house’s minute depiction, the surprising figures of the family might be less understood. Therefore the frames have preceded the portraits. Everyone knows things influence beings. There are public buildings whose effect is visible upon persons living nearby. It would be difficult to be irreligious in the shadow of a cathedral like Bourges. When the soul is everywhere reminded of its destiny by surrounding images, it is less easy to fail of it. Such was our immediate grandfathers’ thought, now abandoned by a generation soon to have no signs, no distinctions, and whose manners and morals would change every decade. If you do not now expect to find the Baron du Guaisnic sword in hand, all here written would be falsehood.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Early in May 1836 the Guenic family consisted of Monsieur and Madame du Guenic, Mademoiselle du Guenic (the baron’s elder sister), and an only son of twenty-one named Gaudebert-Calyste-Louis by old family usage; the father’s name was Gaudebert-Calyste-Charles. Saint Gaudebert and Saint Calyste were forever bound to protect the Guenics.

The Baron du Guenic had left Guerande the moment La Vendée and Brittany rose; he fought through the war with Charette, Cathelineau, La Rochejaquelein, d’Elbée, Bonchamps, and the Prince de Loudon. Prudently, before leaving he sold all his property to his sister, Mademoiselle Zéphirine du Guenic. After the leaders’ deaths the baron, miraculously spared, refused to submit to Napoleon and continued to resist until 1802, when defeated and nearly captured he returned to Guerande, went to Croisic, and from there crossed to Ireland, faithful to the old Breton hatred for England.

The people of Guerande feigned ignorance of the baron’s existence; in twenty years not a single indiscreet word was spoken. Mademoiselle du Guenic collected the rents and sent them to her brother by fishermen. Monsieur du Guenic returned to Guerande in 1813 as quietly as if he had spent a season at Nantes. During his stay in Dublin the old Breton, well past fifty, fell in love with a charming Irishwoman, Fanny O’Brien, daughter of a noble but poor family. She was twenty-one. The baron went to France to obtain marriage papers, returned to Ireland, and about ten months later (early 1814) brought his wife to Guerande, where she gave him Calyste on the very day Louis XVIII landed at Calais—hence the young man’s final name, Louis.

The old and loyal Breton was now seventy-three; his long guerrilla warfare, exile, and perilous sea crossings had prematurely aged him, making him seem much older. Thus the head of the house of Guenic fitted perfectly with the worn grandeur of their dwelling, built when a court still reigned at Guerande.

Monsieur du Guenic was tall, straight, wiry, and lean. His oval face was lined with innumerable wrinkles that formed a net over cheekbones and brow, resembling faces Rembrandt loved to paint. The sunken face bore the marks of outdoor life and watching his country. Yet enough remained to touch the soul. The firm outline of brow, the solemn lines, the rigid nose—wounded only slightly—declared intrepidity without calculation, faith without reserve, obedience without discussion, fidelity without compromise, and love without inconstancy. In him, Breton granite had become man.

The baron had no teeth; his lips, once red, were now violet and drawn inward, giving an expression both threatening and proud. His chin seemed to seek his nose, which, humped in the middle, betrayed his energy and Breton resistance. His marbled skin indicated a sanguine temperament fitted to resist fatigue. His abundant white hair fell in curls on his shoulders. In his black eyes, despite the extinguished face, still glittered the last flames of a generous and loyal soul. Eyebrows and lashes had disappeared; shaving had become difficult, so he let his beard grow fan-shaped. His hands—large, broad, hairy—were those of a soldier and guerrilla, hands that had handled sword and oar, fought among thorns and marshes; recent marks showed he had again joined the Vendée. These hands were a living commentary on the Guenic motto: Fac!

His forehead showed golden temples against a hard narrow brow, broadened by hair loss, adding majesty to that noble ruin. The countenance, material yet natural to the Breton type, presented a certain savagery and stolid calm, resembling Huguenot impassibility—a repose where animal nature dominates and thought is rare. Thought seemed an effort; its seat was more in the heart than the head; it led to acts rather than ideas. The mystery resolves: he had faiths and sentiments inborn, allowing him to dispense with much thought. Institutions and religion thought for him; he reserved his mind, and his kind, for action. He drew thought from his heart like his sword from its scabbard, held aloft on his scutcheon, shining with sincerity. That explains the sale to his sister before the war—provision for death, confiscation, exile. The beauty of character in these two old people (for the sister lived only by and for the brother) cannot be understood by the selfish morals of the present age. An archangel could not have found one thought of personal interest. In 1814, when the rector of Guerande suggested the baron go to Paris to claim recompense from the Bourbons, the sister cried, “Oh, fy! does my brother need to hold out his hand like a beggar?” “It would be thought I served a king from interest,” said the baron. “Besides, it is for him to remember. Poor king! he must be weary indeed of those who harass him.”

This loyal servant, who had spent life and means on Louis XVIII, received the rank of colonel, the cross of Saint-Louis, and a stipend of two thousand francs a year—news the baron accepted as the king’s remembrance. No one undeceived him; the gift had been made by the Duc de Feltre. As gratitude to the king, the baron held a siege at Guerande against General Travot; refusing to surrender the fortress, he evacuated into the woods with a band of Chouans who remained armed until the second Bourbon restoration. Guerande still remembers that siege.

The Baron du Guenic was as illiterate as a peasant; he could read, write, and cipher a little, knew military art and heraldry, but, aside from his prayer-book, had not read three volumes in his life. His clothing was always the same: stout shoes, ribbed stockings, greenish velveteen breeches, a cloth waistcoat, and a loose coat with a collar from which hung the cross of Saint-Louis. A noble serenity reigned on his face; for the last year or so sleep—prelude to death—seemed preparing him for eternal rest. This somnolence did not alarm his wife, his blind sister, or his friends, whose medical knowledge was slight; to them these pauses were natural: the baron had done his duty.

In this ancient mansion the absorbing interests were the fortunes of the dispossessed elder branch, the future of the exiled Bourbons, the Catholic religion, and political innovations in Brittany. The one personal interest was the attachment of all for the only son, Calyste, heir and sole hope of the du Guenic name.

Some years earlier the old Vendean had a return of youth to train his son in manly exercises proper for a gentleman ready at any moment to take arms. When Calyste was sixteen, his father took him to the marshes and forest, teaching war’s rudiments through chase, preaching by example, firm in saddle and sure in shot, indifferent to fatigue, intrepid before obstacles, bidding his son follow him into danger.

So, when the Duchesse de Berry landed to attempt restoration for her son, the father judged it right to take his boy and join her, practicing the family motto. The baron started at night, saying nothing to his wife, taking his son under fire as if to a fête, accompanied by Gasselin, his sole vassal. The three were absent three months without sending news to the baroness, who read the Quotidienne with trembling, nor to the blind sister, whose nerve never faltered. The three guns on the walls had seen service recently. The baron, considering the enterprise useless, left the region before the affair of La Pénissière; otherwise the house of Guenic might have ended in hecatomb.

On a stormy night after parting from Madame, the father, son, and servant returned, surprising the baroness and Mademoiselle du Guenic, though the blind sister recognized their steps in the lane. The baron looked around the anxious circle seated by the antique lamp and said in a tremulous voice, while Gasselin replaced guns and sabres, in words of feudal simplicity: “The barons did not all do their duty.” Then he kissed his wife and sister, sat in his old arm-chair, and ordered supper for his son, Gasselin, and himself. Gasselin had thrown himself before Calyste to protect him and received a sabre cut on his shoulder; the women scarcely thanked him. The baron and guests uttered neither curses nor complaints about conquerors—silence a Breton trait. In forty years no one ever heard contumely from the baron about his adversaries; it was for them to do their duty as he did his. This silence indicates an unalterable will.

This last effort, a flash of waning energy, had caused the present weakness and somnolence of the old man. The fresh defeat and exile of the Bourbons, driven out as miraculously as restored, were a bitter sadness.

About six on the evening this history begins, the baron, who by custom dined at four, fell asleep while his wife read the Quotidienne. His head rested against the arm-chair back beside the fireplace on the garden side.

Near that gnarled ancient tree, and before the fire, the baroness sat, the type of those admirable women found most in England, Scotland, or Ireland—milk-white, golden-haired, the curls wound as if by angels, with a heavenly light in their hair. Fanny O’Brien was one of those sylphs—strong in tenderness, invincible under misfortune, soft as her voice, pure as her blue eyes; skin silken to the touch, beauty beyond painter or word. Still beautiful at forty-two, many would have counted themselves fortunate to marry her as she looked on the autumn display of flowers and fruit.

The baroness held the paper in dimpled fingers, nails cut square like an antique statue’s. Half reclining, feet out to warm, she wore a black velvet gown for the chill. The corsage rose to the throat and molded the shoulders and bosom. Her hair was in ringlets in the English fashion, the rest twisted to the crown and pinned with a tortoiseshell comb. The color, not undecided like other blondes, gleamed like burnished gold. She braided the short nape-locks—a racial sign—concealing the tiny braid in the coif, a detail showing the care she gave her person; it was her pride to please the old baron. When a woman displays in her own home the coquetry many reserve for outside, she is a noble mother and wife; she loves without calculation, does good in secret, and worships. In Fanny the Virgin’s halo seemed to preserve her beauty from time. Her skin had taken warm pearly tones; her brow, broad and well-modeled, caught the light; turquoise eyes shone with incomparable sweetness; soft lashes and slightly sunken temples inspired mute melancholy. Her aquiline thin nose suggested high birth; pure lips wore happy smiles of inexhaustible loving-kindness. She had gained a slight embonpoint lately, but hips and slender waist remained pleasing. Autumn had added perennial flowers to her summer beauty—nobler rounded arms, finer skin grain, fuller outlines. Above all, her open serene face and pure blue eyes expressed illimitable sympathy and angelic tenderness.

At the other hearth, in an arm-chair, the octogenarian sister, like her brother in all except dress, sat listening and knitting stockings—the work requiring no sight. Both eyes had cataracts; she refused an operation despite her sister-in-law’s entreaties. Secretly, she said it was want of courage; truly, she would not let her brother spend twenty-five louis for her. That sum saved would be for the household’s good.

These two old persons set off the baroness’ beauty. Mademoiselle Zéphirine, deprived of sight, was unaware of what eighty years had done to her face. Her pale hollow countenance, the white sightless eyes giving an appearance of death, and three or four solitary projecting teeth, framed by a little brown printed cotton hood quilted like a petticoat and trimmed with a ruche, seemed menacing. She wore a cotillon—short coarse skirt—over a quilted petticoat (a veritable mattress hiding double louis-d’or) and pockets sewn to a belt she unfastened each night and put on each morning like a garment. Her body was encased in the casaquin of Brittany, a spencer of the same cloth, with a pleated collar, the washing of which caused her only dispute with her sister-in-law—she changed it only once a week. From the large wadded sleeves came two withered yet vigorous arms, their brownish-red hands like stocking-machines eternally at work; from time to time she would thrust a long knitting needle between hood and hair to poke her white locks, thrusting it back carelessly without fear of harm. She was straight as a steeple; her erect and imposing carriage might be one of old age’s coquetries proving pride a necessary passion of life. Her smile was gay. She, too, had done her duty.

As soon as the baroness saw her husband asleep, she ceased reading. A ray of sunshine stretched across the old room, burnishing black furniture, touching ceiling carvings, dancing on time-worn chests, and brightening the old oak table; Fanny’s voice and the light made music in the octogenarian blind sister’s heart. The ray sank into the melancholy tones of twilight; the baroness fell into deep meditation, a silence noted by her sister-in-law for the last two weeks, unexplained and unasked about. Mademoiselle continued to knit; the silence deepened until the clicking of needles was plainly heard.

“You have dropped the paper, sister, but you are not asleep,” said Mademoiselle slyly.

Mariotte then entered to light the lamp, which she placed on a small table by the fire, fetched her distaff and ball of thread, and sat on a stool in the window recess to spin. Gasselin busied himself about the offices, saw to the horses, readied the stable, and fed the two hunting-dogs; their joyful barking was the last noise to awaken the echoes in the dark walls. The two dogs and two horses were the only remaining vestiges of the house’s chivalry. An imaginative man sitting on the portico steps might have quivered to the hounds’ baying and the horses’ neighing.

Gasselin was a short, thick, squat Breton, with black hair and sun-browned face, silent, slow, and obstinate as a mule, yet steady. He was forty-two and had been in the household twenty-five years, hired by Mademoiselle at fifteen on hearing of the marriage and the baron’s probable return. He considered himself part of the family, had played with Calyste, loved the horses and dogs, petted them, and wore a blue linen jacket with flapping pockets, matching waistcoat and trousers, blue stockings, and stout hob-nailed shoes; in cold he donned a goatskin.

Mariotte, also over forty, was to woman what Gasselin was to man—same complexion, figure, lively black eyes. It is curious they never married; perhaps it seemed immoral, they were so like brother and sister. Mariotte’s wages were ninety francs a year; Gasselin’s, three hundred. But thousands elsewhere would not have tempted either to leave the Guenic household. Both were under Mademoiselle’s orders, who, from the war to the baron’s return, had ruled the house. When she learned the baron would bring home a mistress, she feared losing the household sceptre, yet she was agreeably surprised to find in Fanny O’Brien a woman of high birth who disliked petty household cares and would prefer plain fare to preparing costly food for herself. Fanny was capable of all duties, strong under necessary privations but without taste for commonplace work. When the baron begged his sister, in his wife’s name, to continue in charge, the old maid kissed the baroness like a sister, made a daughter of her, adored her, and rejoiced to keep control of household management. She ran the house on an almost inconceivable economy, relaxing only for great occasions—lying-in, Calyste’s needs, and similar events.

Though the two servants were used to the stern regime and required no orders—masters’ interests were their own—Mademoiselle insisted on overseeing everything. She knew, without looking, how many nuts in the barn or oats in the bin, carrying on her belt a boatswain’s whistle to summon Mariotte with one note and Gasselin with two.

Gasselin’s greatest happiness was tending the garden, producing fine fruits and vegetables; with so little to do he would have been lost without it. After grooming horses and cleaning the ground-floor rooms, he weeded and watched for a field-mouse or the grub of the cockchafer, joyfully presenting his catch to his masters. He liked going to Croisic on fast-days to buy fish cheaper than at Guerande.

Thus no household was more united in interests, more bound together than this noble family devoted to duty. Masters and servants seemed made for one another. For twenty-five years there had been neither trouble nor discord. The only griefs were the boy’s petty ailments, the only terrors the events of 1814 and 1830. If the same tasks were performed at the same hours, and food obeyed seasons and times, this monotony, like nature’s, varied only by cloud and sun, and was sustained by affection in all hearts—the more fruitful because it sprang from natural causes.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When night had fairly fallen, Gasselin entered the hall and asked his master respectfully if he was still needed.

“You can go out, or go to bed, after prayers,” replied the baron, waking, “unless Madame or my sister—”

The two ladies signaled assent. Gasselin knelt as his masters rose to kneel on their chairs; Mariotte also knelt before her stool. Mademoiselle du Guenic then said the prayer aloud. After it was over, someone rapped at the door on the lane and Gasselin opened it.

“I dare say it is Monsieur le curé; he usually comes first,” said Mariotte.

Everyone recognized the rector’s foot on the portico steps. He bowed respectfully to the three occupants and addressed them with the unctuous civility of a priest. To the somewhat absent-minded greeting of the mistress of the house he replied with ecclesiastical inquiry.

“Are you anxious or ill, Madame la baronne?” he asked.

“Thank you, no,” she replied.

Monsieur Grimont, about fifty, of middle height, lost in his cassock from which issued two stout shoes, showed a plump visage and generally pale skin with yellow spots. His dimpled hands and placid abbatial face suggested a Dutch burgomaster; his straight black hair and lively brown eyes recalled a Breton peasant. His gaiety, that of a man whose conscience is calm, admitted a joke. He lacked the unease of rectors whose power is contested; seen through Guerande, even the most irreligious traveler would recognize him as the sovereign of that Catholic town. Yet before the du Guenics he lowered his spiritual superiority; in their salon he was a chaplain in his seigneur’s house. In church his benediction always favored the Guenic chapel, where their mailed hand and device adorned the keystone.

“I thought Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had already arrived,” said the rector, sitting and taking the baroness’s hand to kiss it. “She is getting unpunctual. Can it be that dissipation is contagious? I see Monsieur le chevalier is again at Les Touches this evening.”

“Don’t say anything about those visits before Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel,” cried the old maid eagerly.

“Ah! mademoiselle,” remarked Mariotte, “you can’t prevent the town from gossiping.”

“What do they say?” asked the baroness.

“The young girls and the old women all say he is in love with Mademoiselle des Touches.”

“A lad of Calyste’s make is playing his proper part in making the women love him,” said the baron.

“Here comes Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel,” said Mariotte.

The gravel in the courtyard crackled under discreet footsteps; a page with a lantern accompanied the coming lady. Seeing him, Mariotte moved her stool to the great hall to talk with him by the lantern’s gleam, economizing the hall’s single oribus candle.

This elderly demoiselle was thin, dried-up, yellow as parchment, wrinkled, with gray eyes, prominent teeth, and the hands of a man. Short, slightly crooked, possibly hump-backed, she stirred a great mass of petticoats when reaching a pocket; a loud jingling of keys and money echoed among her garments. She wore, dangling from one side, the keys of a good housekeeper, and from the other a silver snuff-box, thimble, knitting-needles, and other resonant implements. Instead of Zephirine’s wadded hood she had a green bonnet—once fresh, now yellowing—of a fashion preserved by her nieces and renewed every five years at Nantes. Her gowns, too, were made by her nieces to an immutable pattern. She used the old cane with its short hook and bore the ermine of ancient Breton dukes. In her and her sister the illustrious house of the Pen-Hoels ended. Her younger sister had married a Kergarouet who, despite disapproval, added Pen-Hoel to his name and called himself Vicomte de Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel.

“Heaven has punished him,” said the old lady; “he has nothing but daughters, and the Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel name will be wiped out.”

Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had about seven thousand francs a year from rents. She had come into her property at thirty-six and managed it herself, inspecting it on horseback and displaying the firmness often seen in deformed persons. Her avarice was admired locally; she kept one woman-servant and the page, and her yearly expenses, excluding taxes, did not exceed a thousand francs. Thus she attracted the caresses of the Kergarouet-Pen-Hoels, who spent winters at Nantes and summers on the Loire estate. She was supposed ready to leave her fortune to whichever niece pleased her best; every three months one of the four demoiselles de Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel (the youngest twelve, the eldest twenty) came to spend a few days with her.

A friend of Zephirine du Guenic, Jacqueline de Pen-Hoel, devoted to the Breton grandeur of the du Guenics, had planned since Calyste’s birth to transmit her property to the chevalier by marrying him to whichever niece the Vicomtesse de Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel would bestow. She dreamed of buying back some of the best Guenic property from the farmer engagistes. When avarice has an object it ceases to be a vice; it becomes a means of virtue. Perhaps Zephirine knew Jacqueline’s plan. Perhaps even the baroness, whose soul was occupied by love for her son and tenderness for his father, guessed it, seeing with what wily perseverance Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel brought her favorite niece, Charlotte de Kergarouet, now sixteen. The rector, Monsieur Grimont, was certainly in her confidence; he helped the old maid invest her savings.

But even had Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel possessed three hundred thousand francs in gold, the du Guenics would never have shown any attention that could be construed as seeking her fortune. From true Breton pride, Jacqueline, glad of the supremacy accorded to Zephirine and the du Guenics, always showed herself honored by her relations with Madame du Guenic and her sister-in-law. She even concealed the sacrifice she made each evening in allowing her page to burn in the Guenic hall that singular gingerbread-colored candle called an oribus still used in western France.

Thus this rich old maid was nobility, pride, and grandeur personified. At the moment of this narrative, the Abbe Grimont has revealed that on the evening when the old baron, the young chevalier, and Gasselin secretly departed to join MADAME (to the terror of the baroness and the great joy of all Bretons) Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had given the baron ten thousand francs in gold—an immense sacrifice—to which the abbe added another ten thousand, a tithe collected by him—charging the old hero to offer the whole, in the name of the Pen-Hoels and of the parish of Guerande, to the mother of Henri V.

Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel treated Calyste as if her intentions gave her rights over him; her plans seemed to authorize supervision. Not strict in gallantry—she had the indulgence of old women of the old school—she held in horror modern ways. Calyste might increase her esteem by a few Breton adventures but would lose it considerably if she saw him entangled in what she called innovations. She might disinter a little gold to pay for the results of a love-affair, but if Calyste drove a tilbury or spoke of a visit to Paris she would deem him dissipated and a spendthrift. To her, novel ideas meant the overthrow of crop succession, ruin under the name of improvements, mortgaged lands as the inevitable result of experiments. Prudence was the true method of making fortune; good management meant filling granaries and waiting for a rise rather than risking experiments. By singular chance she often made lucky sales that confirmed her principles. She was thought maliciously clever but was not quick-witted; methodical as a Dutchman, prudent as a cat, and persistent as a priest, those qualities in routine-bound Brittany were practically equivalent to intellect.

“Will Monsieur du Halga join us this evening?” asked Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, taking off her knitted mittens after the usual exchange of greetings.

“Yes, mademoiselle; I met him taking his dog to walk on the mall,” replied the rector.

“Ha! then our mouche will be lively to-night. Last evening we were only four.”

At the word mouche the rector rose and took from a drawer a small round osier basket, a pile of ivory counters yellow with use, and a greasy pack of cards. He arranged counters before each player and set the basket beside the lamp with infantine eagerness and the manner of a man accustomed to this service.

A firm knock at the outer gate echoed through the mansion. The page opened, and presently the long, lean, methodically clothed Chevalier du Halga, former flag-captain to Admiral de Kergarouet, outlined himself in black on the portico.

“Welcome, chevalier!” cried Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel.

“The altar is raised,” said the abbe.

The chevalier was a man in poor health, who wore flannel for his rheumatism, a black-silk skull-cap to protect his head from fog, and a spencer to guard his chest from sudden gusts. He always carried a gold-headed cane and a little bitch named Thisbe. Fussy as a fine lady and speaking low to spare his voice, he had in earlier days been among the most intrepid officers of the old navy, winning the confidence of de Suffren and the friendship of the Comte de Portenduere. His splendid conduct as flag-captain to Admiral Kergarouet was written on his scarred face. Now no one would imagine the voice that ruled storms and the eye that compassed the sea.

The chevalier never smoked, never swore; he was gentle and tranquil as a girl, as concerned about Thisbe’s caprices as an elderly dowager. He moved as an invalid, walking as if stepping on eggs, complaining of wind, sun, or mist, yet showed the whitest teeth in the reddest gums—a fact reassuring as to his maladies, which were rather expensive, consisting of four daily meals of monastic amplitude. His frame, like the baron’s, was bony and indestructibly strong, covered with a parchment-like skin retaining the tawny color from India, whence, however, he did not bring back facts or ideas. He had emigrated with his friends, lost his property, and now ended his days with the cross of Saint-Louis and a pension of two thousand francs from the Invalides de la Marine. The slight hypochondria that made him invent imaginary ills is explained by actual suffering during the emigration. He served in the Russian navy until Emperor Alexander ordered him employed against France; he then resigned and lived at Odessa near the Duc de Richelieu, with whom he returned to France. The duke obtained for this relic of the old Breton navy the pension that enabled him to live. On the death of Louis XVIII he returned to Guerande and eventually became mayor of the city.

For the last fifteen years the rector, the chevalier, and Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had regularly passed their evenings at the hôtel de Guenic, where other noble neighbors also came. It will be readily understood that the du Guenics were at the head of the faubourg Saint-Germain of the old Breton province, where no member of the new administration sent down by the government was ever allowed to penetrate. For the last six years the rector coughed when coming to the crucial words, Domine, salvum fac regem. Politics in Guerande remained at that point.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
IV. A NORMAL EVENING

Mouche is played with five cards to each player and one turned up to show trumps. Each round a player may play or abstain; abstaining costs only his stake unless there are forfeits in the basket. If he plays and wins a trick he is paid pro rata to the stake; win none and he is mouche, paying the whole stake and swelling the next basket. Those who decline throw down their cards; their play is null. Players may exchange cards with the remainder of the pack in sequence, so the first two sometimes take most of it. The turned-up trump belongs to the dealer, who is last and may exchange it for any card in his hand. One powerful card, the knave of clubs, is called Mistigris.

The game provokes cupidity, diplomacy, and poker faces. At the hotel du Guenic each player took twenty counters representing five sous, making the stake five farthings—a significant sum for this company. In extraordinary luck fifty sous could be won—more than anyone in Guerande spent in a day. Consequently Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel played mouche with the passion of a big-game hunter. Mademoiselle Zephirine, who shared with the baroness, attached equal importance, treating one farthing for a chance at five as a serious investment.

An 1825 convention stated the game should cease when anyone losing ten sous wished to retire; politeness forbade continuing without him. The chevalier and the baron, however, found a way around this: when all but one wished to continue, du Halga, a rich free-spending sailor, would offer ten counters to Zephirine or Jacqueline, recompensing them if they won. The baron made similar offers under the pretext of continuing the game. The misers accepted, though not without pressing—a maidens' custom—but only after the baron and chevalier had won, or the offer would insult them.

Mouche became brilliant when a Demoiselle de Kergarouet passed through with her aunt. The Kergarouets insisted on being called simply Kergarouet. The aunt treated the niece to mouche at du Guenic and ordered her to look amiable—easy in Calyste’s presence, whom the four Kergarouet girls adored. Brought up in modern ways, they cared little for five sous; stakes rose. These evenings excited the old blind sister, who signaled stakes to the baroness by pressing her foot a number of times. To play or not when the basket was full provoked inward struggle—cupidity versus fear. If Charlotte de Kergarouet was lucky, her aunt would nevertheless lecture her on reserve and modesty for appearing too decisive.

These nightly games, carried on for twenty years, were sometimes interrupted by gossip or political discussion. Players often sat half an hour talking with cards fanned on their stomachs. If a counter was missing from the basket, everyone declared they had put in their share. Usually the chevalier made up the deficiency and was accused of being absent-minded about his health; when he paid, Zephirine and Jacqueline fretted they themselves might have forgotten. The company enjoyed the small suspicions. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel would almost always accuse the rector of cheating when he won the basket.

“It is singular,” he replied, “that I never cheat except when I win the trick.”

Sometimes talk of royal misfortunes would distract the baron, and evenings could end without gain or loss but with emotion and complaints that mouche was dull. Once, after the Kergarouets arrived, they tried whist and boston at the baroness’s urging but could not understand them; on departure the new games were voted head-splitters and the company returned to mouche—tradition triumphing in Brittany.

While the rector dealt, the baroness asked the Chevalier du Halga the same health questions as the night before. The chevalier made it a point to invent new ailments; he never complained of real wounds but of fancied pains—the doctors have found no remedy for illnesses that don't exist. “Yesterday the trouble was, I believe, in your legs,” said the rector. “It moves about,” said the chevalier. The exchange, with light banter from Zephirine and Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, displayed the chevalier’s old wit and a life rich in memories.

The conversation turned to the death of the Duc de Lenoncourt, and the baron remarked he would be next; his wife gently rebuked him. The company noticed the baron’s melancholy and afterward would say, “Monsieur du Guenic was sad to-night.” The phrase “The Baron du Guenic fails” opened many conversations.

“How is Thisbe?” asked Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel. “The poor thing is like her master,” the chevalier replied, mimicking the dog and exposing his hand—an attempt by a neighbor to see if he had Mistigris or the trump. The baroness observed the rector’s nose grow pale—his sign of holding Mistigris—because of the pleasure that card gave him.

“You had company today,” said the chevalier. “Yes, a cousin of my brother-in-law,” she replied; she told of the marriage of the Comtesse de Kergarouet, a Demoiselle de Fontaine. The chevalier, who knew the admiral, recognized the name. She then astonished them with tales about Mademoiselle des Touches—so strange she could hardly believe them; if true, Calyste would not be so constantly with her. The baron pricked up his ears.

But Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel suddenly had Mistigris and could not continue. “Your play, monsieur le baron,” she said. Zephirine returned to knitting; the rector, informed about Calyste and Mademoiselle des Touches, refrained. The baron inquired again: “What is so extraordinary?” “She smokes,” Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel said. The chevalier commented dryly. Asked about her property, the old maid said she was squandering it.

“The game is mine!” cried the baroness; she had a long run of trumps and Mistigris. The victory horrified Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, who forgot Calyste and Mademoiselle des Touches for a moment. By nine only the baroness and rector remained; the others had gone to bed. The chevalier, as usual, accompanied Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel home, commenting on Zephirine’s delight in engulfing her gains. He had also noticed the baroness’ abstraction.

At Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel’s door she confided the cause: “Calyste is lost unless we marry him promptly. He loves Mademoiselle des Touches, an actress!” “Then send for Charlotte.” “I have; my sister will receive my letter tomorrow,” she replied.

From this sketch of a normal evening one may imagine the hubbub excited throughout Guerande by any stranger’s arrival, stay, or passage.

When silence came from the baron’s and his sister’s rooms, the baroness looked at the rector, who toyed pensively with the counters. “I see that you begin to share my anxiety about Calyste,” she said. “Did you notice Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel’s displeased looks to-night?” he asked. She had the best intentions for Calyste; she loved him as though he were her son. His conduct in the Vendée beside his father and Madame's praises of his devotion had increased that affection. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel intends to execute a deed of gift leaving her property to whichever niece Calyste marries. The rector acknowledged the mother’s hope for a richer Irish match but said it was wise to have two strings to the bow—Pen-Hoel’s savings and well-managed land were not to be despised. He then described Mademoiselle des Touches’ life: a stage-woman and writer, frequenter of theatres and artists, squandering her fortune on pamphleteers, painters, and musicians, under the false name Camille Maupin. She had turned Les Touches into an improper paradise of dissipation; more wine was drunk there in weeks than in all Guerande in a year. Last year the Demoiselles Bougniol let lodgings to men suspected of being Blues; they sang wicked songs and spent time at Les Touches. And this is whom Calyste adores; he would gallop to Nantes for an infamous book but not for the Church. She is not a royalist; if Calyste were called to fight for the cause and she wanted to keep him, the chevalier might let his own father go without him.

“Oh, no!” exclaimed the baroness. The rector feared to test Calyste; the mother would suffer too much. “I will see Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel tomorrow,” he promised. “Don’t be too uneasy. The harm may not be as great as they say. I will find out the truth. Jacqueline trusts me. Calyste is our child and pupil—he will not let the devil inveigle him nor disturb his family’s plans. One fault is not vice.”

“You are only informing me of details,” said the baroness. “Was I not the first to notice the change in my Calyste? A mother keenly feels being second in her son’s heart. This crisis is a trial of motherhood. I had hoped Calyste would love someone noble and beautiful—not a stage-player, a masquerader, an author who feigns sentiments, a creature who will deceive and make him unhappy! She has had adventures—”

“With several men,” said the rector. “Yet this impious creature was born in Brittany! She dishonors her land. I shall preach upon her next Sunday.”
