He was anxiously awaiting a general election, in the hope of being returned to the Chamber of Deputies. He hankered after a peerage; and then—

“The king will be his cousin, won’t he?” said Nanon, la Grande Nanon, Madame Cornoiller, bourgeoise of Saumur, as she listened to her mistress, who was recounting the honors to which she was called.

Nevertheless, Monsieur de Bonfons (he had finally abolished his patronymic of Cruchot) did not realize his ambitions. He died eight days after his election as deputy of Saumur. God, who sees all and never strikes amiss, punished him, no doubt, for his sordid calculations and the legal cleverness with which, accurante Cruchot, he had drawn up his marriage contract, in which husband and wife gave to each other, “in case they should have no children, their entire property of every kind, landed or otherwise, without exception or reservation, dispensing even with the formality of an inventory; provided that said omission of said inventory shall not injure their heirs and assigns, it being understood that this deed of gift is, etc., etc.” This clause explains the profound respect which monsieur le président always testified for the wishes, and above all, for the solitude of Madame de Bonfons. Women cited him as the most considerate and delicate of men, pitied him, and even went so far as to find fault with the passion and grief of Eugenie, blaming her, as women know so well how to blame, with cruel but discreet insinuation.

“Madame de Bonfons must be very ill to leave her husband entirely alone. Poor woman! Is she likely to get well? What is it? Something gastric? A cancer?”—“She has grown perfectly yellow. She ought to consult some celebrated doctor in Paris.”—“How can she be happy without a child? They say she loves her husband; then why not give him an heir?—in his position, too!”—“Do you know, it is really dreadful! If it is the result of mere caprice, it is unpardonable. Poor president!”

Endowed with the delicate perception which a solitary soul acquires through constant meditation and the clear-sightedness with which a mind aloof from life fastens on all that falls within its sphere, Eugenie, taught by suffering and by later education to divine thought, knew well that the president desired her death that he might step into possession of their immense fortune, augmented by the property of his uncle the notary and his uncle the abbé, whom it had lately pleased God to call to himself. The poor solitary pitied the president. Providence avenged her for the calculations and the indifference of a husband who respected the hopeless passion on which she spent her life because it was his surest safeguard. To give life to a child would give death to his hopes—the hopes of selfishness, the joys of ambition, which the president cherished as he looked into the future.

God thus flung piles of gold upon this prisoner to whom gold was a matter of indifference, who longed for heaven, who lived, pious and good, in holy thoughts, succoring the unfortunate in secret, and never wearying of such deeds. Madame de Bonfons became a widow at thirty-six. She is still beautiful, but with the beauty of a woman nearly forty. Her face is white, placid and calm; her voice gentle and self-possessed; her manners simple. She has the noblest qualities of sorrow, the saintliness of one who has never soiled her soul by contact with the world; but she also retains the rigid bearing of an old maid and the petty habits inseparable from the narrow round of provincial life. In spite of her vast wealth, she lives as the poor Eugenie Grandet once lived. The fire is never lighted on her hearth until the day when her father allowed it to be lighted in the hall, and it is put out in conformity with the rules which governed her youthful years. She dresses as her mother dressed. The house in Saumur, without sun, without warmth, always in shadow, melancholy, is an image of her life. She carefully accumulates her income, and might seem parsimonious did she not disarm criticism by a noble employment of her wealth. Pious and charitable institutions, a hospital for old age, Christian schools for children, a public library richly endowed, bear testimony against the charge of avarice which some persons lay at her door. The churches of Saumur owe much of their embellishment to her. Madame de Bonfons (sometimes ironically spoken of as mademoiselle) inspires for the most part reverential respect: and yet that noble heart, beating only with tenderest emotions, has been, from first to last, subjected to the calculations of human selfishness; money has cast its frigid influence upon that hallowed life and taught distrust of feelings to a woman who is all feeling.

“I have none but you to love me,” she says to Nanon.

The hand of this woman stanches the secret wounds in many families. She goes on her way to heaven attended by a train of benefactions. The grandeur of her soul redeems the narrowness of her education and the petty habits of her early life.

Such is the history of Eugenie Grandet, who is in the world but not of it; who, created to be supremely a wife and mother, has neither husband nor children nor family. Lately there has been some question of her marrying again. The Saumur people talk of her and of the Marquis de Froidfond, whose family are beginning to beset the rich widow just as, in former days, the Cruchots laid siege to the rich heiress. Nanon and Cornoiller are, it is said, in the interests of the marquis. Nothing could be more false. Neither la Grande Nanon nor Cornoiller has sufficient mind to understand the corruptions of the world.

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
#############
##### MIDDLE OF THE STORY #####
#############
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

For souls to whom effusiveness comes easily there is a delightful hour between day and night; twilight throws softened lights and reflections on every object and favors a dreamy mood wedded to the play of light and shade. The prevailing silence makes it especially dear to artists, who grow contemplative, step back from pictures they can no longer work on, and pass judgment, rapt by meanings that flash on the inner eye of genius. He who has never stood pensive by a friend in such a poetic hour can hardly grasp its inexpressible soothingness. Favored by the clear-obscure, material skill used to produce illusion entirely disappears. If the work is a picture, the figures seem to speak and walk; shade is shadow, light is day; flesh breathes, eyes move, blood flows, and fabrics change sheen. Imagination heightens every realistic detail and sees only the work’s beauties. At that hour illusion reigns despotically; it wakens a mind’s night, peopled by dreams. It bears the soul aloft to a world of fancies where the artist forgets the real world—yesterday, tomorrow, the future—everything, miseries and goods alike.

At this magic hour a young, talented painter who saw in art nothing but Art itself was perched on a step-ladder working on a large, now nearly finished painting. Criticising and admiring himself, floating on the current of his thoughts, he lost himself in a meditative mood that elevated and soothed the soul. His reverie lasted long. Night fell. Whether he meant to come down or made some ill-judged movement believing himself on the floor—the cause escaped him—he fell, struck his head on a footstool, lost consciousness, and lay motionless for an unknown time.

A sweet voice roused him from his stunned state. When he opened his eyes a bright light made him close them again; through the mist he heard two women whispering and felt two timid hands supporting his head. He soon recovered; by the light of an old-fashioned Argand lamp he saw the most charming face he had seen, a head that for an artist realized the ideal beauty he had formed for himself. Her features recalled the refined delicacy of Prudhon’s school and the poetic sentiment of Girodet: fresh temples, regular eyebrows, pure outline, virginal innocence stamped on every feature. Her figure was slight and graceful. Her dress, simple and neat, revealed neither wealth nor penury.

As he came to, the painter expressed admiration and fumbling thanks. A handkerchief pressed to his forehead; amid the studio smell he recognized the strong odor of ether used to revive him. He saw an old woman, looking like a marquise of the old school, who held the lamp and advised the young girl.

“Monsieur,” said the younger woman in answer to a few questions the painter asked while still vague from the shock, “my mother and I heard the noise of your fall and fancied we heard a groan. The silence after the crash alarmed us, and we hurried up. Finding the key in the latch, we entered and found you lying motionless. My mother fetched what was needed to bathe your head and revive you. You have cut your forehead—there. Do you feel it?”

“Yes, I do now,” he replied.

“Oh, it will be nothing,” said the old mother. “Happily your head rested against this lay-figure.”

“I feel infinitely better,” replied the painter. “I need nothing further but a hackney cab to take me home. The porter’s wife will go for one.”

He tried to repeat his thanks, but the elder lady interrupted him at every sentence: “Tomorrow, monsieur, pray be careful to put on leeches, or to be bled, and drink a few cups of something healing. A fall may be dangerous.”

The young girl stole glances at the painter and the pictures. Her expression revealed perfect propriety; her curiosity seemed rather an absence of mind, and her eyes spoke the natural interest women feel in suffering. The two strangers seemed to forget the painter’s works in his mishap. When he reassured them they left, looking at him with anxious kindness free from insistence or familiarity, without indiscreet questions or invitations. Their noble and simple manners made a quiet impression on the painter; later, recalling details of the incident, he was greatly struck by them.

On the landing the old lady gently observed, “Adelaide, you left the door open.”

“That was to hear me fall,” said the painter with a grateful smile.

“You came down just now, mother,” replied the young girl with a blush.

“Would you like us to accompany you downstairs? The stairs are dark.”

“No, thank you, madame; I am much better.”

“Hold tightly by the rail.”

The two women remained to light him, listening to his steps.

To appreciate the interest this scene aroused, the painter had only a few days earlier established his studio in the attics of this house in the darker, muddier part of the Rue de Suresnes, almost opposite the Church of the Madeleine and near his rooms in the Rue des Champs-Elysees. Fame had recently made him one of the artists most dear to his country; he was beginning to feel free from want, and, as he put it, was enjoying his last privations. Instead of working in cheaper studios near the city gates he had spared himself the long walk and the loss of time now more valuable than before.

No man inspired more interest than Hippolyte Schinner, though he did not lightly reveal the secrets of his life. He was the idol of a necessitous mother who had raised him amid severe privations. Mademoiselle Schinner, daughter of an Alsatian farmer, had never married. Her gentle soul had been cruelly crushed long ago by a rich man who showed no delicacy in his love affairs. The day she lost her illusions was an age of reflection, religious thought, and resignation. She refused the betrayer’s alms, renounced the world, and made a glory of her shame. She devoted herself to maternal love, sought in it her joys instead of social pleasures, worked, and saved a treasure for her son. In after years one day amply repaid her for those sacrifices.

At the last exhibition her son received the Cross of the Legion of Honor. Newspapers hailed an unknown genius, artists acknowledged Schinner as a master, and dealers covered his canvases with gold. At twenty-five Hippolyte, to whom his mother had transmitted her woman’s soul, understood his standing. Eager to restore to his mother the pleasures society had denied her, he lived for her, hoping that fame and fortune would one day make her happy, rich, respected, and surrounded by people of mark. Schinner chose friends among the honorable and distinguished; fastidious in selection, he desired to raise still further a position his talent had placed high. His solitary work from boyhood had left him with beautiful beliefs that grace early days. His adolescent soul was not closed to the shy emotions that make a young man an apart being, full of poetry and virginal hopes, puerile to the worldly but deep for their singleness.

He had gentle, polite manners that spoke to the soul and fascinated even those who did not understand them. His voice, from the heart, stirred noble sentiments and testified to his modesty by ingenuous tones. Those who saw him felt an attraction of moral nature they could not analyze; they might call it galvanism or attempt to express it in formulas.

These details help explain why, in the absence of the porter whom he had sent to the end of the Rue de la Madeleine for a coach, Hippolyte did not question the porter’s wife closely about the two women who had tended him. He replied to her natural inquiries about his accident and the fourth-floor tenants but could not stop her chatter, led by lodge policy and gossip.

“Ah,” she said, “they were, no doubt, Mademoiselle Leseigneur and her mother, who have lived here four years. We do not know exactly what these ladies do; in the morning, till noon, an old woman who is half deaf comes in to help them; in the evening, two or three old gentlemen with ribbon loops, like you, monsieur, come to see them and often stay very late. One comes in a carriage with servants; they say he has sixty thousand francs a year. However, they are very quiet tenants, as you are, monsieur; and economical! they live on nothing, and pay as soon as a letter is brought. It is queer, monsieur, the mother’s name is not the same as the daughter’s. Ah, but when they go for a walk in the Tuileries, mademoiselle is very smart, and she never goes out without being followed by a lot of young men; but she shuts the door in their face, and she is quite right. The proprietor would never allow—”

The coach came; Hippolyte heard no more and went home. His mother dressed his wound and would not allow him to return to the studio the next day. After advice and treatments, Hippolyte remained at home three days. During this enforced idleness his fancy recalled, bit by bit, the scene of his fainting fit. The young girl’s profile returned clearly against the darkness of his inward vision; he saw the mother’s faded features again, felt Adelaide’s touch. He remembered some gesture whose grace memory enhanced; then an attitude, a tone of voice rising like objects from deep waters to the surface.

When he could resume work he went early to his studio; really he hurried to visit his neighbors. When passion wakes, inexplicable pleasures are felt; he mounted the fourth-floor stairs slowly, and his heart throbbed as he neared the humble brown door of Mademoiselle Leseigneur. He fancied similarity between their positions, imagined misfortunes of birth akin to his own. He lingered at the studio to think of them, dined there, then about seven o’clock went to call.

No painter of manners has sufficiently told the curious privacy of certain Parisian existences: fresh elegant toilettes and brilliant women whose homes betray doubtful comfort. If my picture is sometimes bold, blame the truth; the appearance of his neighbors’ rooms greatly influenced Hippolyte Schinner’s feelings and hopes.

The house belonged to one of those proprietors who abhor repairs and decoration, who regard being a Paris homeowner as business. Between miser and usurer, such men are allergic to moving a cupboard or opening an air-hole; when chimneys fall they deprive themselves of theatre nights “on account of repairs.” Hippolyte, who had seen a comical scene about decorative repairs, was not surprised by dark greasy paint, oily stains, and other disagreeable woodwork signs. These stigmata of poverty are not without a sort of poetry to an artist.

Mademoiselle Leseigneur opened the door. Recognizing the young artist she bowed and, with Parisian tact and pride, turned to shut a glass partition through which Hippolyte might have seen linen hung over patent ironing stoves, an old camp-bed, charcoal and irons, a filter, household crockery—utensils of a small household. Muslin curtains screened this capharnaum, lighted by windows facing a neighboring yard.

With an artist’s quick eye Hippolyte saw the uses, furniture, and general condition of this cut-in-half first room. The more honorable half, serving as ante-room and dining-room, was hung with old salmon-rose paper with a flock border patched with wafers. Prints of the battles of Alexander, in gilding-rubbed frames, were arranged on the walls. In the middle stood a massive, old-fashioned mahogany table worn at the edges. A small stove with a thin pipe stood before the chimney, whose hearth was blocked by a cupboard. Chairs showed remains of former splendor—carved mahogany, red morocco seats, gilt nails—scarred like a veteran.

This room was a museum of objects that show both luxury and penury: a splendidly finished telescope hung over a discolored glass on the chimney. Between chimney and partition was a wretched painted sideboard pretending to be mahogany. Slippery red quarries, shabby rugs, and all the furniture shone with hard cleanliness that accentuated oldness. An indescribable odor mixed exhalations from the lumber-room, dining-room vapors, and stair smells, though the window was partly open. Street air fluttered dusty curtains, carefully drawn to hide a window bay where former tenants left ornamental additions—a sort of domestic fresco.

Adelaide hastened to open the inner room door and announced the painter with obvious pleasure. Hippolyte, who had earlier seen similar poverty in his mother’s home, noted these signs with an impression vivid as earliest memories and entered with sympathy rather than scorn.

“Well, monsieur, I hope you no longer feel the effects of your fall,” said the old lady, rising from an antique armchair and offering a seat.

“No, madame. I have come to thank you for the kind care you gave me, and above all mademoiselle, who heard me fall.”

As he spoke—words stamped with the exquisite awkwardness of first love—Hippolyte looked at the young girl. Adelaide lit the Argand lamp, replacing a tallow candle in a large copper candlestick, then placed the lamp on the chimney shelf and sat a little behind the painter to look at him at her ease while appearing interested in the lamp. The flame sputtered in the damp chimney. Seeing the large mirror on the chimney-piece, Hippolyte fixed his eyes on it to admire Adelaide; her stratagem only embarrassed them both.

While talking with Madame Leseigneur—Hippolyte called her so—he examined the room unobtrusively. Egyptian figures on iron fire-dogs were scarcely visible under cinders; two brands struggled before a sham log. An old Aubusson carpet, faded and mended, left the tiled floor cold. Walls were hung with reddish paper imitating figured silk. Opposite the windows he saw a crack and the paper outline of double doors closing a recess where Madame Leseigneur likely slept, poorly disguised by a sofa. Above a mahogany chest of drawers hung a portrait of an officer of rank, so dim he could not judge it well; from what he saw it must have been painted in China. Window-curtains of red silk were as faded as the furniture in red-and-yellow worsted work. On the chest’s marble top was a costly malachite tray with a dozen magnificently painted coffee cups, perhaps Sevres. On the chimney shelf stood an Empire clock—a warrior driving four horses on a chariot whose wheel bore the hours. Tall candlesticks held smoke-yellowed tapers; porcelain vases crowned with dusty artificial flowers stood in the corners.

In the middle of the room a card-table with new packs suggested a painful dilemma: either these two women were perfectly honest or they lived by intrigue and gambling. Yet in looking at Adelaide, so pure-minded, Schinner could not but believe in her innocence and attribute the furniture’s incoherence to honorable causes.

“My dear,” said the old lady to the young one, “I am cold; make a little fire, and give me my shawl.”

Adelaide fetched a cashmere shawl—once costly, now faded and darned—and wrapped her mother in it. She then brought a bundle of small wood from the lumber-room and gallantly threw it on the fire to revive it.

It is hard to reproduce the ensuing conversation. Guided by the tact born of early hardship, Hippolyte did not remark on his neighbors’ poverty; any direct question would be indiscreet. The simplest pity can offend, so his thoughts and words diverged. The two ladies began by talking of painting; women easily guess the embarrassment of a first call and supply devices to end it. Questioning him about his art emboldened him to talk, and the chat’s light nothings led Hippolyte to reflections that revealed his habits and mind. Trouble had prematurely faded the old lady’s face; only prominent features remained, an outline suggesting shrewdness with grace in the eyes—an expression of the old Court that can hint either at artifice or refined delicacy.

A woman’s face hides mystery: the line between frankness and duplicity, intrigue and heart, is inscrutable. A penetrating eye might discern differences; such appreciation lies in intuition alone. The old lady’s face, like her room, made it hard to tell whether the squalor covered vice or highest virtue. But at Schinner’s age the heart’s first impulse is to believe in goodness. As he studied Adelaide’s noble, almost haughty brow and soulful eyes, he breathed the modest fragrance of virtue. He seized an opportunity to discuss portraits and thus examine the faded pastel whose chalk had fallen away.

“You are attached to that picture for the sake of the likeness, no doubt, mesdames, for the drawing is dreadful?” he said, looking at Adelaide.

“It was done at Calcutta, in great haste,” replied the mother, her voice agitated.

She gazed at the formless sketch with the absorption memories of happiness bring; in her face were traces of perennial regret. The painter interpreted her attitude as the mark of a life once happy. He then sat down beside her.

“Madame,” he said, “in a very short time the colors of that pastel will disappear. The portrait will only survive in your memory. Will you allow me to reproduce the likeness on canvas? It will be more permanently recorded. Grant me, as a neighborly favor, the pleasure of doing you this service. There are times when an artist is glad of a respite from greater undertakings by doing work of less lofty pretension; it will be a recreation for me.”

The old lady flushed at the offer, and Adelaide shot a glance of deep feeling. Hippolyte wanted some tie to his neighbors, to gain a right to mingle in their life. His offer appealed to the heart’s affections, gratified his pride as an artist, and could not wound the ladies. Madame Leseigneur accepted, without eagerness or reluctance, with the self-possession of a noble soul, aware of the character of bonds formed by such an obligation.

“I fancy,” said the painter, “that the uniform is that of a naval officer.”

“Yes,” she said, “that of a captain in command. Monsieur de Rouville—my husband—died at Batavia from a wound received in a fight with an English ship off the Asiatic coast. He commanded a frigate of fifty-six guns; the Revenge carried ninety-six. The struggle was unequal, but he defended his ship bravely, held out till nightfall and got away. When I returned to France Bonaparte was not yet in power, and I was refused a pension. When I applied again, quite lately, I was sternly informed that if the Baron de Rouville had emigrated I should not have lost him; that by this time he would have been a rear-admiral; finally, his Excellency quoted degrees of forfeiture. I took this step, urged by friends, only for Adelaide’s sake. I have always hated holding out my hand as a beggar in the name of a grief that deprives a woman of voice and strength. I do not like this money valuation for blood irreparably spilt—”

“Dear mother, this subject always does you harm.”

The Baronne Leseigneur bowed and was silent.

“Monsieur,” said the young girl, “I had supposed a painter’s work was generally fairly quiet?”

Schinner colored, remembering the noise he had made. Adelaide rose at the sound of a carriage stopping at the door. She went into her own room and returned with a pair of tall gilt candlesticks with partly burnt wax candles, which she quickly lit. Without waiting for the bell she opened the outer door where she set the lamp down. The sound of a kiss given and received touched Hippolyte’s heart. His impatience to see the man who treated Adelaide familiarly was not immediately gratified; the newcomers conversed in undertone with the young girl.

At last Mademoiselle de Rouville returned, followed by two men whose costumes and countenances need a long tale. The first, about sixty, wore one of those coats fashioned for Louis XVIII.: neither civil nor military, it might pass for either. Fleurs-de-lis embroidered the lapels and gilt buttons bore the same. Shoulder straps begged for useless epaulettes; a buttonhole blossomed with many colored ribbons. He probably always carried his three-cornered hat with a gold cord, for his powdered hair showed no traces of pressure. He might be taken for fifty and seemed robust. His frank, loyal emigre expression hinted also at libertine habits, the reckless gallantry of the Musketeers. His gestures and manner showed no intention of renouncing royalism, religion, or love affairs.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A fantastic figure stood behind this specimen of “Louis XIV.’s light infantry”—the Bonapartists’ nickname for these venerable Monarchy survivors. He ought to have been the picture’s principal subject, but served only as an accessory. A lean, dry man dressed like the first, seeming his reflection or shadow: his coat older, his powder less white, the fleurs-de-lis dimmer, his shoulder-straps more hopeless and dog’s-eared; his intellect feebler, his life nearer its end. He realized Rivarol’s quip about Champcenetz, “He is the moonlight of me.” He was simply a paler double, the difference between a first and last impression of a lithograph.

The painter found this speechless old Chevalier a mystery. He did not speak; nobody spoke to him. Was he a friend, a poor relation, a companion who followed at the old gallant’s heels? Did he fill a place between dog, parrot, and friend? Had he saved his patron’s fortune, or only his life? Was he the Trim to a Captain Toby? At the Baronne de Rouville’s he provoked curiosity without satisfying it. Who after the Restoration could recall an attachment that, before the Revolution, had bound him to his friend’s wife, dead twenty years?

The leader, least dilapidated of these wrecks, gallantly kissed Madame de Rouville’s hand and sat down by her. The Chevalier bowed and placed himself a couple of chairs away from his model. Adelaide stood behind the old gentleman’s armchair, leaning her elbows on the back in an attitude unconsciously like Guerin’s depiction of Dido’s sister.

Though the gentleman’s familiarity was fatherly, his freedom seemed to annoy the girl. “What, are you sulky with me?” he said, then shot at Schinner one of those shrewd, diplomatic side-looks that ask, when they see a stranger, “Is he one of us?”

“This is our neighbor,” said the Baronne, pointing to Hippolyte. “Monsieur is a celebrated painter; his name must be known to you despite your indifference to the arts.”

The old man saw the intent in suppressing the name and bowed to the young man. “Certainly,” he said. “I heard much about his pictures at the last Salon. Talent has immense privileges,” he added, observing the artist’s red ribbon. “That distinction, which we must earn at the cost of our blood and long service, you win in your youth; but all glory is of the same kindred,” he said, laying his hand on his Cross of Saint-Louis.

Hippolyte murmured thanks and fell silent, satisfied to admire the beautiful girl’s head. He soon forgot the extreme misery of the dwelling; Adelaide’s face stood out against a luminous atmosphere. He answered questions briefly, his attention divided by that inward double consciousness when soul and ear attend different things. Hope poured out before him a thousand visions of happiness; he refused to analyze the evening’s incidents.

After a while the Baroness and her daughter began to play cards with the old gentleman. The Chevalier, faithful to his shadow role, stood behind his friend’s chair watching the game, answering mute inquiries with approving nods and repetition of gestures.

“Du Halga, I always lose,” said the gentleman.

“You discard badly,” replied the Baronne.

“For three months now I have never won a single game,” he said.

“Have you the aces?” asked the old lady.

“Yes, one more to mark,” he answered.

“Shall I come and advise you?” said Adelaide.

“No, no. Stay where I can see you. By Gad, it would be losing too much not to have you to look at!”

At last the game was over. The gentleman pulled out his purse and, throwing two louis-d’or on the table, not without temper, “Forty francs,” he exclaimed, “the exact sum.—Deuce take it! It is eleven o’clock.”

“It is eleven o’clock,” repeated the silent figure, looking at the painter.

Hippolyte, hearing this more distinctly, thought it time to retire. He found a few commonplace remarks to make, took leave of the Baroness, her daughter, and the two strangers, and went away wholly possessed by the first raptures of true love, without attempting to analyze the evening’s little incidents.

The next day the painter felt an ardent desire to see Adelaide again. He might have presented himself at six when he went to his studio, but waited until the afternoon. Blushing like a girl, he shyly asked Mademoiselle Leseigneur, who came to let him in, for the portrait of the Baron.

“But come in,” said Adelaide, who had no doubt heard him come down from the studio.

The painter entered, tongue-tied; happiness dulled his wit. To see Adelaide, hear the rustle of her skirt after longing all morning to be near her, was an intensity that made trifles precious. The old lady was not in the drawing-room. Adelaide brought a chair to stand on to take down the picture; finding she could not reach it without stepping on a chest of drawers, she turned to Hippolyte and, blushing, said, “I am not tall enough. Will you get it down?”

Her modesty was the true motive; he read this and returned one of those looks of intelligence that are the sweetest language of love. Adelaide cast down her eyes with maidenly reserve. Hippolyte, feeling almost timid, took down the picture, examined it in the window light, and then went away saying only, “I will return it soon.”

During that brief moment both felt a storm of agitation, as if a stone flung into a deep lake had set in motion long ripples in their hearts.

Hippolyte returned to his studio with the portrait. His easel awaited a fresh canvas; his palette set, brushes cleaned, the light chosen. He worked with the ardor artists throw into their whims until dinner, then returned that evening to the Baronne’s and stayed from nine till eleven. The same topics, the same arrivals, the same game, the same loss by the old man; only Hippolyte, a little bolder, ventured to chat with the young girl.

A week passed, and their feelings underwent the slow, delightful transformations that bring two souls to understanding. Every day Adelaide’s greeting grew more intimate, her voice more eager, her manner more familiar. They laughed and talked with the simplicity of children newly made friends, as if they had known one another for years. Schinner wished to be taught piquet; being a novice, he blundered and lost almost every game. Without a word of love the lovers knew they were everything to each other. Hippolyte enjoyed his power over his gentle little friend; Adelaide, timid and devoted, allowed many concessions, playing small caprices like a spoilt child. Familiarity with the old Count soon stopped: she perceived the painter’s melancholy from the abrupt tone of his words when the old man unceremoniously kissed her hands or throat.

Mademoiselle Leseigneur began to expect an account of the painter’s actions; she scolded him so effectually for absences that he gave up other friends and went nowhere else. Adelaide’s jealousy showed when she heard that sometimes at eleven he still had visits to pay in Paris’s brilliant drawing-rooms. She declared such a life bad for his health and argued passionately that a man who expended time and charms on several women at once could not love warmly. So the painter, driven by passion and the exactions of a girl in love, lived exclusively in the little apartment that enchanted him.

Never was there a purer or more ardent love. Both trusted and were delicate; their passion grew without the need for sacrifices. A spontaneous affinity made their union close. Two months after their first meeting their lives had become one: in the morning Adelaide could tell by footsteps that “He is there.” When Hippolyte went home to his mother at dinner he always looked in on his neighbors; in the evening he flew back with lover’s punctuality. Adelaide enjoyed the realization of the ideal she naturally dreamt of.

The old gentleman came less often; Hippolyte, who had been jealous, had taken his place at the green table and shared his constant ill-luck. Yet sometimes, amid his happiness, thought of Madame de Rouville’s desperate poverty haunted him. “Strange! twenty francs every evening?” he asked himself, and dared not confess his odious suspicions.

He spent two months on the portrait; when finished, varnished, and framed, he deemed it one of his best works. Madame de Rouville had not spoken of it again. Was this indifference or pride? He would not account for the silence. Joyfully plotting with Adelaide, he planned to hang it when the Baroness was out. One day, while her mother walked in the Tuileries, Adelaide went up to Hippolyte’s studio to see the portrait in its light. She stood speechless in ecstatic contemplation, all a woman’s feeling merged in admiration for the man she loved. The painter, uneasy, leaned forward; she held out her hand, unable to speak, and two tears fell. Hippolyte kissed her hand; for a minute they stood mute, longing to confess their love and not daring. The painter kept her hand in his; their hearts beat wildly. Overcome, Adelaide drew away and said simply, “You will make my mother very happy.”

“What, only your mother?” he asked.

“Oh, I am too happy.”

Frightened by the vehemence stirred in his heart, the painter bent his head. Both, sensing danger, went downstairs and hung the picture in its place. Hippolyte dined for the first time with the Baroness, who, overcome and in tears, embraced him.

That evening the old emigre, the Baron de Rouville’s comrade, paid a visit to announce he had just been promoted to vice-admiral; his land travels through Germany and Russia had been counted as naval campaigns. Seeing the portrait, he shook the painter’s hand and exclaimed, “By Gad! though my old hulk does not deserve to be perpetuated, I would gladly give five hundred pistoles to see myself as like as that is to my dear old Rouville.”

At this hint the Baroness looked at her young friend and smiled with sudden gratitude. Hippolyte suspected the admiral meant to offer the price for both portraits while paying for his own. His pride as an artist, and his jealousy, took offense; he replied, “Monsieur, if I were a portrait-painter I should not have done this one.” The admiral bit his lip and sat down to cards.

The painter stayed near Adelaide, who proposed a dozen hands of piquet, to which he agreed. As he played he noticed an unexpected eagerness in Madame de Rouville to win and finger the old gentleman’s gold. Evil suspicions troubled Hippolyte. Could the Baroness be living by gambling? Was she playing to pay a debt, to meet the rent? Why would this rich old man frequent a fourth-story room where he lost about forty francs each evening? Why had he been so familiar with Adelaide and then given up the rights he had acquired?

These reflections led him to watch the old man and the Baroness more closely. Their sidelong glances at Adelaide displeased him. “Am I being duped?” was his last, horrible thought. He resolved to stay after the departure of the two old men to confirm or dispel his suspicions. He drew out his purse to pay and, carried away by thought, laid it on the table and fell into a brief reverie; then, ashamed of his silence, he rose, answered some commonplace question, and went close to the Baroness to examine her withered features as she talked.

He left, racked by doubts. A few steps down, he turned back to fetch the forgotten purse. “I left my purse here!” he said to Adelaide.

“No,” she answered, reddening.

“I thought it was there,” he said, pointing to the card-table. Not finding it, shamed for Adelaide and the Baroness, he looked at them with blank amazement that made them laugh; he checked himself, felt his waistcoat, and said, “I must have made a mistake. I have it somewhere no doubt.”

In the purse were fifteen louis d’or and some small change. The theft seemed flagrant and denied with effrontery; Hippolyte no longer felt doubt about his neighbors’ morals. He stood on the stairs, legs shaking, dizzy, in a cold sweat, unable to walk, struggling with the shock of his destroyed hopes. He recalled various trifling observations that now corroborated his frightful suspicions and opened his eyes to the character and life of these two women.

Had they waited until the portrait was given them before robbing him? If so, the theft was more odious. He remembered Adelaide’s seeming curiosity at the worn silk netting—might she have been counting coins in his purse while making innocent jests to watch for a moment to steal? He briefly fancied other motives—perhaps the admiral hesitated to marry Adelaide—but checked himself with the thought that if the Baroness hoped to make him marry her daughter, they would not have robbed him.

Clinging to his illusion and the love already rooted, he tried to find an accident: “The purse must have fallen on the floor,” he told himself. He searched himself hastily but did not find it. His memory retraced the truth: he distinctly saw the purse on the green cloth. Even so, he excused Adelaide, thinking misfortune might explain a degrading action; there must be some secret. He refused to admit that that proud face could be a lie.

The next morning he had not slept. The heartache that follows the loss of unrealized bliss made rapid inroads. To lose the future we dreamed of, to renounce it, is a keener pang than losing known happiness. Hope can be better than memory. His spirit was plunged into a shoreless sea; such partial death can lead, in delicate natures, to ruin. Thus Hippolyte wandered under the Tuileries’ fresh shade, absorbed and forgetful.

There he met Francois Souchet, a school-fellow and studio-mate about to set out for Italy. “Why, Hippolyte, what ails you?” asked the sculptor.

“I am most unhappy,” replied Hippolyte gravely.

“Nothing but a love affair can cause you grief. Money, glory, respect—you lack nothing.”

Led imperceptibly into confidences, Hippolyte confessed his love. When Souchet heard “Rue de Suresnes, a young girl on the fourth floor,” he laughed and began to recall what all the young men knew: the mother’s appearance and the air that betrayed her station. “Do you not know her by the way she holds her bag?” he said. Several acquaintances joined; jokes and mockery, lively but painful, followed. Hippolyte’s reticence made him suffer as his heart’s secret was handled so carelessly. He pretended contradiction and asked for proofs, but the jesting resumed. “Come at four o’clock and study their walk,” Souchet advised. Torn by conflicting feelings, Hippolyte parted from his friends, still convinced Adelaide must be superior to such accusations and filled with remorse for suspecting her. He passed the door of their rooms, feeling a pain none could misread. His love was such that, despite the theft, he still worshiped her. He thought of playing the good genius who rescues a woman whose love others have abandoned—romantic and grand to a highly-strung soul.

He sat in his studio, gazed at his picture without working, tears in his eyes, brush in hand but not touching. Night fell; roused by darkness, he went downstairs, met the old admiral, looked darkly at him as he bowed, and fled. He had planned to see the ladies, but the admiral’s presence froze him. For the hundredth time he wondered what interest brought this prodigal, with eighty thousand francs a year, to a fourth story where he lost forty francs every evening; he imagined motives.

In the next days Hippolyte threw himself into his work to conquer passion by idea and composition. Study consoled him, though memories of tender hours with Adelaide persisted. One evening, leaving his studio, he saw the door of the ladies’ rooms half open and Adelaide standing in the window recess. The door and staircase made it impossible not to be seen. He bowed coldly, with a glance of supreme indifference; yet thinking of the pain such coldness would inflict, he shuddered inwardly. That week of disdain would have been terrible; perhaps the purse had been found and she had sought him nightly. This thought filled him with remorse; he asked whether the signs of attachment Adelaide showed did not deserve inquiry and at least justification. Ashamed of resisting his heart for a week, he called that evening on Madame de Rouville.

All his suspicions vanished at the sight of Adelaide, pale and thin. “Good heavens! what is the matter?” he asked, after greeting the Baroness.

Adelaide made no reply, but gave him a look of deep melancholy that pained him. “You must have thought us indifferent?” said the Baroness.

“I was wrong,” he replied. “There are forms of pain we know not how to confide to any one.”

“The sincerity and strength of friendship are not to be measured by time,” the Baroness observed sadly.

“What ails you?” he asked Adelaide.

“Oh, nothing,” said the Baroness, explaining Adelaide had sat up late finishing some woman’s work and would not listen when told a day more or less did not matter.

Hippolyte, blushing with shame at his suspicions, ascribed the loss of his purse to some accident. The evening was delicious to both. Adelaide could read his thoughts; though he could not confess his wrongs, he had come back more in love and affectionate, seeking tacit forgiveness. Yet at Madame de Rouville’s suggestion to play—“Let us have our little game; my old friend Kergarouet will not let me off”—his fears revived. He sat at the card-table; Adelaide said he needed a partner because he did not know piquet.

Throughout the game the Baroness and her daughter exchanged looks of intelligence, alarming Hippolyte more because he was winning. At last a final hand left the lovers in the old lady’s debt. Reaching for some money he saw before him a purse Adelaide had slipped in front of him without his noticing it; the poor child held the old one in her hand and was, to keep her countenance, looking into it for the money to pay her mother. The sight made the blood rush to his heart; he was near fainting.

The substituted purse contained his fifteen gold louis. It was worked with gilt beads; rings and tassels betrayed Adelaide’s taste and the small hoard she had spent on it. It was an impossible, delicate way to show the painter that his gift could only be repaid by some proof of affection.

Overcome, Hippolyte turned to Adelaide and her mother, saw their trembling pleasure, felt mean and foolish; tears rose. He sprang up, clasped Adelaide, pressed her to his heart, and stole a kiss; then, with the simple heartiness of an artist, “I ask for her for my wife!” he exclaimed, looking to the Baroness.

Adelaide looked half-wrathful, and Madame de Rouville, somewhat astonished, considered her reply when a bell rang. The old vice-admiral entered, followed by his shadow and Madame Schinner. Alarmed by calumnies against Adelaide, Hippolyte’s mother had inquired and reported them to the admiral, who declared he would crop all the scoundrels’ ears for them.

Prompted by his wrath, he told Madame Schinner his secret of losing intentionally at cards, because the Baroness’s pride left him no means but these ingenious ones to help her.

After Madame Schinner had paid respects to Madame de Rouville, the Baroness looked at the Comte de Kergarouet, the Chevalier du Halga—friend of the departed Comtesse de Kergarouet—Hippolyte, and Adelaide, and said with a gracious, heartfelt tone, “So we are a family party this evening.”

PARIS, May 1832

Produced by John Bickers, and Dagny

PIERRETTE

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley

                             DEDICATION

  To Mademoiselle Anna Hanska:

  Dear Child,--You, the joy of the household, you, whose pink or
  white pelerine flutters in summer among the groves of
  Wierzschovnia like a will-o’-the-wisp, followed by the tender eyes
  of your father and your mother,--how can I dedicate to _you_ a
  story full of melancholy? And yet, ought not sorrows to be spoken
  of to a young girl idolized as you are, since the day may come
  when your sweet hands will be called to minister to them? It is
  difficult, Anna, to find in the history of our manners and morals
  a subject that is worthy of your eyes, that no choice has been
  left me; but perhaps you will be made to feel how fortunate your
  fate is when you read the story sent to you by
                                                    Your old friend,
                                                         De Balzac.

PIERRETTE

I. THE LORRAINS

At dawn of an October day in 1827 a sixteen-year-old youth, dressed in what modern phrase calls proletary, stood in a small square of Lower Provins. At that hour he could note the houses around the oblong space unnoticed. The mills along the river already whirred; their wheels, echoed from the Upper Town, intensified the silence so that the wheels of a diligence could be heard a league off. The two long sides of the square, separated by an avenue of lindens, were built in the simple style of bourgeois life. No signs of commerce were visible; the porte-cochères of the rich were few and seldom used, except that of Monsieur Martener, a physician with a cabriolet. Some fronts were covered by vines or roses climbing to second-story windows, scenting the air. One end of the square opens on the main street, the gardens reaching the river bank; the other opens onto a street parallel to the main road.

At that quieter end the youth recognized the house he sought: a front of white stone grooved as courses, closed gray blinds, slender iron balconies with yellow-painted rosettes. Above the ground and first floors rose three dormer windows in a slate roof; on the peak of the central one was a new weather-vane of a hunter shooting a hare. Three stone steps led to the door. A leaden pipe discharged sink-water into a gutter on one side, indicating the kitchen; on the other were two windows closely shuttered with heart-shaped openings to admit light, those of the dining-room. Vent-holes to the cellar were closed by painted iron shutters cut in open-work. Everything was new. This restored house, fresh against the older facades, suggested the paltry taste and self-satisfaction of a retired petty shopkeeper.

The young man inspected these details with a pleasure tinged with sadness; his eyes roved from the kitchen to the roof. The rosy glow of the rising sun fell on a calico curtain at one garret window; the others lacked that luxury, and his face brightened. He stepped back, leaned against a linden, and sang in the drawling tone of western France the Breton ditty, a song often sung to newly married couples:

  “We’ve come to wish you happiness in marriage,
    To m’sieur your husband
    As well as to you:

  “You have just been bound, madam’ la mariee,
    With bonds of gold
    That only death unbinds:

  “You will go no more to balls or gay assemblies;
    You must stay at home
    While we shall go.

  “Have you thought well how you are pledged to be
    True to your spouse,
    And love him like yourself?

  “Receive these flowers our hands do now present you;
    Alas! your fleeting honors
    Will fade as they.”

This native air, tender and melancholy, would awaken a Breton maiden’s memories, picturing the manners and feelings of her noble old land. As he finished the first couplet, the singer, never taking his eyes from the attic curtain, saw no sign of life. While he sang the second, the curtain stirred. At “Receive these flowers,” a face appeared; a white hand cautiously opened the casement, and a girl made a sign with her head as he closed with the melancholy thought, “Alas! your fleeting honors will fade as they.”

To her the young workman suddenly produced a yellow flower, common in Brittany and sometimes found in La Brie—the furze, or broom.

“Is it really you, Brigaut?” she said in a low voice.

“Yes, Pierrette, yes. I am in Paris. I have started to make my way; but I’m ready to settle here, near you.”

A window on the first floor beneath Pierrette’s attic creaked. The girl showed terror and whispered: “Run away!”

The lad jumped like a frightened frog to a bend in the street where a mill projected as the square opens into the main thoroughfare; but despite his agility his hob-nailed shoes echoed on the stones, easily distinguished from the mill’s music, and no doubt heard by the person who opened the window.

That person was a woman — an old maid. No man would have torn himself from a morning nap to listen to a minstrel in a jacket; only a maid wakes to songs of love. She opened her blinds with the furtive motion of a bat, looked about, saw nothing, and only heard the flying footfalls. Few sights are more dreadful than the matutinal apparition of an ugly old maid at her window. This keen-eared spinster lacked her usual embellishments; her false front and collarette were absent; she wore a little bag of black silk to cover her skull, revealed beneath a pushed-aside night-cap. This rumpled condition gave a menacing look to her head, such as painters bestow on witches. Wrinkles marked temples, ears, and nape in scarlet lines contrasting with the whiteness of her bed-gown. The gaping of that garment revealed a breast like that of an old peasant who cares little for appearance. The fleshless arm hung like a stick. At her window she seemed tall from the length and angularity of her face, recalling the exaggerated proportions of certain Swiss heads. Her countenance, lacking harmony and softened line, expressed an unfeeling spirit. Usually such features were softened by a commercial smile—a bourgeois smirk that mimicked good humor; in public she might pass for kindly. She owned the house with her brother. He slept so soundly that the orchestra of the Opera-house could not have awakened him.

The old maid stretched her neck from the window, raised cold pale-blue eyes with short lashes toward the attic, tried to see Pierrette, and, finding it useless, retreated like a tortoise drawing in her head. The blinds were then closed, and the silence of the street resumed, broken only by peasants coming in or very early persons moving about.

When there is an old maid in a house, watch-dogs are unnecessary; not the slightest event escapes her observation or comment. This trifling incident was therefore destined to excite grave suppositions and to open the way for one of those obscure family dramas—none the less terrible because secret.

Pierrette did not go back to bed. To her Brigaut’s arrival was an immense event. During the night—Eden of the wretched—she escaped the vexations and fault-findings she bore by day. Like the hero of a ballad, her sleep seemed her happy life; her waking hours a bad dream. She had her only pleasurable waking in three years. The memories of her childhood sang their melodious ditties in her soul. The first couplet was heard in a dream; the second made her spring from bed; at the third she doubted her ears; but when the fourth was sung, standing in her night-gown with bare feet at the window, she recognized Brigaut, the companion of her childhood. Ah, yes! it was truly the well-known square jacket with bobtails, pockets sticking out at the hips—the blue cloth jacket of Brittany; there, too, the printed cotton waistcoat, the linen shirt fastened by a gold heart, the large rolling collar, the earrings, stout shoes, and trousers of blue-gray drilling unevenly colored—those humble durable things of Breton peasantry. The big white-horn buttons that fastened the jacket made the girl’s heart beat. When she saw the bunch of broom her eyes filled with tears; then a dreadful fear drove back the budding happiness. She thought her cousin sleeping in the room below might have heard the noise she made in jumping to the window. The fear was just; the old maid had appeared, and Pierrette made Brigaut the terrified sign which he obeyed without understanding it. Such instinctive submission to a girl’s bidding reveals one of those innocent and absolute affections that blossom now and then in life. Whoever had seen the lad as he ran away would have loved the ingenuous chivalry of his feeling.

Jacques Brigaut was worthy of Pierrette Lorrain, who was just fifteen. Two children! Pierrette could not help crying as she watched his flight in the terror her gesture had conveyed. Then she sat in a shabby armchair before a small table topped by a mirror. Resting her elbows, head in hands, she thought for an hour, calling to memory the Marais, the village of Pen-Hoel, perilous pond voyages in a boat untied from an old willow by little Jacques; the faces of her grandfather and grandmother, her mother’s sufferings, Major Brigaut’s handsome face—in short her careless childhood. It was a dream, a luminous joy on the gloomy present.

Her beautiful chestnut hair escaped in disorder from her cap, rumpled in sleep—a cambric cap she had made. Little ringlets escaped from gray curl-papers at each side of her forehead. From the back hung a heavy braid half unplaited. Her whiteness betrayed the malady of girlhood, chlorosis, which deprives the body of color, destroys appetite, and disorders the organism. Waxy tones marked her flesh. The neck and shoulders betrayed wasted arms, flung forward and crossed upon the table. Her feet seemed enervated, the night-gown barely reached her knees, showing flaccid muscles, blue veins, and impoverished legs. The cold, which she heeded not, turned her lips violet; a sad smile drew up the corners of a sensitive mouth that showed small, ivory-white teeth. Her ears were dainty, and despite the roundness of her face she was lovely. All animation was in the eyes: the iris brown like Spanish tobacco with black flecks and golden reflections round brilliant pupils. Pierrette was made to be gay but was sad. Her lost gaiety still showed in the vivacious eye, the ingenuous grace of her brow, the smooth curve of her chin. The long eyelashes lay upon cheek-bones made prominent by suffering. Paleness rendered the lines and details purely distinct. The ear alone was a little masterpiece of modeling. Perhaps you would like to know her history, and this is it.

Pierrette’s mother was a Demoiselle Auffray of Provins, half-sister by the father to Madame Rogron, mother of the present owners of the house.

Monsieur Auffray had married at eighteen and again near sixty-nine. By his first marriage he had an only, plain daughter, married at sixteen to innkeeper Rogron; by his second he had a charming daughter, so that Madame Rogron was fifty when the half-sister was born and already had two grown children.

The youngest daughter married at eighteen a man of her choice, a Breton officer named Lorrain, captain in the Imperial Guard. The captain, anxious to rise to a colonelcy, exchanged into a line regiment. While he, then a major, and his wife enjoyed themselves in Paris on the allowance of Monsieur and Madame Auffray, or scoured Germany, old Auffray (formerly a grocer) died at eighty-eight without making a will. His property was administered by his daughter, Madame Rogron, and her husband so completely in their interests that nothing remained for the widow beyond the house she lived in on the little square and a few acres. This widow, the mother of Madame Lorrain, was thirty-eight at her husband’s death. Like many widows, she unwisely remarried. She sold the house and land to her step-daughter, Madame Rogron, and married a young physician named Neraud, who wasted her fortune. She died two years later of grief and misery.

Thus the share of her father’s property that should have come to Madame Lorrain almost entirely disappeared, reduced to a small sum of eight thousand francs. Major Lorrain was killed at the battle of Montereau, leaving his wife, then twenty-one, with a little daughter of fourteen months and only a pension and an eventual inheritance from his parents, Monsieur and Madame Lorrain, retail shopkeepers at Pen-Hoel in the Marais. These Lorrains, Pierrette’s grandparents, sold wood, slates, tiles, pipes. Their business, from incapacity or ill-luck, did poorly and gave them scarcely enough. The failure of Collinet at Nantes, caused by 1814 events and a fall in colonial products, deprived them of twenty-four thousand francs they had deposited with that house.

The arrival of their daughter-in-law was welcome. Her pension of eight hundred francs was a handsome income at Pen-Hoel. The eight thousand francs which the widow’s half-brother and sister Rogron sent her from her father’s estate were placed in the Lorrains’ business; they gave her a mortgage on a little house they owned in Nantes, let for three hundred francs and barely worth ten thousand.

Madame Lorrain the younger, Pierrette’s mother, died in 1819. The child was small, delicate, and weakly; the damp climate of the Marais did not agree with her. But the husband’s family persuaded her, to keep her with them, that in no other place could she find a healthier region. She was so petted that her death brought nothing but honor to the old Lorrains.

Some declared that Brigaut, an old Vendeen who had served under Charette and other leaders in the wars against the Republic, counted for much in the younger Madame Lorrain’s willingness to remain in the Marais. All Pen-Hoel saw him—called respectfully Major Brigaut—spending his days and evenings in the Lorrains’ parlor beside the window of the imperial major. Toward the last, the curate of Pen-Hoel urged old Madame Lorrain to have her daughter-in-law marry Brigaut, promising to have the major appointed justice of the peace through the Vicomte de Kergarouet’s influence. The young woman’s death ended the matter.

Pierrette was left with her grandparents, who owed her four hundred francs a year, interest on the little property placed in their hands. This small sum supported her. Growing less fit, they soon faced an active competitor who ruined them. With his connivance, the Lorrains borrowed on notes they could not meet, driving them into bankruptcy. Pierrette’s claim on the house in Nantes was superseded by her grandmother’s legal rights, enforced to secure daily bread. The house sold for nine thousand five hundred francs, of which one thousand five hundred went for costs. The remaining eight thousand came to Madame Lorrain, who lived on their income in a humble place at Nantes called Saint-Jacques, where the two old people had bed and board for a small payment.

As it was impossible to keep Pierrette with them, the old Lorrains wrote to her uncle and aunt Rogron in Provins. These Rogrons were dead. The letter might easily have been lost, but the post persistently traced it to Monsieur Rogron, son of the deceased, a mercer in the rue Saint-Denis in Paris; curiosity about a possible inheritance ensured its transmission, and the Treasury its fee.

These Rogrons, toward whom the old Lorrains, though dreading to part with their dear granddaughter, stretched their supplicating hands, became unexpectedly the masters of Pierrette’s destiny. It is therefore indispensable to explain both their antecedents and their character.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Pere Rogron, the innkeeper of Provins who had married old Auffray’s daughter by his first wife, had an inflamed face, a veiny nose, and cheeks marked by Bacchic redness. Short, fat, pot-bellied, with stout legs and thick hands, he had the shrewdness of a Swiss innkeeper. He was not handsome, and his wife resembled him—never was a couple better matched. Rogron liked good living and to be waited on by pretty girls. He belonged to the class of egoists whose behavior is brutal; he gave way to his vices openly. Grasping, selfish, indecent, and always gratifying his whims, he spent his earnings until his teeth failed. Selfishness remained. In his old days he sold his inn, collected as much as he could of his late father-in-law’s property, and went to live in the little house in the square of Provins, bought for a trifle from the widow of old Auffray, Pierrette’s grandmother.

Rogron and his wife had about two thousand francs a year from twenty-seven local lots, and twenty thousand from selling their inn. Old Auffray’s house, though dilapidated, was inhabited by the Rogrons—old rats in wrack and ruin. Rogron took to horticulture and spent savings enlarging the garden; he carried it to the river’s edge between two walls and built a stone embankment at the end, where nature showed its aquatic flora.

Early in their marriage the Rogrons had a son and a daughter, both hideous; for such human beings degenerate. Put out to nurse cheaply, these children returned after a coarse village upbringing—left to cry for hours while wet-nurses worked in the fields, shut up in damp, dark rooms. Treated thus, their features coarsened; voices grew harsh; they mortified their mother’s vanity, which she tried to correct sternly; their father’s severity made that sternness seem kindness by comparison. Generally left to run loose about stables, courtyards, or the streets, sometimes whipped, sometimes sent to their grandfather Auffray, who disliked them, the children suffered. The Rogrons justified taking the bulk of “the old scoundrel’s” property by accusing him of injustice to the children. Nevertheless Rogron sent his son to school and bought him a man, one of his cartmen, to save him from conscription. As soon as his daughter Sylvie was thirteen he sent her to Paris as an apprentice in a shop, with six hundred francs for her board; two years later he sent his son Jérôme-Denis the same way. Asked by his friends what he meant to do with his children, Pere Rogron was blunt.

“When they are old enough to understand me I shall give ’em a kick and say: ‘Go and make your own way in the world!’” he replied, emptying his glass and wiping his lips. Then, with a wink: “My father gave me three kicks; I shall only give them one; he put one louis into my hand, I shall put ten in theirs, therefore they’ll be better off. After I’m gone, what’s left will be theirs. The notaries can find them and give it to them. What nonsense to bother about children. Mine owe me their life. I’ve fed them, and I don’t ask anything from them,—I call that quits. I began as a cartman, but that didn’t prevent me marrying the daughter of that old scoundrel Auffray.”

Sylvie Rogron, apprenticed in the rue Saint-Denis to shopkeepers from Provins, was “at par” in two years; she earned her living and cost her parents nothing. By nineteen she had a salary of four hundred francs. At twenty she was second demoiselle in the Maison Julliard, wholesale silk dealers at the “Chinese Worm” rue Saint-Denis. Jérôme-Denis entered the Maison Guepin, a large wholesale mercer at the “Three Distaffs.” When Sylvie was twenty-one and forewoman at a thousand francs a year, Jérôme-Denis at eighteen was head-clerk with a salary of twelve hundred francs.

Brother and sister met Sundays and feast-days for cheap amusements; they dined out and visited Saint-Cloud, Meudon, Belleville, or Vincennes. Around the end of 1815 they pooled about twenty thousand francs in savings and bought, from Madame Guenée, the property and good-will of the “Family Sister,” a large retail establishment in the quarter. Sylvie kept the books and did the writing; Jérôme-Denis was master and head-clerk. By 1821, after five years, competition was fierce and it cost them all they had to maintain the business and its reputation.

Though Sylvie was scarcely forty, her natural ugliness, hard work, and a crabbed look made her seem fifty. At thirty-eight Jérôme presented the silliest face to customers: a retreating forehead with three folds, close-cut grizzled hair, and a dull, lifeless expression. His bluish eyes had neither flame nor thought. Short and fat like his father, his figure showed a ridiculous debility rather than brute obesity. His father’s high color had become livid flabbiness peculiar to those confined to back-shops or counting-rooms, forever tying bundles, making change, snarling at clerks, and repeating old phrases to customers.

The small amount of brains the siblings possessed had been wholly absorbed in maintaining their business, getting and keeping money, and learning the market’s laws and usages. Thread, needles, ribbons, pins, buttons, and tailors’ furnishings had taken all their capacity. Outside business they knew nothing; they were ignorant of Paris beyond the Rue Saint-Denis. Their narrow natures saw no field except the shop. They were clever at nagging clerks and young women and proving them to blame. Their happiness lay in busy counters—the glib gabble of clerks using consecrated phrases made their day fine; on really fine days when Parisians strolled caring for no goods, the day was overcast to the Rogrons. “Bad weather for sales,” they said.

Rogron’s skill at tying parcels made him admired by apprentices: he could fold, tie, and see all that happened in the street and the farthest recesses of the shop before handing a parcel back with “Here it is, madame; nothing else today?” But he would have been ruined without his sister. Sylvie had common sense and a genius for trade. She advised purchases and would send him to remote parts of France to save a trifle. Her shrewdness, not employed in the heart’s service, drifted into speculation. The necessity of a business to pay for—this thought kept the machine going and gave it infernal activity.

Rogron was really only head-clerk; he understood nothing of the business as a whole. Self-interest—the great motor of the mind—had failed to instruct him. He was often aghast when his sister ordered some article to be sold below cost to clear out the end of its fashion; later he admired her idiotically. He reasoned neither ill nor well; he was simply incapable of reasoning; but he subordinated himself to his sister because “she is my elder.” Perhaps an existence always solitary, reduced to mere needs, deprived of money and pleasures in youth, explains to physiologists the clownish face, feebleness of mind, and vacant silliness. His sister had steadily prevented him from marrying—afraid to lose her power and seeing only expense and injury in a younger, likely prettier woman.

Silliness behaves by talking or being silent. Silent silliness can be borne; Rogron’s was loquacious. He chattered to his clerks, explaining minutiae and ornamenting his talk with flat jokes—the “chaff” of shopkeeping—and had come to possess a phraseology of his own. This chatterer believed himself an orator. The necessity of explaining to customers, guessing their desires, and even giving them desires, exercises the tongues of retail shopkeepers. The petty dealer acquires the faculty of uttering words and sentences with no meaning but marked success. He explains matters of manufacture customers know nothing of; that alone gives a passing superiority; take him away from his thousand explanations and he is, relatively to thought, like a fish out of water.

Rogron and Sylvie, two mechanisms baptized by mistake, lacked the feelings that give life to the heart. Their natures were shriveled and harsh, hardened by toil, privation, and the memory of suffering during a long apprenticeship to life. Neither complained. They were not implacable so much as impracticable toward others in misfortune. To them, virtue, honor, and loyalty consisted solely in the payment of bills. Irritable and irritating, sordid in economy, they bore a dreadful reputation among the merchants of the rue Saint-Denis. Had it not been for their connection with Provins, where they went three or four times a year and could close the shop for a day or two, they would have had no clerks or young women. But old Rogron sent them all the unfortunate young people of his neighborhood, whose parents hoped to start them in Paris. He obtained these apprentices by boasting of his son’s success. Parents, attracted by the prospect of close training and perhaps eventual succession to the business, sent whichever child was most in the way. But when clerks or young women found a way to escape, they fled with rejoicing that increased the Rogrons’ bad name. New victims were supplied yearly by the indefatigable old father.

From fifteen Sylvie had two faces—the amiable seller and the sour spinster. Her acquired countenance was marvellous mimicry: all smiles and a soft, wheedling voice giving a commercial charm to business. Her real face, projecting from half-opened blinds, would have frightened the most resolute. When the Lorrains’ letter reached them they were in mourning for their father, from whom they inherited the house that had been as good as stolen from Pierrette’s grandmother, also certain lands bought by their father, and moneys acquired by usurious loans and mortgages to the peasantry. The yearly taking of stock was over. The price of the “Family Sister” had, at last, been paid in full. The Rogrons owned about sixty thousand francs’ worth of merchandise, forty thousand in bank or cash, and the value of their business. Sitting on a bench covered with striped-green Utrecht velvet behind their private counter, the siblings consulted what they should do.

All retail shopkeepers aspire to become bourgeois. By selling the good-will of their business they would have over a hundred and fifty thousand francs, not counting their father’s inheritance. By placing their available property in the public Funds they would each obtain about four thousand francs a year, and by taking the proceeds of their business when sold they could repair and improve the house they inherited, making it a good investment. They could then live in Provins. Their forewoman was the daughter of a rich farmer at Donnemarie, burdened with nine children; in five years the farmer had lost seven children, which made the forewoman interesting. Rogron had tried, unsuccessfully, to make her marry him; she showed aversion which baffled him. Mademoiselle Sylvie opposed the match; she sought instead to make the shrewd young woman their successor.

No passing observer can form an idea of the cryptic existence of a certain class of shopkeepers; one wonders on what and why they live. To discover the thin subsoil beneath their lives one must dig. The Parisian shopkeeper nurtures his soul on hope: one dreams of managing a theatre, honors of mayoralty, a country-house ten miles from Paris with a “park,” statues of tinted plaster, fountains that squirt threads of water—on which he will spend a mint; others dream of distinction in the National Guard. Provins filled the Rogrons with fanatical longings. Let it be said to La Champagne’s credit: this love is warranted. Provins rivals Frangistan and the valley of Cashmere; not only does it contain the poesy of Saadi, but it offers medicinal springs. The Provins roses are world-famous. Provins was the promised land that appeared to the two shopkeepers amid the muck of Saint-Denis.

Crossing the gray plains between La Ferté-Gaucher and Provins—a productive desert of wheat—you reach a hill. Suddenly a town watered by two rivers appears; at the rock’s feet stretches a verdant valley of enchanting lines and fugitive horizons. From Paris you pass through Provins along the highroad encumbered with beggars and blind men who follow you with pitiful voices. From Troyes you approach on the valley side. The château, old town, and ramparts terrace the hillside; the new town lies below. Upper and Lower Provins differ: the upper is airy, steep, solemn, with imposing ruins; the lower is a town of mills watered by the Voulzie and the Durtain, a town of inns, shops, retired merchants, diligences, travelling-carriages, and waggons. Its historical memories, melancholy ruins, ravines, gardens, and rivers banked with gardens excite the love of all its children, who, like Auvergnats or Savoyards, leave to make fortunes and always return. “Die in one’s form,” the proverb for hares and faithful souls, seems Provins’ motto.

Thus Jérôme and Sylvie thought constantly of their dear Provins. While Jérôme sold thread he saw the upper town; as he piled button cards he contemplated the valley; when he rolled ribbons he followed the shining rivers. Looking up at shelves he saw ravines where he had escaped his father’s anger and gathered blackberries. The little square in the lower town was his chief object: he imagined improving his house, dreaming of a new front, bedrooms, a salon, billiard-room, dining-room, and a kitchen garden turned into an English pleasure-ground with lawns, grottos, fountains, and statuary. The bedrooms they occupied in Paris were furnished with the merest necessaries, yet in fancy no one had finer furniture. Jérôme, walking, admired upholsterers’ windows and draperies he coveted for his house, and at home would tell his sister he’d found pieces “that will just do.” He bought and soon rejected each thing in favor of the next. The Budget itself could not have paid for his schemes. He wanted everything he saw, but abandoned each for the last. New balconies and ornaments he thought out of place in Paris: “Ah!” he said, “those fine things would look much better at Provins.” Leaning on his doorstep with a vacant eye, he visualized his gilded house, garden, fountain, billiard-table, and flowers.

Sylvie thought deeply, pen in hand, receiving the bourgeoisie of Provins, looking at herself in salon mirrors, admiring a cap. The siblings began to find the rue Saint-Denis atmosphere unhealthy; the market mud made them long for the fragrance of Provins roses. They were victims of genuine nostalgia and monomania, frustrated for now by the need to sell tapes and bobbins before leaving Paris. The promised land of the valley attracted them because they had really suffered crossing the sandy wastes of mercer’s business.

The Lorrains’ letter reached them amid meditations inspired by this future. They knew scarcely anything about their cousin Pierrette Lorrain. Their father had got possession of the Auffray property after they left home and said little of his affairs. They hardly remembered Aunt Lorrain. An hour of genealogical discussion was needed to make her out as the younger sister of their own mother by their grandfather Auffray’s second marriage. It struck them that this second marriage had harmed their interests by dividing the Auffray property between two daughters. In times past their father had sneered about it.

The Rogrons considered the Lorrains’ application through these recollections, which were not favorable to Pierrette. To take charge of an orphan cousin who might become their legal heir if neither married was a matter for debate. They had never seen Pierrette; what trouble would a young girl be? Wouldn’t it commit them to obligations? Could they send her away if they disliked her? Wouldn’t they have to marry her? If Jérôme found a yoke-mate among Provins heiresses they ought to preserve property for his children. A yoke-mate, according to Sylvie, meant a stupid, rich, ugly girl who would let herself be governed. They decided to refuse the Lorrain request. Sylvie agreed to write the answer. Business being urgent, she delayed, and when the forewoman offered to buy the stock and good-will of the “Family Sister,” which the Rogrons accepted, the matter left the old maid’s mind.

Sylvie Rogron and her brother departed for Provins four years before the arrival of Brigaut stirred Pierrette’s life. But the doings of the pair after reaching Provins are as necessary to relate as their life in Paris; for Provins was destined to be not less fatal to Pierrette than the commercial antecedents of her cousins.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When the petty shopkeeper who has gone to Paris returns to the provinces there is always a trace of Parisian ideas that soon fades into provincial habits, leaving only superficial scratches where Paris has touched the town. The ex-shopkeeper’s transition into substantial bourgeois life acts like an illness. No retail shopkeeper can pass with impunity from perpetual chatter to provincial silence, from Parisian bustle to the stillness of a small town. Those who have saved property spend part of it on some long-nursed desire that now absorbs impulses once held in check by business. Some travel or take municipal posts; others hunt, fish, become usurers or speculators. The Rogrons, brother and sister, were seized by the desire to use the trowel and remodel their old house into something charming.

This fixed idea produced on the square of Lower Provins the front Brigaut examined, its interior arrangements, and handsome furnishings. The contractor never drove a nail without consulting the owners, requiring signatures on plans and specifications, and explaining every item’s manufacture and price. For choicer things they said the pieces had belonged to Monsieur Tiphaine or Madame Julliard or other notables. The desire to have things like the rich bourgeois of Provins prevailed.

“Oh, if Monsieur Garceland has it, put it in,” said Mademoiselle Rogron. “His taste is good.”

“Sylvie, see, he wants ovolos in the cornice of the corridor.”

“What an odd name!”

“It means eggs—from the Latin ovum.”

“What queer fellows you architects are!” cried Rogron. “It’s stepping on egg-shells to deal with you.”

“Shall we paint the corridor?” asked the builder.

“Good heavens, no!” cried Sylvie. “That would be five hundred francs more!”

“Oh, the salon and staircase are too pretty not to have the corridor decorated,” urged the man. “That Madame Lesourd had hers painted last year.”
