“Well done! The trick succeeded,” answered Max, now tired and feverish.

“What did you say to old Mouilleron?” asked Francois.

“I told him I had given my assassin some cause to waylay me; that he was dangerous and likely, if I pursued the affair, to kill me before capture. Consequently, I begged Mouilleron and Prangin to make an ostensible active search but really to let the assassin go in peace, unless they wished to see me dead.”

“I do hope, Max,” said Flore, “that you will be quiet at night for some time to come.”

“At any rate, we are delivered from the Parisians!” cried Max. “The fellow who stabbed me had no idea what a service he was doing us.”

The next day the Parisians’ departure was celebrated as a provincial victory over Paris by nearly everyone in Issoudun, except the sober, staid inhabitants who shared Monsieur and Madame Hochon’s view. A few of Max’s friends spoke harshly of the Bridaus.

“Do those Parisians think we are all idiots?” cried one. “They came to fleece and have been shorn themselves,” said another. “They actually consulted a lawyer in Paris—what plan had they?” “A plan to get possession of old Rouget. But the Parisians were not clever enough; that lawyer can’t crow over us Berrichons!” “How abominable!” “That’s Paris for you!” “The Rabouilleuse knew they came to attack her, and she defended herself.” “She did gloriously right!”

To the townspeople the Bridaus were Parisians and foreigners; they preferred Max and Flore.

We can imagine the comfort with which Joseph and Agathe re-entered their lodging in the rue Mazarin. On the journey Joseph recovered spirits lost by arrest and a day’s confinement; but his mother could not be cheered. The Court of Peers was about to try the military conspirators, and that kept Agathe anxious. Philippe’s conduct, despite a clever defender recommended by Desroches, raised suspicions unfavorable to his character. To escape hearing about the trial—lasting twenty days—Joseph, after briefing Desroches fully on Issoudun, set off with Mistigris for the Comte de Serizy’s chateau.

Contemporary histories need not be repeated here: whether Philippe had played an agreed part or was truly an informer, he was condemned to five years’ surveillance by the police and ordered to leave Paris that day for Autun, the director-general’s choice for his exile. The punishment resembled parole with a town for a prison. Learning that the Comte de Serizy, one of the peers on the court-martial, was employing Joseph to decorate his chateau at Presles, Desroches sought an audience and found Monsieur de Serizy favorably disposed; they had met. Desroches explained the brothers’ financial plight, recalled their father’s services and neglect under the Restoration, and urged the minister to induce the director-general of police to change Philippe’s residence from Autun to Issoudun and to grant a dole of sixty francs a month to the former lieutenant-colonel, a matter of shame for the minister of war to deny.

“I will obtain all you ask of me, for I think it just,” replied the count.

Three days later Desroches, armed with authority, fetched Philippe from the Court of Peers’ prison and took him to his house in the rue de Bethizy. There the young barrister read Philippe a plain, unanswerable lecture in which lawyers reduce clients’ sentiment to simple, blunt terms. He humbled the former staff-officer by reproaching his reckless dissipations, his mother’s misfortunes, and the death of Madame Descoings, then explained Issoudun’s situation and probed Maxence Gilet and the Rabouilleuse’s characters. Philippe, who grasped such matters keenly, listened more to this part than to the reproaches.

“Under these circumstances,” Desroches continued, “you can repair some of the injury you have done—so far as possible; you cannot restore life to the poor mother you have nearly killed. But you alone can—”

“What can I do?” asked Philippe.

“I have obtained a change of residence for you from Autun to Issoudun.”

Philippe’s sunken, almost sinister face brightened.

“And,” Desroches went on, “you alone can recover your uncle’s inheritance; half may already be in Gilet’s hands. I suggest no plan; everything depends on local circumstances. You have to deal with a strong, astute man. The way he tried to recover the pictures he’d given to Joseph, and the audacity with which he laid the crime on your brother, shows he’s capable of anything. Be prudent; behave properly out of policy if not decency. Without telling Joseph, whose pride as an artist would revolt, I have sent the pictures to Monsieur Hochon, telling him to give them up to no one but you. By the way, Maxence Gilet is a brave man.”

“So much the better,” said Philippe; “I count on his courage for success; a coward would leave Issoudun.”

“Consult Monsieur Hochon,” urged Desroches. “You have your orders from the police; I have taken your place in the Orleans diligence for half-past seven tonight. I suppose your trunk is ready; come and dine.”

“I own nothing but what I have on my back,” said Philippe, opening his threadbare blue overcoat; “but I only need three things, which you must tell Giroudeau, the uncle of Finot, to send me—my sabre, my sword, and my pistols.”

“You need more than that,” said the lawyer, shuddering at his client’s appearance. “You will receive a quarterly stipend which will clothe you decently.”

“Bless me! are you here, Godeschal?” cried Philippe, recognizing in Desroches’s head-clerk the brother of Mariette.

“Yes, I have been with Monsieur Desroches for two months.”

“And he will stay with me till he gets business of his own,” said Desroches.

“How is Mariette?” asked Philippe, moved.

“She is getting ready for the opening of the new theatre.”

“It would cost her little trouble to get my sentence remitted,” said Philippe. “However, as she chooses!”

After a meagre dinner—Desroches boarded his head-clerk—the two lawyers put the political convict in the diligence and wished him good luck.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
On the second of November, All-Souls’ day, Philippe Bridau presented himself at the commissary of police in Issoudun to register his arrival and, on the official’s advice, took rooms in rue l’Avenier. News that an officer banished for a recent conspiracy had come to town spread quickly and caused more stir when people learned he was the brother of the painter who had been wrongly accused. Maxence Gilet, recovered from his wound, had converted Père Rouget’s mortgages into cash and placed the proceeds in the “grand-livre.” The 140,000-franc mortgage on Rouget’s real estate caused a scandal—news spreads fast in the provinces. Monsieur Hochon, anxious for the Bridaus and still smarting from the terror he had felt at the thought of his house being ransacked, questioned old Monsieur Heron, the Bourges notary, about it.

“If not for me,” Heron declared, “old Rouget would have kept the 50,000-franc income in Maxence Gilet’s name. I advised Mademoiselle Brazier to rely on the will, and cautioned Maxence and his mistress to let this sudden change be forgotten.” Hochon urged protection for the Bridaus.

Maxence and Flore, secure against attack, were gleeful at another nephew’s arrival; they knew they could have old Rouget sign a power of attorney transferring funds to Max or Flore if danger threatened. If the will leaving Flore the principal were revoked, the 50,000-franc income and the newly extracted 140,000-franc mortgage would be good consolation.

The day after arriving, Philippe called on his uncle around ten, in shabby clothes. Flore shivered at his repulsive appearance; Gilet felt the same chill that warns of a hidden enemy. Philippe’s ruined blue greatcoat was buttoned tight to the throat; his trousers were ragged, his boots leaked mud, his hat was greasy, his malacca cane dull. His face had the gambler’s exhaustion: sallow skin, sunken cheeks, red-rimmed lids and a threatening brow; bald except for a fringe, his brilliant eyes were cold steel-blue. He introduced himself hoarsely as Philippe Bridau, a lieutenant-colonel ill-treated by the Bourbons, and complained that he had to live on a full pay of sixty francs a month by police order. He made a pointed show of disdain for relying on his uncle.

“Good-morning, uncle,” he said, offering his hand; Rouget took it. He declared he would sooner cut off his hand than beg from his uncle after the way his mother and brother had been treated in Issoudun, but he had felt it proper to pay respects. Flore invited him to breakfast; he declined, saying he had already eaten. He hinted that he must safeguard the Bridaus’ honor, spoke curtly about pictures at Monsieur Hochon’s for identification, and left. The violence of his tone and the way he slammed the door shocked Flore and Max more than his looks had.

Max and Flore watched him cross to the Hochons’. Gilet, though furious, kept his temper; he knew the need for political prudence. Flore urged Rouget not to admit him again.

Later Philippe entered the Hochon house where curiosity was thick. Madame Hochon shuddered at the father of Agathe’s griefs and the supposed murderer of Madame Descoings; others felt unease. Philippe politely explained he had come to introduce himself and manage living on sixty francs. He spoke well, admitted past faults, and promised reformation. He praised Lousteau the journalist as a “rara avis,” which won the old lady’s favor. Madame Hochon advised him gently; he said prison had taught him much and he would live another life.

Outside, he took Monsieur Hochon aside. In a whisper he warned that the town’s gossip could be ruinous and they must avoid being suspected of advising him publicly. Desroches had instructed Philippe to use Hochon’s counsel discreetly; they agreed to meet “accidentally” in open places when needed. Philippe asked for names of veterans in Issoudun who were not Maxence’s friends—he wanted allies. Hochon named Mignonnet, an artillery captain of the Guard, and Captain Carpentier, a cavalry officer who had replaced Gilet as mayoral clerk and befriended Mignonnet. They discussed Philippe’s need for work; a mutual insurance agency might pay fifty francs a month—enough.

Within a week Philippe had bought on credit a decent suit, boots, gloves, and a hat. Giroudeau sent linen and weapons and a letter to Carpentier, who welcomed him and introduced him to Mignonnet, recommending him as a man of merit. Philippe narrated parts of the conspiracy to them—framed to win admiration. He presented the recent affair as a continuation of earlier army efforts against the Bourbons: a large northern operation and a Paris diversion. Betrayed, the leaders had seen the Paris plot reduced to a barrack affair; when the larger scheme failed Philippe said he broke links, letting only the smaller plot be exposed. His apparent misery and ruined costume served to minimize the conspiracy’s size in the authorities’ eyes. He professed to have played both sides, preserving favor with influential figures while waiting to join whichever party served his interests best. Carpentier and Mignonnet, impressed by the account and his seeming devotion, brought him into their circle.

They recommended him for the insurance post Hochon had mentioned, requiring only three hours a day. Philippe frequented their club; his manners and the officers’ praise earned him respect. Determined to seem sober and respectable, and to lull Gilet’s suspicions, he feigned simple-mindedness while quietly observing his uncle’s property and plotting. Hochon had given him details of Rouget’s wealth, which stirred his covetousness. He and Hochon agreed not to alarm Maxence; if Rouget were spirited off to Bourges the game would be lost.

Philippe dined once a week with Mignonnet, once with Carpentier, and every Thursday with Hochon; soon he had engagements most nights. He never spoke of his uncle or his brother and mother except in connection with their stay in Issoudun. The three officers, decorated men in town, walked together daily; Philippe wore a rosette and carried himself like a man of consequence. To Max’s friends Philippe’s demeanor and story made him seem a “sabreur,” a brave front of the old army.

“His conduct before the Court of Peers shows him either a dupe or a spy,” said Max; others defended him. Philippe, wishing to conceal details of his situation, lived in a secluded house in the faubourg Saint-Paterne with a garden; here he fenced with Carpentier, reviving his skills and learning new tricks. He practiced pistols with Mignonnet and Carpentier, ostensibly for sport but really to make Max believe he preferred pistols in a duel. When he met Gilet he waited for a bow and returned it with a cavalier touch to the hat, as an officer to a private. Gilet gave no sign of impatience and never spoke of Bridau at the Cognettes’, though the pranks of the Order of Idleness were temporarily suspended since Fario’s attack.

Philippe’s contempt for Gilet and Gilet’s restrained bearing became the talk of Issoudun. Some thought Philippe was setting a trap. Potel suggested Max might be baiting him; others speculated about what might grow from the enmity. Rumor set the two up as adversaries.

Philippe had investigated the circumstances of his brother’s arrest and Gilet’s and the Rabouilleuse’s past, and he was brought into contact with Fario the Spaniard. Finding Fario reliable, Philippe made him a confidant and promised him indemnity for past losses—Fario, a willing henchman, put his knowledge of the Knights of Idleness and his services at Philippe’s disposal. The town, abuzz with gossip, expected a deadly clash between the two men who intensely despised one another.

Toward the end of November, Philippe told Hochon he had discovered that Hochon’s grandsons Baruch and François were intimate with Maxence and had spied in his house, informing Max of what was said there. He claimed to have overheard them and learned from Fario of their nocturnal visits to drinking-shops in Maxence’s company; they owed Maxence over three thousand francs. Philippe suspected Max and the Rabouilleuse intended to secure the 50,000-franc income and then run away and marry. They must learn what was happening under Rouget’s roof, Philippe said, but he did not know how to proceed. Hochon promised to think.

Jean-Jacques Rouget suffered as never before since Philippe’s first visit. Flore, fearing some evil to Max, planned to leave Issoudun and marry Max in Paris after getting the Funds’ income transferred. Rouget refused to transfer, insisting Flore be sole heir—he believed she would abandon him if made rich. She tried cajolery and then coldness; Vedie found Rouget one morning with swollen eyes from weeping. For a week he breakfasted alone, perhaps on meager fare.

The day after Philippe’s talk with Hochon, he went to Rouget’s house again. Flore played tender and solicitous in his presence; Gilet stayed away. Philippe, discerning danger, judged it time to strike. He forced Flore to look him in the face and demanded to know if Rouget was free to walk alone with him. Flore stammered that he was. Philippe insisted Rouget accompany him on foot, implying that if he didn’t, the town’s claim that Rouget was under Flore’s dominion would be confirmed. Seeing Rouget hesitate, Philippe pretended to go; he caught Flore making a menacing gesture at her uncle, and told Rouget to meet him at the door in ten minutes or he would take other measures. He left for the Hochons’.

At nine that night, Hochon, Madame Hochon, Monsieur Heron the notary, and the grandchildren gathered. Hochon, enraged, denounced Baruch and François for their nocturnal life with Maxence and for having informed the Knights of Idleness; he accused them of betraying secrets for money and even of causing past crimes by transmitting plans. He read the guardianship accounts: the Borniche children’s net fortune was 70,000 francs, part of their mother’s dowry, but Hochon, as creditor to his daughter, controlled much of it. Baruch’s portion was twenty thousand francs. Hochon told them he would release their property to them but no longer tolerate their conduct; he discharged his guardianship and made their futures contingent on strict behavior. Baruch and François were stunned.

After Heron read the accounts, Hochon told Baruch he would be sent to Paris to study banking under Mongenod; François, who was in debt, would have only six hundred francs a year and would go to Poitiers to study law. Hochon gloated over his quick vengeance: he would watch them and test them by years of conduct. Having settled the legal business and obtained receipts, Heron was preparing to leave when Philippe arrived. Madame Hochon withdrew with her grandsons to see what effect the scene had produced.

Philippe and Hochon spoke in the window embrasure. Hochon had consulted Heron and learned that the security for the 50,000-franc income could not be sold without the owner’s signature or a power of attorney; Rouget had not given any power before a notary since his arrival. If Rouget attempted to give one in Issoudun they would know instantly; if he left town to sign, they would discover it through registration. Therefore, if Rouget left, they would follow him and learn his movements. Philippe reported seeing his uncle cross the street and detailed his last visit. He told Hochon that Fario and a man named Benjamin Bourdet—a soldier Philippe had helped in Vatan—were watching; they would follow Rouget secretly. “If they let him walk with you,” Hochon mused, “Maxence must believe he has found some means to win the game.” Philippe said Maxence was afraid of him but could not evade him; Fario had stirred public censure against Maxence and the gossip was now in motion.

When Philippe left, Flore hurried to Max’s room with a full account of his boldness. Max resolved that before resorting to fighting him they would play a bold card. He told Flore to send at once to Père Cognette for a horse and a char-à-banc; pack their things and go to Vatan pretending to stay, taking Vedie and the twenty thousand francs in Rouget’s drawer. If Philippe brought Rouget to Vatan, Flore must refuse to return unless Rouget signed a power of attorney. As soon as they obtained it, Max would slip off to Paris while she came back to Issoudun. When Jean-Jacques found her gone he would be desperate and likely follow; Max planned to give him a stern talking-to when he did.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
While the foregoing plot was progressing, Philippe was walking arm in arm with his uncle along the boulevard Baron.

“The two great tacticians are coming to close quarters at last,” thought Monsieur Hochon as he watched the colonel marching off with his uncle; “I am curious to see the end of the game, and what becomes of the stake of ninety thousand francs a year.”

“My dear uncle,” said Philippe, whose phraseology had a flavor of his affinities in Paris, “you love this girl, and you are devilishly right. She is damnably handsome! Instead of billing and cooing she makes you trot like a valet; well, that’s simple enough; but she wants to see you six feet underground, so that she may marry Max, whom she adores.”

“I know that, Philippe, but I love her all the same.”

“Well, I have sworn by the soul of my mother, who is your own sister,” continued Philippe, “to make your Rabouilleuse as supple as my glove, and the same as she was before that scoundrel, who is unworthy to have served in the Imperial Guard, ever came to quarter himself in your house.”

“Ah! if you could do that!--” said the old man.

“It is very easy,” answered Philippe, cutting his uncle short. “I’ll kill Max as I would a dog; but--on one condition,” added the old campaigner.

“What is that?” said Rouget, looking at his nephew in a stupid way.

“Don't sign that power of attorney which they want of you before the third of December; put them off till then. Your torturers only want it to enable them to sell the fifty thousand a year you have in the Funds, so that they may run off to Paris and pay for their wedding festivities out of your millions.”

“I am afraid so,” replied Rouget.

“Well, whatever they may say or do to you, put off giving that power of attorney until next week.”

“Yes; but when Flore talks to me she stirs my very soul, till I don’t know what I do. I give you my word, when she looks at me in a certain way, her blue eyes seem like paradise, and I am no longer master of myself,--especially when for some days she had been harsh to me.”

“Well, whether she is sweet or sour, don’t do more than promise to sign the paper, and let me know the night before you are going to do it. That will answer. Maxence shall not be your proxy unless he first kills me. If I kill him, you must agree to take me in his place, and I’ll undertake to break in that handsome girl and keep her at your beck and call. Yes, Flore shall love you, and if she doesn’t satisfy you--thunder! I’ll thrash her.”

“Oh! I never could allow that. A blow struck at Flore would break my heart.”

“But it is the only way to govern women and horses. A man makes himself feared, or loved, or respected. Now that is what I wanted to whisper in your ear--Good-morning, gentlemen,” he said to Mignonnet and Carpentier, who came up at the moment; “I am taking my uncle for a walk, as you see, and trying to improve him; for we are in an age when children are obliged to educate their grandparents.”

They all bowed to each other.

“You behold in my dear uncle the effects of an unhappy passion. Those two want to strip him of his fortune and leave him in the lurch--you know to whom I refer? He sees the plot; but he hasn’t the courage to give up his SUGAR-PLUM for a few days so as to baffle it.”

Philippe briefly explained his uncle’s position.

“Gentlemen,” he remarked, in conclusion, “you see there are no two ways of saving him: either Colonel Bridau must kill Captain Gilet, or Captain Gilet must kill Colonel Bridau. We celebrate the Emperor’s coronation on the day after to-morrow; I rely upon you to arrange the seats at the banquet so that I shall sit opposite to Gilet. You will do me the honor, I hope, of being my seconds.”

“We will appoint you to preside, and sit ourselves on either side of you. Max, as vice-president, will of course sit opposite,” said Mignonnet.

“Oh! the scoundrel will have Potel and Renard with him,” said Carpentier. “In spite of all that Issoudun now knows and says of his midnight maraudings, those two worthy officers, who have already been his seconds, remain faithful to him.”

“You see how it all maps out, uncle,” said Philippe. “Therefore, sign no paper before the third of December; the next day you shall be free, happy, and beloved by Flore, without having to coax for it.”

“You don’t know him, Philippe,” said the terrified old man. “Maxence has killed nine men in duels.”

“Yes; but ninety thousand francs a year didn’t depend on it,” answered Philippe.

“A bad conscience shakes the hand,” remarked Mignonnet sententiously.

“In a few days from now,” resumed Philippe, “you and the Rabouilleuse will be living together as sweet as honey,--that is, after she gets through mourning. At first she’ll twist like a worm, and yelp, and weep; but never mind, let the water run!”

The two soldiers approved of Philippe’s arguments, and tried to hearten up old Rouget, with whom they walked about for nearly two hours. At last Philippe took his uncle home, saying as they parted:--

“Don’t take any steps without me. I know women. I have paid for one, who cost me far more than Flore can ever cost you. But she taught me how to behave to the fair sex for the rest of my days. Women are bad children; they are inferior animals to men; we must make them fear us; the worst condition in the world is to be governed by such brutes.”

It was about half-past two in the afternoon when the old man got home. Kouski opened the door in tears,--that is, by Max’s orders, he gave signs of weeping.

“Oh! Monsieur, Madame has gone away, and taken Vedie with her!”

“Gone--a--way!” said the old man in a strangled voice.

The blow was so violent that Rouget sat down on the stairs, unable to stand. A moment after, he rose, looked about the hall, into the kitchen, went up to his own room, searched all the chambers, and returned to the salon, where he threw himself into a chair, and burst into tears.

“Where is she?” he sobbed. “Oh! where is she? where is Max?”

“I don’t know,” answered Kouski. “The captain went out without telling me.”

Gilet thought it politic to be seen sauntering about the town. By leaving the old man alone with his despair, he knew he should make him feel his desertion the more keenly, and reduce him to docility. To keep Philippe from assisting his uncle at this crisis, he had given Kouski strict orders not to open the door to any one. Flore away, the miserable old man grew frantic, and the situation of things approached a crisis. During his walk through the town, Maxence Gilet was avoided by many persons who a day or two earlier would have hastened to shake hands with him. A general reaction had set in against him. The deeds of the Knights of Idleness were ringing on every tongue. The tale of Joseph Bridau’s arrest, now cleared up, disgraced Max in the eyes of all; and his life and conduct received in one day their just award. Gilet met Captain Potel, who was looking for him, and seemed almost beside himself.

“What’s the matter with you, Potel?”

“My dear fellow, the Imperial Guard is being black-guarded all over the town! These civilians are crying you down! and it goes to the bottom of my heart.”

“What are they complaining of?” asked Max.

“Of what you do at night.”

“As if we couldn’t amuse ourselves a little!”

“But that isn’t all,” said Potel.

Potel belonged to the same class as the officer who replied to the burgomasters: “Eh! your town will be paid for, if we do burn it!” So he was very little troubled about the deeds of the Order of Idleness.

“What more?” inquired Gilet.

“The Guard is against the Guard. It is that that breaks my heart. Bridau has set all these bourgeois on you. The Guard against the Guard! no, it ought not to be! You can’t back down, Max; you must meet Bridau. I had a great mind to pick a quarrel with the low scoundrel myself and send him to the shades; I wish I had, and then the bourgeois wouldn’t have seen the spectacle of the Guard against the Guard. In war times, I don’t say anything against it. Two heroes of the Guard may quarrel, and fight,--but at least there are no civilians to look on and sneer. No, I say that big villain never served in the Guard. A guardsman would never behave as he does to another guardsman, under the very eyes of the bourgeois; impossible! Ah! it’s all wrong; the Guard is disgraced--and here, at Issoudun! where it was once so honored.”

“Come, Potel, don’t worry yourself,” answered Max; “even if you do not see me at the banquet--”

“What! do you mean that you won’t be there the day after to-morrow?” cried Potel, interrupting his friend. “Do you wish to be called a coward? and have it said you are running away from Bridau? No, no! The unmounted grenadiers of the Guard can not draw back before the dragoons of the Guard. Arrange your business in some other way and be there!”

“One more to send to the shades!” said Max. “Well, I think I can manage my business so as to get there--For,” he thought to himself, “that power of attorney ought not to be in my name; as old Heron says, it would look too much like theft.”

This lion, tangled in the meshes Philippe Bridau was weaving for him, muttered between his teeth as he went along; he avoided the looks of those he met and returned home by the boulevard Vilatte, still talking to himself.

“I will have that money before I fight,” he said. “If I die, it shall not go to Philippe. I must put it in Flore’s name. She will follow my instructions, and go straight to Paris. Once there, she can marry, if she chooses, the son of some marshal of France who has been sent to the right-about. I’ll have that power of attorney made in Baruch’s name, and he’ll transfer the property by my order.”

Max, to do him justice, was never more cool and calm in appearance than when his blood and his ideas were boiling. No man ever united in a higher degree the qualities which make a great general. If his career had not been cut short by his captivity at Cabrera, the Emperor would certainly have found him one of those men who are necessary to the success of vast enterprises. When he entered the room where the hapless victim of all these comic and tragic scenes was still weeping, Max asked the meaning of such distress; seemed surprised, pretended that he knew nothing, and heard, with well-acted amazement, of Flore’s departure. He questioned Kouski, to obtain some light on the object of this inexplicable journey.

“Madame said like this,” Kouski replied, “--that I was to tell monsieur she had taken twenty thousand francs in gold from his drawer, thinking that monsieur wouldn’t refuse her that amount as wages for the last twenty-two years.”

“Wages?” exclaimed Rouget.

“Yes,” replied Kouski. “Ah! I shall never come back,” she said to Vedie as she drove away. “Poor Vedie, who is so attached to monsieur, remonstrated with madame. ‘No, no,’ she answered, ‘he has no affection for me; he lets his nephew treat me like the lowest of the low’; and she wept--oh! bitterly.”

“Eh! what do I care for Philippe?” cried the old man, whom Max was watching. “Where is Flore? how can we find out where she is?”

“Philippe, whose advice you follow, will help you,” said Max coldly.

“Philippe?” said the old man, “what has he to do with the poor child? There is no one but you, my good Max, who can find Flore. She will follow you--you could bring her back to me--”

“I don’t wish to oppose Monsieur Bridau,” observed Max.

“As for that,” cried Rouget, “if that hinders you, he told me he meant to kill you.”

“Ah!” exclaimed Gilet, laughing, “we will see about it!”

“My friend,” said the old man, “find Flore, and I will do all she wants of me.”

“Some one must have seen her as she passed through the town,” said Maxence to Kouski. “Serve dinner; put everything on the table, and then go and make inquiries from place to place. Let us know, by dessert, which road Mademoiselle Brazier has taken.”

This order quieted for a time the poor creature, who was moaning like a child that has lost its nurse. At this moment Rouget, who hated Max, thought his tormentor an angel. A passion like that of this miserable old man for Flore is astonishingly like the emotions of childhood. At six o’clock, the Pole, who had merely taken a walk, returned to announce that Flore had driven towards Vatan.

“Madame is going back to her own people, that’s plain,” said Kouski.

“Would you like to go to Vatan to-night?” said Max. “The road is bad, but Kouski knows how to drive, and you’ll make your peace better to-night than to-morrow morning.”

“Let us go!” cried Rouget.

“Put the horse in quietly,” said Max to Kouski; “manage, if you can, that the town shall not know of this nonsense, for Monsieur Rouget’s sake. Saddle my horse,” he added in a whisper. “I will ride on ahead of you.”

Monsieur Hochon had already notified Philippe of Flore’s departure; and the colonel rose from Monsieur Mignonnet’s dinner-table to rush to the place Saint-Jean; for he at once guessed the meaning of this clever strategy. When Philippe presented himself at his uncle’s house, Kouski answered through a window that Monsieur Rouget was unable to see any one.

“Fario,” said Philippe to the Spaniard, who was stationed in the Grande-Narette, “go and tell Benjamin to mount his horse; it is all-important that I shall know what Gilet does with my uncle.”

“They are now putting the horse into the caleche,” said Fario, who had been watching the Rouget stable.

“If they go towards Vatan,” answered Philippe, “get me another horse, and come yourself with Benjamin to Monsieur Mignonnet’s.”

“What do you mean to do?” asked Monsieur Hochon, who had come out of his own house when he saw Philippe and Fario standing together.

“The genius of a general, my dear Monsieur Hochon,” said Philippe, “consists not only in carefully observing the enemy’s movements, but also in guessing his intentions from those movements, and in modifying his own plan whenever the enemy interferes with it by some unexpected action. Now, if my uncle and Max drive out together, they are going to Vatan; Maxence will have promised to reconcile him with Flore, who ‘fugit ad salices,’--the manoeuvre is General Virgil’s. If that’s the line they take, I don’t yet know what I shall do; I shall have some hours to think it over, for my uncle can’t sign a power of attorney at ten o’clock at night; the notaries will all be in bed. If, as I rather fancy, Max goes on in advance of my uncle to teach Flore her lesson,--which seems necessary and probable,--the rogue is lost! you will see the sort of revenge we old soldiers take in a game of this kind. Now, as I need a helper for this last stroke, I must go back to Mignonnet’s and make an arrangement with my friend Carpentier.”

Shaking hands with Monsieur Hochon, Philippe went off down the Petite-Narette to Mignonnet’s house. Ten minutes later, Monsieur Hochon saw Max ride off at a quick trot; and the old miser’s curiosity was so powerfully excited that he remained standing at his window, eagerly expecting to hear the wheels of the old demi-fortune, which was not long in coming. Jean-Jacques’s impatience made him follow Max within twenty minutes. Kouski, no doubt under orders from his master, walked the horse through the town.

“If they get to Paris, all is lost,” thought Monsieur Hochon.

At this moment, a lad from the faubourg de Rome came to the Hochon house with a letter for Baruch. The two grandsons, much subdued by the events of the morning, had kept their rooms of their own accord during the day. Thinking over their prospects, they saw plainly that they had better be cautious with their grandparents. Baruch knew very well the influence which his grandfather Hochon exerted over his grandfather and grandmother Borniche: Monsieur Hochon would not hesitate to get their property for Adolphine if his conduct were such as to make them pin their hopes on the grand marriage with which his grandfather had threatened him that morning. Being richer than Francois, Baruch had the most to lose; he therefore counselled an absolute surrender, with no other condition than the payment of their debt to Max. As for Francois, his future was entirely in the hands of his grandfather; he had no expectations except from him, and by the guardianship account, he was now his debtor. The two young men accordingly gave solemn promises of amendment, prompted by their imperilled interests, and by the hope Madame Hochon held out, that the debt to Max should be paid.

“You have done very wrong,” she said to them; “repair it by future good conduct, and Monsieur Hochon will forget it.”

So, when Francois had read the letter which had been brought for Baruch, over the latter’s shoulder, he whispered in his ear, “Ask grandpapa’s advice.”

“Read this,” said Baruch, taking the letter to old Hochon.

“Read it to me yourself; I haven’t my spectacles.”

  My dear Friend,--I hope you will not hesitate, under the serious circumstances in which I find myself, to do me the service of receiving a power of attorney from Monsieur Rouget. Be at Vatan to-morrow morning at nine o’clock. I shall probably send you to Paris, but don’t be uneasy; I will furnish you with money for the journey, and join you there immediately. I am almost sure I shall be obliged to leave Issoudun, December third.

  Adieu. I count on your friendship; rely on that of your friend,

  Maxence


“God be praised!” exclaimed Monsieur Hochon; “the property of that old idiot is saved from the claws of the devil.”

“It will be if you say so,” said Madame Hochon; “and I thank God,--who has no doubt heard my prayers. The prosperity of the wicked is always fleeting.”

“You must go to Vatan, and accept the power of attorney from Monsieur Rouget,” said the old man to Baruch. “Their object is to get fifty thousand francs a year transferred to Mademoiselle Brazier. They will send you to Paris, and you must seem to go; but you are to stop at Orleans, and wait there till you hear from me. Let no one--not a soul--know where you lodge; go to the first inn you come to in the faubourg Bannier, no matter if it is only a post-house--”

“Look here!” cried Francois, who had rushed to the window at the sudden noise of wheels in the Grande-Narette. “Here’s something new!--Pere Rouget and Colonel Bridau coming back together in the caleche, Benjamin and Captain Carpentier following on horseback!”

“I’ll go over,” cried Monsieur Hochon, whose curiosity carried the day over every other feeling.

Monsieur Hochon found old Rouget in his bedroom, writing the following letter at his nephew’s dictation:

  Mademoiselle,--If you do not start to return here the moment you receive this letter, your conduct will show such ingratitude for all my goodness that I shall revoke the will I have made in your favor, and give my property to my nephew Philippe. You will understand that Monsieur Gilet can no longer be my guest after staying with you at Vatan. I send this letter by Captain Carpentier, who will put it into your own hands. I hope you will listen to his advice; he will speak to you with authority from me.
  Your affectionate

  J.-J. Rouget.


“Captain Carpentier and I MET my uncle, who was so foolish as to follow Mademoiselle Brazier and Monsieur Gilet to Vatan,” said Philippe, with sarcastic emphasis, to Monsieur Hochon. “I have made my uncle see that he was running his head into a noose; for that girl will abandon him the moment she gets him to sign a power of attorney, by which they mean to obtain the income of his money in the Funds. That letter will bring her back under his roof, this very night, or I’m mistaken. I promise to make her as pliable as a bit of whalebone for the rest of her days, if my uncle allows me to take Maxence Gilet’s place; which, in my opinion, he ought never to have had in the first place. Am I not right?--and yet here’s my uncle bemoaning himself!”

“Neighbor,” said Monsieur Hochon, “you have taken the best means to get peace in your household. Destroy your will, and Flore will be once more what she used to be in the early days.”

“No, she will never forgive me for what I have made her suffer,” whimpered the old man; “she will no longer love me.”

“She shall love you, and closely too; I’ll take care of that,” said Philippe.

“Come, open your eyes!” exclaimed Monsieur Hochon. “They mean to rob you and abandon you.”

“Oh! I was sure of it!” cried the poor imbecile.

“See, here is a letter Maxence has written to my grandson Borniche,” said old Hochon. “Read it.”

“What infamy!” exclaimed Carpentier, as he listened to the letter, which Rouget read aloud, weeping.

“Is that plain enough, uncle?” demanded Philippe. “Hold that hussy by her interests and she’ll adore you as you deserve.”

“She loves Maxence too well; she will leave me,” cried the frightened old man.

“But, uncle, Maxence or I,--one or the other of us--won’t leave our footsteps in the dust of Issoudun three days hence.”

“Well then go, Monsieur Carpentier,” said Rouget; “if you promise me to bring her back, go! You are a good man; say to her in my name all you think you ought to say.”

“Captain Carpentier will whisper in her ear that I have sent to Paris for a woman whose youth and beauty are captivating; that will bring the jade back in a hurry!”

The captain departed, driving himself in the old caleche; Benjamin accompanied him on horseback, for Kouski was nowhere to be found. Though threatened by the officers with arrest and the loss of his situation, the Pole had gone to Vatan on a hired horse, to warn Max and Flore of the adversary’s move. After fulfilling his mission, Carpentier, who did not wish to drive back with Flore, was to change places with Benjamin, and take the latter’s horse.

When Philippe was told of Kouski’s flight he said to Benjamin, “You will take the Pole’s place, from this time on. It is all mapping out, papa Hochon!” cried the lieutenant-colonel. “That banquet will be jovial!”

“You will come and live here, of course,” said the old miser.

“I have told Fario to send me all my things,” answered Philippe. “I shall sleep in the room adjoining Gilet’s apartment,--if my uncle consents.”

“What will come of all this?” cried the terrified old man.

“Mademoiselle Flore Brazier is coming, gentle as a paschal lamb,” replied Monsieur Hochon.

“God grant it!” exclaimed Rouget, wiping his eyes.

“It is now seven o’clock,” said Philippe; “the sovereign of your heart will be here at half-past eleven: you’ll never see Gilet again, and you will be as happy ever after as a pope.--If you want me to succeed,” he whispered to Monsieur Hochon, “stay here till the hussy comes; you can help me in keeping the old man up to his resolution; and, together, we’ll make that crab-girl see on which side her bread is buttered.”

Monsieur Hochon felt the reasonableness of the request and stayed: but they had their hands full, for old Rouget gave way to childish lamentations, which were only quieted by Philippe’s repeating over and over a dozen times:--

“Uncle, you will see that I am right when Flore returns to you as tender as ever. You shall be petted; you will save your property: be guided by my advice, and you’ll live in paradise for the rest of your days.”

When, about half-past eleven, wheels were heard in the Grande-Narette, the question was, whether the carriage were returning full or empty. Rouget’s face wore an expression of agony, which changed to the prostration of excessive joy when he saw the two women, as the carriage turned to enter the courtyard.

“Kouski,” said Philippe, giving a hand to Flore to help her down. “You are no longer in Monsieur Rouget’s service. You will not sleep here to-night; get your things together, and go. Benjamin takes your place.”

“Are you the master here?” said Flore sarcastically.

“With your permission,” replied Philippe, squeezing her hand as if in a vice. “Come! we must have an understanding, you and I”; and he led the bewildered woman out into the place Saint-Jean.

“My fine lady,” began the old campaigner, stretching out his right hand, “three days hence, Maxence Gilet will be sent to the shades by that arm, or his will have taken me off guard. If I die, you will be the mistress of my poor imbecile uncle; ‘bene sit.’ If I remain on my pins, you’ll have to walk straight, and keep him supplied with first-class happiness. If you don’t, I know girls in Paris who are, with all due respect, much prettier than you; for they are only seventeen years old: they would make my uncle excessively happy, and they are in my interests. Begin your attentions this very evening; if the old man is not as gay as a lark to-morrow morning, I have only a word to say to you; it is this, pay attention to it,--there is but one way to kill a man without the interference of the law, and that is to fight a duel with him; but I know three ways to get rid of a woman: mind that, my beauty!”

During this address, Flore shook like a person with the ague.

“Kill Max--?” she said, gazing at Philippe in the moonlight.

“Come, here’s my uncle.”

Old Rouget, turning a deaf ear to Monsieur Hochon’s remonstrances, now came out into the street, and took Flore by the hand, as a miser might have grasped his treasure; he drew her back to the house and into his own room and shut the door.

“This is Saint-Lambert’s day, and he who deserts his place, loses it,” remarked Benjamin to the Pole.

“My master will shut your mouth for you,” answered Kouski, departing to join Max who established himself at the hotel de la Poste.

On the morrow, between nine and eleven o’clock, all the women talked to each other from door to door throughout the town. The story of the wonderful change in the Rouget household spread everywhere. The upshot of the conversations was the same on all sides,--

“What will happen at the banquet between Max and Colonel Bridau?”

Philippe said but few words to the Vedie,--“Six hundred francs’ annuity, or dismissal.” They were enough, however, to keep her neutral, for a time, between the two great powers, Philippe and Flore.

Knowing Max’s life to be in danger, Flore became more affectionate to Rouget than in the first days of their alliance. Alas! in love, a self-interested devotion is sometimes more agreeable than a truthful one; and that is why many men pay so much for clever deceivers. The Rabouilleuse did not appear till the next morning, when she came down to breakfast with Rouget on her arm. Tears filled her eyes as she beheld, sitting in Max’s place, the terrible adversary, with his sombre blue eyes, and the cold, sinister expression on his face.

“What is the matter, mademoiselle?” he said, after wishing his uncle good-morning.

“She can’t endure the idea of your fighting Maxence,” said old Rouget.

“I have not the slightest desire to kill Gilet,” answered Philippe. “He need only take himself off from Issoudun and go to America on a venture. I should be the first to advise you to give him an outfit, and to wish him a safe voyage. He would soon make a fortune there, and that is far more honorable than turning Issoudun topsy-turvy at night, and playing the devil in your household.”

“Well, that’s fair enough,” said Rouget, glancing at Flore.

“A-mer-i-ca!” she ejaculated, sobbing.

“It is better to kick his legs about in a free country than have them rot in a pine box in France. However, perhaps you think he is a good shot, and can kill me; it’s on the cards,” observed the colonel.

“Will you let me speak to him?” said Flore, imploring Philippe in a humble and submissive tone.

“Certainly; he can come here and pack up his things. I will stay with my uncle during that time; for I shall not leave the old man again,” replied Philippe.

“Vedie,” cried Flore, “run to the hotel, and tell Monsieur Gilet that I beg him--”

“--to come and get his belongings,” said Philippe, interrupting Flore’s message.

“Yes, yes, Vedie; that will be a good pretext to see me; I must speak to him.”

Terror controlled her hatred; and the shock which her whole being experienced when she first encountered this strong and pitiless nature was now so overwhelming that she bowed before Philippe just as Rouget had been in the habit of bending before her. She anxiously awaited Vedie’s return. The woman brought a formal refusal from Max, who requested Mademoiselle Brazier to send his things to the hotel de la Poste.

“Will you allow me to take them to him?” she said to Jean-Jacques Rouget.

“Yes, but will you come back?” said the old man.

“If Mademoiselle is not back by midday, you will give me a power of attorney to attend to your property,” said Philippe, looking at Flore. “Take Vedie with you, to save appearances, mademoiselle. In future you are to think of my uncle’s honor.”

Flore could get nothing out of Max. Desperate at having allowed himself, before the eyes of the whole town, to be routed out of his shameless position, Gilet was too proud to run away from Philippe. The Rabouilleuse combated this objection, and proposed that they should fly together to America; but Max, who did not want Flore without her money, and yet did not wish the girl to see the bottom of his heart, insisted on his intention of killing Philippe.

“We have committed a monstrous folly,” he said. “We ought all three to have gone to Paris and spent the winter there; but how could one guess, from the mere sight of that fellow’s big carcass, that things would turn out as they have? The turn of events is enough to make one giddy! I took the colonel for one of those fire-eaters who haven’t two ideas in their head; that was the blunder I made. As I didn’t have the sense to double like a hare in the beginning, I’ll not be such a coward as to back down before him. He has lowered me in the estimation of this town, and I cannot get back what I have lost unless I kill him.”

“Go to America with forty thousand francs. I’ll find a way to get rid of that scoundrel, and join you. It would be much wiser.”

“What would people say of me?” he exclaimed. “No; I have buried nine already. The fellow doesn’t seem as if he knew much; he went from school to the army, and there he was always fighting till 1815; then he went to America, and I doubt if the brute ever set foot in a fencing-alley; while I have no match with the sabre. The sabre is his arm; I shall seem very generous in offering it to him,--for I mean, if possible, to let him insult me,--and I can easily run him through. Unquestionably, it is my wisest course. Don’t be uneasy; we shall be masters of the field in a couple of days.”

That it was that a stupid point of honor had more influence over Max than sound policy. When Flore got home she shut herself up to cry at ease. During the whole of that day gossip ran wild in Issoudun, and the duel between Philippe and Maxence was considered inevitable.

“Ah! Monsieur Hochon,” said Mignonnet, who, accompanied by Carpentier, met the old man on the boulevard Baron, “we are very uneasy; for Gilet is clever with all weapons.”

“Never mind,” said the old provincial diplomatist; “Philippe has managed this thing well from the beginning. I should never have thought that big, easy-going fellow would have succeeded as he has. The two have rolled together like a couple of thunder-clouds.”

“Oh!” said Carpentier, “Philippe is a remarkable man. His conduct before the Court of Peers was a masterpiece of diplomacy.”

“Well, Captain Renard,” said one of the townsfolk to Max’s friend. “They say wolves don’t devour each other, but it seems that Max is going to set his teeth in Colonel Bridau. That’s pretty serious among you gentlemen of the Old Guard.”

“You make fun of it, do you? Because the poor fellow amused himself a little at night, you are all against him,” said Potel. “But Gilet is a man who couldn’t stay in a hole like Issoudun without finding something to do.”

“Well, gentlemen,” remarked another, “Max and the colonel must play out their game. Bridau had to avenge his brother. Don’t you remember Max’s treachery to the poor lad?”

“Bah! nothing but an artist,” said Renard.

“But the real question is about the old man’s property,” said a third. “They say Monsieur Gilet was laying hands on fifty thousand francs a year, when the colonel turned him out of his uncle’s house.”

“Gilet rob a man! Come, don’t say that to any one but me, Monsieur Canivet,” cried Potel. “If you do, I’ll make you swallow your tongue,--and without any sauce.”

Every household in town offered prayers for the honorable Colonel Bridau.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Towards four o’clock the following day, the officers of the old army at Issoudun and its environs strolled about the place du Marché, in front of Lacroix’s eating-house, waiting for Colonel Philippe Bridau. The banquet for the coronation was to begin at five. Various groups discussed Max’s disgrace and his dismissal from old Rouget’s house; the officers were to dine at Lacroix’s, and the common soldiers planned a meeting at a nearby wine-shop. Among the officers, Potel and Renard alone defended Max.

“Is it any of our business what takes place among the old man’s heirs?” said Renard.

“Max is weak with women,” Potel remarked.

“There’ll be sabres unsheathed before long,” said an old sub-lieutenant.

“A folly that doesn’t succeed becomes a crime,” Mignonnet observed dryly.

Max joined the old soldiers and was received in significant silence. Potel and Renard took his arms and walked with him, conversing. Presently Philippe appeared in full dress; his air contrasted with the forced gratitude Max showed his supporters. Bridau’s hand was grasped by Mignonnet, Carpentier, and others. This welcome, so unlike the one Max received, dispelled the last feeling of cowardice or prudence awakened in him by Flore’s entreaties and tendernesses.

“We shall fight,” he said to Renard, “and to the death. Therefore don’t talk to me any more; let me play my part well.”

The three Bonapartists returned to the officers’ group. Max bowed first to Bridau, who returned it; their glance was frigid.

“Come, gentlemen, let us take our seats,” said Potel.

“And drink to the health of the Little Corporal, who is now in the paradise of heroes,” cried Renard.

The company entered Lacroix’s long dining-hall. At Philippe’s request the two adversaries were seated opposite each other. Some young men of the town, among them Knights of Idleness, lingered outside, anxious to see what might happen. They deplored the crisis, though each considered a duel inevitable.

Throughout the meal both antagonists displayed vigilance beneath apparent joviality. At dessert Philippe rose and proposed the first toast.

“To the Grand Army!” he cried.

“To the Grand Army!” repeated every voice. Eleven private soldiers, including Benjamin and Kouski, entered and repeated the toast. The old soldiers stood behind the officers.

“I claim the second toast,” said Mignonnet. “Let us drink to those who attempted to restore his son!”

Everyone present, except Maxence Gilet, bowed to Philippe and raised their glasses. Max rose and, amid expectancy, bowed ironically to Philippe.

“May we all meet again at this time next year,” said Max.

His voice, though level, was charged; some muttered “It’s coming!” and Kouski whispered predictions. Tension lingered, but the two spoke in tones of ordinary conversation; there was nothing theatrical except the profound stillness.

“Look here, you others!” Philippe said to the soldiers; “our affairs don’t concern the bourgeoisie—no word, therefore. It is for the Old Guard only.”

“They’ll obey orders, colonel,” Renard replied.

The dessert passed; libations were plentiful. The antagonists and their four seconds arranged that such a duel, with its stakes of fortune and reputation, should not appear an ordinary brawl. Both behaved like gentlemen, calming the anxious crowd. At ten that night the two adversaries agreed on sabres; the rendezvous was behind the chancel of the Capuchins’ church at eight o’clock next morning. Goddet, former army surgeon, would be present. The seconds agreed the combat would last only ten minutes, whatever occurred.

At eleven that night Monsieur and Madame Hochon surprised Colonel Bridau in his rooms. Madame Hochon, tearful, implored him to say his prayers before leaving the house and presented him a relic: a tooth of Sainte Solange mounted on black velvet. She urged him to wear it.

“Will it protect me from a sabre-thrust?” asked Philippe.

“Yes,” she replied.

“Then I have no right to wear that accoutrement any more than if it were a cuirass,” cried Philippe.

The old lady kissed his forehead and gave thirty francs—her all—to Benjamin, asking him to sew the relic into Philippe’s trousers. Benjamin complied, not from belief in its virtue but because the pay and the commission constrained him. Madame Hochon left confident that Sainte Solange would protect him.

At eight the next morning, December third, with cloudy weather, Max, his seconds, and the Pole arrived on the small meadow around the apse of the Capuchins’ church. There Philippe and his seconds waited, Benjamin among them. Potel and Mignonnet paced off twenty-four feet; attendants drew lines with a spade at each end: no man was allowed to retreat beyond his line under pain of dishonor. Each was to stand at his line and advance when the seconds gave the word.

“Do we take off our coats?” asked Philippe coldly.

“Of course,” answered Maxence, adopting a bully’s manner.

They stripped to shirts; their muscles hardly trembled despite the cold. Goddet, the seconds, and two soldiers admired their composure.

Just as the signal was given, Max caught sight of Fario’s sinister face peering through a hole in the church roof. Those eyes, full of hatred and revenge, for a moment dazzled him. Philippe adopted the inside guard that marks a duellist of the first rank. Max sensed inferiority.

“Ah! the lascar!” he thought; “he’s an expert; I’m lost!”

He attempted a moulinet, twirling his sabre to bewilder Philippe and disarm him; but at the first encounter he felt the colonel’s wrist was iron and spring-like. Philippe’s parries were cool and precise. Between two men of such calibre, victory depends on a flash of calculation and a single false movement. At a bad parry by Max the colonel sent the sabre spinning from his hand.

“Pick it up,” he said; “I am not the man to kill a disarmed enemy.”

There was something atrocious in the grandeur of these words; they seemed to show such conscious superiority that the onlookers took them for calculation. When Max resumed position, he had lost coolness and confronted again Philippe’s raised guard. He resolved to redeem his shame by a bold stroke; taking his sabre in both hands he rushed furiously, determined to kill or be killed. Philippe received a cut slashing his forehead and part of his face, but with a terrible oblique moulinet he cleft Max’s head. These two savage blows ended the combat in the ninth minute. Fario came down to gloat over his enemy in convulsions; Philippe was carried back to his uncle’s house.

Thus perished a man who might have achieved great deeds in different circumstances; nature had given him courage, self-possession, and political sagacity, but not the nobility of conduct needed for greatness. He was not much regretted, given the perfidy by which his adversary—worse in character—had brought him into disrepute. His death ended the exploits of the Order of Idleness, to Issoudun’s relief. Philippe had little to fear as the duel’s circumstances were spread with praise for the bravery of both combatants.

“But they had better both have been killed,” remarked Monsieur Mouilleron; “it would have been a good riddance for the Government.”

Flore Brazier’s situation would have been embarrassing but for the condition into which she fell after Max’s death. A brain fever set in, combined with dangerous inflammation from her escapade to Vatan. If she had had ordinary health she might have fled the house where Max’s bed lay and where his murderer suffered; instead she hovered between life and death for three months, attended by Goddet, who also attended Philippe.

As soon as Philippe could write he sent letters.

To Monsieur Desroches he reported he had “killed the most venomous of the two reptiles,” though his own head had been split by a sabre. He noted that the other “viper” remained and that he must come to terms with her because his uncle clung to her. An illness had kept Flore in bed, which he hoped Providence would use to call her soul if she repented. By his account Goddet—“a worthy soul”—and others had been won to his side; Goddet preached resignation and religion to Flore and had promised to keep her in bed for three months. He had bought over the cook, who spread tales suggesting Max would have ill-treated Flore. “All goes well so far,” he wrote; “my uncle, by old Hochon’s advice, has destroyed his will.”

To Monsieur Giroudeau, care of Mademoiselle Florentine, he requested a girl—Cesarine, if available—or some ballet-girl who could appear as the respectable daughter of a brave soldier and stand ready to come at his call; if she answered his purpose he would give her fifty thousand francs on his uncle’s death. He told of his duel and hinted at lively times to come.

Agathe, informed by Madame Hochon, rushed to Issoudun and was given Philippe’s former room. Her maternal tenderness revived as she listened to town praises for her son.

“After all, my child,” said Madame Hochon, “youth must have its fling. The dissipations of a soldier under the Empire must, of course, be greater than those of young men looked after by their fathers. Thanks to your son, Issoudun now breathes and sleeps in peace. Philippe has come to his senses rather late; he told us frankly that those three months in the Luxembourg sobered him. Monsieur Hochon is delighted with his conduct here; everyone thinks highly of it. If he can be kept away from the temptations of Paris, he will end by being a comfort to you.”

Philippe played the saint to his mother because he needed her. He did not wish to resort to Cesarine unless he continued to be an object of horror to Mademoiselle Brazier. He recognized Flore was essential to his uncle’s life and preferred to use her rather than bring in a dancer who might marry the old man. He conceived the idea of making his uncle marry Flore, thus legitimizing her position and keeping her—and the property—within reach. He asked his mother to visit Flore and treat her in a sisterly way.

“I must confess, my dear mother,” he said in an affected tone before Monsieur and Madame Hochon, “that my uncle’s way of life is not becoming; he could, however, make Mademoiselle Brazier respected by the community if he chose. Wouldn’t it be far better for her to be Madame Rouget than the servant-mistress of an old bachelor? She had better obtain a definite right to his property by a marriage contract. If you, or Monsieur Hochon, or some good priest would speak to both parties, you might put a stop to the scandal which offends decent people.”

The next day Agathe and Madame Hochon appeared at Flore’s bedside and repeated Philippe’s words. In Issoudun the colonel was praised as a man of noble character, particularly because of his conduct toward Flore. For a month Flore heard Goddet, Madame Hochon, Agathe, and Rouget all urging a marriage. When, attracted by the idea of becoming Madame Rouget, she grew eager to recover so the ceremony could occur, it was not difficult to make her understand that she would be unwelcome in the Rouget family if she intended to turn Philippe out.

“Besides,” the doctor whispered, “you really owe him this good fortune. Max would never have allowed you to marry old Rouget. And, if you have children, you can revenge Max, for that will disinherit the Bridaus.”

Two months after the duel, in February 1823, the sick woman, urged by those about her and implored by Rouget, consented to receive Philippe; the sight of his scars moved her to tears, and his softened manner calmed her. By Philippe’s wish they were left alone.

“My dear child,” he said, “it is I who, from the start, have advised your marriage with my uncle; if you consent, it will take place as soon as you are recovered.”

“So they tell me,” she replied.

“Circumstances have compelled me to give you pain; it is natural therefore that I should wish to do you all the good I can. Wealth, respect, and a family position are worth more than what you have lost. You wouldn’t have been that fellow’s wife long after my uncle’s death, for I happen to know, through friends of his, that he intended to get rid of you. Come, my dear, let us understand each other and live happily. You shall be my aunt and nothing more. You will take care that my uncle does not forget me in his will; on my side, you shall see how well I will have you treated in the marriage contract. Keep calm, think it over, and we will talk later. All sensible people, indeed the whole town, urge you to put an end to your illegal position; no one will blame you for receiving me. It is well understood in the world that interests go before feelings. By the day of your marriage you will be handsomer than ever. The pallor of illness has given you an air of distinction, and on my honor, if my uncle did not love you so madly, you should be the wife of Colonel Bridau.”

Philippe left after dropping into Flore’s mind the idea of revenge through children—a hint that stirred her. In this scene Philippe echoed, in miniature, the manipulative speech of a schemer: personal calculation masked as sentiment has formidable power over the heart. Nature herself works by self-interest.

At the beginning of April 1823, Rouget’s hall hosted a splendid dinner to celebrate the signing of the marriage contract between Mademoiselle Flore Brazier and the old bachelor. Guests included Monsieur Heron, the witnesses, Mignonnet, Carpentier, Hochon, Goddet, the mayor, the curate, Agathe Bridau, Madame Hochon and her friend Madame Borniche. The bride was much impressed by the protection offered by Philippe and the two ladies. Flore was dazzling. The curate, who had been instructing her for the last fortnight, was to allow her first communion the following day. The local journals praised the marriage as a revival of religion and a moral correction; the “Journal du Cher” and the “Journal de l’Indre” printed accounts that lauded Philippe’s supposed disinterestedness.

By the contract Rouget secured to Flore a dower of one hundred thousand francs and a life annuity of thirty thousand.

After the wedding, Agathe returned to Paris triumphant and told Joseph and Desroches the “good news.”

“Your son Philippe is too wily a man not to keep his paw on that inheritance,” said Desroches.

“You, and Joseph too, will always be unjust to that poor boy,” said Madame Bridau. She defended her son’s conduct as statesmanlike, a reaction to idle powers misapplied.

“Why do you suppose that he will not succeed by honest means?” she demanded.

“You will see!” exclaimed Desroches. “Fortunate or unfortunate, Philippe will remain the man of the Rue Mazarin, the murderer of Madame Descoings, the domestic thief. But don’t worry yourself; he will manage to appear honest to the world.”

The morning after the marriage, Philippe took Madame Rouget aside.

“My dear aunt,” he said, “you now belong to the family. Thanks to me, the law has tied the knot. Now, no nonsense. I intend that you and I should play above board. I know the tricks you will try against me; and I shall watch you like a duenna. You will never go out of this house except on my arm; and you will never leave me. As to what passes within the house, damn it, you’ll find me like a spider in the middle of his web. Here is something,” he continued, showing her a letter, “which will prove to you that I could, while you were lying ill upstairs, have turned you out of doors without a penny. Read it.”

The letter was from the theatrical set—Mariette and friends—detailing a plan to provide a trained actress to entrap Rouget and secure the property for Philippe. It described the girl’s arts and the social circle prepared to exploit any fortune, and suggested the pleasures awaiting Philippe in Paris. The letter shook Madame Rouget; terror and dependency betrayed her.

“I trust you,” he said, fixing her with terrible meaning, “as you see; but I expect some return. I have made you my aunt intending to marry you some day. You are worth more to me than Esther in managing my uncle. In a year from now we must be in Paris; you will amuse yourself much better there than here; it is a perpetual carnival. I shall return to the army and become a general, and you will be a great lady. There’s our future; now work for it. But I must have a pledge to bind this agreement. You are to give me, within a month from now, a power of attorney from my uncle, which you must obtain under pretence of relieving him of the fatigues of business. Also, a month later, I must have a special power to transfer the income in the Funds. When that stands in my name, you and I have an equal interest in marrying each other. There it all is, my beautiful aunt, as plain as day. Between you and me there must be no ambiguity. I can marry my aunt at the end of a year’s widowhood; but I could not marry a disgraced girl.”

He left without waiting for an answer. Vedie, clearing the table, found her mistress pale and sweating despite the season. Flore felt as if she had fallen to the bottom of a precipice; the future seemed black and full of monstrous shapes. The damp chill of vaults, an instinctive fear of the man, oppressed her; yet a voice told her she deserved to have him for her master. She was helpless.

Flore Brazier had had a room of her own in Rouget’s house; now Madame Rouget belonged to her husband and was deprived of the freedom of a servant-mistress. In the horrible situation she discovered the hope of having a child, but soon saw that impossible under Philippe’s constant watch. Benjamin was Philippe’s innocent spy; Vedie trembled under him. Flore felt deserted and utterly helpless; she feared death. Without knowing how he might manage it, she felt certain that if he suspected pregnancy her doom would be sealed. The sound of his voice, the veiled glitter of his gambler’s eye, the slightest movement of the soldier—politeness cloaking brutality—made her shudder. The power of attorney he demanded he obtained as soon as he wanted it; Flore fell under his dominion as France had fallen under Napoleon.

Like a butterfly caught in wax, Rouget rapidly dissipated his remaining strength. In his decay the nephew remained as cold and impassible as diplomats in convulsed times.

Philippe, now skeptical of Napoleon II., wrote to the minister of war through intermediaries to offer his services to the King. He explained that Napoleon was gone; having been faithful, he was now free to serve the monarch. He asked for consideration and reinstatement at his rank, professing entire submission and assuring the minister of his fidelity. Accompanying the letter were requests for permission to go to Paris on urgent family business and endorsements from the mayor, the sub-prefect, and the commissary of police of Issoudun, praising Philippe’s conduct and citing newspaper articles about Rouget’s marriage.

Two weeks later, Philippe received permission and a letter informing him that by order of the King he was, as a preliminary favor, reinstated lieutenant-colonel in the royal army.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
CHAPTER XVII

Lieutenant-Colonel Bridau returned to Paris with his aunt and the helpless Rouget, and three days after their arrival escorted them to the Treasury, where Jean-Jacques assigned his income to Philippe. The exhausted old man and the Rabouilleuse were plunged by their nephew into the dissipations of actresses, journalists, artists, and equivocal women among whom Philippe had already wasted his youth; old Rouget found excitements that soon killed him. Instigated by Giroudeau, Lolotte, one of the handsomest of the Opera ballet-girls, was the amiable assassin of the old man. Rouget died after a splendid supper at Florentine’s, and Lolotte blamed a slice of pâté de foie gras; as the Strasburg masterpiece could make no defence, it was considered settled that the old man died of indigestion.

Madame Rouget thrived amid this décolleté society; but Philippe put her in charge of Mariette, and that monitress prevented the widow—whose mourning was diversified with a few amusements—from committing any actual follies.

In October, 1823, Philippe returned to Issoudun with a power of attorney from his aunt to liquidate his uncle’s estate; the business was soon over, and he returned to Paris in March, 1824, with sixteen hundred thousand francs—the net proceeds of old Rouget’s property, not counting the pictures, which had never left Monsieur Hochon’s hands. Philippe put the whole into Mongenod and Sons, where young Baruch Borniche was employed, on Hochon’s assurances of solvency. This house took his funds at six per cent per annum, on condition of three months’ notice for withdrawal.

One day Philippe visited his mother and invited her to his marriage, witnessed by Giroudeau, Finot, Nathan, and Bixiou. By the contract, the widow Rouget, whose portion was a million francs, secured to her future husband her whole fortune should she die childless. No invitations were sent; Philippe had designs. He lodged his wife in an appartement in the rue Saint-Georges, bought ready-furnished from Lolotte. Madame Bridau the younger thought it delightful; her husband rarely set foot in it. Without her knowledge, Philippe purchased in the rue de Clichy a magnificent hotel for two hundred and fifty thousand francs, paying one hundred and fifty thousand down and taking two years for the rest. He spent large sums altering the interior and furnishing it; in fact, he put his income for two years into this outlay. The restored pictures, then estimated at three hundred thousand francs, shone there.

The accession of Charles X. brought greater court favor to the family of the Duc de Chaulieu, whose eldest son, the Duc de Rhetore, was in the habit of seeing Philippe at Tullia’s. Under Charles X., the elder branch, believing itself secure, followed advice to encourage adherence of former Imperial soldiers. Philippe, who had likely made invaluable revelations about the conspiracies of 1820 and 1822, was appointed lieutenant-colonel in the regiment of the Duc de Maufrigneuse. That nobleman felt bound to protect the man from whom he had taken Mariette. The corps-de-ballet went for something, therefore, in the appointment. Moreover, it was decided in private councils to give a tinge of liberalism to the Dauphin’s surroundings. Philippe, now a sort of equerry to the Duc de Maufrigneuse, was presented to the Dauphin and Dauphine, who liked brusque soldierly characters noted for past fidelity. Philippe understood the part the Dauphin had to play and turned this spurious liberalism to his profit by getting appointed aide-de-camp to a marshal who stood well at court.

In January, 1827, Philippe, promoted in the Royal Guard as lieutenant-colonel in a regiment commanded by the Duc de Maufrigneuse, solicited ennoblement. Under the Restoration, nobility became a perquisite to “roturiers” serving in the Guard. Colonel Bridau had bought the estate of Brambourg and asked to entail it under the title of count. The favor was granted through his intimacies in high society, where he now appeared with the luxury of horses, carriages, and liveries; in short, the surroundings of a great lord. As soon as he saw himself gazetted as Comte de Brambourg, he began to frequent the house of a lieutenant-general of artillery, the Comte de Soulanges.

Insatiable and backed by mistresses of influential men, Philippe solicited the honor of being one of the Dauphin’s aides-de-camp. He had the audacity to tell the Dauphin that “an old soldier, wounded on many a battle-field and who knew real warfare, might, on occasion, be serviceable to Monseigneur.” Philippe, who could assume all tones of sycophancy, became in the highest social regions exactly what the position required; just as at Issoudun, he had copied Mignonnet’s respectability. He had a fine establishment and gave fêtes and dinners, admitting none of his old friends whose position might compromise him. He was pitiless to companions of former debauches and curtly refused Bixiou when that lively satirist asked him to say a word for Giroudeau, who wanted re-entry into the army after Florentine’s desertion.

“The man has neither manners nor morals,” said Philippe.

“Ha! did he say that of me?” cried Giroudeau. “Of me, who helped him get rid of his uncle!”

“We’ll pay him off yet,” said Bixiou.

Philippe intended to marry Mademoiselle Amelie de Soulanges and become a general commanding a Guard regiment. He asked so many favors that, to keep him quiet, they made him a Commander of the Legion of Honor and of Saint Louis. One rainy evening, as Agathe and Joseph returned home along the muddy streets, they met Philippe in full uniform, bedizened with orders, leaning back in a coupe lined with yellow silk, whose armorial bearings were surmounted with a count’s coronet. He was on his way to a fête at the Élysée-Bourbon; the wheels splashed his mother and brother as he waved a patronizing greeting.

“He’s going it, that fellow!” said Joseph. “Nevertheless, he might send us something better than mud in our faces.”

“He has such a fine position, in such high society, that we ought not to blame him for forgetting us,” said Madame Bridau. “When a man rises to so great a height, he has obligations to repay, sacrifices to make; it is natural he should not come to see us, though he may think of us all the same.”

“My dear fellow,” said the Duc de Maufrigneuse one evening to the new Comte de Brambourg, “I am sure your addresses will be favorably received; but to marry Amelie de Soulanges you must be free. What have you done with your wife?”

“My wife?” said Philippe, with a gesture, look, and accent worthy of Frederick Lemaitre. “Alas! I have the melancholy certainty of losing her. She has not a week to live. My dear duke, you don’t know what it is to marry beneath you. A woman who was a cook, who has the tastes of a cook! Who dishonors me—ah! I am much to be pitied. I explained my position to Madame la Dauphine. At the time of the marriage, it was a question of saving the family a million which my uncle left to that person. Happily, my wife took to drinking; at her death, I come into possession of that million, which is now in Mongenod and Sons. I have thirty thousand francs a year in the five per cents, and my entailed landed property brings forty thousand more. If Monsieur de Soulanges gets a marshal’s baton, I am on the high-road, with my title, to becoming general and peer of France. That will be the proper end of an aide-de-camp of the Dauphin.”

After the Salon of 1823, one of the leading painters obtained the management of a lottery-office near the Markets for Joseph Bridau’s mother. Agathe was soon able to exchange it for an office in the rue de Seine, in a house where Joseph had his atelier. The widow now hired an agent herself and was no longer an expense to her son. And yet, as late as 1828, though she directed an office owed to Joseph’s fame, Madame Bridau still doubted that fame, which was hotly contested as all true glory is. The great painter, struggling with genius, had enormous wants; he did not earn enough to pay for the luxuries that his relations and position in the young School of Art demanded. Though sustained by his Cénacle friends and by Mademoiselle des Touches, he did not please the bourgeoisie. The bourgeoisie, from whom money comes, never unties its purse for genius that is in question; unfortunately, Joseph had the classics and the Institute and their critics against him. Though backed by Gros and Géricault (Gérard), by whose influence he was decorated after the Salon of 1827, he obtained few orders. Moreover, Joseph gave way too much to imaginative fancies, producing inequality in his work which enemies used to deny his talent.

“High art is at a low ebb,” said his friend Pierre Grassou, who made daubs for bourgeois tastes.

“You ought to have a whole cathedral to decorate; that’s what you want,” declared Schinner; “then you would silence criticism with a master-stroke.”

Such speeches alarmed Agathe and confirmed her long-held judgment about Philippe and Joseph. Facts sustained that judgment in a provincial woman’s mind. Philippe, her favorite, was now a great man; his early errors were only youth’s ebullitions. Joseph, whose productions she could not appreciate—having seen them in progress too often—seemed no more advanced in 1828 than in 1816. Poor Joseph owed money and was bowed by debt; she felt he had chosen a worthless career that gave no return. She could not conceive why they had given him the Legion cross.
