Philippe, rich enough to cease gambling, a guest at Madame’s fêtes, the brilliant colonel appearing at reviews and processions in splendid uniforms with two crosses, realized all her maternal dreams. One day of public ceremony effaced from Agathe’s mind the sight of Philippe’s earlier misery; on that day he passed her at the same spot, in attendance on the Dauphin, with plumes in his shako and a pelisse gorgeous with gold and fur. Agathe, who to her artist son was now a devoted sister-figure, felt herself the mother of none but the dashing aide-de-camp. Proud of Philippe, she felt he made her life easy and happy—forgetting that the lottery-office enabling her to live came through Joseph.

One day Agathe noticed Joseph more worried than usual by his color-man’s bill and resolved, though cursing his profession in her heart, to free him from debts. She kept the house with the office’s proceeds and never asked Joseph for a farthing; she had no money of her own but relied on Philippe’s good heart and purse. For three years she had expected his visits; she imagined that if she appealed he would bring an enormous sum, and pictured the happiness of giving it to Joseph, whose judgment of his brother she found unfair.

Without telling Joseph, she wrote the following letter to Philippe:

  To Monsieur le comte de Brambourg:

  My dear Philippe,—You have not given the least little word of remembrance to your mother for five years. That is not right. You should remember the past, if only for the sake of your excellent brother. Joseph is now in need of money, and you are floating in wealth; he works, while you are flying from fête to fête. You now possess, all to yourself, the property of my brother. Little Borniche tells me you cannot have less than two hundred thousand francs a year. Well, then, come and see Joseph. During your visit, slip into the skull a few thousand-franc notes. Philippe, you owe them to us; nevertheless, your brother will feel grateful to you, not to speak of the happiness you will give

  Your mother,

  Agathe Bridau, née Rouget

Two days later the concierge brought to the atelier the terrible reply:

  My dear Mother,—A man does not marry a Mademoiselle Amelie de Soulanges without the purse of Fortunatus, if under the name of Comte de Brambourg he hides that of

  Your son,

  Philippe Bridau

Agathe fell half-fainting on the sofa; the letter dropped. The slight noise and her smothered exclamation startled Joseph, who had been rubbing in a sketch. He leaned his head round the canvas, saw his mother stretched out, and rushed to lift what seemed lifeless. He carried Agathe to her bed and sent for Horace Bianchon. When he could question her she told him of her letter and its reply. Joseph went to the atelier, picked up Philippe’s concise brutality, which had broken his mother’s heart and shattered the edifice of trust. When Joseph returned to her bedside he was silent; he did not speak of his brother during the three weeks of the poor woman’s death agony. Bianchon, who watched with devotion, told Joseph the truth on the first day of her seizure.

“At her age,” he said, “and under the circumstances, all we can hope is to make her death as little painful as possible.”

She herself felt so surely called that she asked for Abbé Loraux, her confessor for over twenty-two years. Alone with him, having poured out her griefs, she said—as she had said to Madame Hochon and repeated to herself—“What have I done to displease God? Have I not loved Him with all my soul? Have I wandered from the path of grace? What is my sin? Can I be guilty of wrong when I know not what it is? Have I time to repair it?”

“No,” said the old man gently. “Alas! your life seems pure and your soul spotless; but the eye of God is keener than that of his ministers. I see the truth too late; for you have misled even me.”

Hearing such words from lips always peaceful, Agathe rose suddenly and opened wide her eyes in terror.

“Tell me! tell me!” she cried.

“Be comforted,” said the priest. “Your punishment is a proof that you will receive pardon. God chastens his elect. Woe to those whose misdeeds meet with fortunate success; they will be kneaded again in humanity until sorely punished and brought to maturity. Your life, my daughter, has been one long error. You gave your heart to an unnatural son, in whom you made your glory, and you have misunderstood the child who is your true glory. You have been so unjust that you never saw the striking contrast between the brothers. You owe the comfort of your life to Joseph, while your other son has pillaged you repeatedly. The poor son, who loves you without equal return, gives you all the comfort your life has had; the rich son, who never thinks of you, despises you and desires your death—”

“Oh no,” she cried.

“Yes,” resumed the priest. “Your humble position stands in the way of his proud hopes. Mother, these are your sins! You have sinned by ignorance. Love Joseph now; give him your heart and pray for him, as I shall for you.”

The mother’s eyes opened to her life’s course. Illuminated, she wept. The old priest, moved by her repentance, left the room hastily.

Joseph returned about two hours after the confessor had left. He had been to borrow the money needed to pay pressing debts and sat quietly, thinking his mother asleep. He sprang up with a chill as he heard her say, brokenly, “Will he forgive me?”

“What is it, mother?” he exclaimed, shocked at her expression.

“Ah, Joseph! can you pardon me, my child?” she cried.

“For what?” he said.

“I have never loved you as you deserved to be loved.”

“Oh, what an accusation!” he cried. “Not loved me? For seven years have we not lived alone together? Have you not taken care of me? Are you not my gentle and indulgent companion? I was saying to Grassou only yesterday: ‘What comforts me is that I have such a good mother. She sees to everything; she takes care of my material wants without troubling me.’”

“No, Joseph, no; you have loved me, but I have not returned love for love. Ah! would I could live a little longer—Give me your hand.”

Agathe took his hand, kissed it, held it to her heart, and looked long at him, letting him see tenderness she had hitherto bestowed only on Philippe. The painter, apt at judging expression, saw how his mother’s heart opened and took her to his breast, crying, “My mother! oh, my mother!”

“Ah! I feel that I am forgiven!” she said. “God will confirm the child’s pardon of its mother.”

“You must be calm: don’t torment yourself; hear me. I feel loved enough in this one moment for all the past,” he said, laying her back on pillows.

During the two weeks’ struggle between life and death, each effusion of feeling seemed an expression of a lifetime. The mother thought only of her son; sustained by love, she was unaware of her sufferings. D’Arthez, Michel Chrestien, Fulgence Ridal, Pierre Grassou, and Bianchon often kept Joseph company, the friends serving as a band of brothers in thought and action. Joseph, inspired by feeling and genius, divined in his mother’s occasional expressions a desire hidden in her heart and said to d’Arthez, “She has loved that brigand Philippe too well not to want to see him before she dies.”

Joseph begged Bixiou, who frequented Bohemian regions where Philippe was still occasionally found, to persuade that shameless son to play, out of pity, a little comedy that might fold the mother in an illusory peace. Bixiou, an observing misanthropic scoffer, undertook the mission. When he told the Comte de Brambourg—who received him in a bedroom hung with yellow damask—Philippe laughed.

“What the devil do you want me to do there?” he cried. “The only service the poor woman can render me is to die as soon as she can; she would be a sorry figure at my marriage with Mademoiselle de Soulanges. The less my family is seen, the better my position. You can easily understand that I should like to bury the name of Bridau under all the monuments in Père-Lachaise. My brother irritates me by bringing the name into publicity. Look at it as if it were your own: if you were a deputy, with a tongue like yours, you would be as much feared as Chauvelin; you would be made Comte Bixiou, and director of the Beaux-Arts. Once there, how should you like it if your grandmother Descoings were to turn up? Would you want that worthy woman hanging on you? Damn it, you’d wish her six feet under ground. Come, breakfast with me, and let us talk of something else. I am a parvenu, my dear fellow, and I know it. I don’t choose that my swaddling-clothes shall be seen. My son will be more fortunate than I. The scamp will wish me dead; I expect it—or he won’t be my son.”

He rang for breakfast. “The fashionable world wouldn’t see you in your mother’s bedroom,” said Bixiou. “What would it cost you to seem to love that poor woman for a few hours?”

“Whew!” cried Philippe, winking. “So you come from them, do you? I’m an old camel, who knows all about genuflections. My mother makes the excuse of her last illness to get something out of me for Joseph. No, thank you!”

When Bixiou related this to Joseph, the poor painter was chilled to the soul.

“Does Philippe know I am ill?” asked Agathe the day after Bixiou’s errand.

Joseph left, suffocating with emotion. The Abbé Loraux, sitting by the bed, took her hand and said, “Alas! my child, you have never had but one son.” The words, which Agathe understood, conveyed a shock that began the end. She died twenty hours later.

In the delirium preceding death, she murmured, “Whom does Philippe take after?”

Joseph followed his mother to the grave alone. Philippe had gone on business to Orléans; in reality he was driven from Paris by the letter Joseph wrote a moment after Agathe’s last sigh:

  Monster! my poor mother has died of the shock your letter caused her. Wear mourning, but pretend illness; I will not suffer her assassin to stand at my side before her coffin.

  Joseph B.

Joseph, who no longer had the heart to paint though grief needed the distraction of labor, was surrounded by friends who vowed never to leave him alone. Thus, two weeks after Agathe’s funeral, Bixiou sat in the atelier with a group when the servant brought a letter from an old woman below.

  Monsieur,—To you, whom I scarcely dare to call my brother, I am forced to address myself, if only on account of the name I bear.--

Joseph turned the page and read the signature: “Comtesse Flore de Brambourg.” He shuddered, foreseeing new atrocity from his brother.

“That brigand,” he cried, “is the devil’s own. And he calls himself a man of honor! And he wears crosses on his breast! And he struts at court instead of being bastinadoed! And the scoundrel is called Monsieur le Comte!”

“There are many like him,” said Bixiou.

“After all,” said Joseph, “the Rabouilleuse deserves her fate. She’s not worth pitying; she’d have had my neck wrung without saying, ‘He’s innocent.’”

Joseph flung the letter; Bixiou caught and read it aloud:

  Is it decent that the Comtesse Bridau de Brambourg should die in a hospital, no matter what her faults? If so, will you, who are the friend of Doctor Bianchon, ask him for a permit to let me enter a hospital?

  The person who carries this letter has been eleven consecutive days to the hotel de Brambourg, rue de Clichy, without getting any help from my husband. Poverty prevents me from employing a lawyer to demand what is due me, that I may die with decency. Nothing can save me, I know that. If you are unwilling to see your unhappy sister-in-law, send me, at least, the money to end my days. Your brother desires my death; he has always desired it. He warned me that he knew three ways of killing a woman, but I had not the sense to foresee the one he has employed.

  In case you will relieve me and judge for yourself my misery, I live in the rue du Houssay, corner of the rue Chantereine, on the fifth floor. If I cannot pay my rent to-morrow I shall be put out--and then, where can I go?

  Your sister-in-law,

  Comtesse Flore de Brambourg.

“What a pit of infamy!” cried Joseph; “there is something under it all.”

“Let us send for the woman who brought the letter; we may get the preface,” said Bixiou.

The woman appeared, a mass of old gowns fringed with mud, mounted on thick legs with ragged stockings and leaking shoes. Above the mound rose a head like Charlet’s scavenger-women, caparisoned with a filthy bandanna.

“What is your name?” said Joseph as Bixiou sketched her, leaning on an umbrella dated year II.

“Madame Gruget, at your service. I’ve seen better days,” she said. “If my poor girl hadn’t loved some one too much, you wouldn’t see me what I am. She drowned herself in the river, my poor Ida,—saving your presence! I’ve had the folly to nurse up a quaterne, and that’s why, at seventy-seven, I’m obliged to take care of sick folks for ten sous a day, and go—”

“—without clothes?” said Bixiou. “My grandmother nursed up a trey, but she dressed properly.”

“Out of my ten sous I pay for lodging—”

“What’s wrong with the lady you nurse?”

“In the first place, she hasn’t any money; and she has a disease that scares doctors. She owes me for sixty days’ nursing; that’s why I keep on. The husband, who is a count—she is really a countess—will no doubt pay me when she is dead; so I’ve lent her all I had. I haven’t anything left; all I did have has gone to the pawn-brokers. She owes me forty-seven francs and twelve sous, besides thirty francs for nursing. She wants to kill herself with charcoal. I tell her it ain’t right; I’ve had to get the concierge to look after her while I’m gone, or she’ll jump out the window.”

“But what’s the matter with her?” said Joseph.

“Ah! monsieur, the doctor from the Sisters’ hospital came; but as to the disease,” said Madame Gruget, modestly, “he told me she must go to the hospital. The case is hopeless.”

“Let us go and see her,” said Bixiou.

“Here,” said Joseph, “take these ten francs.”

Plunging his hand into his skull and taking out all his remaining money, Joseph called a coach and went for Bianchon, who was fortunately home. Bixiou ran to the rue de Bussy for Desroches. The four friends reached Flore’s retreat in the rue du Houssay an hour later.

“That Mephistopheles on horseback, named Philippe Bridau,” said Bixiou as they climbed the steep stair, “has sailed his boat cleverly to get rid of his wife. You know Lousteau? well, Philippe paid him a thousand francs a month to keep Madame Bridau in the society of Florine, Mariette, Tullia, and the Val-Noble. When Philippe saw his crab-girl so used to pleasure and dress that she couldn’t do without them, he stopped paying and left her to get it as she could—it is easy to know how. By the end of eighteen months the brute forced his wife lower and lower; then, by the help of a young officer, he gave her a taste for drinking. As he rose, his wife sank; the countess is now in the mud. I don’t know what means Philippe has lately taken to get rid of her. I am anxious to study this drama, for I am determined to avenge Joseph. Alas, friends,” he added, leaving them in doubt whether jesting, “give a man over to a vice and you’ll get rid of him. Didn’t Hugo say: ‘She loved a ball, and died of it’? So it is. Vice! vice! my dear friends, do you want to know what vice is? It is the Bonneau of death.”

“Then you’ll die of a joke,” said Desroches, laughing.

Above the fourth floor, they climbed a ladder-like attic stair. Though Joseph remembered Flore’s beauty, he was unprepared for the hideous spectacle. In a room with bare walls under the attic roof, on a cot with a scanty mattress, a woman lay green as a body drowned, thin as a consumptive. Her head, nearly hairless, was bound with a checked handkerchief; circles round the hollow eyes were red; eyelids like the pellicle of an egg. Nothing remained of the captivating body but an ignoble bony structure. As Flore saw Joseph, two great tears rolled down her cheeks.

“She can still weep!” whispered Bixiou. “A strange sight—tears from dominos! It’s like the miracle of Moses.”

“How burnt up!” cried Joseph.

“In the fires of repentance,” said Flore. “I cannot get a priest; I have nothing, not even a crucifix, to help me see God. Ah, monsieur!” she cried, raising arms like carved wood, “I am a guilty woman; but God never punished any one as he has punished me! Philippe killed Max, who advised dreadful things, and now he has killed me. God uses him as a scourge!”

“Leave me alone with her,” said Bianchon, “and let me see if the disease is curable.”

“If you cure her, Philippe Bridau will die of rage,” said Desroches. “I will draw up a statement of her condition. He has not brought her before the courts as an adulteress, and thus her conjugal rights stand: he shall have the shame of a suit. But first we must remove the Comtesse to Doctor Dubois’s private hospital in the rue du Faubourg-Saint-Denis. She will be cared for there. Then I will summon the count for restoration of the conjugal home.”

“Bravo, Desroches!” cried Bixiou. “What a pleasure to do so much good that will make some people feel so badly!”

Ten minutes later Bianchon joined them. “I am going straight to Despleins,” he said. “He can save her by an operation. Ah! he will take care of the case, for her abuse of liquor has developed a disease thought lost.”

“Wag of a mangler! Isn’t there but one disease in life?” cried Bixiou.

Bianchon was already gone. Two hours later Flore was removed to Dubois’s hospital. Three weeks later the “Hospital Gazette” published an account of one of modern surgery’s boldest operations on a case designated by the initials “F. B.” The patient died—more from exhaustion produced by misery and starvation than from treatment.

No sooner did she die than the Comte de Brambourg went, in deep mourning, to call on the Comte de Soulanges and inform him of his sad loss. Soon after, fashion whispered that the Comte de Soulanges would marry his daughter to a parvenu of merit about to be appointed brigadier-general and given command of a Royal Guard regiment. De Marsay told Eugène de Rastignac this news at the Rocher de Cancale, where Bixiou happened to be.

“It shall not take place!” said the witty artist to himself.

Among the many old friends Philippe refused to recognize were some unable to revenge themselves; but he had wounded Bixiou, who, thanks to brilliant qualities, was everywhere received and never forgave an insult. One day at the Rocher de Cancale, before many well-bred people, Philippe had replied to Bixiou—who spoke of visiting him at the hotel de Brambourg—“You can come and see me when you are made a minister.”

“Am I to turn Protestant before I can visit you?” said Bixiou, feigning misunderstanding; but he thought, “You may be Goliath, but I have my sling.”

The next day he went to an actor friend, metamorphosed himself by dress into a secularized priest with green spectacles, drove to the hotel de Soulanges, and, received by the count on a message that he had serious business, related in a feigned voice the whole story of the dead countess and the secret particulars confided to him by Bianchon; the histories of Agathe’s death, of old Rouget’s death (which the Comte had boasted of), of Madame Descoings’s death, of the theft from the newspaper, and of Philippe’s early private morals.

“Monsieur le comte, don’t give him your daughter until you have made every inquiry; interrogate his former comrades,—Bixiou, Giroudeau, and others.”

Three months later the Comte de Brambourg gave a supper to du Tillet, Nucingen, Eugène de Rastignac, Maxime de Trailles, and Henri de Marsay. The host accepted their half-consolatory condolences about his rupture with Soulanges with nonchalance.

“You can do better,” said Maxime de Trailles.

“How much must a man have to marry a demoiselle de Grandlieu?” asked Philippe of de Marsay.

“You? They wouldn’t give you the ugliest of the six for less than ten millions,” answered de Marsay insolently.

“Bah!” said Rastignac. “With an income of two hundred thousand francs you can have Mademoiselle de Langeais; she is thirty, ugly, and penniless; that ought to suit you.”

“I shall have ten millions two years from now,” said Philippe Bridau.

“It is now the 16th of January, 1829,” cried du Tillet, laughing. “I have been hard at work for ten years and have not made as much as that yet.”

“We’ll take counsel of each other,” said Bridau; “you shall see how well I understand finance.”

“How much do you really own?” asked Nucingen.

“Three millions, excluding my house and estate, which I shall not sell; in fact, I cannot, for the property is now entailed and goes with the title.”

Nucingen and du Tillet exchanged a sly glance; du Tillet said to Philippe, “My dear count, I shall be delighted to do business with you.”

De Marsay intercepted the look, which meant, “We will have those millions.” The two bank magnates were central in political affairs and could, at a given time, manipulate matters at the Bourse to play a sure game against Philippe when probabilities seemed for him but were secretly against him.

The occasion came. In July, 1830, du Tillet and Nucingen had helped the Comte de Brambourg make fifteen hundred thousand francs; he trusted them. Philippe, owing his rise to the Restoration, was misled by profound contempt for “civilians”; he believed in the triumph of the Ordonnances and was bent on playing for a rise; du Tillet and Nucingen, sure of a revolution, played against him for a fall. The crafty pair confirmed his judgment and seemed to share his convictions; they encouraged his hopes of doubling his millions and apparently took steps to help. Philippe fought like a man with four millions depending on the struggle. His devotion was so noticeable that he received orders to go to Saint-Cloud with the Duc de Maufrigneuse and attend a council. This mark probably saved his life; for when the order came on July 25th he was intending to charge and sweep the boulevards, where he would undoubtedly have been shot by his friend Giroudeau, who commanded a division of attackers.

A month later nothing was left of Colonel Bridau’s immense fortune but his house and furniture, his estates, and the pictures from Issoudun. He committed the folly of believing in the Restoration of the elder branch, to which he remained faithful until 1834. Jealous of Giroudeau’s colonelcy, he re-entered the service. Unluckily, in 1835 he obtained command of a regiment in Algeria, where he remained three dangerous years, always hoping for a general’s epaulets. But some malignant influence—General Giroudeau—continually balked him. Grown hard and brutal, Philippe exceeded ordinary severity and was hated, despite bravery à la Murat.

At the beginning of 1839, while making a dash upon Arabs during a retreat before superior forces, he flung himself against the enemy with only a single company and fell in with the main body. The battle was bloody and terrible, man to man, and few horsemen escaped. Seeing their colonel surrounded, these men, at some distance, were unwilling to perish attempting to rescue him. They heard his cry: “Your colonel! to me! a colonel of the Empire!” but they rejoined the regiment. Philippe met a horrible death: the Arabs hacked him to pieces with scimitars and cut off his head.

Joseph, who was married about this time through Comte de Serizy to the daughter of a millionaire farmer, inherited his brother’s Paris house and the estate of Brambourg by the entail, which Philippe, had he foreseen this, would have broken. The chief pleasure Joseph derived from his inheritance was the fine collection of paintings from Issoudun. He now possesses an income of sixty thousand francs, and his father-in-law continues to pile up five-franc pieces. Though Joseph Bridau paints magnificent pictures and renders important services to artists, he is not yet a member of the Institute. As result of a clause in the deed of entail, he is now Comte de Brambourg, a fact which often makes him roar with laughter among his atelier friends.

DEDICATION

To Madame la Duchesse de Castries.

THE ILLUSTRIOUS GAUDISSART

CHAPTER I

The commercial traveller, a personage unknown to antiquity, is one of the striking figures created by the manners and customs of our present epoch. May he not, in some conceivable order of things, be destined to mark for coming philosophers the great transition which welds a period of material enterprise to the period of intellectual strength? Our century will bind the realm of isolated power, abounding in creative genius, to the realm of universal but levelling might; equalizing products, spreading them among the masses, and being itself controlled by the principle of unity—the final expression of all societies. Do we not find the dead level of barbarism succeeding the saturnalia of popular thought and the last struggles of civilizations which accumulated the treasures of the world in one direction?

The commercial traveller! Is he not to the realm of ideas what our stage-coaches are to men and things? He is their vehicle; he sets them going, carries them along, rubs them up with one another. He takes from the luminous centre a handful of light, and scatters it broadcast among the drowsy populations of the duller regions. This human pyrotechnic is a scholar without learning, a juggler hoaxed by himself, an unbelieving priest of mysteries and dogmas which he expounds all the better for his want of faith. Curious being! He has seen everything, known everything, and is up in all the ways of the world. Soaked in the vices of Paris, he affects to be the fellow-well-met of the provinces. He is the link which connects the village with the capital; though essentially neither Parisian nor provincial—he is a traveller. He sees nothing to the core: men and places he knows by their names; as for things, he looks merely at their surface, and he has his little tape-line with which to measure them. His glance shoots over all things and penetrates none. He occupies himself with a great deal, yet nothing occupies him.

Jester and jolly fellow, he keeps on good terms with all political opinions, and is patriotic to the bottom of his soul. A capital mimic, he knows how to turn on the smiles of persuasion, satisfaction, and good-nature, or drop them for the normal expression of his natural man. He is compelled to be an observer of a certain sort in the interests of his trade. He must probe men with a glance and guess their habits, wants, and above all their solvency. To economize time he must come to quick decisions as to his chances of success—a practice that makes him more or less a man of judgment; on the strength of which he sets up as a judge of theatres, and discourses about those of Paris and the provinces.

He knows all the good and bad haunts in France, “de actu et visu.” He can pilot you, on occasion, to vice or virtue with equal assurance. Blest with the eloquence of a hot-water spigot turned on at will, he can check or let run, without floundering, the collection of phrases which he keeps on tap, and which produce upon his victims the effect of a moral shower-bath. Loquacious as a cricket, he smokes, drinks, wears a profusion of trinkets, overawes the common people, passes for a lord in the villages, and never permits himself to be “stumped,”—a slang expression all his own. He knows how to slap his pockets at the right time, and make his money jingle if he thinks the servants of the second-class houses which he wants to enter (always eminently suspicious) are likely to take him for a thief. Activity is not the least surprising quality of this human machine. Not the hawk swooping upon its prey, not the stag doubling before the huntsman and the hounds, nor the hounds themselves catching scent of the game, can be compared with him for the rapidity of his dart when he spies a “commission,” for the agility with which he trips up a rival and gets ahead of him, for the keenness of his scent as he noses a customer and discovers the sport where he can get off his wares.

How many great qualities must such a man possess! You will find in all countries many such diplomats of low degree; consummate negotiators arguing in the interests of calico, jewels, frippery, wines; often displaying more true diplomacy than ambassadors themselves, who, for the most part, know only the forms of it. No one in France can doubt the powers of the commercial traveller; that intrepid soul who dares all, and boldly brings the genius of civilization and the modern inventions of Paris into a struggle with the plain commonsense of remote villages, and the ignorant and boorish treadmill of provincial ways. Can we ever forget the skilful manoeuvres by which he worms himself into the minds of the populace, bringing a volume of words to bear upon the refractory, reminding us of the indefatigable worker in marbles whose file eats slowly into a block of porphyry? Would you seek to know the utmost power of language, or the strongest pressure that a phrase can bring to bear against rebellious lucre, against the miserly proprietor squatting in the recesses of his country lair?—listen to one of these great ambassadors of Parisian industry as he revolves and works and sucks like an intelligent piston of the steam-engine called Speculation.

“Monsieur,” said a wise political economist, the director-cashier-manager and secretary-general of a celebrated fire-insurance company, “out of every five hundred thousand francs of policies to be renewed in the provinces, not more than fifty thousand are paid up voluntarily. The other four hundred and fifty thousand are got in by the activity of our agents, who go about among those who are in arrears and worry them with stories of horrible incendiaries until they are driven to sign the new policies. Thus you see that eloquence, the labial flux, is nine tenths of the ways and means of our business.”

To talk, to make people listen to you—that is seduction in itself. A nation that has two Chambers, a woman who lends both ears, are soon lost. Eve and her serpent are the everlasting myth of an hourly fact which began, and may end, with the world itself.

“A conversation of two hours ought to capture your man,” said a retired lawyer.

Let us walk round the commercial traveller, and look at him well. Don’t forget his overcoat, olive green, nor his cloak with its morocco collar, nor the striped blue cotton shirt. In this queer figure—so original that we cannot rub it out—how many divers personalities we come across! In the first place, what an acrobat, what a circus, what a battery, all in one, is the man himself, his vocation, and his tongue! Intrepid mariner, he plunges in, armed with a few phrases, to catch five or six thousand francs in the frozen seas, in the domain of the red Indians who inhabit the interior of France. The provincial fish will not rise to harpoons and torches; it can only be taken with seines and nets and gentlest persuasions. The traveller’s business is to extract the gold in country caches by a purely intellectual operation, and to extract it pleasantly and without pain. Can you think without a shudder of the flood of phrases which, day by day, renewed each dawn, leaps in cascades the length and breadth of sunny France?

You know the species; let us now take a look at the individual.

There lives in Paris an incomparable commercial traveller, the paragon of his race, a man who possesses in the highest degree all the qualifications necessary to the nature of his success. His speech is vitriol and likewise glue—glue to catch and entangle his victim and make him sticky and easy to grip; vitriol to dissolve hard heads, close fists, and closer calculations. His line was once the hat; but his talents and the art with which he snared the wariest provincial had brought him such commercial celebrity that all vendors of the “article Paris” paid court to him, and humbly begged that he would deign to take their commissions.

Thus, when he returned to Paris in the intervals of his triumphant progress through France, he lived a life of perpetual festivity in the shape of weddings and suppers. When he was in the provinces, the correspondents in the smaller towns made much of him; in Paris, the great houses feted and caressed him. Welcomed, flattered, and fed wherever he went, it came to pass that to breakfast or to dine alone was a novelty, an event. He lived the life of a sovereign, or, better still, of a journalist; in fact, he was the perambulating “feuilleton” of Parisian commerce.

His name was Gaudissart; and his renown, his vogue, the flatteries showered upon him, were such as to win for him the surname of Illustrious. Wherever the fellow went—behind a counter or before a bar, into a salon or to the top of a stage-coach, up to a garret or to dine with a banker—everyone said, the moment they saw him, “Ah! here comes the illustrious Gaudissart!” No name was ever so in keeping with the style, the manners, the countenance, the voice, the language, of any man. All things smiled upon our traveller, and the traveller smiled back in return. “Similia similibus”—he believed in homoeopathy. Puns, horse-laugh, monkish face, skin of a friar, true Rabelaisian exterior, clothing, body, mind, and features, all pulled together to put a devil-may-care jollity into every inch of his person. Free-handed and easy-going, he might be recognized at once as the favourite of grisettes, the man who jumps lightly to the top of a stage-coach, gives a hand to the timid lady who fears to step down, jokes with the postillion about his neckerchief and contrives to sell him a cap, smiles at the maid and catches her round the waist or by the heart; gurgles at dinner like a bottle of wine and pretends to draw the cork by sounding a filip on his distended cheek; plays a tune with his knife on the champagne glasses without breaking them, and says to the company, “Let me see you do _that_”; chaffs the timid traveller, contradicts the knowing one, lords it over a dinner-table and manages to get the titbits for himself. A strong fellow, nevertheless, he can throw aside all this nonsense and mean business when he flings away the stump of his cigar and says, with a glance at some town, “I’ll go and see what those people have got in their stomachs.”

When buckled down to his work he became the slyest and cleverest of diplomats. All things to all men, he knew how to accost a banker like a capitalist, a magistrate like a functionary, a royalist with pious and monarchical sentiments, a bourgeois as one of themselves. In short, wherever he was he was just what he ought to be; he left Gaudissart at the door when he went in, and picked him up when he came out.

Until 1830 the illustrious Gaudissart was faithful to the article Paris. In his close relation to the caprices of humanity, the varied paths of commerce had enabled him to observe the windings of the heart of man. He had learned the secret of persuasive eloquence, the knack of loosening the tightest purse-strings, the art of rousing desire in the souls of husbands, wives, children, and servants; and what is more, he knew how to satisfy it. No one had greater faculty than he for inveigling a merchant by the charms of a bargain, and disappearing at the instant when desire had reached its crisis. Full of gratitude to the hat-making trade, he always declared that it was his efforts in behalf of the exterior of the human head which had enabled him to understand its interior: he had capped and crowned so many people, he was always flinging himself at their heads, etc. His jokes about hats and heads were irrepressible, though perhaps not dazzling.

Nevertheless, after August and October, 1830, he abandoned the hat trade and the article Paris, and tore himself from things mechanical and visible to mount into the higher spheres of Parisian speculation. “He forsook,” to use his own words, “matter for mind; manufactured products for the infinitely purer elaborations of human intelligence.” This requires some explanation.

The general upset of 1830 brought to birth, as everybody knows, a number of old ideas which clever speculators tried to pass off in new bodies. After 1830 ideas became property. A writer, too wise to publish his writings, once remarked that “more ideas are stolen than pocket-handkerchiefs.” Perhaps in course of time we may have an Exchange for thought; in fact, even now ideas, good or bad, have their consols, are bought up, imported, exported, sold, and quoted like stocks. If ideas are not on hand ready for sale, speculators try to pass off words in their stead, and actually live upon them as a bird lives on the seeds of his millet. Pray do not laugh; a word is worth quite as much as an idea in a land where the ticket on a sack is of more importance than the contents. Have we not seen libraries working off the word “picturesque” when literature would have cut the throat of the word “fantastic”? Fiscal genius has guessed the proper tax on intellect; it has accurately estimated the profits of advertising; it has registered a prospectus of the quantity and exact value of the property, weighing its thought at the intellectual Stamp Office in the Rue de la Paix.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Having become an article of commerce, intellect and its products must obey the laws that bind other manufactured interests. Thus ideas, conceived by idle Parisians—who nevertheless fight many a moral battle over champagne and pheasants—are handed at birth from brain to commercial travellers employed to spread them “urbi et orbi,” seasoned with advertisement and prospectus, by which they catch in their rat-trap the departmental rodent called subscriber, sometimes stockholder, occasionally corresponding member or patron, but invariably fool.

“I am a fool!” many a poor country proprietor has said, when, caught by the prospect of being the first to launch a new idea, he finds that he has launched his thousand or twelve hundred francs into a gulf.

“Subscribers are fools who never can be brought to understand that to go ahead in the intellectual world they must start with more money than they need for the tour of Europe,” say the speculators.

Consequently there is endless warfare between the recalcitrant public, which refuses to pay Parisian imposts, and the tax-gatherer who, living by his receipt of custom, lards the public with new ideas, turns it on the spit of lively projects, roasts it with prospectuses, basting with flattery, and finally gobbles it up with some toothsome sauce. Moreover, since 1830 what honors and emoluments have been scattered throughout France to stimulate the zeal and self-love of the “progressive and intelligent masses”! Titles, medals, diplomas, a sort of legion of honor for martyrs, have followed each other rapidly. Speculators in the manufactured products of the intellect have developed a spice, a ginger, all their own. From this have come premiums, forestalled dividends, and that conscription of noted names levied without the knowledge of the unfortunate writers who bear them, who thus find themselves associated with more enterprises than there are days in the year; the law takes no account of the theft of a patronymic. Worse is the rape of ideas which these caterers for the public mind, like slave-merchants, tear from the parental brain before they are matured, and drag half-clothed before the eyes of their sultan—the terrible public—which, if they don’t amuse it, will cut off their heads by curtailing the ingots and emptying their pockets.

This madness of our epoch reacted upon the illustrious Gaudissart, and here follows how it happened. A life-insurance company, having been told of his irresistible eloquence, offered him an unheard-of commission, which he accepted. The bargain concluded, our traveller was put in training by the secretary-general, who schooled him in the business: its dark holes, its dialect, the particular public to be gullible, impromptu replies, and unanswerable arguments—sharpening the file of the tongue which was about to operate upon the life of France.

The puppet amply rewarded the pains bestowed upon him. The heads of the company boasted of the illustrious Gaudissart so loudly that the financial managers of two then-prominent newspapers—since defunct—were seized with the idea of employing him to get subscribers. The proprietors of the “Globe,” an organ of Saint-Simonism, and the “Movement,” a republican journal, each invited him to a conference and proposed paying ten francs a head for every subscriber if he brought in a thousand, but only five francs if he got five hundred. The cause of political journalism did not interfere with his pre-accepted life-insurance work; the bargain was struck. Gaudissart even demanded indemnity from the Saint-Simonians for eight days spent studying their doctrines, asserting a prodigious effort of memory and intellect was necessary to get to the bottom of that “article,” though he asked nothing from the republicans. He inclined to republican ideas and had already dipped into conspiracies; he had been arrested and released for want of proof; and, as he told the newspaper proprietors, he had lately grown a mustache and needed only a hat of certain shape and a pair of spurs to represent the Republic suitably.

CHAPTER II

For a week this commanding genius went every morning to be Saint-Simonized at the “Globe,” and every afternoon to the life-insurance company, where he learned financial diplomacy. His aptitude and memory were prodigious; by April 15, the usual opening of the spring campaign, he could set out. Two large commercial houses, alarmed at declining business, begged him not to desert the article Paris and seduced him with large offers. The king of travellers yielded; enormous premiums and old friendships swayed him.

“Listen, my little Jenny,” he said in a hackney-coach to a pretty florist.

All truly great men delight in allowing themselves to be tyrannized over by a feeble being, and Gaudissart had found his tyrant in Jenny. He was bringing her home at eleven from the Gymnase, where he had taken her in full dress to a first-tier box.

“On my return, Jenny, I shall refurnish your room in superior style. That big Matilda, who pesters you with comparisons and her India shawls, won’t have a word to say then. I consecrate to the adornment of your room all the ‘Children’ I shall get in the provinces.”

“That’s a pretty thing to say! Monster of a man! Do you dare to talk to me of your children? Do you suppose I am going to stand that sort of thing?”

“Oh, what a goose you are, my Jenny! That’s only a figure of speech in our business.”

“A fine business, then!”

“Well, but listen; if you talk all the time you’ll always be in the right.”

“I mean to be. You take things easy!”

“You don’t let me finish. I have taken under my protection a superlative idea—a newspaper written for children. In our profession, when travellers have caught, say, ten subscribers to the ‘Children’s Journal,’ they say, ‘I’ve got ten Children,’ just as I say when I get ten subscriptions to the ‘Movement,’ ‘I’ve got ten Movements.’ Now don’t you see?”

“That’s all right. Are you going into politics? If you do you’ll get into Saint-Pelagie, and I shall have to trot down there after you. Oh! if one only knew what one puts one’s foot into when we love a man, by my word of honor we would let you alone to take care of yourselves, you men! However, if you are going away to-morrow we won’t talk of disagreeable things—that would be silly.”

The coach stopped before a pretty fourth-story house in the Rue d’Artois, the abode of Mademoiselle Jenny Courand, reputed privately married to the illustrious Gaudissart, a rumor he did not deny. To maintain her supremacy, Jenny required innumerable attentions and threatened to dismiss him for the slightest omission. She ordered him to write from every town and render a minute account of his proceedings.

“How many ‘Children’ will it take to furnish my chamber?” she asked, throwing off her shawl by a good fire.

“I get five sous for each subscriber.”

“Delightful! And is it with five sous that you expect to make me rich? Perhaps you are like the Wandering Jew with your pockets full of money.”

“But, Jenny, I shall get a thousand ‘Children.’ Think that children have never had a newspaper to themselves before. But what a fool I am to try to explain matters to you—you can’t understand such things.”

“Can’t I? Then tell me—tell me, Gaudissart, if I’m such a goose why do you love me?”

“Just because you are a goose—a sublime goose! Listen, Jenny. I am going to undertake the ‘Globe,’ the ‘Movement,’ the ‘Children,’ the insurance business, and some of my old article Paris; instead of earning a miserable eight thousand a year, I’ll bring back twenty thousand at least from each trip.”

“Unlace me, Gaudissart, and do it right; don’t tighten me.”

“Yes, truly,” said the traveller complacently. “I shall become a shareholder in the newspapers, like Finot, one of my friends, the son of a hatter, who now has thirty thousand francs income, and is going to make himself a peer of France. When one thinks of that little Popinot—ah, mon Dieu! I forgot to tell you that Monsieur Popinot was named minister of commerce yesterday. Why shouldn’t I be ambitious too? Ha! ha! I could easily pick up the jargon of those fellows who talk in the chamber, and bluster with the rest of them. Now, listen to me:—

“Gentlemen,” he said, standing behind a chair, “the Press is neither a tool nor an article of barter: it is, under its political aspects, an institution. We are bound, as legislators, to consider all things politically, and therefore we must examine the Press and ask if it is useful or noxious, if it should be encouraged or taxed or free. These are serious questions. I feel that I do not waste the time of this Chamber by examining this article—the Press—and explaining to you its qualities. We are on the verge of an abyss. Undoubtedly the laws have not the nap they ought to have—Hein?” he said, looking at Jenny. “All orators put France on the verge of an abyss. Don’t I know their dodges? I’m up to all the tricks of all the trades. Do you know why? Because I was born with a caul; my mother has got it, but I’ll give it to you. You’ll see! I shall soon be in the government.”

“You!”

“Why shouldn’t I be the Baron Gaudissart, peer of France? Haven’t they twice elected Monsieur Popinot as deputy? He dines with Louis-Philippe. There’s Finot; he is going to be, they say, a member of the Council. Suppose they send me as ambassador to London? I tell you I’d nonplus those English! No man ever got the better of Gaudissart, and nobody ever will. Yes, I say it! No one ever outwitted me, and no one can—in any walk of life, politics or impolitics. But, for the time being, I must give myself wholly to the capitalists; to the ‘Globe,’ the ‘Movement,’ the ‘Children,’ and my article Paris.”

“You will be brought up with a round turn, you and your newspapers. I’ll bet you won’t get further than Poitiers before the police will nab you.”

“What will you bet?”

“A shawl.”

“Done! If I lose that shawl I’ll go back to the article Paris and the hat business. But as for getting the better of Gaudissart—never! never!”

And the illustrious traveller threw himself into a Napoleonic attitude. Gaudissart was thirty-eight, of medium height, stout and fat like men who roll about in stage-coaches, with a round face, ruddy cheeks, and regular features of the type sculptors adopt as models for Abundance, Law, Force, Commerce. His stomach swelled in a pear shape; he caught Jenny up and kissed her.

“Hold your tongue, young woman!” he said. “What do you know about Saint-Simonism, Fourierism, heroic enterprise, or woman’s freedom? I’ll tell you what they are—ten francs for each subscription, Madame Gaudissart.”

“On my word of honor, you are going crazy, Gaudissart.”

“More and more crazy about you,” he replied, flinging his hat upon the sofa.

The next morning, after a glorious breakfast with Jenny, Gaudissart departed on horseback to work the chief towns of his district, assigned by his various enterprises. After forty-five days beating the country between Paris and Blois, he remained two weeks at Blois to write correspondence and make short visits. The night before leaving Blois for Tours he indited a letter to Mademoiselle Jenny Courand which proves their tie and his triumphs: he boasted of lodging two millions between Paris and Blois, selling many Ternaux shawls at Orléans, lamented that the “Movement” did not move the republicans, recounted making a hundred “Globes,” and rejoiced at getting two thousand “Children” subscriptions. He described cajoling a dynastic diner into subscribing to the “Children’s Journal” rather than quarrel, and promised to do Amboise in two days and “topple” Tours.

CHAPTER III

Five days later Gaudissart started from the Hotel des Faisans in Tours and went to Vouvray, a rich district where the public mind seemed susceptible. Trotting along the embankment, he admired the landscape, little dreaming his commercial infallibility was about to perish in Vouvray.

A note on the Tourangian mind: stamped by Rabelais’ spirit, Touraine’s people are polished, witty, poetic, voluptuous, yet their impulses subside quickly. The softness of the atmosphere, ease of life, and jovial manners smother sentiment of art and enervate will. At home the Tourangian sits like an Indian on his mat or a Turk on his divan, employing wit in laughing at neighbors and making merry all his days. Touraine is like Rabelais’ Abbaye of Theleme—good cheer reigns.

The illustrious Gaudissart encountered in Vouvray one of those indigenous jesters whose wit is perfection. The Tourangians especially hated and villified Saint-Simonian doctrines, their hatred taking the form of superb disdain and witty maliciousness. For his sins, after arriving and consulting the landlord Mitouflet, Gaudissart betook himself to the town’s merry-maker, a former dyer named Monsieur Vernier, influential among the small proprietors, a leader of gossip and squabbles.

“Monsieur Vernier,” Gaudissart began, “I come to solicit your aid. I am sent into the provinces on an enterprise of the utmost importance, undertaken by bankers who—”

“Who mean to win our tricks,” said Vernier, used to travellers.

“Precisely,” replied Gaudissart. “But with your fine tact, Monsieur, you must be aware we can’t win tricks unless it is their interest to play at cards. I am no longer a common traveller. The most distinguished bankers in Paris take part in this affair; the most respectable houses are concerned, and their interests guarantee—” and Gaudissart drew forth his string of phrases.

Vernier let him go the length of his tether, listening with apparent interest which completely deceived him. But after “guarantee” Vernier ceased attention and plotted to play him a malicious trick: to fetch the town’s supposed banker—Margaritis, a half-insane man—and have Gaudissart deal with him.

At the head of the Valley Coquette lived Margaritis, of Italian origin, half-insane, married, childless, cared for by a devoted wife. He carried two long-bladed knives, sometimes threatened his wife, and insisted on keeping a sack of flour and two puncheons of wine in the cellar. He made incessant demands for newspapers and, for seven years, read the same old “Indre-et-Loire” without perceiving the repetition. When company came he often sat silent, then at ten o’clock on a certain clock he would rise with mechanical precision and say sternly, “Go away!” He had lucid intervals when he advised on wine sales; at times he became annoying, ransacking closets and stealing delicacies. Once in five years his being told “the good-man does not hear anything” drove him to frenzy, knife flourishing.

The evening before Gaudissart reached Vouvray, Madame Margaritis had found it hard to deceive her husband, who insisted on watching his puncheons; she had begged neighbor Pierre Champlain to roll two puncheons into their cellar to quiet him. Vernier told his wife he would take Benoîte Fontanieu as witness to the scene, to spread the joke.

Vernier escorted Gaudissart, who believed he had made a conquest of both ladies with wit and humor, to La Fuye, the modest house of Margaritis. The salon opened to the field; Margaritis, seated in a yellow Utrecht-velvet arm-chair, did not stir. Madame Vernier introduced the traveller and bade him “put away your mathematics and talk.” Margaritis rose and invited Gaudissart to sit.

“Monsieur has doubtless been in business?” Gaudissart began.

“Public business,” answered Margaritis. “I pacificated Calabria under Murat.”

“In that case we shall quickly understand each other,” said Gaudissart.

Margaritis’s peculiarities—pear-shaped bald head, sunken eyes, thin nose, long chin—gave him a look between a retired professor and a rag-picker. Gaudissart, turning his watch-key with a rotatory click that kept the lunatic quiet, launched into his patter about life insurance. Time, he said, is the most precious social wealth; to economize time is to become wealthy. The bankers and capitalists who had seen the waste of rising men's time had “capitalized” future prospects, cashing talents by discounting time, representing in money the products developed by time—the moral qualities and talents as living forces. He explained with literal examples: suppose a painter has a glorious future; the company will recognize and insure his intellectual capital, paying his family in case of death; the painter pays a small annual premium, three percent, to secure his family and thus free himself of anxieties distracting him from success. Insurance, Gaudissart called it, is the exchequer of beneficence, a mutual insurance against poverty, the cashing of talent.

“That is usury!” cried Margaritis.

Gaudissart switched tack and offered wine; Margaritis poured Vouvray of 1819 and claimed it as Head of Vouvray, a wine for kings. The lunatic delighted in pointing out the etymology: capital, caput—head—the Head of Vouvray. He even offered to sell his puncheons.

Gaudissart returned to his argument: the insured man, once recognized and holding company paper as security, could borrow on it at the Bourse and among bankers; civilization denies bread to the men who give it luxury, he declared, and insurance bridges the gulf. The company also offered annuities on a sliding scale superior to tontines. Margaritis urged another glass; Gaudissart praised the wine.

When Gaudissart turned to the “Globe” and Saint-Simonian doctrine, Margaritis fell into position and professed comprehension, though answering with local touches: “Twice; on foot.” Gaudissart praised the paper as full of life, science, and as something to make time pass agreeably in the country. It cost eighty francs; Margaritis would not pay but offered seven francs for the “Children’s Journal” and consented to push the “Globe”’s interests if Gaudissart took his wine, bargaining humorously over the price. The two men strolled among the vines; the three ladies and Vernier watched, laughing at the spectacle of the urbane traveller and the lunatic discussing, gesticulating, and walking with vehemence.

Finally Gaudissart sat at a card-table and wrote an order for delivery of the two casks. Margaritis read it, counted out seven francs for the “Children’s Journal,” and gave them to the traveller. Gaudissart, a Norman who required reciprocal agreements, demanded Margaritis sign a bond to deliver the two puncheons. Margaritis did. Gaudissart left humming triumphantly.

CHAPTER V

At the Soleil d’Or Gaudissart conversed with landlord Mitouflet, an old soldier, about Margaritis. The host, who never laughed at a jest but took it with a soldier’s gravity, hinted that Margaritis was a lunatic.

“May be; the good-man knows a deal. He knows too much for other folks,” said Mitouflet.

Gaudissart, enraged when Vernier’s prank was revealed, marched to the dyer’s salon where Vernier was laughing with friends. “Monsieur,” he began, “you are a scoundrel and a blackguard; under pain of being thought a turn-key—you will give me satisfaction for the insult you dared to offer me in sending me to a lunatic! Do you hear me, Monsieur Vernier, dyer?”

Vernier, delighted at the audience, replied that it was right to make fun of a man who wanted them to feather his nest by subscribing to a paper preaching a new religion that denied inheritance. “Père Margaritis said things a great deal more sensible,” he added.

Gaudissart flew at Vernier to slap him but the crowd interposed; his wig fell on Mademoiselle Clara Vernier’s head. He declared he would be at the Soleil d’Or until morning, ready to show what giving satisfaction meant; he had “fought in July.” Vernier answered he would “fight in Vouvray,” and make the traveller stay longer than he imagined.

For the first time the prince of travellers did not dine jovially. The town was in a ferment over the duel. Madame Fontanieu and the assistant-mayor implored Mitouflet to arrange things to avoid a violent death. He took it upon himself. In the evening he brought pens and paper to Gaudissart.

“If you are going to fight to-morrow,” Mitouflet said, “you had better make some settlement of your affairs; perhaps you have letters to write. Are you a good swordsman? I have foils.” They put on foils and masks; Mitouflet’s experience won him sixty-two passes. He advised pistols, suggesting they be loaded to fire wide so both could retire with honor. Gaudissart grumbled about possibly killing the man; Mitouflet calmed him.

Next morning the adversaries met by the bridge of La Cise. Vernier nearly shot a cow; they embraced after firing in the air. “Your joke was rough, but it was a good one,” said Gaudissart; Vernier announced twenty subscriptions to the “Children’s Journal.” “Why shouldn’t we all breakfast together?” said Gaudissart. Men who fight are often those who come to good understanding.

Back at the inn Gaudissart wanted to send a summons to Margaritis for delivery of the two casks. “But he has not got them,” said Vernier. Gaudissart demanded indemnity and considered himself outwitted if he failed to pursue the claim. Madame Margaritis, alarmed, brought thirty francs to the traveller; Gaudissart accepted it and left Vouvray content with the settlement, though recognizing the region’s resistance to new ideas.

On returning from his trip through the southern departments, Gaudissart rode in a diligence and explained the enigmas of life to a young man as if to an infant. Passing Vouvray the young man exclaimed at the fine site; Gaudissart, patronizing, replied, “Yes, Monsieur, but not habitable on account of the people. You get into duels every day. Why, it is not three months since I fought one just there,” pointing to the bridge, “with a damned dyer; but I made an end of him—he bit the dust!”

Produced by John Bickers, and Dagny

THE MUSE OF THE DEPARTMENT

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by James Waring

DEDICATION

To Monsieur le Comte Ferdinand de Gramont.

MY DEAR FERDINAND,--If fortune allows these lines to endure, they will be a small return for the trouble you have taken—you, the Hozier, the Cherin, the King-at-Arms of these Studies of Life; you to whom the Navarreins, Cadignans, Langeais, Blamont-Chauvrys, Chaulieus, Arthez, Esgrignons, Mortsaufs, Valois—the hundred great names that form the Aristocracy of the “Human Comedy”—owe their mottoes and armorial bearings. Indeed, “the Armorial of the Etudes, devised by Ferdinand de Gramont, gentleman,” is a complete manual of French Heraldry, forgetting nothing, not even the arms of the Empire, and I shall preserve it as a monument of friendship and Benedictine patience. What profound knowledge of the feudal spirit is seen in the motto of the Beauseants, Pulchre sedens, melius agens; in that of the Espards, Des partem leonis; in that of the Vandenesses, Ne se vend. And what elegance in the thousand details of learned symbolism showing how far accuracy has been carried in my work, to which you, the poet, have contributed.

Your old friend,
DE BALZAC.

THE MUSE OF THE DEPARTMENT

On the skirts of Le Berry stands a town watered by the Loire that attracts the traveler’s eye. Sancerre crowns the topmost height of a chain of hills, the last of the range that gives variety to the Nivernais. The Loire floods the flats at the foot of these slopes, leaving a yellow alluvium fertile, except where sand from its terrible risings—like those of the Vistula—has spoiled them.

The hill on which Sancerre's houses are grouped lies far from the river, so the little port Saint-Thibault thrives on Sancerre’s life. There wine is shipped and oak staves are landed, with produce from the upper and lower Loire. By the time this story begins the suspension bridges at Cosne and Saint-Thibault had been built, so travelers from Paris by the southern road were no longer ferried across. This shows the great cross-shuffle of 1830 was past; the House of Orleans had long favored substantial improvements, though somewhat like a husband spending his wife’s marriage portion.

Except on the little plateau, the streets are steep; the town is surrounded by slopes called the Great Ramparts, the highroads of the place.

Outside the ramparts lies a belt of vineyards. Wine is the chief industry, yielding aromatic vintages near Burgundy’s so that the vulgar palate is deceived. Thus Sancerre finds in Paris wine-shops the quick market indispensable for liquor that will not keep more than seven or eight years. Below lie a few villages—Fontenoy and Saint-Satur—almost suburbs, recalling the smiling vineyards about Neuchâtel in Switzerland.

The town still bears much of its ancient aspect: narrow streets paved with pebbles carted up from the Loire, and some old houses remain. The citadel, relic of feudal power, endured one of the most terrible sieges of our religious wars, when French Calvinists outdid the ferocious Cameronians of Walter Scott’s tales.

Sancerre, rich in a greater past but widowed of military importance, is doomed to a less glorious future, for trade runs on the Loire’s right bank. The sketch shows it will be left lonelier despite two bridges to Cosne.

Sancerre, the pride of the left bank, numbers at most three thousand five hundred inhabitants, while Cosne has now more than six thousand. Within half a century the roles of these opposite towns have reversed. The advantage of situation remains with the historic town, whence the view is enchanting, the air pure, vegetation splendid, and residents—friendly, in harmony with nature, devoid of Puritanism, though two-thirds are Calvinists. Under such conditions, despite small-town disadvantages and the officious eye that makes private life almost public, the spirit of township—a sort of patriotism, though not a substitute for love of home—flourishes.

Thus Sancerre is proud to have given birth to Horace Bianchon, a glory of modern medicine, and Etienne Lousteau, an author of secondary rank and one of our most successful journalists. The district under Sancerre, distressed to find itself ruled by seven or eight large landowners—the wire-pullers of elections—tried to shake off the electoral yoke of a creed that had reduced it to a rotten borough. This little conspiracy, plotted by a handful whose vanity was provoked, failed because jealousy of one man’s inevitable elevation roused the others. This showed the scheme’s radical defect; the suggested remedy was to rally round a champion at the next election—one of the two men who gloriously represented Sancerre in Paris.

This idea was advanced for the provinces; since 1830 nomination of parochial dignitaries has increased so much that real statesmen have become rare in the lower chamber. In fact, this doubtful plan was hatched by the Superior Woman of the borough, dux femina fasti, for personal interest. It was so rooted in her past and crucial to her future prospects that it can scarcely be understood without a sketch of her antecedent career.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Sancerre at that time could boast of a Superior Woman, long misprized but now, about 1836, enjoying an extensive local reputation. Two Sancerrois in Paris were also attaining, each in his line, to high distinction and fashion. Etienne Lousteau wrote reviews for a paper with eight thousand subscribers; and Bianchon, already chief physician to a hospital, Officer of the Legion of Honor, and member of the Academy of Sciences, had just been made a professor.

If the word did not to many readers imply blame, one might say George Sand created “Sandism,” so true is it that morally every good has a reverse. Sandism’s sentimentality can be trying, yet it has a good side: the woman it attacks bases her superiority on scorned feelings; she is a blue-stocking of sentiment and somewhat less of a bore, love partly neutralizing literature. The chief result of George Sand’s celebrity was to reveal that France has an enormous number of superior women who, until now, generously left the field to the Maréchal de Saxe’s granddaughter.

The Superior Woman of Sancerre lived at La Baudraye, a town- and country-house within ten minutes of the town, in the village or suburb of Saint-Satur. The present La Baudrayes, as often happens, have thrust themselves in and substitute for those La Baudrayes whose name, glorious in the Crusades, figured in Le Berry’s history.

The story must be told.

In the time of Louis XIV a sheriff named Milaud, whose forefathers had been fervent Calvinists, was converted at the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes. To encourage conversion in a Calvinist stronghold, the King gave Milaud an appointment in the “Waters and Forests,” granted him arms, the title Sire de la Baudraye, and the fief of the old La Baudrayes. The descendants of the famous Captain la Baudraye later fell into one of the snares laid for heretics by the new decrees and were hanged—an unworthy deed of the great King.

Under Louis XV Milaud de la Baudraye rose from squire to Chevalier and had influence enough to obtain for his son a cornet’s commission in the Musketeers. This officer perished at Fontenoy, leaving a child to whom Louis XVI subsequently granted, by patent, the privileges of a farmer-general in remembrance of his father’s death.

This financier, a fashionable wit great at charades, capping verses and posies to Chloris, lived in society, clung to the Duc de Nivernais, and fancied he must follow the nobility into exile; but he took care to carry his money with him. Thus the rich émigré was able to assist more than one family of high rank.

In 1800, tired of hoping and perhaps of lending, he returned to Sancerre and bought back La Baudraye out of vanity and imagined pride—quite intelligible in a sheriff’s grandson—though under the Consulate his prospects were slender; all the more so because the ex-farmer-general had small hopes that his heir would perpetuate the new race of La Baudraye.

Jean Athanase Polydore Milaud de la Baudraye, his only son, delicate from birth, was plainly the child of a man whose constitution had been exhausted by excesses and late marriage, bringing degeneracy into high society. During the emigration Madame de la Baudraye, a girl of no fortune chosen for her birth, patiently reared this sallow, sickly boy with the devoted love mothers show such changelings. Her death—she was a Casteran de la Tour—contributed to Monsieur de la Baudraye’s return to France.

This Lucullus of the Milauds, when he died, left his son the fief, stripped indeed of its fines and dues, but graced with weathercocks bearing his coat-of-arms, a thousand louis-d’or, and certain receipts for claims on very distinguished émigrés enclosed in a pocketbook full of verses, the wrapper inscribed Vanitas vanitatum et omnia vanitas.

Young La Baudraye did not die; he owed his life to habits of monastic strictness, to the economy of action Fontenelle preached as the religion of the invalid, and, above all, to the air of Sancerre and the influence of its fine elevation, whence a panorama over the Loire valley extends for forty leagues.

From 1802 to 1815 young La Baudraye added several plots to his vineyards and devoted himself to the culture of the vine. The Restoration at first seemed so insecure that he dared not go to Paris to claim his debts; but after Napoleon’s death he tried to turn his father’s collection of autographs into money, though he did not understand the philosophy that mixed IOUs and copies of verses. But the winegrower wasted time trying to impress his identity on the Duke of Nivernais “and others,” and returned to Sancerre to his vintage without having obtained anything but offers of service.

The Restoration raised the nobility to such lustre that La Baudraye wished to justify his ambitions by having an heir. This result of matrimony he considered doubtful, or he would not have so long postponed the step; however, finding himself still above ground in 1823 at forty-three—a length of years which no doctor, astrologer, or midwife would have dared promise—he hoped to earn the reward of his sober life. Yet his choice showed such lack of prudence regarding his frail constitution that the malicious wit of a country town suspected some deep calculation.

At that time Monseigneur the Archbishop of Bourges had converted to Catholicism a young woman, the daughter of one of the citizen families that had upheld Calvinism and, thanks to obscurity or compromise, escaped persecution under Louis XIV. The Piedefers—respectable cloth merchants who had once supported the Reformation—had suffered reverses. In Louis XVI’s reign Abraham Piedefer fell into difficulties and at his death in 1786 left his two children in extreme poverty. One, Tobie Piedefer, went to the Indies, leaving the meagre inheritance to his elder brother. During the Revolution Moise Piedefer bought nationalized land, pulled down abbeys and churches with ancestral zeal, and married a Catholic, the only daughter of a member of the Convention who had perished on the scaffold. This ambitious Piedefer died in 1819, leaving a little girl of remarkable beauty. Brought up in the Calvinist faith, she was named Dinah, following the sect’s custom of choosing Biblical rather than Roman Catholic names.

Mademoiselle Dinah Piedefer was placed by her mother in the Demoiselles Chamarolles’ school in Bourges and was soon distinguished for mind as well as beauty; but she was snubbed by girls of birth and fortune destined to play greater parts in the world than a plebeian’s daughter dependent on the settlement of the Piedefer estate. Having raised herself above her companions, Dinah aimed to remain on a level with them for life. She determined, therefore, to renounce Calvinism in the hope that the Cardinal would favor his proselyte and interest himself in her prospects. You may judge of Mademoiselle Dinah’s superiority, since at seventeen she converted solely from ambition.

The Archbishop, convinced Dinah would adorn society, was anxious to see her married. But every family to whom he made advances recoiled from a damsel with the looks of a princess, reputed the cleverest of Mademoiselle Chamarolles’ pupils and who always shone at the prize-giving. A thousand crowns a year, the share she might hope from La Hautoy when divided between mother and daughter, would be trifling compared to the expenses a husband would incur by the personal advantages of so brilliant a creature.

As soon as these facts reached little Polydore de la Baudraye—they were the talk of every circle in the Cher—he went to Bourges just when Madame Piedefer, a devotee at high services, had almost made up her mind and her daughter’s to take the first comer with well-lined pockets—the first chien coiffé, as they say in Le Berry. If the Cardinal was delighted to receive Monsieur de la Baudraye, Monsieur de la Baudraye was even more pleased to receive a wife from the Cardinal’s hands. The little gentleman only demanded a formal promise that His Eminence would support his claims with the President of the Council to enable him to recover his debts from the Duc de Navarreins “and others” by a lien on their indemnities. This method, however, seemed to the Minister then occupying the Pavillon Marsan too sharp, and he gave the vine-owner to understand his business would be attended to in good time.

It is easy to imagine the excitement produced in the Sancerre district by news of Monsieur de la Baudraye’s imprudent marriage.

“It is quite intelligible,” said President Boirouge; “the little man was very much startled, I am told, at hearing handsome young Milaud, the Attorney-General’s deputy at Nevers, say to Monsieur de Clagny as they looked at La Baudraye’s turrets, ‘That will be mine some day.’—‘But,’ says Clagny, ‘he may marry and have children.’—‘Impossible!’—So you may imagine how such a changeling as little La Baudraye must hate that colossal Milaud.”

At Nevers there was a plebeian branch of the Milauds, grown rich in the cutlery trade, that had brought its representative up to the civil service under Marchangy, now dead.

It will be useful to set aside the baser material interests that occupied Monsieur de la Baudraye, by briefly relating his negotiations in Paris. This will also shed light on certain mysterious contemporary political phenomena that hampered the Ministry during the Restoration.

The Ministers’ promises were so illusory that Monsieur de la Baudraye decided to go to Paris when the Cardinal’s presence was required there by the Chambers’ sitting.

This is how the Duc de Navarreins, the principal debtor threatened by Monsieur de la Baudraye, got out of the scrape.

The country gentleman, lodging at the Hotel de Mayence, Rue Saint-Honoré near the Place Vendôme, one morning received a visit from a confidential agent of the Ministry, an expert in “winding up” business. This elegant person, stepping from an elegant cab and neatly dressed, was shown to the third floor, to a small room where he found his provincial concocting coffee over a bedroom fire.

“Is it to Monsieur Milaud de la Baudraye that I have the honor—”

“Yes,” said the little man, draping himself in his dressing-gown.

After examining the garment—an illicit offspring of an old china wrapper of Madame Piedefer’s and a gown of the late Madame de la Baudraye—the emissary considered the man, the dressing-gown, and the tin saucepan on the little stove so characteristic that he deemed it needless to beat about the bush.

“I will lay a wager, monsieur,” said he audaciously, “that you dine for forty sous at Hurbain’s in the Palais-Royal.”

“Pray, why?”

“Oh, I know you, having seen you there,” replied the Parisian gravely. “All the princes’ creditors dine there. You know you recover scarcely ten per cent on debts from these fine gentlemen. I would not give you five per cent on a debt to be recovered from the estate of the late Duc d’Orléans—nor even,” he added in a low voice, “from MONSIEUR.”

“So you have come to buy up the bills?” said La Baudraye, thinking himself clever.

“Buy them!” said his visitor. “Why, what do you take me for? I am Monsieur des Lupeaulx, Master of Appeals, Secretary-General to the Ministry, and I have come to propose an arrangement.”

“What is that?”

“Of course, monsieur, you know the position of your debtor—”

“Of my debtors—”

“Well, monsieur, you understand their position; they stand high in the King’s good graces but have no money and must make a good show. Again, you know the political difficulties. The aristocracy must be rehabilitated against a strong third estate. The King’s idea—of which France does him scant justice—is to create a peerage as a national institution analogous to the English peerage. To realize this we need years—and millions. Noblesse oblige. The Duc de Navarreins, who is, as you know, first gentleman of the Bedchamber to the King, does not repudiate his debt, but he cannot—Now, be reasonable—consider politics. We are emerging from the pit of the Revolution—and you yourself are noble—He simply cannot pay—”

“Monsieur—”

“You are hasty,” said des Lupeaulx. “Listen. He cannot pay in money. Well then; you, a clever man, can take payment in favors—royal or ministerial.”

“What! When in 1793 my father put down one hundred thousand—”

“My dear sir, recrimination is useless. Listen to a simple statement in political arithmetic: the collectorship at Sancerre is vacant; a certain paymaster-general of the forces has a claim on it, but he has no chance of getting it; you have the chance—and no claim. You will get the place. You will hold it for three months, then resign, and Monsieur Gravier will give twenty thousand francs for it. In addition, the Order of the Legion of Honor will be conferred on you.”

“Well, that is something,” said the wine-grower, tempted more by the money than the red ribbon.

“But then,” said des Lupeaulx, “you must show your gratitude to His Excellency by restoring to Monseigneur the Duc de Navarreins all your claims on him.”

La Baudraye returned to Sancerre as Collector of Taxes. Six months later he was superseded by Monsieur Gravier, regarded as one of the most agreeable financiers who had served under the Empire, and whom Monsieur de la Baudraye introduced to his wife.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
As soon as he was released from his functions, Monsieur de la Baudraye returned to Paris to settle with some debtors. He became referendary under the Great Seal, a baron, and an Officer of the Legion of Honor. He sold the referendary post, visited his last debtors, and reappeared at Sancerre as Master of Appeals, appointed Royal Commissioner to a Nivernais commercial association with a six-thousand-franc sinecure. So the worthy La Baudraye, who was supposed to have committed a financial blunder, had in fact done very good business in the choice of a wife.

Thanks to frugal economy and an indemnity for his father’s estate, nationalized and sold in 1793, by 1827 he could realize his dream. By paying four hundred thousand francs down and binding himself to further instalments, after six years of tight living he purchased the estate of Anzy on the Loire, about two leagues above Sancerre, and its magnificent castle built by Philibert de l’Orme, admired by connoisseurs and for five centuries the property of the Uxelles family. At last he was one of the great landowners of the province. An entail had been created by letters patent dated December 1820, including the estates of Anzy, La Baudraye, and La Hautoy—though this may not have fully compensated Dinah for living in unconfessed penuriousness until 1835.

This sketch of the first Baron de la Baudraye’s financial policy explains the man. Those familiar with rural manias will recognize in him the land-hunger that becomes a consuming passion to the exclusion of every other—a sort of avarice displayed in the open air, which often leads to ruin when mortgage interest outstrips the produce of the soil. Those who, from 1802 to 1827, had laughed at the little man trotting to Saint-Thibault and attending to his business like a merchant living on his vineyards found their answer when the ant-lion seized his prey after the extravagance of the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse culminated in the sale of that splendid property.
