Although her marriage was essentially negative, Madame du Bousquier saw some advantages: a remarkable man about her was preferable to solitude. Du Bousquier was preferable to a dog or canaries. He showed for his wife a sentiment more real and less selfish than that felt by servants or hopeful heirs. Later she regarded her husband as an instrument of divine wrath for past desires: she had brought sorrow on Madame Granson and hastened her uncle’s death; obedient to religion that bids one kiss the rod, she praised him publicly but wept in prayer.

With feelings bruised and immolated, compelled to make her husband happy and attached to him by habit and a dutiful affection, her life became perpetual contradiction. She had married a man she hated but whom she must care for. Often she walked with angels when du Bousquier ate her preserves with satisfaction. She watched to see his slightest wish satisfied, seeing in his small attentions proofs of his oblivious duty. When he kissed her forehead and called her “a good woman,” tears came to her eyes. Du Bousquier likely made some concessions, enough to earn her respect; Catholic virtue did not require total dissimulation. Often she sat mutely and listened as men she admired ranged against the Church; she shuddered at the ruin she foresaw. A stupid word from her was cut short by his glance.

The worries stupefied her; she found it easier and more dignified to concentrate on inner thoughts and resign herself to a life purely animal—slave-like submission as meritorious. She gave herself to severe religious practices and ceased thinking of Satan’s vanities. She presented to the world a union of Christian virtue; du Bousquier was certainly one of the luckiest men in France.

“She will be a simpleton to her last breath,” said the former collector, who nevertheless dined with her twice a week.

This history would be incomplete without noting the coincidence of the Chevalier de Valois’s death with that of Suzanne’s mother. The chevalier died with the monarchy, in August, 1830. He had joined Charles X.’s cortege at Nonancourt and piously escorted it to Cherbourg with the Troisvilles and others. He had carried fifty thousand francs—the sum of his savings—offering it to a faithful friend of the king for transmission, speaking of his approaching death and declaring the money came originally from the king’s goodness. Whether his zeal moved the abandoned Bourbon is unknown; Cesarine, residuary legatee, received only six hundred francs a year from his estate. The chevalier returned to Alencon, weakened by grief and fatigue, and died on the very day Charles X. arrived on a foreign shore.

Madame du Val-Noble and her protector, fearing liberal vengeance, lingered in the village where Suzanne’s mother died. At the chevalier’s sale, Suzanne bought the famous snuff-box’s portrait of Princess Goritza for a thousand francs; two years later a collector obtained it from Madame du Val-Noble. If the dead could know what happens after them, the chevalier’s head would blush upon its left cheek.

If this history pricks possessors of relics to make codicils and bequeath such souvenirs to loving hands, it will serve the romantic portion of the community. It contains a higher moral: the need for a new species of education, chairs of anthropology—Germany outstripping France here. Modern myths are even less understood than ancient ones; myths press us from every point, serving all theories. If Mademoiselle Cormon had been a reader or student, had there been in the Orne a professor of anthropology, or even if she had read Ariosto, the frightful disasters of her conjugal life might never have occurred. She would probably have known why Angelica prefers Medoro to Orlando—Medoro as the mythic courtier, Orlando the myth of impotent revolutions. We publish this opinion of a pupil of Monsieur Ballanche without assuming responsibility.

No information reached us about the fate of the negroes’ heads in diamonds. You may see Madame du Val-Noble every evening at the Opera; thanks to the chevalier’s education she has almost the air of a well-bred woman.

Madame du Bousquier still lives; is not that as much as to say she still suffers? After sixty—the age women allow confessions—she told Madame du Coudrai she had never been able to endure the idea of dying an old maid.

DEDICATION

To Baron Von Hammer-Purgstall, Member of the Aulic Council, Author
of the History of the Ottoman Empire.

Dear Baron,--You have taken such warm interest in my long, vast
“History of French Manners in the Nineteenth Century,” you have
given me so much encouragement to persevere with my work, that you
have a right to be associated with some portion of it. Are you not
one of the most important representatives of conscientious, studious
Germany? Will not your approval win for me the approval of others,
and protect this attempt of mine? So proud am I to have gained your
good opinion that I have striven to deserve it by continuing my
labors with the unflagging courage characteristic of your methods of
study and of that exhaustive documentary research without which your
monumental work could not have been given to letters. Your sympathy
with such labor as you yourself have bestowed upon the brilliant
civilization of the East has often sustained my ardor through nights
of toil given to the details of our modern civilization. And will
not you, whose naive kindliness can only be compared with that of our
own La Fontaine, be glad to know of this?

May this token of my respect find you at Dobling, dear Baron, and put
you and yours in mind of one of your most sincere admirers and friends.

DE BALZAC.

There stands a house at a corner of a street, in the middle of a town,
in one of the least important prefectures in France, but the name of the
street and the name of the town must be suppressed here. Everyone will
appreciate the motives of this reticence demanded by convention;
for if a writer takes upon himself the office of annalist of his own
time, he is bound to touch on many sore subjects. The house was called
the Hotel d’Esgrignon; but let d’Esgrignon be deemed a mere fancy name,
neither more nor less connected with real people than the conventional
Belval, Floricour, or Derville of the stage, or the Adalberts and
Mombreuses of romance. After all, the names of the principal characters
will be disguised; though in this history the chronicler would prefer
to conceal the facts under contradictions, anachronisms, improbabilities,
and absurdities, the truth will out in spite of him. You uproot a
vine-stock, as you imagine, and the stem will send up lusty shoots.

The “Hotel d’Esgrignon” was nothing more nor less than the house in
which the old Marquis lived; or, in the style of ancient documents,
Charles Marie Victor Ange Carol, Marquis d’Esgrignon. It was only an
ordinary house, but the townspeople and tradesmen had begun by calling
it the Hotel d’Esgrignon in jest, and ended after a score of years by
giving it that name in earnest.

The name of Carol, or Karawl as the Thierrys would have spelt it, was
glorious among the names of the Northmen who conquered Gaul and
established the feudal system. Never had Carol bent his head before
King or Communes, the Church or Finance. Entrusted in the days of yore
with the keeping of a French March, the title of marquis in their
family meant no shadow of imaginary office; it had been a post of honor
with duties to discharge. Their fief had always been their domain.
Provincial nobles were they in every sense; they boasted an unbroken
line of descent; they had been neglected by the court for two hundred
years; they were lords paramount in a province where the people looked
up to them with superstitious awe. The house of d’Esgrignon, buried in
its remote border country, was preserved like the charred piles of one
of Caesar’s bridges in a river bed. For thirteen hundred years the
daughters of the house had been married without a dowry or taken the
veil; the younger sons of every generation had been content with their
mother’s dower and gone forth to be captains or bishops; some had made
a marriage at court; one cadet became an admiral, a duke, and a peer of
France, and died without issue. Never would the Marquis of the elder
branch accept the title of duke.

“I hold my marquisate as His Majesty holds the realm of France, and on
the same conditions,” he told the Constable de Luynes, a very paltry
fellow in his eyes at that time.

You may be sure that d’Esgrignons lost their heads on the scaffold
during the troubles. The old blood showed itself proud and high even in
1789. The Marquis of that day would not emigrate; he was answerable for
his March. The reverence in which he was held by the countryside saved
his head; but the hatred of the genuine sans-culottes was strong enough
to compel him to pretend to fly, and for a while he lived in hiding.
Then, in the name of the Sovereign People, the d’Esgrignon lands were
dishonored by the District, and the woods sold by the Nation despite the
personal protest made by the Marquis, then turned forty. Mlle.
d’Esgrignon, his half-sister, saved some portions of the fief, thanks to
the young steward of the family, who claimed on her behalf the partage
de presuccession, the right of a relative to a portion of the emigre’s
lands. To Mlle. d’Esgrignon, therefore, the Republic made over the
castle itself and a few farms. Chesnel [Choisnel], the faithful steward,
was obliged to buy in his own name the church, the parsonage house, the
castle gardens, and other places to which his patron was attached--the
Marquis advancing the money.

The slow, swift years of the Terror went by, and the Marquis, whose
character had won the respect of the whole country, decided that he
and his sister ought to return to the castle and improve the property
which Maitre Chesnel--for he was now a notary--had contrived to save
for them out of the wreck. Alas! was not the plundered and dismantled
castle all too vast for a lord shorn of all his ancient rights; too
large for the landowner whose woods had been sold piecemeal, until he
could scarce draw nine thousand francs of income from the pickings of
his old estates?

It was in the month of October 1800 that Chesnel brought the Marquis
back to the old feudal castle, and saw with deep emotion the patron
standing in the empty courtyard, gazing upon the moat, now filled with
rubbish, and the castle towers razed to the level of the roof. The
descendant of the Franks looked for the missing Gothic turrets and the
weather vanes which used to rise above them; and his eyes turned to the
sky, as if asking of heaven the reason of this social upheaval. No one
but Chesnel could understand the profound anguish of the great
d’Esgrignon, now known as Citizen Carol. For a long while the Marquis
stood in silence, drinking in the influences of the place, the ancient
home of his forefathers; then he flung out a most melancholy
exclamation.

“Chesnel,” he said, “we will come back again some day when the troubles
are over; I could not bring myself to live here until the edict of
pacification has been published; _they_ will not allow me to set my
scutcheon on the wall.”

He waved his hand toward the castle, mounted his horse, and rode back
beside his sister, who had driven over in the notary’s shabby
basket-chaise.

The Hotel d’Esgrignon in the town had been demolished; a couple of
factories now stood on the site of the aristocrat’s house. So Maitre
Chesnel spent the Marquis’ last bag of louis on the purchase of the
old-fashioned building in the square, with its gables, weather-vane,
turret, and dovecote. Once it had been the courthouse of the bailiwick,
and subsequently the presidial; it had belonged to the d’Esgrignons
from generation to generation; and now, for five hundred louis d'or,
the present owner made it over with the title given by the Nation to
its rightful lord. And so, half in jest, half in earnest, the old house
was christened the Hotel d’Esgrignon.

In 1800 little difficulty was made over erasing names from the fatal
list, and some few emigres began to return. Among the first nobles to
come back were the Baron de Nouastre and his daughter. They were
completely ruined. M. d’Esgrignon generously offered them shelter; and
in his house, two months later, the Baron died, worn out with grief.
The Nouastres came of the best blood in the province; Mlle. de Nouastre
was a girl of two-and-twenty; the Marquis married her to continue his
line. But she died in childbirth, a victim to the unskilfulness of her
physician, leaving, fortunately, a son to bear the d’Esgrignon name.
The old Marquis--he was but fifty-three, but adversity had added years--
saw the death of the loveliest of human creatures, a noble woman in
whom the charm of the sixteenth century lived again. With her death
the joy died out of his old age. It was one of those terrible shocks
which reverberate through the years that follow. For a few moments he
stood beside the bed, kissed her forehead, turned away, drew out his
watch, broke the mainspring, and hung it up beside the hearth. It
was eleven o’clock in the morning.

“Mlle. d’Esgrignon,” he said, “let us pray God that this hour may not
prove fatal yet again to our house. My uncle the archbishop was murdered
at this hour; at this hour also my father died----”

He knelt and buried his face in the coverlet; his sister did the same.
Mlle. d’Esgrignon burst into tears; but the old Marquis looked with dry
eyes at the child, round the room, and again on his dead wife. To the
stubbornness of the Frank he united the fortitude of a Christian.

These things came to pass in the second year of the nineteenth century.
Mlle. d’Esgrignon was then twenty-seven. She was a beautiful woman. An
ex-contractor for forage to the armies of the Republic, a man of the
district, with an income of six thousand francs, persuaded Chesnel to
carry a proposal of marriage to the lady. The Marquis and his sister
were alike indignant with such presumption in their man of business,
and Chesnel was almost heartbroken; he could not forgive himself for
yielding to the Sieur du Croisier’s blandishments. The Marquis’ manner
with his old servant changed somewhat; never again was there quite the
old affectionate kindliness, which might almost have been taken for
friendship. From that time forth the Marquis was grateful, and his
sincere gratitude continually wounded the poor notary’s feelings. To
some sublime natures gratitude seems an excessive payment; they would
rather have that sweet equality which springs from similar ways of
thought, and the blending of two spirits by choice. And Maitre Chesnel
had known the delights of such high friendship; the Marquis had raised
him to his own level. The old noble looked on the good notary as
something more than a servant, something less than a child; he was the
voluntary liege man of the house, a serf bound to his lord by ties of
affection. There was no balancing of obligations; the sincere affection
on either side put them out of the question.

In the Marquis’ eyes Chesnel’s official dignity was nothing; his old
servitor was merely disguised as a notary. As for Chesnel, the Marquis
was, as always, a being of a divine race; he believed in nobility; he
did not blush to remember that his father had announced at the salon
that “My Lord Marquis is served.” His devotion to the fallen house was
due not so much to creed as to egoism; he looked on himself as one of the
family. So his vexation was intense. Once he ventured to allude to his
mistake in spite of the Marquis’ prohibition, and the old noble answered
gravely--“Chesnel, before the troubles you would not have permitted
yourself to entertain such injurious suppositions. What can these new
doctrines be if they have spoiled _you_?”

Maitre Chesnel had gained the confidence of the whole town; his high
integrity and considerable fortune made him important. From that time
he felt decided aversion for the Sieur du Croisier; and though there
was little rancor in his composition, he set others against the
sometime forage-contractor. Du Croisier, on the other hand, was a man
to bear a grudge and nurse a vengeance for years. He hated Chesnel and
the d’Esgrignons with the smothered, all-absorbing hate found in a
country town. His rebuff ruined him with the malicious provincials
among whom he had come to live, thinking to rule. He betook himself in
desperation to a wealthy old maid, and met with a second refusal. Thus
failed the ambitious schemes with which he had started. He had lost
his hope of a marriage with Mlle. d’Esgrignon, which would have opened
the Faubourg Saint-Germain of the province to him; and after the second
rejection, his credit fell away so much that it was almost as much as
he could do to keep his position in the second rank.

In 1805, M. de la Roche-Guyon, the oldest son of an ancient family
which had intermarried with the d’Esgrignons, made proposals in form
through Maitre Chesnel for Mlle. Marie Armande Clair d’Esgrignon.
She declined to hear the notary.

“You must have guessed before now that I am a mother, dear Chesnel,”
she said; she had just put her nephew, a fine little boy of five,
to bed.

The old Marquis rose and went up to his sister, but just returned from
the cradle; he kissed her hand reverently, and as he sat down again
found words to say:

“My sister, you are a d’Esgrignon.”

A quiver ran through the noble girl; the tears stood in her eyes. M.
d’Esgrignon, the father of the present Marquis, had married a second
wife, the daughter of a farmer of taxes ennobled by Louis XIV. It was
a shocking mesalliance in the eyes of his family, but fortunately of
no importance, since a daughter was the one child of the marriage.
Armande knew this. Kind as her brother had always been, he looked on
her as a stranger in blood. And this speech of his had just recognized
her as one of the family.

And was not her answer the worthy crown of eleven years of noble life?
Her every action since coming of age bore the stamp of pure devotion;
love for her brother was a sort of religion with her.

“I shall die Mlle. d’Esgrignon,” she said simply, turning to the
notary.

“For you there could be no fairer title,” returned Chesnel, meaning to
convey a compliment. Poor Mlle. d’Esgrignon reddened.

“You have blundered, Chesnel,” said the Marquis, flattered by the
steward’s words, but vexed that his sister had been hurt. “A
d’Esgrignon may marry a Montmorency; their descent is not so pure as
ours. The d’Esgrignons bear or, two bends, gules,” he continued, “and
nothing during nine hundred years has changed their scutcheon; as it
was at first, so it is to-day. Hence our device, Cil est nostre, taken
at a tournament in the reign of Philip Augustus, with the supporters,
a knight in armor or on the right, and a lion gules on the left.”

“I do not remember that any woman I have ever met has struck my
imagination as Mlle. d’Esgrignon did,” said Emile Blondet, to whom
contemporary literature is indebted for this history among other things.
“Truth to tell, I was a boy, a mere child at the time, and perhaps my
memory-pictures of her owe something of their vividness to a boy’s
natural turn for the marvelous.

“If I was playing with other children on the Parade, and she came to
walk there with her nephew Victurnien, the sight of her in the distance
thrilled me with very much the effect of galvanism on a dead body. Child
as I was, I felt as though new life had been given me.

“Mlle. Armande had hair of tawny gold; there was a delicate down on
her cheek, with a silver gleam which I loved to catch, and those dreamy
emerald eyes, which sent a flash of fire through me whenever they fell
upon my face. I used to pretend to roll on the grass before her in our
games, only to try to reach her little feet, and admire them on a closer
view. The soft whiteness of her skin, her delicate features, the clear
lines of her forehead, the grace of her slender figure, took me with a
sense of surprise, while as yet I did not know that her shape was
graceful. I admired as children pray at that age, without clearly
understanding why they pray. When my piercing gaze attracted her notice,
when she asked me (in that musical voice of hers) ‘What are you doing,
little one? Why do you look at me?’--I used to come nearer and wriggle
and bite my finger-nails, and redden and say, ‘I do not know.’ And if
she chanced to stroke my hair with her white hand, and ask me how old I
was, I would run away and call from a distance, ‘Eleven!’

“Every princess and fairy of my visions looked and walked like Mlle.
d’Esgrignon; and afterwards, when my drawing-master gave me heads from
the antique to copy, I noticed that their hair was braided like hers.
Still later, when the foolish fancies had vanished one by one, Mlle.
Armande remained vaguely in my memory as a type; that Mlle. Armande for
whom men made way respectfully, following the tall brown-robed figure
with their eyes along the Parade and out of sight. Her exquisitely
graceful form, the rounded curves sometimes revealed by a chance gust
of wind, revisited my young man’s dreams. Later yet, when I thought
seriously over certain mysteries of human thought, it seemed to me
that the feeling of reverence was first inspired in me by something
expressed in Mlle. d’Esgrignon’s face and bearing. The wonderful calm
of her face, the suppressed passion in it, the dignity of her movements,
the saintly life of duties fulfilled,--all this touched and awed me.
Children are more susceptible than people imagine to subtle influences;
they never make game of real dignity; they feel the charm of real
graciousness, and beauty attracts them, for childhood itself is
beautiful, and there are mysterious ties between things of the same
nature.

“Mlle. d’Esgrignon was one of my religions. To this day I can never
climb the staircase of some old manor-house but my imagination pictures
Mlle. Armande standing there, like the spirit of feudalism. I can never
read old chronicles but she appears before my eyes in the shape of some
famous woman of old times; she is Agnes Sorel, Marie Touchet, Gabrielle;
and I lend her all the love that was lost in her heart, all the love
that she never expressed. The angel shape seen in glimpses through the
haze of childish fancies visits me now sometimes across the mists of
dreams.”

Keep this portrait in mind; it is a faithful sketch of character.
Mlle. d’Esgrignon is one of the most instructive figures in this story;
she affords an example of the mischief that may be done by the purest
goodness for lack of intelligence.

Two-thirds of the emigres returned to France during 1804 and 1805, and
almost every exile from the Marquis’s province came back to the land of
his fathers. There were defections. Men of good birth entered Napoleon’s
service, went into the army or held court places, and others allied with
upstart families. All who cast in their lot with the Empire retrieved
their fortunes and recovered estates, thanks to the Emperor’s
munificence; these for the most part went to Paris and stayed. But some
eight or nine families still remained true to the proscribed noblesse
and loyal to the fallen monarchy. The La Roche-Guyons, Nouastres,
Verneuils, Casterans, Troisvilles, and the rest were some rich, some
poor; but money scarcely counted for anything among them. They took an
antiquarian view of themselves; for them the age and preservation of
the pedigree was the one all-important matter. Of these families, the
Marquis d’Esgrignon was the acknowledged head. His house became their
cenacle. There His Majesty, Emperor and King, was never anything but
“M. de Bonaparte”; there “the King” meant Louis XVIII., then at Mittau;
there the Department was still the Province, and the prefecture the
intendance.

The Marquis was honored among them for his admirable behavior, his
loyalty as a noble, his undaunted courage; even as he was respected
throughout the town for his misfortunes, his fortitude, his steadfast
adherence to his convictions. The man admirable in adversity was invested
with the majesty of ruined greatness. His chivalrous fair-mindedness was
so well known that litigants often referred disputes to him for
arbitration. Gentlemen and the authorities showed as much indulgence
for his prejudices as respect for his character; but there was another
large section of the new society destined to be known after the
Restoration as the Liberal party; and these, with du Croisier as their
unacknowledged head, laughed at an aristocratic oasis which nobody
might enter without proof of irreproachable descent. Their animosity
was the more bitter because honest country squires and many worthy folk
in the town thought all the best society was in the Marquis’s salon.
The prefect himself, the Emperor’s chamberlain, made overtures to the
d’Esgrignons, humbly sending his wife (a Grandlieu) as ambassadress.

Wherefore, those excluded from the miniature provincial Faubourg
Saint-Germain nicknamed the salon “The Collection of Antiquities,” and
called the Marquis “M. Carol.” The receiver of taxes, for instance,
addressed his applications to “M. Carol (ci-devant des Grignons),”
maliciously adopting the obsolete way of spelling.

“For my part,” said Emile Blondet, “if I recall my childhood memories,
the nickname of ‘Collection of Antiquities’ always made me laugh, in
spite of my respect--my love, I ought to say--for Mlle. d’Esgrignon.
The Hotel d’Esgrignon stood at the angle of two busy thoroughfares,
not five hundred paces from the market place. Two of the drawing-room
windows looked upon the street and two upon the square; the room was
like a glass cage, everyone passing could look through it from side to
side. I was only a boy of twelve then, but I thought the salon one of
those rare curiosities which seem to lie on the borderland between
reality and dreams.

“The room, the ancient Hall of Audience, stood above cellars with
grated air-holes, once the prison cells of the old court-house, now
a kitchen. I do not know that the magnificent chimney-piece of the
Louvre seemed more wonderful to me than the vast open hearth of the
salon d’Esgrignon when I saw it for the first time. It was covered with
tracery. Over it stood an equestrian portrait of Henri III., set in a
carved gilded frame. The ceiling spaces between the chestnut cross-beams
were decorated with scroll-work; there was a little faded gilding along
the angles. The walls were covered with Flemish tapestry, six scenes
from the Judgment of Solomon, framed in golden garlands, with satyrs
and cupids among the leaves. The parquet floor had been laid by the
present Marquis, and Chesnel had picked up the furniture at sales of
wrecked chateaux between 1793 and 1795; so there were Louis Quatorze
consoles, tables, clock-cases, andirons, candle-sconces and tapestry
chairs, which completed a stately room, large out of proportion to the
house. Luckily there was an equally lofty ante-chamber, the ancient
Salle des Pas Perdus of the presidial, which communicated with the
magistrate’s deliberating chamber, used by the d’Esgrignons as a dining-room.

“Beneath the old paneling, amid the threadbare braveries of a bygone
day, some eight or ten dowagers were drawn up in state in a quavering
line; some palsied, others dark and shriveled like mummies; some erect,
others bowed, but all tricked out in more or less fantastic costumes
far removed from the fashion of the day, with be-ribboned caps and
old discolored lace. No painter nor caricature ever caught the haunting
fascination of those aged women; they come back to me in dreams; their
puckered faces shape themselves in my memory whenever I meet an old
woman who puts me in mind of them. Whether it is that misfortune has
initiated me into the secrets of irremediable disaster, or that I have
come to understand Old Age and Regret, nowhere and never again have I
seen among the living or in the faces of the dying the wan look of certain
gray eyes that I remember, nor the dreadful brightness of others that
were black.

“Neither Hoffmann nor Maturin ever gave me such a thrill of terror as
when I watched the automaton movements of those bodies sheathed in
whalebone. The paint on actors’ faces never caused me a shock; I could
see below it the rouge in grain. Years had leveled those women’s faces
and furrowed them with wrinkles till they looked like heads on wooden
nutcrackers carved in Germany. Peeping through the window-panes, I
gazed at the battered bodies and ill-jointed limbs; their anatomy was
a mystery I never attempted to explain. The lantern jaws, the protuberant
bones, the abnormal development of the hips; their movements as they
came and went seemed no less extraordinary than their sepulchral
immobility at the card-tables.

“The men looked gray and faded like the ancient tapestries, in dress
they were much more like the men of the day, but even they were not
altogether convincingly alive. Their white hair, their withered waxen
faces, their devastated foreheads and pale eyes revealed kinship to the
women, and neutralized any reality borrowed from costume.

“The very certainty of finding these folk seated at the same hours
invested them in my eyes with a sort of theatrical interest; there was
something unearthly about them.

“Whenever, in after times, I have gone through museums of old furniture
in Paris, London, Munich, or Vienna, with the gray-headed custodian who
shows you the splendors of time past, I have peopled the rooms with
figures from the Collection of Antiquities. Often, as little schoolboys
we used to propose to go and take a look at the curiosities in their
glass cage, for the fun of the thing. But as soon as I caught sight of
Mlle. Armande’s sweet face, I used to tremble; and there was a trace
of jealousy in my admiration for the lovely child Victurnien, who
belonged, as we all instinctively felt, to a different and higher order
of being. It struck me as something indescribably strange that the young
fresh creature should be there in that cemetery awakened before the
time. We could not have explained our thoughts yet felt that we were
bourgeois and insignificant in the presence of that proud court.”

The disasters of 1813 and 1814, which brought about Napoleon’s downfall,
gave new life to the Collection of Antiquities, and the hope of
recovering past importance; but the events of 1815, the foreign
occupation, and the vacillating policy of the Government until the fall
of M. Decazes, deferred the fulfilment of the expectations of the
personages Blondet described. This story, therefore, only begins to
shape itself in 1822.

In 1822 the Marquis d’Esgrignon’s fortunes had not improved despite
the Restoration. Of all the nobles hardly hit by Revolutionary
legislation, his case was hardest. Like other great families, the
d’Esgrignons before 1789 derived the greater part of their income from
rights as lords of the manor in dues paid by those who held of them;
and naturally, the old seigneurs had reduced the size of holdings to
swell quit-rents and heriots. Families in this position were hopelessly
ruined. They were not affected by the ordinance by which Louis XVIII.
put the emigres into possession of such of their lands as had not been
sold; and at a later date it was impossible that the law of indemnity
should indemnify them. Their suppressed rights were revived in the
shape of a land tax known as domaines, but the money went into the
State coffers.

The Marquis belonged to that small section of the Royalist party which
would hear of no compromise with those they styled, not Revolutionaries,
but revolted subjects, or, in parliamentary language, they had no dealings
with Liberals or Constitutionnels. Such Royalists, nicknamed Ultras by
the opposition, took for leaders those orators of the Right who with
M. de Polignac protested against the charter granted by Louis XVIII.
This they regarded as an ill-advised edict extorted from the Crown only
to be annulled later. And therefore, so far from co-operating with the
King to bring about a new condition of things, the Marquis stood aloof,
an upholder of the strictest sect of the Right in politics, until his
vast fortune should be restored. Nor did he admit the thought of the
indemnity which occupied the Villele ministry, a design of strengthening
the Crown by putting an end to those fatal distinctions of ownership
which still lingered.

The miracles of the Restoration of 1814, the greater miracle of
Napoleon’s return in 1815, the portents of a second flight of the
Bourbons, and a second reinstatement, all took the Marquis by surprise
at sixty-seven. At that age, the most high-spirited men were not so
much vanquished as worn out; their activity in remote retreats had
turned into immovable conviction; almost all were shut in by the easy
round of daily country life. Could worse luck befall a political party
than to be represented by old men when its ideas are already stigmatized
as old-fashioned?

When the legitimate sovereign appeared firmly seated on the throne in
1818, the Marquis asked what a man of seventy should do at court; what
duties he could discharge there. The noble d’Esgrignon was fain to be
content with the triumph of Monarchy and Religion while he waited for
results of that indecisive victory which proved to be simply an armistice.
He continued as before, lord-paramount of his salon, so felicitously
named the Collection of Antiquities.

But when the victors of 1793 became the vanquished, the nickname first
given in jest became used in bitter earnest. The town was no more free
than others from hatreds bred of party spirit. Du Croisier, contrary to
expectation, married the old maid who had refused him at first; carrying
her off from his rival, the darling of the aristocratic quarter, a
certain Chevalier. That marriage prolonged the provincial war and
hastened the separation of great and little noblesse, aristocratic and
bourgeois elements which had been united by Napoleonic rule. After the
pressure was removed, there followed a sudden revival of class divisions
which did much harm to the country.

The most national of all sentiments in France is vanity. The wounded
vanity of the many induced a thirst for Equality; though Equality is
an impossibility. The Royalists pricked the Liberals in sensitive spots,
especially in the provinces, where either party accused the other of
unspeakable atrocities. In those days the blackest deeds were done in
politics to secure public opinion, to catch the votes of that public
of fools which holds up hands for those clever enough to serve out
weapons. Individuals are identified with their political opinions,
and opponents in public life became private enemies. It is difficult
in a country town to avoid man-to-man conflict over interests or
questions which in Paris appear in a more general theoretical form;
political combatants rise to a higher level. M. Laffitte, for example,
though drawing fire on the Ministry, would have given them asylum in
his house if they had fled on 29 July 1830. Benjamin Constant sent a
copy of his work on Religion to the Vicomte de Chateaubriand with a
flattering letter. At Paris men are systems, whereas in the provinces
systems are identified with men; men with restless passions who must
always confront one another, spy upon each other, and pull opponents’
speeches to pieces, living like duelists on the watch for a chance to
thrust six inches of steel between an antagonist’s ribs. Each must try
to get under his enemy’s guard, and a political hatred becomes as
all-absorbing as a duel to the death. Epigram and slander are used to
bring the party into discredit.

In such warfare, waged ceremoniously and without rancor on the side of
the Antiquities, while du Croisier’s faction used poisoned weapons,
the advantages of wit and delicate irony lay with the nobles. But the
wounds made by the tongue and the eyes, by gibe or slight, are the last
to heal. When the Chevalier turned his back on mixed society and
entrenched himself on the Mons Sacer of the aristocracy, his witticisms
were directed at du Croisier’s salon; he stirred up fires of war, not
knowing how far the spirit of revenge would urge the rival faction.
None but purists and loyal gentlemen and women entered the Hotel
d’Esgrignon; they committed no indiscretions; they had their ideas,
true or false, noble or trivial, but there was nothing to laugh at in
all this. If the Liberals meant to make the nobles ridiculous, they
were obliged to fasten on the political actions of their opponents;
the intermediate party, composed of officials who paid court to higher
powers, kept the nobles informed of Liberal doings, and much of it was
laughable. Du Croisier’s adherents smarted under a sense of inferiority,
which increased their thirst for revenge.

In 1822, du Croisier put himself at the head of the manufacturing
interest of the province, as the Marquis headed the noblesse. Each
represented his party. But du Croisier, instead of openly standing on
the extreme Left, adopted the opinions later associated with the 221
deputies.

By this position he could keep touch with magistrates, local officials,
and capitalists of the department. Du Croisier’s salon, at least equal
in power to the salon d’Esgrignon, larger, younger, and more energetic,
made itself felt over the countryside; the Collection of Antiquities
remained inert, a passive appendage of a central authority often
embarrassed by its partisans; for not merely did they encourage the
Government in mistaken policy, but some of its fatal blunders were
made owing to their pressure.

The Liberals had never contrived to carry their candidate. The
department declined to obey them knowing du Croisier, if elected, would
sit on the Left Centre benches, and as far as possible to the Left.
Du Croisier was in correspondence with the Brothers Keller, bankers,
one of whom shone among “the nineteen deputies of the Left,” that
phalanx famed by the Liberal press. This same M. Keller was related by
marriage to the Comte de Gondreville, a Constitutional peer who remained
in favor with Louis XVIII. For these reasons the Constitutional
Opposition was prepared to vote at the last moment not for their own
candidate but for du Croisier if he could gain enough Royalist votes;
but at every election du Croisier was regularly thrown out by the
Royalists. The leaders of that party, taking their tone from the Marquis,
had fathomed and gauged him; and with each defeat du Croisier and his
party waxed more bitter. Nothing so stirs up strife as the failure of
some snare set with elaborate pains.

In 1822 there seemed a lull in hostilities that had been kept up with
great spirit during the first four years of the Restoration. The salon
du Croisier and the salon d’Esgrignon, having measured strength and
weakness, were probably waiting for opportunity, that Providence of
party strife. Ordinary persons were content with the surface quiet
which deceived the Government; but those who knew du Croisier better
were aware that the passion of revenge in him, as in men whose whole
life is mental activity, is implacable, especially when political
ambitions are involved. About this time du Croisier, who used to turn
white and red at the bare mention of d’Esgrignon or the Chevalier,
and shuddered at the name of the Collection of Antiquities, chose to
wear the impassive countenance of a savage. He smiled upon his enemies,
hating them the more, watching them the more narrowly. One of his own
party, who seconded him in these calculations of cold wrath, was the
President of the Tribunal, M. du Ronceret, a little country squire who
had vainly endeavored to gain admittance among the Antiquities.

The d’Esgrignons’ little fortune, carefully administered by Maitre
Chesnel, was barely sufficient for the worthy Marquis’ needs; for
though he lived without ostentation, he lived like a noble. The
governor found by his Lordship the Bishop for the hope of the house,
the young Comte Victurnien d’Esgrignon, was an elderly Oratorian who
must be paid a salary, although he lived with the family. The wages of
a cook, a waiting-woman for Mlle. Armande, an old valet for M. le Marquis,
and a couple of other servants, together with the daily household
expenses, and the cost of an education for which nothing was spared,
absorbed the whole family income, in spite of Mlle. Armande’s economies
and Chesnel’s careful management, and the servants’ affection. As yet,
Chesnel had not been able to set about repairs at the ruined castle;
he was waiting till the leases fell in to raise the rent of the farms,
for rents had been rising lately, partly on account of improved methods
of agriculture, partly by the fall in the value of money, of which the
landlord would get the benefit at the expiration of leases granted in
1809.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The Marquis knew nothing of the household’s management or finances. Nearing the end of his life, he and his circle clung to the illusion that the d’Esgrignon house, ignored at Court and by government, would revive in his handsome heir, Victurnien—who would go to Court, enter royal service, and marry into some illustrious family. His intimates—the Troisvilles, La Roche-Guyon, the Casterans, and the Duc de Verneuil—encouraged him. Their faith might have seemed plausible if forty years of history could be erased; but the restored monarchy and public opinion demanded not only noble birth but money, political relevance, and uninterrupted prestige. The d’Esgrignons lacked continuity and the modern resources that mattered; their old renown could not substitute for wealth and influence. The Troisvilles’ daughter’s marriage to General Montcornet, instead of flattering antiquated pride, nearly split social circles—showing how times had changed.

Only Chesnel, the family’s old notary and former steward, saw clearly. Devoted but practical, he had witnessed the Revolution’s violence and its quieter triumph in new social ideas and vested interests. He understood that the deaths and confiscations of the Revolution had become binding facts and that the new order would not be overturned; his attachment to the d’Esgrignons was therefore affectionate and clear-sighted. He tried to warn the Marquis tactfully; the Marquis met these warnings with a prophet’s smile and the conviction that history would repeat in their favor. Chesnel feared the damage the family’s superstitions and self-delusions would do to Victurnien’s mind and habits.

Idolized by his father and aunt, Victurnien was thoroughly spoiled yet justified their hopes in many ways. Mlle. Armande, his aunt, loved him as a mother might but lacked maternal intuition and severity; she could not foresee danger as a real mother could. Chesnel’s pragmatic vigilance resembled a mother’s instinct more closely than the aunt’s blind adoration, but the household generally paid little heed to his counsels—he had even fallen into a kind of disgrace after proposing an imprudent marriage for the family.

Victurnien was taught from infancy to assume his superiority: peers among great nobles, the King as his only superior, and the rest of mankind as inferiors. With beauty, tact, a quick mind, and thorough education, he had every advantage. He was of average height, graceful, an excellent horseman and marksman, and intellectually promising—qualities that might have made him a great man. But indulged and unopposed in youth, he became insolent, audacious, and self-willed. The Chevalier, a charming old roue, misled the boy by glorifying the reckless manners and witty immorality of an earlier era, and the Chevalier’s jocular maxims mingled with the family’s rigid emphasis on honor. Thus good and bad counsel shaped Victurnien.

At eighteen, Victurnien entered society and quickly misread appearances. Accustomed to deference at home and to unguarded talk in the presence of an elderly father, he did not see the hypocrisies and practical cautions of the larger world. His reckless behavior—shooting over neighbors’ lands, amorous imprudences requiring settlements and legal cover-ups—led to lawsuits and scandals quietly hushed by Chesnel, who paid to avert public shame. Encouraged by adroit enemies—particularly Du Croisier, who plotted revenge against the family—Victurnien’s faults were fanned. President du Ronceret and other officials, while outwardly lenient, fostered liberal clamor and public suspicion to discredit the nobles; du Croisier hoped to drive the d’Esgrignons into ruin through the young man’s vices. A young instigator, Fabien du Ronceret, befriended Victurnien to lead him on.

Between eighteen and twenty-one, Victurnien ran up considerable debts—nearly eighty thousand francs paid out by Chesnel, who hid this from Mlle. Armande and the Marquis. The family’s mode of life left little margin: the Marquis’s income barely covered the household. Victurnien’s tailor bills, tastes for English saddles and carriages, livery, and fashionable accessories swallowed his allowance. Chesnel, with private savings (about twelve thousand livres yearly), had been the young man’s secret benefactor, paying off lawsuits and bailing him out repeatedly. Chesnel hoped to see Victurnien settle into a sensible marriage, but the young man’s temperament—capable of lofty thought yet habitually surrendering to impulse and pleasure—made him dangerously likely to repeat mistakes.

Chesnel’s patience and warnings met with shrugging complacency from the Chevalier and indulgence from the family. The Chevalier dismissed debt as par for a gentleman’s course, recounted extravagant examples of aristocratic generosity, and minimized the danger. Chesnel, however, foresaw ruin. His fear and affection prompted him to plan a decisive measure: to have Victurnien sent to Paris and presented at court, hoping the change would sober the boy and provide opportunities befitting his rank.

One autumn evening in 1822, after cards and salon talk, the household confronted the truth about Victurnien’s debts. The Chevalier forced the disclosure to the Marquis: Chesnel had lent an enormous sum—“something like a hundred thousand livres”—to the Count. The Marquis was mortified that his devoted steward would be the family’s creditor; pride and the old notions of dignity made it painful to ask Chesnel for help in a straightforward way. Nevertheless, they resolved to send Victurnien to Paris to present himself to the King and to secure an appointment for him; they accepted Chesnel’s loan rather than humiliate the family by other means.

Chesnel explained that the Count’s companions and enemies in town—the president, Du Croisier and his circle—were seeking vengeance by ensnaring Victurnien, provoking scandals to ruin the family. He had, accordingly, borrowed money himself, mortgaging Le Jard, and had made arrangements in confidence so the loan would not appear as a direct family shame. Mlle. Armande and the Marquis were moved; Chesnel kissed her hand and, with paternal tenderness, urged that Paris provide the boy with more amusement and distraction than provincial society could, while also placing him where discretion and better company might discipline him.

Preparations were hurried. The Marquis and Mlle. Armande instructed Victurnien that he was to go to court, serve His Majesty, and there ascertain a career—army, navy, diplomacy, or royal household. They promised a modest provision and sent Josephin, Victurnien’s trusted valet, with him as companion. Chesnel, meanwhile, wrote to an old notary friend in Paris, Maître Sorbier, begging vigilant oversight of the young man’s conduct and intimating funds would be placed at Sorbier’s disposition to support him. Chesnel’s letter revealed his deep emotional tie to the family: he felt he had reared and protected the d’Esgrignons through revolution and decline, and that the young Count’s fate was intimately his concern.

Victurnien accepted a monthly allowance of two thousand francs and left amid the household’s blessings. He felt no genuine regret; provincial life had been gentle but narrow, and Paris promised to amplify his vanity and gratify his tastes. In the capital he discovered the city’s scale and the fact that noble names no longer guaranteed power or employment; the Court’s attention was limited, and new social forces and money mattered most. Undeterred, he took to luxury and fashion as necessary to command respect and assumed a posture of independence that hid need.

In Paris, he sought to recover a position he imagined due to his birth. He bought rooms, stables, and equerries, all at great expense. His family’s reputation and the Faubourg Saint-Germain’s curiosity opened many doors: old family names still worked as social passports. He found a circle of fashionable young men—de Marsay, Rastignac, de Ronquerolles, Maxime de Trailles, and others—who moved easily in the world he coveted and initiated him into its pleasures. The Vidame de Pamiers, a discreet source of gossip and introductions in the Faubourg, welcomed Victurnien and took him to salons and dinners where he met writers and courtiers, and where the Vidame encouraged him toward conquest and social display.

At a dinner with the Vidame, de Marsay, Rastignac, and the young writer Emile Blondet, Victurnien encountered the pleasures and perils of Parisian society. The Vidame introduced him to Mlle. des Touches’ salon and to fashionable circles where literature and wit mixed with vanity and intrigue. The Vidame, who kept a finger on public taste, predicted the young Count’s rapid rise—and fall—if he pursued such extremes.

Victurnien was introduced to the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, a dazzling and complex beauty who immediately attracted him and whom others envied. The Duchesse presented herself as an ethereal, virginal figure—an affectation that masked cleverness and self-regard. She resolved to play an innocence that would grant her influence, a role she adopted to conceal and manipulate her passions. Her carefully constructed purity, full of artful glances and guarded gestures, made her all the more alluring; she used the appearance of sanctity to amplify the power of her charm.

Thus Victurnien found himself at the center of fashionable life. His family’s name, his beauty, and the patronage of the Faubourg made him a desirable figure; his own vanity made him determined to sustain an image of wealth and consequence. Chesnel’s precautions were circumvented by the mechanics of Parisian finance and the goodwill—or opportunism—of notaries eager for patronage. The young Count’s expenses mounted: his lodgings, dress, horses, and the cost of entering high society quickly devoured the money set aside. Despite Chesnel’s hopes that Paris would rescue him from provincial follies, the city offered new temptations, new alliances, and new risks that promised either brilliant social ascent or irreversible ruin.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
By the end of 1823 the Kellers had advanced Victurnien two hundred thousand francs, and neither Chesnel nor Mlle. Armande knew it. He had also taken two thousand crowns from Chesnel at intervals to conceal his sources. He wrote deceitful letters to his poor father and aunt, who lived happily deceived. The insidious current of Paris life was preparing a dreadful catastrophe for the great house; only du Croisier knew. He rubbed his hands as he passed the Antiquities, hoping to secure not merely ruin but dishonor for the d’Esgrignons. He had been sure of it since discovering that the young Count’s burden of debt was growing too heavy.

Du Croisier’s first step was to rid himself of his most hated enemy, the good old notary Chesnel. The old man lived in the Rue du Bercail in a house with a little paved courtyard and a small garden. At half-past five he usually sat after dinner in his black leather-covered armchair, with a painted pasteboard contrivance protecting his stockinged legs from the fire; he often ate too much and enjoyed planning how his savings might round the d’Esgrignon estates. Chesnel’s affections were wrapped in that single attachment; he took pride in rebuilding the house and hoped to be remembered by future d’Esgrignons.

His old housekeeper entered with alarm. “M. du Croisier wanting to speak to you—” she said.

“M. du Croisier!” The name gave Chesnel a stab of cold; he dropped the tongs. Du Croisier came in, bowed, sat, and produced a bill for two hundred and twenty-seven thousand francs, principal and interest, for sums advanced to Victurnien in bills of exchange drawn upon du Croisier and honored by him. He demanded immediate payment, threatening proceedings against the heir-presumptive. Chesnel turned the unlucky letters over and begged secrecy; du Croisier agreed to keep the secret if paid within forty-eight hours. Pressed for money, Chesnel had obliged manufacturers and resorted to the usual financial fictions. His eyes were dim; he could scarcely keep back tears. The only way to raise the money was to mortgage his lands to their full value.

But when du Croisier learned Chesnel could not repay, he suddenly offered to buy the old lawyer’s property. The sale was completed within two days. Poor Chesnel could not bear the thought of the heir of the house undergoing five years’ imprisonment for debt; shortly nothing remained to him but his practice, the sums due to him, and the house in which he lived. Stripped of lands, he paced his oak-paneled office and thought no longer of Le Jard but of Victurnien.

“What will become of him? He ought to come back; they must marry him to some rich heiress,” he said, with dim eyes and a heavy head.

He did not know how to break the news to Mlle. Armande; the man who had paid the family’s debts quaked at the thought. He went from the Rue du Bercail to the Hotel d’Esgrignon with pulses throbbing like a girl’s, fearing the ruin of illusions.

Mlle. Armande had just received a charming, hypocritical letter. Her nephew was the happiest man alive: he had been to baths, traveled in Italy with Mme. de Maufrigneuse, and sent a journal full of love—Venice, Milan, Florence, the Apennines, Chiavari—painting happiness everywhere. The poor aunt was enthralled. She saw the far-off country of love, sat in Victurnien’s gondola, felt the Duchess’ hand in his; she drank in the letter. Repressed passion had ripened; she loved the couple ardently and excused a married woman’s love for Victurnien; to refuse it would have been unpardonable. Mlle. Armande was not an angelic type but a slender, serene figure; at this moment blue buds were unfolding in her saintly eyes. She loved them as apart from life.

Even in her Venetian reverie, someone appeared on the garden walk—Chesnel. The sound of his tread and the hopelessness on his face recalled her to herself.

“What is it?” she cried.

“All is lost!” said Chesnel. “M. le Comte will bring dishonor upon the house if we do not set it in order.” He held out the bills and described the recent agony in simple, touching words.

“He is deceiving us! The miserable boy!” cried Mlle. Armande, feeling her heart surge.

“Let us say mea culpa, mademoiselle,” Chesnel replied stoutly; “we have allowed him his own way; he needed stern guidance—he had no mother.” Fate sometimes deals terribly with a decaying noble house, she thought, with tears.

The Marquis came up; he had been reading the letter sent by his son from Italy. Victurnien’s itinerary was told from an aristocrat’s point of view: he had been magnificently welcomed by the great families, owed in part to his name and perhaps the Duchess. The old Marquis swung himself off with a coxcomb’s air, recounting ancestral glories. The two companions in unhappiness sat together on a garden bench, saying little, watching the father walk away in his contentment.

“What will become of him now?” Mlle. Armande asked.

“Du Croisier has sent instructions to the Kellers; he is not to be allowed to draw any more without authorization.”

“And there are debts.”

“I am afraid so.”

“If he is left without resources, what will he do?”

“I dare not answer that.”

“But he must be drawn out of that life; he must come back to us, or he will have nothing left.”

“And nothing else,” Chesnel said gloomily. Mlle. Armande could not yet understand the full force of his words.

“Is there any hope of getting him away from that woman—the Duchess? Perhaps she leads him on.”

“He would not stick at a crime to be with her,” Chesnel tried to suggest, preparing her for an intolerable thought.

“Crime!” she cried. “Oh, Chesnel, no one but you would think of such a thing!” she added with a withering look. “There is but one crime a noble can commit—the crime of high treason; and when he is beheaded, the block is covered with a black cloth, as for kings.”

“The times have changed,” said Chesnel, shaking his head. Victurnien had thinned the last of his white hairs. That thought soothed Mlle. Armande’s indignation, but she did not realize what Chesnel meant.

“To-morrow we will decide,” she said. “It needs thought. At worst, we have our lands.”

“Yes,” Chesnel replied. “You and M. le Marquis own the estate conjointly; the larger part is yours. You can raise money on it without saying a word to him.”

That evening players at whist and other games noticed Mlle. Armande’s agitation. “That poor heroic child!” said the old Marquise de Casteran; “she must be suffering still. A woman never knows what her sacrifices to her family may cost her.”

Next day it was arranged that Mlle. Armande should go to Paris to snatch her nephew from perdition. If any one could carry him off, it was the woman whose motherly heart yearned over him. She determined to go to the Duchess and tell her all. Some pretext was needed for the journey; for appearances Mlle. Armande allowed it to be thought she suffered from a complaint requiring consultation with celebrated physicians. Goodness knows whether the town talked of this! But she saw that far more than her reputation was at stake. She set out with Chesnel’s last bag of louis; she took it and her capuchine and mittens without comment. “Generous girl!” he said, putting her into the carriage with her maid.

Du Croisier had thought out his revenge as provincials do—meticulously, with every contingency provided for. Had he sounded Victurnien’s nature so well that he foresaw how easily the young Count would lend himself to his schemes? Or was he merely profiting on a long-watched opportunity? Who warned du Croisier of the moment—the Kellers, or President du Ronceret’s son finishing law studies in Paris?

Du Croisier wrote to Victurnien that the Kellers had been instructed to advance no more money; the letter was timed to arrive as the Duchess and the Count faced poverty cunningly hidden. In the letter informing Victurnien the Kellers would make no further advances without security, a wide blank space was left between the formal text and the signature. It was easy to tear off the upper part and convert the lower into a bill of exchange for any amount. The missive had been enclosed in an envelope so the other side of the sheet was blank. When it arrived, Victurnien was in the lowest despair. After two years of luxurious, thoughtless life, he found himself face to face with inexorable poverty: an absolute impossibility to procure money.

There had been throes before the journey’s end. With the Duchess’ help he had extorted various sums from bankers, but those very amounts were about to start up as overdue bills, with stern summonses from the Bank of France and the commercial court. All through the last weeks he had felt the point of the Commander’s sword: at every supper-party a creeping warning that the sirocco of debt was near. For five years his purse had always been replenished. After Chesnel came du Croisier (he thought), then surely another gold-mine would pour out its wealth. Besides, he was winning at play; luck had saved him unpleasant steps before; often a wild hope sent him to the Salon des Etrangers only to lose the winnings at whist. His life had become like Don Giovanni’s immortal finale—disorder, confusion, a frantic struggle to shut out debts and duels. He lived through this infernal poem alone, seeing visions of a friendless outcast reading the last words on the last page—THE END!

Yes; for him all would be at an end and soon. He already saw the ironical eyes of associates amusing themselves over his downfall. Some played high and could not spare a banknote for an intimate. There was no help—Chesnel must be ruined; he had devoured Chesnel’s living.

He sat with the Duchess in their box at the Italiens, the house envying them, and while he smiled the Furies were tearing at his heart. To give some sense of his despair: he had even taken out pistols and thought of suicide. He who would never brook insult was abusing himself in language no man hears except from himself.

He left du Croisier’s letter open on the bed. Josephin had brought it at nine. Victurnien’s furniture had been seized, but he slept none the less. After the Opera he and the Duchess had gone to a voluptuous retreat where they often spent hours after brilliant court balls. Appearances were cleverly saved; their love-nest seemed a garret to all but was luxuriously arranged within. Now on the brink of ruin, he longed to bid farewell to the dainty nest. Adversity would break the enchanted eggs; there would be no brood of white doves, no brilliant tropical birds of fancy. In three days he must be gone; his bills had been placed in the hands of money-lenders, and law proceedings had reached the last stage.

An evil thought crossed him. He would flee with the Duchess to some undiscovered nook in America, but with a fortune, leaving creditors to confront their bills. To carry out the plan he had only to cut off the lower portion of du Croisier’s letter bearing the signature and fill in figures to turn it into a bill, presenting it to the Kellers. There was a dreadful struggle with temptation; tears were shed; but the honor of the family triumphed—subject to one condition. Victurnien wanted to be sure of his beautiful Diane; he would do nothing unless she consented to their flight. So he went to the Duchess in the Rue Faubourg Saint-Honoré and found her in coquettish morning dress.

Mme. de Maufrigneuse was somewhat pensive. Cares gnawed her mind, but she met them gallantly. Of all feminine types, some combine strength and insight with a faculty for prompt decision and a resolution that would shake a man’s nerves; these powers lie beneath an appearance of graceful helplessness. Such women can ascend to the highest feelings or show selfish insensibility. The Duchess, pondering her fall, laid love aside to consider her safety: refuge abroad, confessing to the King, fascinating a du Tillet or a Nucingen, or gambling on the stock exchange—all were reviewed calmly. She could stand aloof and watch the crash as if a spectator instead of being buried beneath it. That power was great but repulsive.

While dressing for a drive she had collected her thoughts and faced the possibilities of terrific downfall. She plucked love out of her heart to consider necessities and was ready to set the passion back when her coronet was safe. She had none of Richelieu’s hesitation or Napoleon’s early doubts; she resolved.

Victurnien came in. With all his suppressed capacity he was in a state of terror; his heart beat violently; he was afraid to touch the cornerstone that upheld his life with Diane. The cleverest men sometimes deceive themselves to avoid self-humiliation. Victurnien committed himself.

“What is the matter?” Diane asked.

“I am hopelessly in debt. I have come to the end.”

“Pshaw! it is nothing,” she said, smiling. “Money can always be arranged; nothing is irretrievable except disasters in love.”

Relieved by her swift comprehension, he unrolled the bright web of his life’s past two years and a half—the seamy side—setting it off with delicate scorn for men and things. He spoke as an aristocrat; the Duchess listened.

She sat with a knee raised, elbow on knee, face in hand; her eyes never left him while a thousand thoughts flitted under their blue surface. Her forehead was calm, mouth intent—grave with love—her lips knotted by his. To have her listening thus seemed a divine love. When Victurnien proposed flight, he cried, “You are an angel!”

The Duchess made no immediate answer; her mind was absorbed in secret plans. “Good, very good,” she said at last. “But that is not the question, dear. Let us think of your affairs. Yes, we will go, and the sooner the better. Arrange it; I will follow. It is glorious to leave Paris and the world.”

When a duchess speaks such words the sacrifice is made. Diane’s promise settled him; he could conceal sordid details better because she did not inquire. Victurnien would not go till the promise was sealed; he needed courage from happiness before doing a deed that might be badly construed. He counted on his aunt and father and Chesnel to hush up the affair; Chesnel might think of some compromise. Besides, this “business” was the only way to raise money on the family estate. With three hundred thousand francs he and Diane would lead a happy hidden life in Venice.

Next day Victurnien drew a bill for three hundred thousand francs and took it to the Kellers. They advanced the money, for du Croisier happened then to have a balance; but they wrote that he must not draw again without notice. Du Croisier, much astonished, asked for a statement of accounts. It was sent. Everything was explained. The day of his vengeance had arrived.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When Victurnien had drawn "his" money, he took it to Mme. de Maufrigneuse. She locked the banknotes in her desk and proposed to bid the world farewell by going to the Opera for the last time. Victurnien was thoughtful, absent, uneasy. He began to reflect. He thought his seat in the Duchess’ box might cost him dear; that perhaps, once the three hundred thousand francs were safe, it would be better to travel post, to fall at Chesnel’s feet, and tell him all. Before they left the opera-house, the Duchess gave Victurnien a glance; her eyes shone with the desire to go back once more to bid farewell to the nest she loved. And the boy lost a night.

The next day, at three o’clock, he was back again at the Hotel de Maufrigneuse; he had come to take the Duchess’ orders for that night’s escape. "Why should we go?" she asked. "I have thought it all out. The Vicomtesse de Beauseant and the Duchesse de Langeais disappeared. If I go too, it will be commonplace. We will brave the storm. It will be far finer. I am sure of success."

Victurnien’s eyes dazzled; he felt as if his skin were dissolving and blood oozing out all over him.

"What is the matter with you?" cried the fair Diane, noticing a hesitation which a woman never forgives. A truly adroit lover will hasten to agree with any fancy Woman may take, and suggest reasons for doing otherwise, while leaving her free to change her mind and intentions as often as she pleases. Victurnien was angry for the first time, angry with the wrath of a weak man of poetic temperament; it was a storm of rain and lightning, but no thunder followed. The angel on whose faith he had risked more than his life, the honor of his house, was very roughly handled.

"So," said she, "we have come to this after eighteen months of tenderness! You are unkind, very unkind. Go away! I do not want to see you again. I thought you loved me. You do not."

"I do not love you?" he repeated, thunderstruck.

"No, monsieur."

"And yet—" he cried. "Ah! if you but knew what I have just done for your sake!"

"And how have you done so much for me, monsieur? As if a man ought not to do anything for a woman who has done so much for him."

"You are not worthy to know it!" Victurnien cried in a passion of anger.

"Oh!"

After that sublime "Oh!" Diane bowed her head on her hand and sat, still, cold, implacable as angels may be expected to be, since they share none of humanity’s passions. At the sight of the woman he loved in this terrible attitude, Victurnien forgot his danger. Had he not wronged the most angelic creature on earth? He longed for forgiveness, threw himself before her, kissed her feet, pleaded, wept. Two whole hours the unhappy young man spent in folly, only to meet the same cold face, while great silent tears, dropping one by one, were dried as soon as they fell lest the unworthy lover should try to wipe them away. The Duchess was acting a great agony, one of those hours which stamp the woman who passes through them as august and sacred.

Two more hours went by. By this time the Count had gained possession of Diane’s hand; it felt cold and spiritless. The beautiful hand, with all its treasures, might have been supple wood; there was nothing of Diane in it; he had taken it, it had not been given. As for Victurnien, the spirit had ebbed from his frame, he had ceased to think. He would not have seen the sun in heaven. What was to be done? What course should he take? What resolution should he make? Victurnien sank into a bewildered stupor; darkness settled over his brain. Visions of past rapture flitted across the misty gloom like the figures Raphael painted against a black background; to these he must bid farewell. Inexorable and disdainful, the Duchess played with the tip of her scarf. She looked in irritation at Victurnien from time to time; she coquetted with memories, spoke to her lover of his rivals as if anger had finally decided her to prefer one of them to a man who could so change in a moment after twenty-eight months of love.

"Ah! that charming young Felix de Vandenesse, so faithful as he was to Mme. de Mortsauf, would never have permitted such a scene! He can love, can de Vandenesse! De Marsay, that terrible de Marsay, such a tiger as everyone thought him, was rough with other men; but like all strong men, he kept his gentleness for women. Montriveau trampled the Duchesse de Langeais under foot, as Othello killed Desdemona, in a burst of fury which at any rate proved the extravagance of his love. It was not like a paltry squabble. There was rapture in being so crushed. Little, fair-haired, slim men loved to torment women; they could only reign over poor, weak creatures; it pleased them to have some ground for believing they were men. The tyranny of love was their one chance of asserting power. She did not know why she had put herself at the mercy of fair hair. Such men as de Marsay, Montriveau, and Vandenesse, dark-haired and well grown, had a ray of sunlight in their eyes."

It was a storm of epigrams. Her speeches, like bullets, came hissing past his ears. Every word Diane hurled at him was triple-barbed; she humiliated, stung, wounded him with an art that was all her own, as half a score of savages can torture an enemy bound to a stake.

"You are mad!" he cried finally, at the end of his patience, and out he went in God knows what mood. He drove as if he had never handled reins, locked his wheels with other vehicles, collided with the curbstone in the Place Louis-Quinze, went he knew not whither. The horse, left to its own devices, bolted for the stable along the Quai d’Orsay; but as he turned into the Rue de l’Universite, Josephin appeared to stop the runaway.

"You cannot go home, sir," the old man said, with a scared face; "they have come with a warrant to arrest you."

Victurnien thought he had been arrested on the criminal charge, albeit there had not been time for the public prosecutor to receive instructions. He had forgotten the matter of the bills of exchange, which had for days past been stirred up in the form of orders to pay, brought by officers of the court with bailiffs, men in possession, magistrates, commissaries, policemen, and other representatives of social order. Like most guilty creatures, Victurnien had forgotten everything but his crime.

"It is all over with me," he cried.

"No, M. le Comte, drive as fast as you can to the Hotel du Bon la Fontaine, in the Rue de Grenelle. Mlle. Armande is waiting there for you, the horses have been put in, she will take you with her."

Victurnien, in his trouble, caught like a drowning man at the branch that came to hand; he rushed to the inn, reached the place, and flung his arms about his aunt. Mlle. Armande cried as if her heart would break; one might have thought she had a share in her nephew’s guilt. They stepped into the carriage. A few minutes later they were on the road to Brest, and Paris lay behind them. Victurnien uttered not a sound; he was paralyzed. When aunt and nephew began to speak, they talked at cross purposes; Victurnien, still laboring under the unlucky misapprehension which flung him into Mlle. Armande’s arms, thought of his forgery; his aunt had debts and bills on her mind.

"You know all, aunt," he had said.

"Poor boy, yes, but we are here. I am not going to scold you just yet. Take heart."

"I must hide somewhere."

"Perhaps.... Yes, it is a very good idea."

"Perhaps I might get into Chesnel’s house without being seen if we timed our arrival to the middle of the night?"

"That will be best. We shall be better able to hide this from my brother. Poor angel! how unhappy he is!" said she, petting the unworthy child.

"Ah! now I begin to know what dishonor means; it has chilled my love."

"Unhappy boy; what bliss and what misery!" And Mlle. Armande drew his fevered face to her breast and kissed his forehead, cold and damp though it was. Following the excellent scheme suggested by the prodigal son, he was brought by night to the quiet house in the Rue du Bercail; but chance ordered that by so doing he ran straight into the wolf’s jaws. That evening Chesnel had been arranging to sell his connection to M. Lepressoir’s head-clerk. M. Lepressoir was the notary employed by the Liberals, just as Chesnel’s practice lay among aristocratic families. The young fellow’s relatives were rich enough to pay Chesnel the considerable sum of a hundred thousand francs in cash.

Chesnel was rubbing his hands. "A hundred thousand francs will go a long way in buying up debts," he thought. "The young man is paying a high rate of interest on his loans. We will lock him up down here. I will go yonder myself and bring those curs to terms."

Chesnel, honest Chesnel, upright, worthy Chesnel, called his darling Comte Victurnien’s creditors "curs."

Meanwhile his successor was making his way along the Rue du Bercail just as Mlle. Armande’s traveling carriage turned into it. Any young man might feel curiosity if he saw a traveling carriage stop at a notary’s door in such a town and at such an hour; the young man in question was sufficiently inquisitive to stand in a doorway and watch. He saw Mlle. Armande alight.

"Mlle. Armande d’Esgrignon at this time of night!" said he to himself. "What can be going forward at the d’Esgrignons’?"

At the sight of mademoiselle, Chesnel opened the door circumspectly and set down the light he carried; when he looked out and saw Victurnien, Mlle. Armande’s first whispered word made the whole thing plain. He looked up and down the street; it seemed deserted; he beckoned, and the young Count sprang out of the carriage and entered the courtyard. All was lost. Chesnel’s successor had discovered Victurnien’s hiding place.

Victurnien was hurried into the house and installed in a room beyond Chesnel’s private office. No one could enter it except across the old man’s dead body.

"Ah! M. le Comte!" exclaimed Chesnel, no longer merely notary.

"Yes, monsieur," the Count answered, understanding the old friend’s exclamation. "I did not listen to you; and now I have fallen into the depths, and I must perish."

"No, no," the good man answered, looking triumphantly from Mlle. Armande to the Count. "I have sold my connection. I have been working for a long time now, and think of retiring. By noon to-morrow I shall have a hundred thousand francs; many things can be settled with that. Mademoiselle, you are tired," he added; "go back to the carriage and sleep. Business to-morrow."

"Is he safe?" she asked, looking at Victurnien.

"Yes."

She kissed her nephew; a few tears fell on his forehead. Then she went.

"My good Chesnel," said the Count, when they began to talk of business, "what are your hundred thousand francs in such a position as mine? You do not know the full extent of my troubles, I think."

Victurnien explained the situation. Chesnel was thunderstruck. But for the strength of his devotion, he would have succumbed to the blow. Tears streamed from eyes that might well have had no tears left. For a few moments he was a child again, bereft of senses; he stood like a man who should find his house on fire, and through a window see the cradle ablaze and hear the hiss of the flames on his children’s curls. He rose to his full height; he seemed to grow taller; he raised his withered hands and wrung them despairingly and wildly.

"If only your father may die and never know this, young man! To be a forger is enough; a parricide you must not be. Fly, you say? No. They would condemn you for contempt of court! Oh, wretched boy! Why did you not forge my signature? I would have paid; I should not have taken the bill to the public prosecutor. Now I can do nothing. You have brought me to a stand in the lowest pit in hell!--Du Croisier! What will come of it? What is to be done? If you had killed a man, there might be some help. But forgery—forgery! And time—the time is flying," he went on, shaking his fist towards the old clock. "You will want a sham passport now. One crime leads to another. First," he added after a pause, "first of all we must save the house of d’Esgrignon."

"But the money is still in Mme. de Maufrigneuse’s keeping," exclaimed Victurnien.

"Ah!" exclaimed Chesnel. "Well, there is some hope left—a faint hope. Could we soften du Croisier, or buy him over? He shall have all the lands if he likes. I will go to him; I will wake him and offer him all we have.--Besides, it was not you who forged that bill; it was I. I will go to jail; I am too old for the hulks, they can only put me in prison."

"But the body of the bill is in my handwriting," objected Victurnien, without surprise at this reckless devotion.

"Idiot!... that is, pardon, M. le Comte. Josephin should have written it," the old notary cried wrathfully. "He is a good creature; he would have taken it all on his shoulders. But there is an end of it; the world is falling to pieces," the old man continued, sinking exhausted into a chair. "Du Croisier is a tiger; we must be careful not to rouse him. What time is it? Where is the draft? If it is at Paris, it might be bought back from the Kellers; they might accommodate us. Ah! but there are dangers on all sides; a single false step means ruin. Money is wanted in any case. But there! nobody knows you are here, you must live buried in the cellar if needs be. I will go at once to Paris as fast as I can; I can hear the mail coach from Brest."

In a moment the old man recovered the faculties of his youth—his agility and vigor. He packed clothes for the journey, took money, brought a six-pound loaf to the little room beyond the office, and turned the key on his child by adoption.

"Not a sound in here," he said, "no light at night; and stop here till I come back, or you will go to the hulks. Do you understand, M. le Comte? Yes, to the hulks! if anybody in a town like this knows that you are here."
