############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
As I read that letter I felt the maternal heart beneath my fingers; I understood why the countess had forbidden me to open it in Touraine—she feared I would fall at her feet and weep.

I met my brother Charles, until then a stranger. He showed a haughtiness that kept us apart and prevented brotherly affection. Kindliness depends on similarity of soul; there was no point of contact between us. He preached social maxims as if I knew nothing, setting me off as a foil to his qualities. But inward solitude had sharpened my perceptions; having known Madame de Mortsauf I could see through such pretensions. I accepted my primogeniture more willingly, not as my brother’s dupe.

I frequented the Duchesse de Lenoncourt’s alone; Henriette’s name was never spoken there, except by the simple old duke, whose welcome suggested his daughter had commended me to his care. Just as I was overcoming the awkwardness of a youth newly entering the world and learning to use Henriette’s maxims, the events of March 20 altered everything.

My brother followed the court to Ghent; by Henriette’s advice I went there too with the Duc de Lenoncourt. The duke’s kindness became sincere protection when he saw my devotion to the Bourbons; he presented me to the king. Devotion counted at Ghent, and Louis XVIII judged men with a clear eye—I had the happiness of pleasing him.

A letter from Madame de Mortsauf, brought by a Vendean emissary, enclosed a note telling me Jacques was ill. Torn between care of her son and household obligations, the countess needed a friend to lighten her burden. Though ambitious and eager to rival my brother—then at the Congress of Vienna—I resolved, moved by the countess’s sad face, to leave Ghent and return to Clochegourde in the king’s service. Fortune favored me: the emissary could not return and the king wanted a faithful messenger. The Duc de Lenoncourt requested the mission for me; after an audience with the king I set out, able to serve my true sovereign and to go back to Henriette.

I carried the king’s instructions successfully in Paris and in the Vendée. By late May, being denounced to Bonapartist authorities, I fled, traveling on foot through parks and woods across the Bocage and Poitou. I reached Saumur, Chinon, and then the woods of Nueil, where the count found me and brought me safely to Clochegourde.

“Jacques is better,” he said. When we entered the salon Henriette exclaimed, “Felix!” We looked at one another with the hunger of two souls making up for lost months. She had prayed for my safety and prepared my room. At dinner the news of Waterloo and the Allies’ march to Paris was of political consequence, but for us the small domestic joys mattered: she told me, with shining pleasure, “You shall have ice to-day!”—an ingenuous proof of affection that stirred me. Her health and spirit revived in days; my heart opened in response. Absence, for ardent souls, strengthens love; reunion was like spring reviving a valley. Her recovery was delicate; she told me of nights by Jacques’s bedside, of having lived for three months within herself, in a dark palace, alternately listening for moans and a voice.

When she spoke of visions and said, “While I slept my heart watched,” the count interrupted harshly, calling her “half crazy.” He wounded her; she asked whether he had no indulgence for her weakness as a woman. The count’s habitual arrows pierced her, and though he regretted the murmur immediately, the pain showed the long strain of his treatment.

I tried to divert her mind to household matters. She told me of the success of her agricultural plans—the new leases, planted trees, and income—hoping that if the king returned their pension would be restored and Jacques’s fortune secured. She revealed a kind of spiritual insight: in fits of deep meditation she saw luminous or misty visions of her children, which she took as divine signs; she saw me brilliantly illuminated and heard a mental voice guiding me. I clung to her, cried that my obedience was due to none but her, and she smiled. She advised me, when the king returned, to go to Paris—“it may be degrading to beg, but dangerous to be out of the way of receiving favors.” She called me “my son” in her heart, and took my arm contentedly.

That evening I witnessed a scene that moved me: the whole household knelt in prayer, made solemn by Madeleine’s childish voice. The count presented me with backgammon; the countess brought her embroidery and said it had languished while her child was ill. That night, my love burning, I went to her door and listened to her breathing; foolish and dangerous, it was the impulse that urges some men to the brink of ruin to know if they can bear it.

I plucked flowers and made bouquets; I spent days at Clochegourde and dined at Frapesle sometimes. The French army held Tours; though my presence seemed to strengthen the countess, she urged me to go to Paris by Châteauroux and Orléans, to be near the king. I promised to write every night. The duke’s patronage was decisive: he told me the king had spoken of me and asked whether I lived. That night I was appointed master of petitions to the Council of State and given a private place in Louis XVIII’s service—a confidential position that became the source of my prosperity. Madame de Mortsauf had judged rightly; she guided and purified me. The post brought rooms at the Château, a carriage, allowances, and proximity to the sovereign—advantages as important as talent in that world.

The king once teased me, calling me “Mademoiselle de Vandenesse” for my reserve, but he favored me. He arranged that I should have a colleague and leave for six months; I returned to Touraine a changed man—dressed with Parisian refinement and feeling the satisfaction of having repaid, in part, the woman whose counsels had shaped me. The new avenue to Clochegourde, the iron gate, the circular enclosure—small marks of prosperity—impressed me. I had hoped to surprise the countess, but she preferred to be prepared.

When she saw the young man I had become she blushed, was pale, yet let me kiss her hand. She feared my being lost to the world; she begged that I promise never to abandon her. We voiced the impossible balances: I swore, “Sacredly... forever,” and she, half in jest, made me promise to remain a child at Clochegourde—she could refuse nothing to a child. In private she asked directly if I loved her “sacredly,” and I answered in vows that combined chivalry and hope, with the restraint of conscience. She told me to be a child there and a man with the king; in effect, she wanted my devotion within safe limits.

The count, jealous and full of contradictions, resumed his complaints and oddities. He loved his wife yet made her life intolerable by petty tyranny and medical fancies—changing orders about windows, food, and temperature, inventing maladies, and expecting others to adapt. He attacked her for every imagined fault, blaming her when the children fell ill. The household suffered under his whims; servants were exhausted. Henriette had to shield the children, serving them at different hours to spare them his outbursts.

After some days I pressed her: had she erred in making the count idle? She replied that her thoughts were consumed by the children’s health and that diversions would only shift the burden back upon her. She had even thought of silk-worm nurseries, but feared they would only increase his tyranny. She knew that weakness breeds insatiable demands: “what you yield to them to-day they exact to-morrow.” Monsieur de Mortsauf delighted in getting the better of her but deceived himself about his goodness.

One morning she dragged me into the vineyard, frantic with fear that the count “will kill me!” She felt the strain unbearable—never a respite, always walking among thorns. She dared not decide on separation after fifteen years’ marriage; her devotion to her children forbade desertion. She resolved to consult her confessor, the new Abbé Birotteau, for courage. When the count approached we hid; he complained of nausea and went to bed. We took our chance to go in a punt on the river—the quiet beauty of nature eased our spirits. The boat ride, simple pleasures, and mutual attention deepened our intimacy. Yet when a huntsman warned that the count was ill, her first thought was for her son; she breathed more freely learning it was the count. In that instant I realized: though she loved me, her love was of a different order—maternal, pious, not the tumultuous passion I had imagined. I reproached myself gently for not making more decisive claims, for fearing to bind what seemed to be love too tightly.

The count’s illness grew grave. I hurried to Tours for Dr. Origet and obtained a surgeon; bleeding saved him from death. The countess blamed herself, thinking she had brought on the crisis; she wept and offered acts of contrition. The doctor prescribed nursing and a long convalescence; she and I shared the watches. He warned that the moral state of patient and nurses is potent: “a single word, a fear expressed, has the effect of poison.” Origet watched us with scrutiny but, seeing our devotion, he respected us.

For fifty-two days the count hovered between life and death; Henriette and I each watched twenty-six nights. Our shared devotion and duties brought us into a new intimacy. The count’s room became tidy; we learned to serve each other in countless small attentions. She took my hand readily in service; we became, in effect, half-married in devotion. She entrusted me increasingly with household affairs so that she could nurse. In the evenings we discussed estate business and children; I sat where a husband might sit, and she encouraged me with innocent pleasure. Illness stripped the world’s conventions; in that concentrated duty we revealed our true selves.

Two small moments revealed deeper feelings, though never consummated: once she came quietly to my watch and I found her with moist, trembling hands; another time I surprised her weeping by the window. When the count showed signs of convalescence, his old habits reappeared, and he resumed quarrels over food and management, tormenting the household again. Yet the doctor’s orders gave Henriette authority; she governed him with calm firmness and sometimes yielded to pity. She had learned to be stricter, and I took joy in seeing her exercise control. But in private she would indulge him, and her compassion made me both happy and jealous.

She once asked me to accompany her to church to return thanks for the count’s recovery; I left her at the door to visit friends, and she reproached me tenderly for being “a man.” She confessed that such questions tortured her: could she abandon the old man who was the father of her children? If she failed in duty, the children would be exposed to his domination and perhaps harm. She could not sacrifice them. The thought of marrying me, of turning away from duty to embrace personal happiness, was morally impossible for her. She lived by feelings intertwined with conscience and religion. I, more impatiently passionate, could have sold eternity for a day; she resisted such compromises.

One afternoon, while the count sat in the sun and his face extinguished all hope of earthly passion, I received a letter from Tours bearing the king’s signet—my recall. The countess read it and we felt the sudden emptiness: we were necessary to one another and feared the future apart. She knelt and gave me a lock of hair—precious, symbolic—asking me to keep it. We kissed in a solemn, pure way; there was a sacrifice in the gesture. That evening I left; we parted at the walnut-tree where I had first seen her years earlier. She traveled with Madeleine as I returned to Paris.

In Paris my life resumed its duties: the work of the council and my position kept me occupied. I wrote to the countess weekly; she answered twice a month. The letters and our daily duties made our attachment a disciplined devotion—a private religion whose observances preserved feeling. My story at court spread; my reserve and loyalty attracted notice. Society was polite and women were kind; after the Duc de Berry’s marriage the court regained splendor and the city revived with the presence of European notabilities. I was drawn into salons but remained inwardly distant, sustained by the memory of Clochegourde.

Five months after leaving, in midwinter, the countess wrote despairing of Jacques’s serious illness—though he was now out of danger, precautions for his lungs troubled her. Then Madeleine fell ill: the little girl, once gay and blooming, had become pale and anemic. The countess, exhausted by Jacques’s long illness, found no courage to carry these added cares. The household relapsed into the old storms; Monsieur de Mortsauf regained power. She wrote that she felt “turned to stone by grief,” asking if I could love her now when she was “callous, ungrateful, turned to stone by grief.” She feared the loss of the children, the emptiness of life, and confessed that Clochegourde was very sad.

Throughout these events her nature remained noble and self-sacrificing. She had the courage to consult pastors and to bear the trials for her children’s sake. My love for her had been chastened into something that combined worship, tenderness, and sorrow. I remained her friend, steward, and confidant, conscious that our intimacy had grown out of faithful service rather than conquest. The count’s illness had opened to me the inner life of the one woman I loved—her conscience, her maternal heart, and her piety—a love that was at once celestial and austere, and which, in the end, prevented the consummation I desired but sanctified the devotion I offered.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
It was when I felt most alive, suffused with morning light and the hope of crimson evenings, that I met in the salons of the Élysée-Bourbon one of those celebrated women who rule society. Immensely rich, of pure lineage, married to an old British peer, she combined beauty, grace, manners, and wit that dazzled before they charmed. She reigned because she possessed the “iron hand in the velvet glove.”

You know the English character: distance, the coldness of the Channel they set between themselves and anyone not properly presented. Humanity seems an ant-hill to them; they know only the few they admit. To them the people are as if they were not. The British make law and routine omnipotent; they have polished fortifications around the Englishwoman, who becomes irresistibly attractive. No people have trained married women so carefully in hypocrisy by keeping them between two extremes—death or social station—allowing no middle path; for them it is all or nothing. This, with the scorn their customs teach, makes the Englishwoman a being apart: forced to be virtuous yet ready to yield, condemned to live a lie in her heart, delightful outwardly—everything in England rests on appearances. Hence their special charm: an enthusiasm for love that is their life, meticulous care of person, and a delicate passion, as Shakespeare rendered in Juliet.

You, who envy them, know these white sirens: impenetrable yet fathomable, who believe love suffices love, who turn enjoyments to satiety by never varying them; whose soul has one note only—an ocean of love in themselves. My relations with the Marchioness of Dudley had a disastrous fame. At an age when the senses rule and when my own had been repressed, the image of the saint at Clochegourde shone so vividly that I could resist seductions. It was the lustre of this fidelity that attracted Lady Dudley’s attention. My resistance inflamed her passion. What she chiefly desired, like many Englishwomen, was singularity; she wanted pepper with her heart’s food. The dull regularity of their lives leads them to adore the romantic and welcome difficulty. The more I retreated, the more impassioned Lady Dudley became. The struggle, which she gloried in, excited curiosity and seemed to make triumph obligatory. Ah! I might have been saved if some good friend had then repeated her cruel comment on my relations with Madame de Mortsauf: “I am wearied to death of these turtle-dove sighings.”

Without excusing my fault, observe, Natalie, that a man has fewer means of resisting a woman than she has of escaping him. Our manners forbid the brutality of repressing a woman, whereas repression with your sex is both allurement to ours and imposed by conventions. Masculine self-conceit ridicules a man’s modesty; we leave you the monopoly of that virtue so you may grant us favors; reverse the case, and man succumbs before sarcasm.

Though protected by my love, I was not entirely insensible to pride, devotion, and beauty. When Arabella laid at my feet the homage of a ballroom where she reigned, when she watched my glance to see if I approved her dress and trembled with pleasure in pleasing me, I was affected. She occupied a social position where I could not escape her; I could not refuse invitations in the diplomatic circle; rank carried her everywhere, and with feminine cleverness she contrived that the mistress of the house seat me beside her. On such occasions she whispered, “If I were loved like Madame de Mortsauf, I should sacrifice all.” She promised discretion and asked only to love me. After an evening when she made herself irresistible she came to me. The scandal echoed through England; Lady Dudley abandoned her place, gave up her wealth, and endeavored to eclipse by sacrifice the virtue that had caused her ruin. She delighted, like the devil on the temple’s pinnacle, in showing me all the riches of her passionate kingdom.

Read me indulgently. This concerns one of the most interesting problems of human life—a crisis to which most men are subjected. This beautiful, slender, milk-white creature, so yielding and gentle, has, in truth, an iron nature. No horse can conquer her wrist; her hand seems soft but never tires. She is slender, muscular, so strong she fears no struggle; men cannot follow her on horseback. Her body never perspires; it inhales the fire of the atmosphere and lives in water under pain of not living at all. Her love is African; her desires are whirlwinds of the desert—azure, love-laden, changeless skies. What a contrast to Clochegourde! the east and the west; one drawing moisture for herself, the other exuding her soul, wrapping her dear ones in luminous atmosphere.

Have you reflected on the meaning of English manners, customs, and morals? Is it not the deification of matter, a carefully applied Epicureanism? England is materialist—religion and morality exist, but divine spirituality is absent. England excels in that science of existence which makes women’s slippers exquisite, linens fragrant, furniture polished, tea served at fixed hours; in short, it makes matter a clean, shining pulp in which the soul expires of enjoyment and monotony, a life without contrasts that turns you into a machine.

Thus I knew, in British luxury, a woman perhaps unique among her sex, who caught me with love excited by my indifference, to which I opposed stern continence—one of those loves with overwhelming charm, an electricity that carries us to the skies. A love monstrously ungrateful, which laughs at the bodies it kills; love without memory, cruel, like the policy of the English nation; a love to which most men yield. You see the problem? Man is matter and spirit; animality ends and the angel begins. There is the struggle between the future destiny we sense and the influence of anterior instincts—carnal love and divine love. One man combines them, another abstains; some seek satisfaction from the whole sex, others idealize one woman who is their universe; some float between matter and soul, others spiritualize the body, demanding of it what it cannot give.

If you consider the dislikes and affinities of different organisms, and the mistakes from hopes of those who live by mind, heart, or action, who think, feel, or act, you will have indulgence for sorrows to which the world is pitiless. Lady Dudley gratified the instincts, organs, appetites—the mistress of the body; Madame de Mortsauf was the wife of the soul. The mistress’ love has limits; matter is finite and saturable; often I felt a void even near Lady Dudley. Infinitude is the heart’s region; love had no limits at Clochegourde. I loved Lady Dudley passionately; she was magnificent in animality and superior in mind, her sparkling satire wide-ranging. But I adored Henriette. At night I wept with happiness, in the morning with remorse.

Some women hide jealousy under angelic kindness; they are like Lady Dudley, over thirty, knowing how to feel and calculate; they press the juices of today and think of the future. They can stifle a natural moan with the will of a huntsman. Without naming Madame de Mortsauf, Arabella sought to kill her in my soul, where she always found her, her passion increasing with consciousness of that invincible love. Intending to triumph by comparisons, she was never suspicious or complaining; like a lioness with prey she watched that nothing disturb her feast, guarding me like a captive. I wrote to Henriette under her eyes, but she never read a line; she never sought to know to whom my letters were addressed. I had liberty; she seemed to think, “If I lose him it shall be my own fault,” and proudly relied on a love that would have given me her life—she often told me she would kill herself if I left her. “In India that is a distinction reserved for the higher classes,” she said once; “we should rise to greatness by unparalleled devotions. To love with our heads up, in defiance of law; to die for the idol we have chosen; to never betray that man; these are heights common women cannot reach.”

Pride was her instrument; she flattered vanities by deifying them. She put me so high that she might live at my feet; the seductions of her spirit were expressed in attitudes of subservience and submission. How shall I describe those first six months when I was lost in enervating enjoyments, in the meshes of a love fertile in pleasures and varied with cleverness learned by experience, yet hiding that knowledge beneath transports of passion? These pleasures, the revelation of the poetry of the senses, bind young men to older women; they are the chain of the galley-slave, leaving an ineffaceable brand and filling the soul with distaste for pure, innocent love decked only with flowers.

Recalling my early dreams at Clochegourde, I found many sophistries to excuse draining that jeweled cup. Often, lost in lassitude, my soul floating far from earth, I thought these pleasures might abolish matter and let spirit soar. Sometimes Lady Dudley, profiting by my exaltation, wrung blasphemies from my lips against the angel at Clochegourde. Once a traitor, I became a scoundrel. I continued to write to Madame de Mortsauf in the tone of the lad she had first known; but her gift of second sight terrified me when I thought what ruin an indiscreet word might bring. Often, in pleasure, a sudden horror seized me; I heard Henriette’s name above me, like the voice in Scripture demanding, “Cain, where is thy brother Abel?”

At last my letters went unanswered. Seized with anxiety, I wished to leave for Clochegourde. Arabella did not oppose it; she even proposed to accompany me to Touraine, seeing the journey might detach me from my rival; blind with fear and guilelessly unsuspicious, I did not see her trap. Lady Dudley proposed humble concessions: she would stay near Tours, disguised, refrain from daylight outings, meet me only in the evening. I left Tours on horseback, choosing the road I had traveled on foot six years earlier, and stopped beneath my walnut-tree. From there I saw Madame de Mortsauf in white on the terrace. Instantly I rode to her across country. She heard the step of the swallow of the desert and when I pulled up at the terrace she said, “Oh, you here!”

Those three words blasted me. She knew my treachery. Who had told her? Her mother, whose hateful letter she later showed me. The feeble, indifferent voice, once so full of life, revealed settled grief, the breath of flowers cut and left to wither. The tempest of infidelity had torn through her soul, leaving a desert where verdure had been. I led my horse through the gate; he lay on the grass at my command and the countess came forward slowly, exclaiming, “What a fine animal!” She stood with folded arms lest I try to take her hand; I guessed her meaning.

“I will let Monsieur de Mortsauf know you are here,” she said, leaving me.

I stood confounded, watching her noble, slow figure mount the terraces of her château, pride and torture combined. I cursed Arabella in an imprecation that might have killed her had she heard. Presently the whole family came down; Jacques ran eagerly; Madeleine, a gazelle with mournful eyes, walked with her mother. Monsieur de Mortsauf greeted me warmly. Madame de Mortsauf stood with her back toward us under pretext of showing the horse to Madeleine. “Ha, the devil! that’s what women are,” cried the count; “admiring your horse!” Madeleine turned and I kissed her hand, looking at the countess who colored.

“Madeleine seems much better,” I said.

“Poor little girl!” said the countess, kissing her forehead.

“Yes, for the time being they are all well,” answered the count. “Except me, Felix; I am as battered as an old tower about to fall.”

“The general is still depressed,” I remarked to Madame de Mortsauf.

“We all have our blue devils—is not that the English term?” she replied.

The party walked toward the vineyard with a sense that something serious had happened. She had no wish to be alone with me, though I was her guest.

“But about your horse? why isn’t he attended to?” said the count.

“You see I am wrong if I think of him, and wrong if I do not,” said the countess.

“Well, yes,” said the count; “there is a time to do things, and a time not to do them.”

“I will attend to him,” I said, finding the greeting intolerable. “No one but myself can put him into his stall; my groom is coming by the coach from Chinon.”

“I suppose your groom is from England,” she said.

“That is where they all come from,” remarked the count, who brightened as his wife seemed depressed. Her coldness gave him an opportunity to oppose her, and he overwhelmed me with friendliness.

“My dear Felix,” he said, taking my hand, “pray forgive Madame de Mortsauf; women are whimsical. But it is owing to their weakness; they cannot have the evenness of temper we owe to our strength of character. She really loves you, I know it; only—”

While the count spoke Madame de Mortsauf moved away to leave us alone. “Felix,” said the count softly, looking at his wife, “I don’t know what is going on in Madame de Mortsauf’s mind, but for the last six weeks her disposition has completely changed. She, so gentle, so devoted hitherto, is now extraordinarily peevish.”

Manette later told me the countess had fallen into a depression making her indifferent to the count’s provocations. No longer finding a soft surface to wound, the man became uneasy as a child whose prey ceases to move. He now needed a confidant, as the hangman a helper.

“Try to question Madame de Mortsauf,” he urged. “Find out what is the matter. A woman always has secrets from her husband; perhaps she will tell you. I would sacrifice everything to make her happy, even half my days or fortune. She is necessary to my life. If I have not that angel at my side as I grow old I shall be the most wretched of men. I do desire to die easy. Tell her I shall not be here long. Yes, Felix, my poor friend, I am going fast. I hide the truth from everyone; why worry them? The trouble is in the stomach. I have discovered the true cause: my sensibility is killing me. All our feelings affect the gastric centre.”

“Then do you mean,” I said, smiling, “that the best-hearted people die of their stomachs?”

“Don’t laugh, Felix; too keen sensibility irritates the mucous membrane of the stomach; secretions change, digestion becomes capricious; sharp pains come, worsen, and the disorder reaches a crisis like a slow poison; the mucous membrane thickens, the pylorus indurates and forms a scirrhus, of which the patient dies. I have reached that point. Look at my yellow skin, feverish eyes, excessive thinness. I am withering. I brought the seeds of the disease home from the emigration; my marriage, which might have healed, increased the wound. I have ceaseless fears for my children, domestic jars, a fortune to remake, economies that required privations. I have even worse trouble: though Blanche is an angel, she does not understand me; she is as silly as a child. When our fortune was remade, it was too late; the appetite was vitiated. Then my severe illness, mismanaged by Origet. I have not six months to live.”

I listened in terror. On meeting the countess I had noticed her yellow skin and feverish eyes. I led the count toward the house seeming to listen, but my thoughts were all with Henriette; I wanted to observe her. We found her in the salon, listening to a mathematics lesson for Jacques while showing Madeleine an embroidery stitch. Formerly she would have laid aside every occupation the day of my arrival to be with me. My love was so deep that I drove back grief and thought only of the fatal yellow tint on that celestial face, which resembled the halo painters give saints. I felt the icy wind of death pass over me. When her eyes, no longer softened by former light, fell upon me I shuddered; I noticed changes caused by grief I had not seen outdoors. The slender lines upon her forehead had deepened; her temples with violet veins seemed burning and concave; her eyes were sunk, their circles browned—discolored like fruit in decay. I, who had sought to pour happiness into her soul, embittered the spring she hoped would refresh life and renew courage. I sat beside her and said with tears, “Are you satisfied with your health?”

“Yes,” she answered, plunging her eyes into mine. “My health is there,” she added, motioning to Jacques and Madeleine.

Madeleine, fifteen, had victoriously overcome anaemia and was now a woman. She had grown tall; roses had bloomed in her cheeks; she had lost childlike unconcern and dropped her eyes; her movements were slow and infrequent like her mother’s; her figure slim, the bust developing; an instinct of coquetry smoothed her magnificent black hair. She was like a delicate medieval statuette. Health had made her cheeks velvety and her throat silken. She was to live, the bud of a superb development. Jacques, fragile at seventeen, had a large head and feverish eyes with a deep voice, seeming to hide danger beneath roses. It was Henriette’s intellect and soul devouring a body without stamina; Jacques had the milk-white skin and high color of young English women doomed to consumption—an appearance that deceives. Seeing him drawing algebraic figures I shivered at death hidden beneath the roses and thanked my ignorance. Henriette’s maternal pleasure shone.

“When I see my children thus, happiness stills my griefs,” she said. “If other affections fail, the duties done and crowned with success are compensation. Jacques will be like you, a man of highest education; Madeleine will have a noble heart; my motherhood, once tried and tortured, is happy now. My life is full, rich. God makes my joy blossom in these sanctified affections, and turns to bitterness those that might have led me astray—”

“Good!” cried the abbé. “Monsieur le vicomte begins to know as much as I—”

Just then Jacques coughed. “Enough for today,” the countess said, “above all, no chemistry. Go for a ride, Jacques.” She let her son kiss her with tender, dignified pleasure. “Go, dear, but take care.”

“But,” I said, “do you feel ill?”

“Oh, sometimes, in my stomach. If I were in Paris I should have the honors of gastritis.”

“My mother suffers very much and often,” said Madeleine.

“Ah! does my health interest you?” she said, and my eyes counted the roses on the salon sofa.

“This situation is intolerable,” I whispered.

“Did I create it?” she asked. “Dear child,” she said aloud with cruel levity, “don’t you read history? France and England are enemies. Madeleine knows that; a broad, cold sea separates them.”

The vases on the mantel had given place to candelabra, no doubt to deprive me of filling them with flowers; I found them later in my room. When my servant arrived I went out to give orders; he had brought certain things I wished to place in my room.

“Felix,” said the countess, “do not make a mistake. My aunt’s old room is now Madeleine’s. Yours is over the count’s.”

Though guilty, I had a heart; those words were dagger thrusts coldly given at its tenderest spot. Moral sufferings depend on sensitiveness. The countess had trod each round of life’s ladder; but for that reason the kindest woman was now as cruel as she had been beneficent. I looked at Henriette, but she averted her head. I went to my room and burst into tears. Henriette heard me as she entered with a bunch of flowers.

“Henriette,” I said, “will you never forgive a wrong that is indeed excusable?”

“Do not call me Henriette,” she said. “She no longer exists, poor soul; but you may feel sure of Madame de Mortsauf, a devoted friend, who will listen and love you. Felix, we will talk later. If you have any tenderness for me let me grow accustomed to seeing you. When words will not rend my heart, if ever I recover courage, I will speak; not till then. Look at the valley,” she added, pointing to the Indre, “it hurts me, I love it still.”

“Ah, perish England and all her women! I will send my resignation to the king; I will live and die here, pardoned.”

“No, love her; love that woman! Henriette is not. This is no play, and you should know it.”

She left, betraying in her tone the extent of her wounds. I ran after her and held her. “Do you no longer love me?”

“You have done me more harm than all my other troubles put together. Today I suffer less, therefore I love you less. Be kind; do not increase my pain; if you suffer, remember that I live.”

She withdrew her hand cold and moist from mine and darted through the corridor.

At dinner the count subjected me to an unexpected torture. “So the Marchioness of Dudley is not in Paris?” he said.

I blushed but answered, “No.”

“She is not in Tours?”

“She is not divorced; she can go back to England. Her husband would be glad if she would return.”

“Has she children?” asked Madame de Mortsauf in a changed voice.

“Two sons,” I replied.

“Where are they?”

“In England, with their father.”

“Come, Felix,” interposed the count; “be frank; is she as handsome as they say?”

“How can you ask him such a question?” cried the countess. “Is not the woman you love always the handsomest?”

“Yes, always,” I said firmly, with a glance she could not sustain.

“You are a happy fellow,” said the count. “Ha! in my young days I should have gone mad over such a conquest—”

“Hush!” said Madame de Mortsauf, reminding him of Madeleine by a look.

After the table I followed the countess to the terrace. “How is it possible that some women can sacrifice their children to a man?” she exclaimed.

“Yes, and such women would give even more if they had it; they sacrifice everything.”

The world suddenly reversed before her; the grandeur of the thought struck her. She stood dumb, then rose grand and saintly.

“Love her well, Felix,” she said with tears. “She shall be my happy sister. I will forgive her the harm she has done me if she gives you what you could not have here. You are right; I have never told you that I loved you, and I never have loved you as the world loves. But if she is a mother how can she love you so?”

“Dear saint,” I answered, “I must be less moved than I am to explain how you soar above her. She is a woman of earth, daughter of decaying races; you are the child of heaven, an angel worthy of worship; you have my heart, she my flesh only. She knows this and it fills her with despair; she would change parts with you even for cruellest martyrdom. But all is irremediable: to you the soul, the thoughts, the pure love; to her the desires and joys of passing passion; to you remembrance forever, to her oblivion—”

“Tell me that again!” she cried, sitting down and bursting into tears. “If that be so, virtue, purity, a mother’s love are not mistakes. Oh, pour that balm upon my wounds! Repeat the words that bear me back to heaven.” She turned to me, praying for a look or a sacred word—she forgave me for the sufferings of the last two months.

“Henriette, there are mysteries in men’s lives of which you know nothing,” I said. “I met you at an age when heart feelings stifle desires; many scenes, the memory of which will kindle my soul to death, must have told you that age was drawing to a close, and your constant triumph still prolonged its mute delights. A love without possession is maintained by exasperated desire, but there comes a moment when all is suffering within—for in this we have no resemblance to you. We possess a power we cannot abdicate. Nature cannot long be silenced; some trifling accident awakens it to violence. No, I have not loved, but I have thirsted in the desert.”

“The desert!” she said bitterly. “How he reasons! faithful hearts are not so learned.”

“Henriette, do not quarrel with me for a phrase. No, my soul has not vacillated, but I have not been master of my senses. That woman is not ignorant you are the only one I ever loved. She plays a secondary part in my life; she knows it and is resigned. I have the right to leave her as men leave courtesans.”

“And then?”

“She tells me she will kill herself,” I answered, thinking the threat would startle her. But she smiled disdainfully. “My dear conscience,” I continued, “if you would take into account my resistance and the seductions that led to my fall you would understand the fatal—”

“Yes, fatal!” she cried. “I believed in you too much. I believed you capable of priestly virtue. All is over. I owe you much; you have extinguished in me the fires of earthly life. The worst of the way is over; age is coming on. I am ailing now, soon ill; I can never be the brilliant fairy who showers you with favors. Be faithful to Lady Dudley. Madeleine, whom I was training to be yours, ah! who will have her now? Poor Madeleine!” she repeated. “I would you had heard her say to me when you came: ‘Mother, you are not kind to Felix!’ Dear creature!”

She looked in the warm setting sun. Seized with compassion for our shipwrecks she turned back to memories of our pure past. We were silent, eyes passing from the valley to fields, from Clochegourde to Frapesle, peopling the dream with my bouquets and fragrant language. It was her last hour of pleasure, enjoyed with the purity of her Catholic soul. She believed my words and saw where I placed her—in the skies.

“My friend,” she said, “I obey God; his hand is in all this.”

I did not know until later the depth of her words. We slowly returned up the terraces. She took my arm and leaned upon it resignedly, bleeding still but with a bandage.

“Human life is thus,” she said. “What had Monsieur de Mortsauf done to deserve his fate? It proves a better world exists. Alas, for those who walk in happier ways!”

She judged life so truly that cold statements revealed her disgust for all below. At the portico she dropped my arm and said, “If God has given us the desire for happiness, ought he not to take charge of innocent souls who have found sorrow only here? Either that must be so, or God is not, and our life is a cruel jest.”

She entered quickly and I found her crouching on the sofa.

“What is the matter?” I asked.

“I no longer know what is virtue,” she replied; “I have no consciousness of my own.”

We were silent, petrified, hearing those words fall like a stone. “If I am mistaken in my life she is right in hers,” she added at last. Thus her last struggle followed her last happiness. When the count came in she complained of illness. I conjured her to tell me where she suffered; she refused and went to bed, leaving me prey to remorse. Madeleine went with her mother, and next day I heard the countess had been seized with nausea from the day’s violent excitements. Thus I, who longed to give my life for hers, was killing her.

“Dear count,” I said to Monsieur de Mortsauf, who made me play backgammon, “I think the countess very seriously ill. There is time to save her; send for Origet and persuade her to follow his advice.”

“Origet, who half killed me?” cried the count. “No, no; I’ll consult Carbonneau.”

Those days were anguish—the beginning of paralysis of the heart—my vanity mortified, soul rent. One must have been the centre of all to understand the horror of the void about me. All things were the same, but the spirit that gave life to them was extinct. I now understood lovers’ desire never to see each other again when love has flown. To be nothing where once we were everything! I came at last to envy the dismal ignorance of my youth. My despair became so great that I thought the countess felt pity for it. One day after dinner, while walking by the river, I made a last effort for forgiveness. I sent Jacques on with his sister, left the count, and took Henriette to the punt.

“Henriette,” I said, “one word of forgiveness, or I fling myself into the Indre! I have sinned—it is true; but am I not like a faithful dog? I return ashamed. Strike me, bruise me, but give me back your heart.”

“Poor child,” she said, “are you not always my son?”

She took my arm and rejoined her children, leaving me to the count, who began to talk politics. “Let us go in,” I said; “you are bare-headed and the dew may injure you.”

“You pity me, my dear Felix,” he answered; “you understand me; my wife never tries to comfort me—on principle, perhaps.”

Never would she have walked home with her husband; it was now I who had to find excuses to join her. I found her with her children, explaining backgammon rules to Jacques.

“See there,” said the count, “it is for them that I am neglected. Husbands are always suppressed. The most virtuous woman has ways of satisfying her desire to rob conjugal affection.”

She said nothing and continued. “Jacques,” he called.

Jacques objected slightly. “Your father wants you; go at once, my son,” said his mother, pushing him. “They love me by order,” the old man said, sometimes perceiving the truth.

“Monsieur,” she answered, passing her hand over Madeleine’s smooth tresses, “do not be unjust; life is not easy. Perhaps the children are the virtues of a mother.”

“My dear,” said the count, “what you say signifies that women with no children would have no virtue and would leave husbands in the lurch.”

The countess rose hastily and took Madeleine to the portico.

“That’s marriage, my dear fellow,” remarked the count. “Do you mean to imply I am talking nonsense?” he cried, taking his son and going after her with a furious look.

“On the contrary, Monsieur, you frightened me. Your words hurt me cruelly,” she replied in a hollow voice. “If virtue does not consist in sacrificing everything to our children and husband, what is virtue?”

“Sac-ri-ficing!” cried the count, each syllable a blow. “What have you sacrificed to your children? What do you sacrifice to me? Speak! Answer. What is going on here?”

“Monsieur,” she replied, “would you be satisfied to be loved for love of God, or to know your wife virtuous for virtue’s sake?”

“Madame is right,” I interposed; “the noblest thing is to attribute our virtues to those whose happiness is our work. If a woman is involuntarily subject to feelings other than those society imposes, the more irresistible that feeling, the more virtuous she is in smothering it, sacrificing herself to husband and children. This theory does not apply to me, who show the contrary, nor to you whom it will never concern.”

“You have a noble soul, Felix,” said the count, slipping his arm round his wife’s waist and drawing her to say, “Forgive a poor sick man who wants to be loved more than he deserves.”

“There are some hearts all generosity,” she said, resting her head on his shoulder. The scene made her tremble; her comb fell, her hair rolled down, and she turned pale. The count, holding her, groaned as she fainted; he caught her and carried her to the salon, and we surrounded her. She held my hand as if to tell me we alone knew the secret of that simple scene.

“I do wrong,” she said to me in a low voice when the count left to fetch orange-flower water. “I have many wrongs to repent of towards you; I wished to fill you with despair when I ought to have received you mercifully. Dear, you are kindness itself, and I alone can appreciate it. Yes, I know there is kindness prompted by passion. Men have various ways—some from contempt, others from impulse, calculation, or indolence. But you, my friend, you have been absolutely kind.”

“If that be so,” I replied, “remember that all that is good or great in me comes through you. You know well that I am of your making.”

“That word is enough for any woman’s happiness,” she said as the count reentered. “I feel better,” she added, rising; “I want air.”

We went to the acacia-scented terrace. She had taken my right arm and pressed it to her heart, expressing sad thoughts she called dear. No doubt she would gladly have been alone with me, but she feared the shadows or walking where the Indre’s course could be seen. The country calms the old but excites the young; leafed ways and moonlight stir weak resolves. Two strokes of the bell announced prayer. The countess shivered.

“Dear Henriette, are you ill?”

“There is no Henriette,” she said. “Do not bring her back. She was capricious and exacting; now you have a friend whose courage was strengthened by words heaven dictated. We will talk later. It is my day to lead prayers.”

As she begged God’s help a tone entered her voice that struck all present. Did she foresee the emotion she would endure from my forgetfulness toward an engagement with Arabella?

“We have time to make three kings before the horses are harnessed,” said the count, dragging me back to the salon. “You can drive with my wife, I’ll go to bed.”

The game was stormy. The countess heard the count either from her room or Madeleine’s. “You show strange hospitality,” she said reentering.

I looked amazed; I could not accustom myself to her change; formerly she would have protected me from the count; then it gladdened her that I shared her sufferings for love. “I would give my life,” I whispered, “if I could hear you say again, ‘Poor dear, poor dear!’”

She lowered her eyes, remembering that moment, yet turned to me beneath her lids, joyfully. The count, losing the game, said he was tired and gave it up; we walked on the lawn while awaiting the carriage. When the count left us, such pleasure shone on my face that Madame de Mortsauf questioned me by a look.

“Henriette does exist,” I said. “You love me still. You wound me with an intention to break my heart. I may yet be happy!”

“There was but a fragment of that poor woman left, and you have now destroyed even that,” she said. “God be praised, he gives me strength to bear my righteous martyrdom. Yes, I still love you, and I might have erred; the Englishwoman shows me the abyss.”

We got into the carriage and the coachman asked orders.

“Take the road to Chinon by the avenue, and come back by Charlemagne moor and the road to Saché.”

“What day is it?” I asked eagerly.

“Saturday.”

“Then don’t go that way, madame, the road will be crowded with poultry-men and their carts.”

“Do as I told you,” she said. We knew each other’s tones too well. Henriette understood all.

“You did not think of the poultry-men when you appointed this evening,” she said with irony. “Lady Dudley is at Tours, and she is coming here to meet you; do not deny it. ‘What day is it?—the poultry-men!’ Did you ever take notice of such things in our old drives?”

“It only shows that at Clochegourde I forget everything,” I answered.

“She is coming to meet you?”

“Yes.”

“At what hour?”

“Half-past eleven.”

“Where?”

“On the moor.”

“Do not deceive me; is it not at the walnut-tree?”

“On the moor.”

“We will go there,” she said, “and I shall see her.”

Hearing this I resolved to marry Lady Dudley and end the miserable struggle threatening my sensibilities. My sullen silence wounded the countess, whose grandeur I misjudged.

“Do not be angry,” she said in her golden voice. “This is my punishment. You can never be loved as you are here,” she continued, laying my hand on her heart. “I confess it; but Lady Dudley has saved me. To her the stains; to me the glorious love of angels! I have traversed tracts of thought since you returned. I have judged life. To live for this earthly life is too debasing—the selfishness of the senses reigns over spirituality. The pleasures of passion are stormy, followed by enervating anxieties that impair the soul. I came to the sea and saw these tempests; I must escape to higher regions; I would perish on the shores of this vast sea. I see in you, as in others who grieved me, the guardian of my virtue. My life has been consistent with itself. The heart which should have taken root in my first affection was closed to me; death took my aunt; Monsieur de Mortsauf, to whom I vowed myself, has repeatedly, without knowing it, smitten me; his love is the simple egotism of our children. My children are my virtues. Have you not seen how my heart was harrowed for them? When I gave my hair did I not give myself? Why did I so often dress in white? because I seemed your lily; did you not see me here all in white? I have loved my children less, for intense affection is stolen from natural affections. Felix, do you not see that all suffering has meaning? That woman is the instrument of God’s anger; I will meet her without hatred; under pain of being neither Christian, wife, nor mother, I ought to love her. If I contributed to keep your heart unsoiled, that Englishwoman ought not to hate me. A woman should love the mother of the man she loves; I am your mother. What place have I sought in your heart? that left empty by Madame de Vandenesse. Yes, you have always complained of my coldness; yes, I am indeed your mother only. Forgive me, then, the involuntary harshness with which I met you on your return; a mother ought to rejoice that her son is so well loved—”

She laid her head on my breast, repeating, “Forgive me! oh, forgive me!” in a voice new and agonizing.

“You, Felix,” she continued animatedly, “you are the friend who can do no wrong. You have lost nothing in my heart; do not blame yourself. It was selfish in me to ask you to sacrifice joys to an impossible future; to realize it a woman must abandon her children, abdicate her position, renounce eternity. Many times I thought you higher than I; you were great, I petty and criminal. Well, it is settled; I can be to you no more than a light from above, sparkling and cold, unchanging. Only let me not love the brother I have chosen without return. Love me, cherish me! The love of a sister has no dangerous tomorrow. Turn our doubtful attachment into this sweet and sacred love. I will begin tonight by taking Lady Dudley’s hand.”

She did not weep; by these words she cast aside the last veil and showed how many ties she had linked to me and how many chains I had hewn apart. Our emotions were so great we did not notice the heavy rain.

“Will Madame la comtesse wait here under shelter?” asked the coachman, pointing to Ballan’s inn.

She assented and we stayed near half an hour under the vault, surprising the inn-people. When the storm became a “brouee,” letting moonlight through, the coachman drove, and to my delight turned back the way we came.

“Follow my orders,” said the countess, gently.

We crossed Charlemagne moor amid renewed rain. Halfway I heard Arabella’s dog; a horse came from under oaks, jumped a ditch, and carried Lady Dudley to the moor to meet us.

“What pleasure to meet a love thus if it can be done without sin,” said Henriette.

The dog’s barking had told Arabella I was in the carriage. When we reached her she urged her horse to the road's side with equestrian skill for which she was famed and which to Henriette seemed marvelous.

“Amedee,” she said, the name in her English pronunciation charming.

“He is here, madame,” said the countess, looking at the fantastic creature visible in moonlight.

You know how swiftly two women examine each other. The Englishwoman recognized her rival and, gloriously English, gave us a look of insular contempt and disappeared into the underbrush.

“Drive quickly to Clochegourde,” cried the countess, for that cutting look was an axe blow.

The coachman turned to the Chinon road. As we neared the moor we heard Arabella’s furious gallop. “She is going; you will lose her forever,” said Henriette.

“Let her go,” I answered, “and without regret.”

“Oh, poor woman!” cried the countess with compassionate horror. “Where will she go?”

“Back to La Grenadière, near Saint-Cyr,” I said, “where she is staying.”

Just as we entered Clochegourde’s avenue Arabella’s dog barked joyfully and bounded up to the carriage.

“She is here before us!” cried the countess; then added, “I have never seen a more beautiful woman. What a hand and figure! Her complexion outdoes the lily; her eyes are like diamonds. But she rides too well; she loves to display her strength; I think her violent, too active, too bold for our conventions. The woman who recognizes no law listens only to her caprices. Those who seek to shine do not have constancy. Love needs tranquility; I picture it like a vast lake where two beings live on a flowery isle far from the world. Still, love must take the character’s imprint. Lady Dudley is the strong woman who can traverse distances and act with a man’s vigor; she would rescue her lover and kill jailers; while others can only love with their whole souls, kneel to pray, and die. Which suits you best? That is the question. Yes, Lady Dudley must surely love; she has made many sacrifices. Perhaps she will love you when you have ceased to love her!”

“Dear angel,” I said, “let me ask: how is it you know these things?”

“Every sorrow teaches; I have suffered much and my knowledge is vast.”

My servant, hearing orders, held my horse ready in the avenue. Arabella’s dog had scented the horse, and his mistress, drawn by curiosity, followed the animal through the woods.

“Go and make your peace,” said Henriette, smiling without sadness. “Say to Lady Dudley how much she mistakes my intention; I wished to show her the treasure which has fallen to her; my heart holds none but kind feelings, neither anger nor contempt. Explain that I am her sister, not her rival.”

“I shall not go,” I said.

“Have you never discovered,” she said loftily, “that certain propitiations are insulting? Go!”

I rode to Lady Dudley to learn her mind. The dog led me to an oak and Arabella galloped up crying, “Come! away! away!” I followed her to Saint-Cyr, which we reached about midnight.

“That lady is in perfect health,” said Arabella as she dismounted, her satire meaning “I should have died!”

“I forbid you to utter any sarcasms about Madame de Mortsauf,” I said.

“Do I displease your Grace by remarking upon the perfect health of one so dear to your heart? Frenchwomen hate, I am told, even their lover’s dog. In England we love all that our masters love; we hate all they hate. Permit me therefore to love this lady as much as you love her. Only, my dear child,” she added clasping me in arms damp with rain, “if you betray me, I shall not be found lying or standing, not in a carriage with lackeys, nor on moors, nor in my bed; I shall live no longer. I was born in Lancashire, where women die for love. Know you, and give you up? I will yield you to none, not even Death.”

She led me to her rooms where comfort spread its charms. “Love her, dear,” I said warmly. “She loves you sincerely, not in jest.”

“Sincerely! you poor child!” she said, unfastening her habit. While her waiting-woman arranged her hair I tried to show Arabella Henriette’s noble character, repeating many of her great thoughts. Arabella seemed inattentive but lost nothing.

“I am delighted,” she said, “to learn your taste for pious conversation. There’s an old vicar on one estate who writes sermons better than anyone; the country-people like him. I’ll write to my father to send him by steamboat; you can meet him in Paris, and once you have heard him you will never wish to listen to any one else—he won’t give shocks but will lull you. Every evening you can satisfy your passion for sermons with dinner. English morality, I assure you, is superior to that of Touraine as our cutlery and horses are to your knives and turf. Do me the kindness to listen; promise me. I am only a woman; I can love and die for you; but I never studied at Eton or Oxford; I am no doctor of laws or reverend; I can’t preach morality; I am altogether unfit. I don’t blame your tastes; you might have others more depraved and I should conform, for I want you to find near me everything you like—pleasures of love, of food, of piety, good claret, and virtuous Christians. Shall I wear hair-cloth to-night? She is very lucky to suit you in morality. From what college did she graduate? Poor I, who can only give myself, who can only be your slave—”

“Then why did you rush away when I wanted to bring you together?”

“Are you crazy, Amedee? I could go from Paris to Rome disguised as a valet; I would do the most unreasonable thing for your sake; but how can you expect me to speak to a woman on the public road who has never been presented—and who would have preached to me? I speak to peasants; if hungry I would ask a workman to share his bread and pay him in guineas—that is proper; but to stop a carriage on the highway is not in my code. You poor child know only how to love; you don’t know how to live. Besides, I am not like you yet; I don’t like morality. Still, I am capable of great efforts; I will learn to preach; you shall have no more kisses without verses of the Bible interlarded.”

She used and abused her power when she saw my ardent eyes. She triumphed, and I complacently told myself the woman who loses all, sacrifices the future, and makes love her only virtue is far above Catholic polemics.

“So she loves herself better than she loves you?” Arabella went on. “She sets something not you above you. Is that love? How can women value anything except what you value in us? No woman, no matter how fine a moralist, is the equal of a man. Tread upon us, kill us; never embarrass your lives on our account. It is for us to die, for you to live, great and honored. For us the dagger in your hand; for you our pardoning love. Does the sun think of gnats in his beams? they stay while they can and when he withdraws they die—”

“Or fly somewhere else,” I said.

“Yes, somewhere else,” she replied with indifference that would have piqued any man. “Do you really think it is worthy of womanhood to make a man eat his bread buttered with virtue and to persuade him religion is incompatible with love? Am I a reprobate? A woman either gives herself or she refuses. But to refuse and moralize is a double wrong, contrary to the rule of the right in all lands. Here you will get excellent sandwiches prepared by your servant Arabella, whose sole morality is to imagine caresses no man has yet felt and which the angels inspire.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############

PREPARER’S NOTE

Two Poets is part one of a trilogy and begins the story of Lucien, his sister Eve, and his friend David in the provincial town of Angoulême. Part two, A Distinguished Provincial at Paris, centers on Lucien’s Parisian life. Part three, Eve and David, returns to Angoulême. In many references parts one and three are combined under the title Lost Illusions, while A Distinguished Provincial at Paris is titled separately. Lucien’s story continues later in Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life.

DEDICATION

To Monsieur Victor Hugo,

It was your birthright to be, like a Rafael or a Pitt, a great poet at an age when others are children; it was your fate, as with Chateaubriand and every man of genius, to struggle against jealousy lurking in newspapers and the subterranean places of journalism. For this reason I desired that your victorious name should help to win a victory for this work, which some say is an act of courage as well as a veracious history. If there had been journalists in Molière’s time, who can doubt they would have been within the province of the playwright? Why should Comedy, qui castigat ridendo mores, exempt one power when the Parisian press spares none? I am happy to subscribe myself your sincere admirer and friend,

DE BALZAC.

TWO POETS

When this story opens, the Stanhope press and the modern ink roller were not yet common in small provincial printing houses. Even in Angoulême, linked to paper-mills and Parisian typography, the machinery was primitive: wooden presses, leather ink-balls, and a heavy movable marble “impression-stone.” This obsolete gear, to which Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard had a near-superstitious affection, figures in this chronicle.

Séchard had been a journeyman pressman, nicknamed a “bear” in compositors’ slang—so called for the pacing of the pressman from ink-table to press. “Bears” called compositors “monkeys” for their nimble fingering of type from the many compartments of the cases.

In the disastrous year 1793, Séchard, fifty and married, escaped the great Requisition that swept most workmen into the army. He was the only hand left in the printing-house; when the master died, leaving a widow but no children, the business seemed doomed because the solitary “bear” could not become a “monkey” and, as a pressman, could neither read nor write. A Representative of the People, hurried to publish Convention Decrees, bestowed a master printer’s license on Séchard and requisitioned the establishment. Jérôme-Nicolas accepted the dangerous patent, bought the business from the widow with his wife’s savings, and took over the plant at half value—but he still had to print the Republic’s decrees without mistakes.

At this crisis Séchard was fortunate to find a noble Marseillais unwilling to emigrate or risk his head, who agreed to work disguised in a provincial printer’s jacket. M. le Comte de Maucombe set up, read, and corrected the decrees that forbade citizens to harbor aristocrats; the “bear” printed and posted them, and both remained safe.

In 1795, when the Terror had passed, Séchard needed a new jack-of-all-trades to be compositor, reader, and foreman; an Abbé who declined the oath succeeded the Count after the First Consul restored public worship. The Abbé later became a Bishop at the Restoration; in time the Count and the Abbé met again on the same bench in the House of Peers.

By 1795 Jérôme-Nicolas had not learned to read or write; by 1802 he still had not; yet by allowing a large margin for “wear and tear” in his estimates, he managed to pay a foreman’s wages. Once an easy-going journeyman, he became a terror to his “bears” and “monkeys.” Where poverty ceases, avarice begins. On glimpsing the possibility of making a fortune, covetousness sharpened a practical faculty for business—greedy, suspicious, and keen-eyed. He scorned theory; he could estimate at a glance the cost of printing per page or sheet for every kind of type. He proved to unlettered customers that large type costs more to move; if small type was discussed, he argued it was more difficult to handle. Setting up type was the one part of the craft he did not know; afraid of undercharging, he always made a heavy profit. He never took his eyes off compositors paid by the hour. If a paper manufacturer was in trouble, he bought up stock cheap and warehoused it. So he became his own landlord and owned the old house that had been a printing office for ages.

He had every sort of luck. He was left a widower with one son. He sent the boy to grammar school, not so much for the child’s sake as to train a successor; and Séchard treated the lad harshly to prolong parental rule, making him work at case on holidays and telling him he must learn to earn his living to repay his father’s toil.

The Abbé left and Séchard promoted one of his four compositors to foreman, chosen on the future bishop’s recommendation as an honest, intelligent man. These measures, Séchard thought, would tide him over until his son could take over what he expected to be a flourishing business.

David Séchard’s school career was brilliant. Old Séchard, as a self-made man without education, entertained considerable contempt for book learning; yet when he sent his son to Paris to study higher typography, he urged him to save money in the “working man’s paradise” and made clear he was not to draw on the paternal purse. David learned his trade and completed his education without costing his father a farthing; Didot’s foreman became a scholar. At the end of 1819 he was summoned home to take the helm.

Until then Nicolas Séchard’s establishment had enjoyed a monopoly of all regional official printing plus the prefecture and diocese—connections that should have been profitable. But at that moment the firm of Cointet Brothers, paper manufacturers, applied for the second printer’s license in Angoulême. Séchard had kept the license dormant during war-induced commercial atrophy but neglected to buy it outright; this parsimony threatened ruin. Séchard rejoiced when he heard the coming struggle would be fought by his son and not by himself—“a young fellow from the Didots will pull through,” he thought.

The septuagenarian sighed for an easy life. Though his knowledge of higher typography was slight, he was master of “tipple-ography,” an art in which he indulged. Bound by the laws of etymology to be a dry subject, he suffered from an inextinguishable thirst. His wife had controlled the passion in her life, but old age revived it. The habit altered his appearance: a large nose, veined cheeks like vine-leaves, and bloated purple patches gave his face a truffled look. His small gray eyes, under thick brows, glimmered with the cunning of avarice that had supplanted fatherly instinct. Those eyes retained their craftiness even in drink. Séchard resembled one of La Fontaine’s friars with a fringe of grizzled hair about a bald pate—short, corpulent, like an old-fashioned lamp burning much oil for a small wick; excess confirmed his body’s condition.

For thirty years he had worn the municipal three-cornered hat still seen on provincial towncriers. His breeches and waistcoat were greenish velveteen, with an old brown greatcoat, gray cotton stockings, and shoes with silver buckles. This costume suited the man so completely it seemed part of his nature. If the old printer had been given to blind greed, it showed plainly in his manner of abdicating.

Knowing his son had learned the business thoroughly at Didot’s, Jérôme-Nicolas had long plotted the bargain he meant to drive with David. All that the father made, the son was to lose, but in business Jérôme-Nicolas knew nothing of father or son. At first he regarded David as his only child; later he saw him as the natural purchaser of the business and therefore an antagonist whose duty it was to get the better of him. The transformation of sentiment into self-seeking, slow and veiled in more educated people, was swift and direct in the old “bear,” who showed the superiority of shrewd tipple-ography over book-learned typography.

David returned home and the old man greeted him with the cordiality that cunning folk assume for business. He fussed over him—giving his arm, cautioning where to set his foot, warming his bed, making supper. After a sumptuous dinner and much potation, Jérôme-Nicolas began, between hiccoughs, with “Now for business.” David begged to postpone; the old “bear” would not. He had dragged the chain fifty years and would not wait another hour; his son would be “gaffer” tomorrow.

A word about the premises: the printing-house had stood since Louis XIV’s reign at the angle of Rue de Beaulieu and Place du Mûrier. The ground floor was a single large room lit by a street casement and a sash window to the yard; a side passage led to the private office. In the provinces, typography excited curiosity, so customers entered through the street glass door and descended three steps because the workshop floor lay below street level. Newcomers, staring at sheets strung across the ceiling, often ran into cases or tie-bars; they watched compositors pick out type from the hundred and fifty-two compartments, read copy, and assemble lines, only to trip over a ream of damp paper or bump against a bench—delighting the boys, “bears,” and “monkeys.” No one passed through the shop without mishap. Two glass-windowed cages extended into the yard for foreman and master. The yard walls were decorated with trellised vines—tempting, given the owner’s reputation. One side held the kitchen, the other the woodshed, and a ramshackle penthouse against the hall housed paper trimming and damping; there forms—masses of set-up type—were washed. Inky streams from there mixed with kitchen waste and ran into the kennel in the street, so peasants thought the Devil was taking a wash inside the establishment.

Above the shop, the house had three rooms on the first floor and two attic rooms. The first room served as dining-room and lobby; it was the same length as the passage below, minus the old wooden staircase, and lit by a narrow casement and a bull’s-eye into the yard. Its chief trait was a cynical simplicity born of money-making greed: bare whitewashed walls, a dirty brick floor never scoured, three rickety chairs, a round table, and a sideboard between bedroom and sitting-room doors. Windows and doors were dingy. Reams of paper or printed matter cluttered the floor; remains of Séchard’s dinners, empty bottles, and plates lay among packages.

The bedroom, lit to the yard by a leaded window, was hung with old-world tapestry typical in provincial houses on Corpus Christi Day. Its furniture was the old Seigneur Rouzeau’s: a vast four-post bed with canopy, valances, and crimson serge quilt; a couple of worm-eaten armchairs, two tapestry-covered walnut chairs, an aged bureau, and a mantel clock. The room remained as Rouzeau had left it.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The sitting-room had been modernized by the late Mme. Séchard; the walls had a powder-blue wainscot, panels papered with sepia Oriental scenes, and six lyre-backed chairs with blue leather cushions. Two clumsy arched windows looked onto the Place du Mûrier and were without curtains; no clock, sconce, or mirror crowned the mantel-shelf, for Mme. Séchard had died before finishing her scheme; and the “bear,” seeing no profit in further improvements, had left it as it was.

Pede titubante, Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard brought his son into the sitting-room and pointed to a sheet—a foreman’s valuation of the plant prepared under his direction.

“Read that, my boy,” said Jérôme-Nicolas, rolling a wine-dark eye from the paper to David and back. “‘Three wooden presses, held in position by iron tie-bars, cast-iron plates—’”

“An improvement of my own,” the father added.

“‘—Together with all the implements, ink-tables, balls, benches, et cetera, sixteen hundred francs!’” David cried, letting the sheet fall. “These presses are old sabots not worth a hundred crowns; they’re only fit for firewood.”

“Sabots?” shouted the old man. “Sabots? Take the inventory and come downstairs. You’ll see whether your paltry iron contrivances will work like these solid old tools, tried and trusty. You won’t slander honest presses that go like mail coaches and will last a lifetime without repairs. Sabots! My fatherly sabots have carried me twenty years; they helped make you what you are.”

Old Séchard lurched down the worn staircase that shook under his tread, threw open the workshop door, flew to the nearest press—oiled and cleaned for the occasion—and pointed out the strong oaken cheeks polished by the apprentice.

“Isn’t it a love of a press?”

A wedding announcement lay in the press. The old “bear” folded the frisket and tympan, ran in the carriage, worked the lever, drew it out and lifted the frisket and tympan with all the agility of the youngest. The press creaked loudly, as though some bird had dashed against a window and flown away. “Where is the English press that could go at that pace?” he asked his astonished son.

He repeated the manoeuvre at the second and third presses. Seeing a patch overlooked by the apprentice on the third, he swore and rubbed it with his coat skirt, like a horse-dealer polishing an animal he wants to sell. “With those three presses, David, you can make your nine thousand francs a year without a foreman. As your future partner, I’m opposed to replacing them by your cursed cast-iron machinery that wears out the type. You in Paris have been making such a to-do about that damned Englishman’s invention—foreigners who make fortunes for ironfounders. You wanted Stanhopes, did you? Thanks for your Stanhopes, costing two thousand five hundred francs apiece—twice as much as my three jewels—and they maul your type to pieces. I haven’t book-learning like you, but remember, the life of the Stanhope is the death of the type. These three presses will do; people in Angoulême won’t ask more. You may print with wood or iron or gold, they’ll never pay you more.”

David read on: “‘Item, five thousand pounds weight of type from M. Vaflard’s foundry—’” The Didots’ apprentice could not help smiling.

“Laugh away! After twelve years of wear that type is as good as new. That is what I call a typefounder! M. Vaflard is honest and uses hard metal; the best typefounder is the one you go to least often.”

“‘—Taken at ten thousand francs,’” pursued David. “Ten thousand francs, father! Two francs a pound! The Didots only ask thirty-six sous for their Cicero! These nail-heads of yours will only fetch old-metal price—fivepence a pound.”

“You call M. Gillé’s italics, running-hand and round-hand, ‘nail-heads,’ do you? M. Gillé used to be printer to the Emperor! Type at six francs a pound—masterpieces bought five years ago. Some sorts are as bright as when they left the foundry. Look!” He pounced on packets of unused sorts and held them out.

David saw there was no bridging the gap; it was take it or leave it. The ropes across the ceiling and every small item were included in the inventory; jobbing chases, wetting-boards, paste-pots, rinsing-troughs, lye-brushes, all valued with miserly exactitude. The total came to thirty thousand francs, including the license and goodwill. David asked himself if it was feasible.

Old Séchard, uneasy at his son’s silence, preferred stormy argument to wordless acceptance. He believed chaffering indicated someone who knew how to look after his interests: “A man who will pay anything you ask will pay nothing,” he thought. He extolled hot-presses and cutting-presses and the virtues of old tools. “Old tools are always the best tools,” he said; “in our line they ought to fetch more than the new, like goldbeaters’ tools.” Hideous vignettes, masks for theatrical posters, and other old ornaments became treasures in the old man’s vinous eloquence. “Paris is Paris; the provinces are the provinces. If a man came from L’Houmeau asking for wedding cards, he’d want a Cupid and garlands, not a plain M after the Didots’ style. Your inventions won’t take in the provinces for another hundred years.”

David was sensitive and affectionate, disliking dispute, and his loftiness left him at a disadvantage in bargaining with his father, who appeared sincere and credited with good intentions; David took his covetousness for attachment to familiar tools. Still, Jérôme-Nicolas had bought the place from Rouzeau’s widow for ten thousand francs in assignats; thirty thousand in coin seemed exorbitant.

“Father, you are cutting my throat!” exclaimed David.

“I,” cried the old toper, pointing to the ceiling ropes, “I who gave you life? Do you know what the license is worth? The sheet of advertisements alone at fivepence a line brought in five hundred francs last month. Turn up the books and see what we make by placards, the prefecture registers, the mayor’s work, and the bishop’s. You are a do-nothing who won’t get on. You haggle over the horse that will carry you to Marsac.”

Attached to the valuation was a deed of partnership. The father would let house and premises to the new firm for twelve hundred francs per annum, reserving one of the garret rooms for himself. Until David paid the purchase money in full, profits were to be divided equally; once paid off, David would be sole proprietor.

David mentally calculated the value of the license, goodwill, and paper stock, leaving plant out. He thought it possible to clear the debt and accepted the conditions. The old man, used to peasants’ bargaining, wondered whether David had hidden money or was planning not to pay. He tried to get some payment on account; David kept his purse closed.

Next day the old man had the apprentice move household stuff to the attic until an empty market cart could take it back to the country. David entered on the day he was installed into the printing-house—three bare unfurnished rooms—without a sou to pay his men. When he asked his father, as partner, to contribute to working expenses, the old man pretended not to understand: he’d found the printing-house but wasn’t bound to find money too. Pressed, he answered that when he paid Rouzeau’s widow he had had not a penny left. If he, a poor ignorant working man, had made his way, Didot’s apprentice should do still better. Had not David earned money thanks to an education paid for by his father’s sweat? Now the education should be useful.

“What have you done with your ‘polls?’” he demanded, meaning David’s savings.

“Why, had I not to live? and books to buy besides?” David answered indignantly.

“Oh! you bought books? You’ll make a poor man of business. A man who buys books is hardly fit to print them,” retorted the “bear.”

David endured the humiliating shame of a parent’s contempt and niggardly explanations. He saw he was alone and that his father was trying to make money out of him; with a philosophical curiosity he tested how far the old man would go. He asked about his mother’s fortune; perhaps it might pay working expenses.

“Your mother’s fortune?” echoed old Séchard. “Why, it was her beauty and intelligence!”

David understood at once. Any legal claim would be long, expensive, and disgraceful. He accepted the heavy burden of his engagements, resolved to work. “After all, if I have a rough time of it, so had the old man; besides, I shall be working for myself, shall I not?”

“I am leaving you a treasure,” said Séchard, uneasy at his son’s silence.

“What treasure?” asked David.

“Marion!” said his father.

Marion, a big country girl, was indispensable—damping paper, cutting it, cooking, washing, marketing, unloading paper carts, collecting accounts, cleaning ink-balls. Had she known how to read, she might have set up type.

Old Séchard set out on foot for the country. Delighted by the sale, he was not at ease about payment. A vendor’s agony of uncertain completion succeeded his pleasure.
