Middle of the story: central turning point, a pivotal moment dramatically reshaping character and plot. ################################################ MIDDLE OF STORY. ################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

The incident recorded in this sketch took place in late November 1809, when Napoleon’s fugitive empire reached the height of its splendor. Wagram’s trumpets still echoed in Austria. Peace was being signed with the Coalition. Kings and princes orbited Napoleon, dragging all Europe in his train, a spectacle later displayed at Dresden. Paris witnessed entertainments never more superb than those before and after the sovereign’s marriage to an Austrian archduchess. Never, even in the Monarchy’s most splendid days, had so many crowned heads thronged the Seine; never had the French aristocracy been so rich or resplendent. The diamonds on women’s dresses and the gold and silver embroidery on uniforms contrasted strongly with Republic penury, making the wealth of the globe seem to roll through Paris drawing-rooms. Intoxication seemed to have turned the brains of this ephemeral Empire. All the military, even their chief, reveled like parvenus in treasure won for them by a million men with worsted epaulettes, their demands satisfied by a few yards of red ribbon.

At this time most women affected the light morals of Louis XV’s reign. Whether imitating the fallen monarchy, or because certain Imperial family members set the example, as malcontents alleged, both sexes flung themselves into pleasure with an intrepidity that seemed to forebode the end. Another cause: women’s infatuation with the military became a frenzy, consonant with the Emperor’s views, so he did not check it. Frequent calls to arms, which made every treaty an armistice, left passions open to sudden endings as abrupt as the Commander-in-chief’s decisions about busbys, pelisses, and aiguillettes that fascinated the fair sex. Hearts were as nomadic as the regiments. Between the first and fifth bulletins from the Grand Armée a woman might be successively mistress, wife, mother, and widow.

Was it the prospect of early widowhood, the hope of a jointure, or the promise of a name in history that made soldiers attractive? The certainty that the secret of their passions would be buried on the field? Or the noble charm of courage? Perhaps all these reasons, which future historians will weigh, contributed to their readiness to abandon themselves to intrigues. In any case, laurels hid many errors; women ardently preferred brave adventurers, whom they regarded as the fount of honor, wealth, or pleasure; and for young girls an epaulette — the hieroglyphic of a future — signified happiness and liberty.

A feature of the period was an unbridled mania for everything glittering: fireworks and diamonds were more prized. Men, as greedy as women for these translucent pebbles, displayed them lavishly. Possibly the need to carry plunder in portable form made gems fashionable in the army. A man was not ridiculous then, as he might be later, if his shirt-frill or fingers blazed with large diamonds. Murat, Oriental by nature, set the example of preposterous luxury to modern soldiers.

The Comte de Gondreville, formerly Citizen Malin, whose elevation had made him famous and who now lived as a Lucullus of the Conservative Senate, postponed an entertainment for the peace only to court Napoleon by trying to eclipse earlier flatterers. Ambassadors from friendly Powers, important personages, and even a few princes assembled in the wealthy senator’s drawing-rooms, eying favors to come. Dancing flagged; everyone watched for the Emperor, whose presence the Count had promised. Napoleon would have come but for a scene that evening with Josephine — the incident that foreshadowed the couple’s divorce. This episode, then secret but recorded by history, did not reach the courtiers and only kept Napoleon away.

The prettiest women in Paris, eager to attend on mere hearsay, besieged the house with luxury, coquetry, and beauty. The financial world, proud of its riches, challenged the splendor of generals and officials recently gorged with orders, titles, and honors. Wealthy families seized these grand balls to introduce heiresses to Napoleon’s Praetorian Guard, in the foolish hope of exchanging fortunes for uncertain favors. Women trusting beauty alone came to test their power. There, as elsewhere, amusement was a blind: calm, smiling faces and placid brows covered sordid interests; expressions of friendship were lies, and more than one man distrusted his friends more than his enemies.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
These remarks are necessary to explain the incidents of the little imbroglio which is the subject of this study, and the picture, softened as it is, of the tone then dominant in Paris drawing-rooms.

“Turn your eyes a little towards the pedestal supporting that candelabrum—do you see a young lady with her hair drawn back a la Chinoise?—There, in the corner to the left; she has bluebells in the knot of chestnut curls on her head. Do you not see her? She is so pale you might fancy she was ill, delicate-looking, and very small; there—now she is turning her head this way; her almond-shaped blue eyes, so soft, look as if they were made for tears. Look, look! She is bending forward to see Madame de Vaudremont below the crowd of heads in constant motion; the high head-dresses prevent her having a clear view.”

“I see her now, my dear fellow. You had only to say that she had the whitest skin of all the women here; I should have known whom you meant. I had noticed her before; she has the loveliest complexion I ever admired. From hence I defy you to see against her throat the pearls between the sapphires of her necklace. But she is a prude or a coquette, for the tucker of her bodice scarcely lets one suspect the beauty of her bust. What shoulders! what lily-whiteness!”

“Who is she?” asked the first speaker.

“Ah! that I do not know.”

“Aristocrat!—Do you want to keep them all to yourself, Montcornet?”

“You of all men to banter me!” replied Montcornet, with a smile. “Do you think you have a right to insult a poor general like me because, being a happy rival of Soulanges, you cannot even turn on your heel without alarming Madame de Vaudremont? Or is it because I came only a month ago into the Promised Land? How insolent you can be, you men in office, who sit glued to your chairs while we are dodging shot and shell! Come, Monsieur le Maitre des Requetes, allow us to glean in the field of which you can only have precarious possession from the moment when we evacuate it. The deuce is in it! We have a right to live! My good friend, if you knew the German women, you would, I believe, do me a good turn with the Parisian you love best.”

“Well, General, since you have vouchsafed to turn your attention to that lady, whom I never saw till now, have the charity to tell me if you have seen her dance.”

“Why, my dear Martial, where have you dropped from? If you are ever sent with an embassy, I have small hopes of your success. Do not you see a triple rank of the most undaunted coquettes of Paris between her and the swarm of dancing men that buzz under the chandelier? And was it not only by the help of your eyeglass that you were able to discover her at all in the corner by that pillar, where she seems buried in the gloom, in spite of the candles blazing above her head? Between her and us there is such a sparkle of diamonds and glances, so many floating plumes, such a flutter of lace, of flowers and curls, that it would be a real miracle if any dancer could detect her among those stars. Why, Martial, how is it that you have not understood her to be the wife of some sous-prefet from Lippe or Dyle, who has come to try to get her husband promoted?”

“Oh, he will be!” exclaimed the Master of Appeals quickly.

“I doubt it,” replied the Colonel of Cuirassiers, laughing. “She seems as raw in intrigue as you are in diplomacy. I dare bet, Martial, that you do not know how she got into that place.”

The lawyer looked at the Colonel of Cuirassiers with an expression as much of contempt as of curiosity.

“Well,” proceeded Montcornet, “she arrived, I have no doubt, punctually at nine, the first of the company perhaps, and probably she greatly embarrassed the Comtesse de Gondreville, who cannot put two ideas together. Repulsed by the mistress of the house, routed from chair to chair by each newcomer, and driven into the darkness of this little corner, she allowed herself to be walled in, the victim of the jealousy of the other ladies, who would gladly have buried that dangerous beauty. She had, of course, no friend to encourage her to maintain the place she first held in the front rank; then each of those treacherous fair ones would have enjoined on the men of her circle on no account to take out our poor friend, under pain of the severest punishment. That, my dear fellow, is the way in which those sweet faces, in appearance so tender and so artless, would have formed a coalition against the stranger, and that without a word beyond the question, ‘Tell me, dear, do you know that little woman in blue?’—Look here, Martial, if you care to run the gauntlet of more flattering glances and inviting questions than you will ever again meet in the whole of your life, just try to get through the triple rampart which defends that Queen of Dyle, or Lippe, or Charente. You will see whether the dullest woman of them all will not be equal to inventing some wile that would hinder the most determined man from bringing the plaintive stranger to the light. Does it not strike you that she looks like an elegy?”

“Do you think so, Montcornet? Then she must be a married woman?”

“Why not a widow?”

“She would be less passive,” said the lawyer, laughing.

“She is perhaps the widow of a man who is gambling,” replied the handsome Colonel.

“To be sure; since the peace there are so many widows of that class!” said Martial. “But my dear Montcornet, we are a couple of simpletons. That face is still too ingenuous, there is too much youth and freshness on the brow and temples for her to be married. What splendid flesh-tints! Nothing has sunk in the modeling of the nose. Lips, chin, everything in her face is as fresh as a white rosebud, though the expression is veiled, as it were, by the clouds of sadness. Who can it be that makes that young creature weep?”

“Women cry for so little,” said the Colonel.

“I do not know,” replied Martial; “but she does not cry because she is left there without a partner; her grief is not of to-day. It is evident that she has beautified herself for this evening with intention. I would wager that she is in love already.”

“Bah! She is perhaps the daughter of some German princeling; no one talks to her,” said Montcornet.

“Dear! how unhappy a poor child may be!” Martial went on. “Can there be anything more graceful and refined than our little stranger? Well, not one of those furies who stand round her, and who believe that they can feel, will say a word to her. If she would but speak, we should see if she has fine teeth.

“Bless me, you boil over like milk at the least increase of temperature!” cried the Colonel, a little nettled at so soon finding a rival in his friend.

“What!” exclaimed the lawyer, without heeding the Colonel’s question. “Can nobody here tell us the name of this exotic flower?”

“Some lady companion!” said Montcornet.

“What next? A companion! wearing sapphires fit for a queen, and a dress of Malines lace? Tell that to the marines, General. You, too, would not shine in diplomacy if, in the course of your conjectures, you jump in a breath from a German princess to a lady companion.”

Montcornet stopped a man by taking his arm—a fat little man, whose iron-gray hair and clever eyes were to be seen at the lintel of every doorway, and who mingled unceremoniously with the various groups which welcomed him respectfully.

“Gondreville, my friend,” said Montcornet, “who is that quite charming little woman sitting out there under that huge candelabrum?”

“The candelabrum? Ravrio’s work; Isabey made the design.”

“Oh, I recognized your lavishness and taste; but the lady?”

“Ah! I do not know. Some friend of my wife’s, no doubt.”

“Or your mistress, you old rascal.”

“No, on my honor. The Comtesse de Gondreville is the only person capable of inviting people whom no one knows.”

In spite of this very acrimonious comment, the fat little man’s lips did not lose the smile which the Colonel’s suggestion had brought to them. Montcornet returned to the lawyer, who had rejoined a neighboring group, intent on asking, but in vain, for information as to the fair unknown. He grasped Martial’s arm, and said in his ear:

“My dear Martial, mind what you are about. Madame de Vaudremont has been watching you for some minutes with ominous attentiveness; she is a woman who can guess by the mere movement of your lips what you say to me; our eyes have already told her too much; she has perceived and followed their direction, and I suspect that at this moment she is thinking even more than we are of the little blue lady.”

“That is too old a trick in warfare, my dear Montcornet! However, what do I care? Like the Emperor, when I have made a conquest, I keep it.”

“Martial, your fatuity cries out for a lesson. What! you, a civilian, and so lucky as to be the husband-designate of Madame de Vaudremont, a widow of two-and-twenty, burdened with four thousand napoleons a year—a woman who slips such a diamond as this on your finger,” he added, taking the lawyer’s left hand, which the young man complacently allowed; “and, to crown all, you affect the Lovelace, just as if you were a colonel and obliged to keep up the reputation of the military in home quarters! Fie, fie! Only think of all you may lose.”

“At any rate, I shall not lose my liberty,” replied Martial, with a forced laugh.

He cast a passionate glance at Madame de Vaudremont, who responded only by a smile of some uneasiness, for she had seen the Colonel examining the lawyer’s ring.

“Listen to me, Martial. If you flutter round my young stranger, I shall set to work to win Madame de Vaudremont.”

“You have my full permission, my dear Cuirassier, but you will not gain this much,” and the young Maitre des Requetes put his polished thumb-nail under an upper tooth with a little mocking click.

“Remember that I am unmarried,” said the Colonel; “that my sword is my whole fortune; and that such a challenge is setting Tantalus down to a banquet which he will devour.”

“Prrr.”

This defiant roll of consonants was the only reply to the Colonel’s declaration, as Martial looked him from head to foot before turning away.

The fashion of the time required men to wear at a ball white kerseymere breeches and silk stockings. This pretty costume showed to great advantage the perfection of Montcornet’s fine shape. He was five-and-thirty, and attracted attention by his stalwart height, insisted on for the Cuirassiers of the Imperial Guard whose handsome uniform enhanced the dignity of his figure, still youthful in spite of the stoutness occasioned by living on horseback. A black moustache emphasized the frank expression of a thoroughly soldierly countenance, with a broad, high forehead, an aquiline nose, and bright red lips. Montcornet’s manner, stamped with a certain superiority due to the habit of command, might please a woman sensible enough not to aim at making a slave of her husband. The Colonel smiled as he looked at the lawyer, one of his favorite college friends, whose small figure made it necessary for Montcornet to look down a little as he answered his raillery with a friendly glance.

Baron Martial de la Roche-Hugon was a young Provencal patronized by Napoleon; his fate might probably be some splendid embassy. He had won the Emperor by his Italian suppleness and a genius for intrigue, a drawing-room eloquence, and a knowledge of manners, which are so good a substitute for the higher qualities of a sterling man. Through young and eager, his face had already acquired the rigid brilliancy of tinned iron, one of the indispensable characteristics of diplomatists, which allows them to conceal their emotions and disguise their feelings, unless, indeed, this impassibility indicates an absence of all emotion and the death of every feeling. The heart of a diplomate may be regarded as an insoluble problem, for the three most illustrious ambassadors of the time have been distinguished by perdurable hatreds and most romantic attachments.

Martial, however, was one of those men who are capable of reckoning on the future in the midst of their intensest enjoyment; he had already learned to judge the world, and hid his ambition under the fatuity of a lady-killer, cloaking his talent under the commonplace of mediocrity as soon as he observed the rapid advancement of those men who gave the master little umbrage.

The two friends now had to part with a cordial grasp of hands. The introductory tune, warning the ladies to form in squares for a fresh quadrille, cleared the men away from the space they had filled while talking in the middle of the large room. This hurried dialogue had taken place during the usual interval between two dances, in front of the fireplace of the great drawing-room of Gondreville’s mansion. The questions and answers of this very ordinary ballroom gossip had been almost whispered by each of the speakers into his neighbor’s ear. At the same time, the chandeliers and the flambeaux on the chimney-shelf shed such a flood of light on the two friends that their faces, strongly illuminated, failed, in spite of their diplomatic discretion, to conceal the faint expression of their feelings either from the keen-sighted countess or the artless stranger. This espionage of people’s thoughts is perhaps to idle persons one of the pleasures they find in society, while numbers of disappointed numskulls are bored there without daring to own it.

Fully to appreciate the interest of this conversation, it is necessary to relate an incident which would presently serve as an invisible bond, drawing together the actors in this little drama, who were at present scattered through the rooms.

At about eleven o’clock, just as the dancers were returning to their seats, the company had observed the entrance of the handsomest woman in Paris, the queen of fashion, the only person wanting to the brilliant assembly. She made it a rule never to appear till the moment when a party had reached that pitch of excited movement which does not allow the women to preserve much longer the freshness of their faces or of their dress. This brief hour is, as it were, the springtime of a ball. An hour after, when pleasure falls flat and fatigue is encroaching, everything is spoilt. Madame de Vaudremont never committed the blunder of remaining at a party to be seen with drooping flowers, hair out of curl, tumbled frills, and a face like every other that sleep is courting—not always without success. She took good care not to let her beauty be seen drowsy, as her rivals did; she was so clever as to keep up her reputation for smartness by always leaving a ballroom in brilliant order, as she had entered it. Women whispered to each other with a feeling of envy that she planned and wore as many different dresses as the parties she went to in one evening.

On the present occasion Madame de Vaudremont was not destined to be free to leave when she would the ballroom she had entered in triumph. Pausing for a moment on the threshold, she shot swift but observant glances on the women present, hastily scrutinizing their dresses to assure herself that her own eclipsed them all.

The illustrious beauty presented herself to the admiration of the crowd at the same moment with one of the bravest colonels of the Guards’ Artillery and the Emperor’s favorite, the Comte de Soulanges. The transient and fortuitous association of these two had about it a certain air of mystery. On hearing the names announced of Monsieur de Soulanges and the Comtesse de Vaudremont, a few women sitting by the wall rose, and men, hurrying in from the side-rooms, pressed forward to the principal doorway. One of the jesters who are always to be found in any large assembly said, as the Countess and her escort came in, that “women had quite as much curiosity about seeing a man who was faithful to his passion as men had in studying a woman who was difficult to enthrall.”

Though the Comte de Soulanges, a young man of about two-and-thirty, was endowed with the nervous temperament which in a man gives rise to fine qualities, his slender build and pale complexion were not at first sight attractive; his black eyes betrayed great vivacity, but he was taciturn in company, and there was nothing in his appearance to reveal the gift for oratory which subsequently distinguished him, on the Right, in the legislative assembly under the Restoration.

The Comtesse de Vaudremont, a tall woman, rather fat, with a skin of dazzling whiteness, a small head that she carried well, and the immense advantage of inspiring love by the graciousness of her manner, was one of those beings who keep all the promise of their beauty.

The pair, who for a few minutes were the centre of general observation, did not for long give curiosity an opportunity of exercising itself about them. The Colonel and the Countess seemed perfectly to understand that accident had placed them in an awkward position. Martial, as they came forward, had hastened to join the group of men by the fireplace, that he might watch Madame de Vaudremont with the jealous anxiety of the first flame of passion, from behind the heads which formed a sort of rampart; a secret voice seemed to warn him that the success on which he prided himself might perhaps be precarious. But the coldly polite smile with which the Countess thanked Monsieur de Soulanges, and her little bow of dismissal as she sat down by Madame de Gondreville, relaxed the muscles of his face which jealousy had made rigid. Seeing Soulanges, however, still standing quite near the sofa on which Madame de Vaudremont was seated, not apparently having understood the glance by which the lady had conveyed to him that they were both playing a ridiculous part, the volcanic Provencal again knit the black brows that overshadowed his blue eyes, smoothed his chestnut curls to keep himself in countenance, and without betraying the agitation which made his heart beat, watched the faces of the Countess and of M. de Soulanges while still chatting with his neighbors. He then took the hand of Colonel Montcornet, who had just renewed their old acquaintance, but he listened to him without hearing him; his mind was elsewhere.

Soulanges was gazing calmly at the women, sitting four ranks deep all round the immense ballroom, admiring this dado of diamonds, rubies, masses of gold and shining hair, of which the lustre almost outshone the blaze of waxlights, the cutglass of the chandeliers, and the gilding. His rival’s stolid indifference put the lawyer out of countenance. Quite incapable of controlling his secret transports of impatience, Martial went towards Madame de Vaudremont with a bow. On seeing the Provencal, Soulanges gave him a covert glance, and impertinently turned away his head. Solemn silence now reigned in the room, where curiosity was at the highest pitch. All these eager faces wore the strangest mixed expressions; every one apprehended one of those outbreaks which men of breeding carefully avoid. Suddenly the Count’s pale face turned as red as the scarlet facings of his coat, and he fixed his gaze on the floor that the cause of his agitation might not be guessed. On catching sight of the unknown lady humbly seated by the pedestal of the candelabrum, he moved away with a melancholy air, passing in front of the lawyer, and took refuge in one of the cardrooms. Martial and all the company thought that Soulanges had publicly surrendered the post, out of fear of the ridicule which invariably attaches to a discarded lover. The lawyer proudly raised his head and looked at the strange lady; then, as he took his seat at his ease near Madame de Vaudremont, he listened to her so inattentively that he did not catch these words spoken behind her fan:

“Martial, you will oblige me this evening by not wearing that ring that you snatched from me. I have my reasons, and will explain them to you in a moment when we go away. You must give me your arm to go to the Princess de Wagram’s.”

“Why did you come in with the Colonel?” asked the Baron.

“I met him in the hall,” she replied. “But leave me now; everybody is looking at us.”

Martial returned to the Colonel of Cuirassiers. Then it was that the little blue lady had become the object of the curiosity which agitated in such various ways the Colonel, Soulanges, Martial, and Madame de Vaudremont.

When the friends parted, after the challenge which closed their conversation, the Baron flew to Madame de Vaudremont, and led her to a place in the most brilliant quadrille. Favored by the sort of intoxication which dancing always produces in a woman, and by the turmoil of a ball, where men appear in all the trickery of dress, which adds no less to their attractions than it does to those of women, Martial thought he might yield with impunity to the charm that attracted his gaze to the fair stranger. Though he succeeded in hiding his first glances towards the lady in blue from the anxious activity of the Countess’ eyes, he was ere long caught in the fact; and though he managed to excuse himself once for his absence of mind, he could not justify the unseemly silence with which he presently heard the most insinuating question which a woman can put to a man:

“Do you like me very much this evening?”

And the more dreamy he became, the more the Countess pressed and teased him.

While Martial was dancing, the Colonel moved from group to group, seeking information about the unknown lady. After exhausting the good-humor even of the most indifferent, he had resolved to take advantage of a moment when the Comtesse de Gondreville seemed to be at liberty, to ask her the name of the mysterious lady, when he perceived a little space left clear between the pedestal of the candelabrum and the two sofas, which ended in that corner. The dance had left several of the chairs vacant, which formed rows of fortifications held by mothers or women of middle age; and the Colonel seized the opportunity to make his way through this palisade hung with shawls and wraps. He began by making himself agreeable to the dowagers, and so from one to another, and from compliment to compliment, he at last reached the empty space next the stranger. At the risk of catching on to the gryphons and chimaeras of the huge candelabrum, he stood there, braving the glare and dropping of the wax candles, to Martial’s extreme annoyance.

The Colonel, far too tactful to speak suddenly to the little blue lady on his right, began by saying to a plain woman who was seated on the left:

“This is a splendid ball, madame! What luxury! What life! On my word, every woman here is pretty! You are not dancing—because you do not care for it, no doubt.”

This vapid conversation was solely intended to induce his right-hand neighbor to speak; but she, silent and absent-minded, paid not the least attention. The officer had in store a number of phrases which he intended should lead up to: “And you, madame?”—a question from which he hoped great things. But he was strangely surprised to see tears in the strange lady’s eyes, which seemed wholly absorbed in gazing on Madame de Vaudremont.

“You are married, no doubt, madame?” he asked her at length, in hesitating tones.

“Yes, monsieur,” replied the lady.

“And your husband is here, of course?”

“Yes, monsieur.”

“And why, madame, do you remain in this spot? Is it to attract attention?”

The mournful lady smiled sadly.

“Allow me the honor, madame, of being your partner in the next quadrille, and I will take care not to bring you back here. I see a vacant settee near the fire; come and take it. When so many people are ready to ascend the throne, and Royalty is the mania of the day, I cannot imagine that you will refuse the title of Queen of the Ball which your beauty may claim.”

“I do not intend to dance, monsieur.”

The curt tone of the lady’s replies was so discouraging that the Colonel found himself compelled to raise the siege. Martial, who guessed what the officer’s last request had been, and the refusal he had met with, began to smile, and stroked his chin, making the diamond sparkle which he wore on his finger.

“What are you laughing at?” said the Comtesse de Vaudremont.

“At the failure of the poor Colonel, who has just put his foot in it—”

“I begged you to take your ring off,” said the Countess, interrupting him.

“I did not hear you.”

“If you can hear nothing this evening, at any rate you see everything, Monsieur le Baron,” said Madame de Vaudremont, with an air of vexation.

“That young man is displaying a very fine diamond,” the stranger remarked to the Colonel.

“Splendid,” he replied. “The man is the Baron Martial de la Roche-Hugon, one of my most intimate friends.”

“I have to thank you for telling me his name,” she went on; “he seems an agreeable man.”

“Yes, but he is rather fickle.”

“He seems to be on the best terms with the Comtesse de Vaudremont?” said the lady, with an inquiring look at the Colonel.

“On the very best.”

The unknown turned pale.

“Hallo!” thought the soldier, “she is in love with that lucky devil Martial.”

“I fancied that Madame de Vaudremont had long been devoted to M. de Soulanges,” said the lady, recovering a little from the suppressed grief which had clouded the fairness of her face.

“For a week past the Countess has been faithless,” replied the Colonel. “But you must have seen poor Soulanges when he came in; he is still trying to disbelieve in his disaster.”

“Yes, I saw him,” said the lady. Then she added, “Thank you very much, monsieur,” in a tone which signified a dismissal.

At this moment the quadrille was coming to an end. Montcornet had only time to withdraw, saying to himself by way of consolation, “She is married.”

“Well, valiant Cuirassier,” exclaimed the Baron, drawing the Colonel aside into a window-bay to breathe the fresh air from the garden, “how are you getting on?”

“She is a married woman, my dear fellow.”

“What does that matter?”

“Oh, deuce take it! I am a decent sort of man,” replied the Colonel. “I have no idea of paying my addresses to a woman I cannot marry. Besides, Martial, she expressly told me that she did not intend to dance.”

“Colonel, I will bet a hundred napoleons to your gray horse that she will dance with me this evening.”

“Done!” said the Colonel, putting his hand in the coxcomb’s. “Meanwhile I am going to look for Soulanges; he perhaps knows the lady, as she seems interested in him.”

“You have lost, my good fellow,” cried Martial, laughing. “My eyes have met hers, and I know what they mean. My dear friend, you owe me no grudge for dancing with her after she has refused you?”

“No, no. Those who laugh last, laugh longest. But I am an honest gambler and a generous enemy, Martial, and I warn you, she is fond of diamonds.”

With these words the friends parted; General Montcornet made his way to the cardroom, where he saw the Comte de Soulanges sitting at a bouillotte table. Though there was no friendship between the two soldiers, beyond the superficial comradeship arising from the perils of war and the duties of the service, the Colonel of Cuirassiers was painfully struck by seeing the Colonel of Artillery, whom he knew to be a prudent man, playing at a game which might bring him to ruin. The heaps of gold and notes piled on the fateful cards showed the frenzy of play. A circle of silent men stood round the players at the table. Now and then a few words were spoken—pass, play, I stop, a thousand Louis, taken—but, looking at the five motionless men, it seemed as though they talked only with their eyes. As the Colonel, alarmed by Soulanges’ pallor, went up to him, the Count was winning. Field-Marshal the Duc d’Isemberg, Keller, and a famous banker rose from the table completely cleaned out of considerable sums. Soulanges looked gloomier than ever as he swept up a quantity of gold and notes; he did not even count it; his lips curled with bitter scorn, he seemed to defy fortune rather than be grateful for her favors.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Courage,” said the Colonel. “Courage, Soulanges!” Then, to help him, he added, “Come with me. I have some good news for you, but on one condition.”

“What is that?” asked Soulanges.

The Comte de Soulanges rose abruptly, tucking his winnings into his handkerchief; his face was so savage that none of the players objected to his leaving with their money. Every countenance relaxed when his morose face vanished from the circle of light at the gaming-table.

“Those fiends of soldiers are always as thick as thieves at a fair!” said a diplomat who had taken Soulanges’ place. One pallid, fatigued face turned to the newcomer and, with a glance that flashed and died like a diamond, said, “When we say military men, we do not mean civil, Monsieur le Ministre.”

“My dear fellow,” said Montcornet to Soulanges, leading him into a corner, “the Emperor spoke warmly of you this morning, and your promotion to field-marshal is certain.”

“The Master does not love the Artillery.”

“No, but he adores the nobility, and you are an aristocrat. The Master said,” added Montcornet, “that those who married in Paris during the campaign were not to be considered in disgrace. Well?”

The Comte de Soulanges looked as if he understood nothing.

“And now I hope,” the Colonel went on, “that you will tell me if you know a charming little woman sitting under a huge candelabrum—”

At these words the Count’s face lit up; he seized the Colonel’s hand. “My dear General,” said he in a changed voice, “if any man but you had asked me such a question, I would have cracked his skull with this mass of gold. Leave me, I entreat you. I feel more like blowing out my brains—I hate everything I see; I am going. This gaiety, this music, these stupid faces, all laughing, are killing me!”

“My poor friend!” replied Montcornet, giving the Count’s hand a friendly pressure, “you are too vehement. What would you say if I told you that Martial is thinking so little of Madame de Vaudremont that he is smitten with that little lady?”

“If he speaks to her,” cried Soulanges, stammering with rage, “I will thrash him as flat as his own portfolio, even if the coxcomb were in the Emperor’s lap!”

He sank into an easy-chair Montcornet had led him to. The colonel slowly withdrew, seeing Soulanges in a fury beyond consolation.

When Montcornet returned to the ballroom, Madame de Vaudremont was the first person his eyes found; her usually calm face showed ill-concealed agitation. A chair was vacant near hers, and the Colonel sat down.

“I dare wager something vexed you?” he said.

“A mere trifle, General. I want to be gone; I promised to go to the Grand Duchess of Berg’s ball, and must look in first at the Princesse de Wagram’s. Monsieur de la Roche-Hugon, who knows this, amuses himself by flirting with the dowagers.”

“That is not the whole secret of your disturbance, and I will bet a hundred louis that you will remain here the whole evening.”

“Impertinent man!”

“Then I have hit the truth?”

“Well, tell me, what am I thinking of?” said the Countess, tapping the Colonel’s fingers with her fan. “I might even reward you if you guess rightly.”

“I will not accept the challenge; I have too much the advantage of you.”

“You are presumptuous.”

“You are afraid of seeing Martial at the feet—”

“Of whom?” cried the Countess, affecting surprise.

“Of that candelabrum,” replied the Colonel, glancing at the fair stranger.

“You have guessed it,” replied the coquette, hiding her face behind her fan. “Old Madame de Lansac, as malicious as a monkey,” she went on after a pause, “has just told me that Monsieur de la Roche-Hugon is running into danger by flirting with that stranger, who sits here this evening like a skeleton at a feast. I would rather see a death’s head than that face, cruelly beautiful and as pale as a ghost. She is my evil genius. Madame de Lansac,” she added with a flash and gesture of annoyance, “who only goes to balls to watch while pretending to sleep, has made me miserably anxious. Martial shall pay dearly for playing me such a trick. Urge him, meanwhile, since he is your friend, not to make me so unhappy.”

“I have just seen a man who promises to blow his brains out if he speaks to that little lady. And he is a man, madame, to keep his word. But then I know Martial; such threats are to him an encouragement. And, besides—we have wagered,” the Colonel lowered his voice.

“Can it be true?” said the Countess.

“On my word of honor.”

“Thank you, my dear Colonel,” replied Madame de Vaudremont, with an inviting glance.

“Will you dance with me?”

“Yes, but the next quadrille. During this one I want to see what comes of this intrigue and to learn who the blue lady is; she looks intelligent.”

The Colonel, seeing she wished to be alone, retired, satisfied to have begun his attack.

At entertainments like this, women such as Madame de Lansac gather to watch younger mariners. Madame de Lansac could easily read the Countess’s agitation. Though the lady might fan herself gracefully and smile on young men, the Dowager—one of the most clear-sighted, mischief-loving survivors of the eighteenth century—read her heart through the mask. The old woman detected the slightest movements—the faint quiver of the cheeks, the curve of the eyebrows, the least curl of the lips—these were, to the Duchess, like the print of a book. From her large armchair, the coquette of the past, talking to a diplomat who sought anecdotes, admired how bravely the younger woman disguised annoyance and sorrow. Madame de Vaudremont felt as much sorrow as she feigned cheerfulness; she had believed Martial a man whose talents would adorn her life with power, and she now perceived her mistake, dangerous to her pride and reputation. Like many women then, sudden passion increased her vehemence; souls that love much often suffer as much as those consumed by one affection. Her liking for Martial, though new, still wounded deeply: losing a living hope is acute.

The old Duchess, watching for an opportunity to speak to the Countess, dismissed her Ambassador; compared with a lover’s quarrel every other interest paled, even to an old woman. Madame de Lansac shot a sardonic glance which made the Countess fear her fate lay in the dowager’s hands. There are looks between women like torches at a climax; only those who knew that Duchess could appreciate the terror she inspired.

Madame de Lansac was tall; her features suggested “That must have been a handsome woman!” She powdered and rouged to hide wrinkles; her eyes looked dim beside the carmine. She wore many diamonds and dressed with enough taste to avoid ridicule. Her sharp nose promised epigram, her teeth preserved a Voltairean smile; her exquisite politeness softened her mischief, making it impossible to call her malicious.

Her eyes lit up; a triumphant glance and smile—“I promised you as much!”—shot across the room, bringing a blush to the young stranger’s pale cheeks under the chandelier. The alliance between Lansac and the stranger did not escape Madame de Vaudremont, who scented a mystery and determined to penetrate it.

At that instant the Baron de la Roche-Hugon, after unsuccessfully questioning the dowagers about the blue lady, turned to the Comtesse de Gondreville, who replied, “A lady the ‘ancient’ Duchesse de Lansac introduced to me.”

He glanced to the Duchess’ armchair, intercepted the look she sent the stranger; though they had been on bad terms, he decided to speak.

“If the old witch affects friendliness,” thought the Baron, “she’ll play me a trick.—Madame,” he said, “you have, I am told, undertaken the charge of a very precious treasure.”

“Do you take me for a dragon?” said the old lady. “But of whom are you speaking?” she added with a sweetness that revived Martial’s hopes.

“Of that little lady, unknown to all, whom the jealousy of these coquettes has imprisoned in that corner. You, no doubt, know her family?”

“Yes,” said the Duchess. “But what concern have you with a provincial heiress, married some time since, a woman of good birth, whom you none of you know? She goes nowhere.”

“Why does she not dance? She is such a pretty creature! May we conclude a treaty of peace? If you will tell me what I want to know, I promise you that a petition for the restitution of the woods of Navarreins by the Commissioners of Crown Lands shall be strongly urged on the Emperor.”

The Baron’s suggestion was a trick: he proposed to petition for an estate belonging to an elder branch of the Navarreins under false pretense. The Duchess replied soothingly.

“Monsieur,” she said with deceptive gravity, “bring the Comtesse de Vaudremont across to me. I promise you I will reveal to her the mystery of the interesting unknown. You see, every man in the room is as curious as you. All eyes are turned toward the corner where my protegee has so modestly placed herself; she is reaping all the homage the women wished to deprive her of. Happy the man she chooses for her partner!” She fixed her eyes on Madame de Vaudremont with one of those looks saying plainly, “We are talking of you.” Then she added, “I imagine you would rather learn the stranger’s name from your handsome Countess than from mine.”

There was such marked defiance in the Duchess’ attitude that Madame de Vaudremont rose, took the chair Martial placed for her, and, without noticing him, said, “I can guess, madame, that you are talking of me; but I admit my want of perspicacity; I do not know whether it is for good or evil.”

Madame de Lansac pressed the young woman’s pretty hand in her dry, wrinkled fingers and answered in a low, compassionate tone, “Poor child!”

The women looked at each other. Madame de Vaudremont understood Martial was in the way and dismissed him with an imperious, “Leave us.”

The Baron, ill-pleased at seeing the Countess under the spell of the dangerous sibyl who had drawn her to her side, gave one of those looks a man can give—potent over a blinded heart, but ridiculous in the eyes of a woman beginning to criticise him.

“Do you think you can play the Emperor?” said Madame de Vaudremont, turning three-quarters of her face to fix an ironical sidelong gaze on the lawyer.

Martial, a man of the world with wit and acumen, would not risk breaking with a woman favored at Court and whom the Emperor wished to see married. He also counted on provoking her jealousy as the surest means of discovering the secret of her coolness, and withdrew willingly when a new quadrille put everyone in motion.

Making room for dancers, the Baron leaned against a marble console, folded his arms, and watched the two ladies. He followed the glances they directed to the stranger. Comparing the Countess with the new beauty, made alluring by mystery, he fell prey to the detestable self-interest of adventurous lady-killers; he hesitated between a fortune within his grasp and the indulgence of his caprice. The blaze of light gave strong relief to his anxious face against the white silk hangings, so that he might have seemed an evil genius. Even from a distance some observers could say, “There is another poor wretch who seems to be enjoying himself!”

The Colonel, meanwhile, shoulder against the doorway post between ballroom and cardroom, could laugh undetected under his ample moustache; it amused him to watch the dance—pretty heads turning in obedience to the figures; he read in faces, like those of the Countess and Martial, the secrets of their agitation; and then, seeing the gloomy looks of the Comte de Soulanges, still seated on the sofa, and the plaintive expression of the fair unknown, on whose features hopes and involuntary dread alternated, he felt like king of the feast. In this moving picture he saw a presentment of the world, and he laughed as he found himself the object of inviting smiles from many beautiful women. A Colonel of the Imperial Guard, equal to a Brigadier-General, was one of the best matches in the army.

It was nearly midnight. Conversation, gambling, dancing, flirtations, petty rivalries, and scheming had reached that pitch of ardor that makes a young man exclaim, “A fine ball!”

“My sweet little angel,” said Madame de Lansac to the Countess, “you are now at an age when in my day I made many mistakes. Seeing you endure a thousand deaths just now, it occurred to me I might give you some charitable advice. To go wrong at two-and-twenty means spoiling your future; is it not tearing the gown you must wear? My dear, it is not much later that we learn to wear it without crumpling it. Go on making clever enemies and friends without conduct, and you will see what a pleasant life you will some day lead!”

“Oh, madame, it is very hard for a woman to be happy, do not you think?” the Countess eagerly exclaimed.

“My child, at your age you must choose between pleasure and happiness. You want to marry Martial, who is not foolish enough to make a good husband, nor passionate enough to remain a lover. He is in debt, my dear; he is the man to run through your fortune; still, that would be nothing if he could make you happy. Do not you see how aged he looks? He must have been ill; he is making the most of what is left him. In three years he will be a wreck. He will be ambitious; perhaps he may succeed. I do not think so. What is he? A man of intrigue, who may have business skill and gossip agreeably; but he is too presumptuous for any sterling merit; he will not go far. Besides—only look at him. Is it not written on his brow that, at this very moment, what he sees in you is not a young and pretty woman, but the two million francs you possess? He does not love you, my dear; he is reckoning you as if you were an investment. If you are bent on marrying, find an older man with an assured position halfway on his career. A widow’s marriage ought not to be a trivial love affair. Is a mouse to be caught a second time in the same trap? A new alliance ought now to be a good speculation on your part, and in marrying again you ought at least to have a hope of being some day addressed as Madame la Maréchale!”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
As she spoke, both women fixed their eyes on Colonel Montcornet’s handsome face.

“If you prefer to play the delicate flirt and not marry again,” the Duchess went on bluntly, “well, my child, you better than anyone know how to raise storms and disperse them. But I beg you, never make it your pleasure to disturb families, destroy unions, and ruin wives’ happiness. I have played that perilous game. For a triumph of vanity some poor virtuous soul is murdered—for there really are virtuous women—and we may make ourselves mortally hated. I learned, a little too late, that, as the Duc d’Albe said, one salmon is worth a thousand frogs. Genuine affection brings a thousand times more happiness than transient passions. I came to preach to you; you are the reason for my visit in this house, which stinks of the lower class. Have I just seen actors here? Formerly we received them in our boudoir, but never in the drawing-room! Why do you look at me with such amazement? Listen. If you want to play with men, wring the hearts only of those whose lives are unsettled and who have no duties; the others never forgive errors that cost them happiness. Profit by my experience. That luckless Soulanges, whose head you’ve turned these past fifteen months—do you know what you struck? His whole life. He has been married two years; worshiped by a charming wife he loves but neglects; she lives in tears and embittered silence. Soulanges has had remorse more terrible than his pleasures were sweet. And you, artful little thing, have deserted him. Well, come and see your work.”

The old lady took Madame de Vaudremont’s hand, and they rose.

“There,” said Madame de Lansac, indicating the stranger, pale and trembling under the candles, “that is my grandniece, the Comtesse de Soulanges; today she yielded to my persuasion and consented to leave the sorrowful room where the sight of her child gives little consolation. You see her? You think her charming? Then imagine, dear Beauty, what she must have been when happiness and love shed their glory on that face now blighted.”

The Countess looked away, lost in sad reflection.

The Duchess led her to the card-room door; after looking round as if searching for someone—“And there is Soulanges!” she said.

The Countess shuddered as she saw, in the dimmest corner, Soulanges’s pale, set face in an easy-chair. His indifferent posture and rigid brow betrayed his suffering. Players passed him as if he were dead. The picture of the weeping wife and dejected husband, separated amid festivity like two halves of a lightning-blasted tree, perhaps held prophetic meaning for the Countess. She feared she saw an image of future revenge. Her heart was not yet so dried up; she pressed the Duchess’s hand and thanked her with a childlike smile.

“My dear child,” the old lady whispered, “remember henceforth that we are just as capable of repelling a man’s attentions as of attracting them.”

“She is yours if you are not a simpleton.” These words were whispered into Colonel Montcornet’s ear by Madame de Lansac while the Countess was still absorbed in compassion at the sight of Soulanges, for she still loved him enough to wish to restore him to happiness and promised herself she would exert her charms to make him return to his wife.

“Oh! I will talk to him!” she said to Madame de Lansac.

“Do nothing of the kind, my dear!” cried the old lady as she returned to her armchair. “Choose a good husband and shut your door to my nephew. Believe me, a wife cannot accept her husband’s heart as another woman's gift; she is a hundred times happier believing she reconquered it. By bringing my niece here I have given her an excellent chance of regaining her husband's affection. All I need is for you to play the Colonel.” She pointed to the Baron’s friend, and the Countess smiled.

“Well, madame, do you at last know the name of the unknown?” Martial asked the Countess with pique when he saw her alone.

“Yes,” said Madame de Vaudremont, looking him in the face.

Her features showed as much roguery as fun. Her smile and bright eyes were like a will-o’-the-wisp. Martial, believing she still loved him, assumed the coquetting graces a man uses before the woman he loves. He said with a fatuous air:

“And will you be annoyed with me if I seem to attach great importance to your telling me that name?”

“Will you be annoyed with me,” answered Madame de Vaudremont, “if a remnant of affection prevents my telling you and if I forbid you from making the smallest advances to that young lady? It might cost you your life.”

“To lose your good graces, madame, would be worse than to lose my life.”

“Martial,” said the Countess severely, “she is Madame de Soulanges. Her husband would blow your brains out—if, indeed, you have any—”

“Ha! ha!” laughed the coxcomb. “What! the Colonel can leave in peace the man who robbed him of your love, and then would fight for his wife! What a subversion of principles! I beg of you to allow me to dance with the little lady. You will then be able to judge how little love that heart of ice could feel for you; for, if the Colonel disapproves of my dancing with his wife after allowing me to—”

“But she loves her husband.”

“A still further obstacle that I shall have the pleasure of conquering.”

“But she is married.”

“A whimsical objection!”

“Ah!” said the Countess, with a bitter smile, “you punish us alike for our faults and our repentance!”

“Do not be angry!” exclaimed Martial eagerly. “Oh, forgive me, I beseech you. There, I will think no more of Madame de Soulanges.”

“You deserve that I should send you to her.”

“I am off,” said the Baron, laughing, “and I shall return more devoted to you. You will see the prettiest woman cannot capture the heart that is yours.”

“That is to say, that you want to win Colonel Montcornet’s horse?”

“Ah! Traitor!” said he, threatening his friend with his finger. The Colonel smiled and joined them; the Baron gave him the seat near the Countess, saying to her with a sardonic accent: “Here, madame, is a man who boasted that he could win your good graces in one evening.”

He went away, pleased to have piqued the Countess and done Montcornet an ill turn; but he had not appreciated the irony of Madame de Vaudremont’s speech and did not perceive she had come as far to meet his friend as he had to meet her, though both were unconscious.

At that moment, as the lawyer fluttered to the candelabrum where Madame de Soulanges sat, pale and timid and alive only in her eyes, her husband came to the ballroom door, his eyes flashing with anger. The old Duchess, watchful, flew to her nephew, begged him to give her his arm and find her carriage, affecting mortal boredom to prevent an outbreak. Before going she shot a glance of intelligence at her niece, indicating the enterprising knight about to address her; the signal seemed to say, “There he is, avenge yourself!”

Madame de Vaudremont caught these looks; a sudden light dawned; she feared she was the dupe of the cunning, practiced old woman.

“That perfidious Duchess,” said she to herself, “has perhaps been amusing herself by preaching morality to me while playing me some spiteful trick.”

At this thought Madame de Vaudremont’s pride was roused more than her curiosity to untangle the intrigue. Absorbed, she was no longer mistress of herself. The Colonel, interpreting her embarrassment to his advantage, grew more ardent and pressing. The old, blasé diplomats, amused by the play of faces, had never seen so many intrigues at once. The passions agitating the two couples showed variations at every step in the crowded rooms and reflected in other countenances. The spectacle of so many vivid passions—lovers’ quarrels, pleasing revenges, cruel favors, flaming glances—made the onlookers feel their impotence more keenly.

At last the Baron found a seat by Madame de Soulanges. His eyes stole a long look at her neck, fresh as dew and fragrant as field flowers. He admired up close the beauty that had amazed him from afar. He could see a small, well-shod foot and measure a slender, graceful shape. Then women wore their sash tied close under the bosom in imitation of Greek statues, a pitiless fashion for flawed busts. As he cast furtive glances, Martial was enchanted with her perfection.

“You have not danced once this evening, madame,” said he in soft and flattering tones. “Not, I should suppose, for lack of a partner?”

“I never go to parties; I am quite unknown,” replied Madame de Soulanges coldly, not having understood her aunt’s hint that she should attract the Baron.

Martial twisted the diamond on his left hand to give himself countenance; its rainbow fires seemed to flash a sudden light in the Countess’s mind; she blushed and looked at him with an undefinable expression.

“Do you like dancing?” asked the Provençal to reopen the conversation.

“Yes, very much, monsieur.”

At this reply their eyes met. The young man, surprised by her earnest accent that aroused a vague hope, questioned the lady’s eyes.

“Then, madame, am I not overbold in offering myself to be your partner for the next quadrille?”

Artless confusion colored the Countess’s white cheeks.

“But, monsieur, I have already refused one partner—a military man—”

“Was it that tall cavalry colonel whom you see over there?”

“Precisely so.”

“Oh! he is a friend of mine; feel no alarm. Will you grant me the favor I dare hope for?”

“Yes, monsieur.”

Her tone betrayed emotion so new and deep the lawyer’s world-worn soul was touched. Overcome by schoolboy shyness, he lost confidence; his southern brain caught fire and his phrases seemed graceless beside her bright, subtle replies. Fortunately the quadrille was forming. Standing by his beautiful partner, he felt more at ease. For many men dancing is a phase of being; they think they can influence a woman's heart more by bodily graces than intellect. Martial now wished to display his most effective seductions, judging by the pretentiousness of his movements.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
He led his conquest to the quadrille where the room’s most brilliant women danced. As the orchestra began the first figure, the Baron felt immense gratification surveying the ladies forming that square and noting that Madame de Soulanges’ dress might rival Madame de Vaudremont’s, who, by chance, stood vis-à-vis him and the lady in blue. All eyes turned to Madame de Soulanges; a flattering murmur showed she was the subject of every man’s comment. Admiration and envy were so eager that the young woman, abashed by a triumph she seemed to disclaim, modestly looked down, blushed, and grew more charming. When she raised her eyes she looked at her ravished partner as if to transfer that glory to him, showing she cared more for his praise than for the rest. She seemed to yield to the guileless admiration that begins love, with youth’s simple faith. Though modest and new to ballroom arts, she knew—like an accomplished coquette—how to lift her eyes to his at the right moment and drop them with assumed bashfulness.

When the new figure, invented by Trenis, brought Martial face to face with the Colonel, he laughed, “I have won your horse.”

“Yes, but you have lost eighty thousand francs a year!” retorted Montcornet, glancing at Madame de Vaudremont.

“What do I care?” replied Martial. “Madame de Soulanges is worth millions!”

At the quadrille’s end whispers circulated. Less pretty women made moral speeches to their partners, commenting on the budding liaison between Martial and the Comtesse de Soulanges. The handsomest wondered at her easy surrender. The men could not understand the Baron’s luck, not finding him particularly fascinating. Some indulgent women said it was unfair to judge the Countess too hastily; young wives would be in a hapless plight if an expressive look or a few graceful steps could compromise a woman.

Martial alone knew his happiness. During the last figure, forming the moulinet, his fingers clasped the Countess’s, and he fancied her grip through the thin perfumed kid answered his amorous appeal.

“Madame,” he said as the quadrille ended, “do not go back to that odious corner where you have been hiding until now. Is admiration the only benefit you can get from the jewels that adorn your white neck and coiffure? Come and take a turn through the rooms to enjoy the scene and yourself.”

Madame de Soulanges yielded to her seducer, whom he thought he could secure the more by showing her off. Side by side they walked two or three times among the crowded salons. The Comtesse, evidently uneasy, paused at each door before entering, only going in after stretching her neck to look at the men. This alarm, which crowned the Baron’s satisfaction, did not lift until he said, “Make yourself easy; he is not here.”

They reached an immense picture gallery in a wing of the mansion, where they could feast on a collation prepared for three hundred. As supper was about to begin, Martial led the Countess to an oval boudoir looking onto the garden, where rare flowers and shrubs made a scented bower under sky-blue hangings. The murmurs of the festivity died away. The Countess, at first startled, refused firmly to follow; but glancing in a mirror and seeing they could be seen, she sat on an ottoman with tolerable grace.

“This room is charming,” she said, admiring the hangings looped with pearls.

“All here is love and delight!” said the Baron.

In the mysterious light he saw uneasiness, modesty, and eagerness on her agitated face that enchanted him. The young lady smiled, and the smile seemed to end the struggle within her; insinuatingly she took his left hand and drew from his finger the ring on which she had fixed her eyes.

“What a fine diamond!” she exclaimed in the artless tone of a girl yielding to first temptation.

Martial, troubled by her involuntary touch as she drew off the ring, looked at her with eyes as glittering as the gem.

“Wear it,” he said, “in memory of this hour, and for the love of—”

She looked at him with such rapture that he did not finish; he kissed her hand.

“You give it me?” she said, astonished.

“I wish I had the whole world to offer you!”

“You are not joking?” she asked, huskily.

“Will you accept only my diamond?”

“You will never take it back?”

“Never.”

She put the ring on her finger. Martial, confident of coming happiness, was about to put his arm about her waist when she rose and said calmly: “I accept the diamond, monsieur, the less scruple because it belongs to me.”

The Baron was speechless.

“Monsieur de Soulanges took it lately from my dressing-table, and told me he had lost it.”

“You are mistaken, madame,” said Martial, nettled. “It was given me by Madame de Vaudremont.”

“Precisely so,” she said with a smile. “My husband borrowed it from me, he gave it to her, she made it a present to you; my ring has made a little journey, that is all. This ring will perhaps tell me all I do not know, and teach me the secret of always pleasing. Monsieur, if it had not been my own, you may be sure I should not have risked paying so dearly for it; for a young woman, it is said, is in danger with you. But, you see,” and she touched a spring within the ring, “here is M. de Soulanges’ hair.”

She fled into the crowded rooms so swiftly it seemed useless to follow; Martial, utterly confounded, had no mind to press the adventure further. The Countess’ laugh found an echo in the boudoir, where he now perceived, between two shrubs, the Colonel and Madame de Vaudremont, both laughing heartily.

“Will you have my horse, to ride after your prize?” said the Colonel.

The Baron met the banter poured upon him by Madame de Vaudremont and Montcornet with good grace, which secured their silence as to the evening’s events when his friend exchanged his charger for a rich and pretty young wife.

As the Comtesse de Soulanges drove across Paris from the Chaussée d’Antin to the Faubourg Saint-Germain, her soul was prey to many alarms. Before leaving the Hotel Gondreville she passed through the rooms but found neither her aunt nor her husband, who had gone away without her. Terrible suspicions tortured her ingenuous mind. Having witnessed her husband’s torments since Madame de Vaudremont had chained him to her car, she had hoped repentance would restore him. It was with unspeakable repugnance that she had consented to the scheme plotted by her aunt, Madame de Lansac, and in that moment she feared she had made a mistake.

The evening’s experience had saddened her innocent soul. Alarmed at first by the Count’s look of suffering and dejection, she became more so on seeing her rival’s beauty, and the corruption of society gripped her heart. As she crossed the Pont Royal she threw away the desecrated hair at the back of the diamond that had once been a token of pure affection. She wept remembering her long grief, and shuddered as she reflected that a woman who wishes for peace must bury such sufferings deep within without complaint.

“Alas!” she thought, “what can women do when they do not love? What is the fount of their indulgence? I cannot believe that, as my aunt tells me, reason is all-sufficient to maintain them in such devotion.”

She was still sighing when her man-servant let down the handsome carriage-step; she flew into the hall of her house, rushed upstairs, and was startled to see her husband sitting by the fire.

“How long is it, my dear, since you have gone to balls without telling me beforehand?” he asked in a broken voice. “You must know that a woman is always out of place without her husband. You compromised yourself strangely by remaining in the dark corner where you had ensconced yourself.”

“Oh, my dear, good Leon,” she said coaxingly, “I could not resist the happiness of seeing you without your seeing me. My aunt took me to this ball, and I was very happy there!”

This speech disarmed his assumed severity; he had been blaming himself while dreading her return, fearing she knew of an infidelity he had hoped to hide. As is the way of guilty lovers, he tried, by faultfinding, to escape her anger. Happy to see him smile and find him in a room he had lately shunned, the Countess looked so tenderly that she blushed and cast down her eyes. Her clemency enraptured Soulanges; he seized and kissed her hand gratefully—gratitude being often part of love.

“Hortense, what is that on your finger that has hurt my lip so much?” he asked, laughing.

“It is my diamond which you said you had lost, and which I have found.”

General Montcornet did not marry Madame de Vaudremont, despite their brief understanding; she was among the victims of the terrible fire that gave fame to the ball given by the Austrian ambassador on the occasion of Napoleon’s marriage to the daughter of Emperor Joseph II.

JULY, 1829.

PAZ

(La Fausse Maitresse)

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley

DEDICATION

Dedicated to the Comtesse Clara Maffei.

PAZ

(LA FAUSSE MAÎTRESSE)

I

In September, 1835, one of the richest heiresses of the faubourg Saint-Germain, Mademoiselle du Rouvre, the only daughter of the Marquis du Rouvre, married Comte Adam Mitgislas Laginski, a young Polish exile.

We write these Polish names as pronounced to spare readers the Slav consonant fortifications.

The Marquis du Rouvre had squandered nearly a princely fortune originally obtained through his marriage to a Demoiselle de Ronquerolles. On her mother’s side Clémentine du Rouvre had the Marquis de Ronquerolles for an uncle, and Madame de Serizy for an aunt. On her father’s side another uncle was the eccentric Chevalier du Rouvre, an old bachelor enriched by land speculation. The Marquis de Ronquerolles lost both his children to cholera; Madame de Serizy’s only son perished in Africa. By a curious turn, Clémentine became, despite her father’s dissipation, a great heiress. The Marquis de Ronquerolles, Madame de Serizy, and the Chevalier du Rouvre agreed to settle fortunes on their niece, each pledging ten thousand francs a year from her marriage.

Comte Adam Laginski, though an exile, was not an expense to France. He belonged to an ancient Polish family allied to princely houses. But heraldry counted for little with the rising bourgeoisie of Louis-Philippe’s Paris. When Adam first appeared in 1833 on the boulevard des Italiens and at the Jockey-Club he lived the life of a young man taking pleasure in Parisian dissipation; at first he was thought a student.

By this period Polish nationality had fallen in French estimation because of political reactions. The Movement against Resistance had made a conquered people, long celebrated in France, the butt of ridicule. Poles were associated with the republican party, though Poland was a republic of aristocrats. The bourgeoisie treated Polish exiles with contempt in 1835. There are indeed two sorts of Polish exiles: republican Poles, sons of Lelewel, and noble Poles, led by Prince Adam Czartoryski—fire and water. Exiles always bring their hatreds and divisions. This explains virulent attacks on Czartoryski and the dislike of noble exiles by Parisian shopkeeping Caesars.

In 1834 Adam Mitgislas Laginski was a butt for Parisian pleasantry.

“He is rather nice, though he is a Pole,” said Rastignac.

“All these Poles pretend to be great lords,” said Maxime de Trailles, “but this one does pay his gambling debts, and I begin to think he must have property.”

Without wishing to offend, one may note that the light-hearted, inconsistent Sarmatian character partly justified Parisian satire; the French would behave similarly in the same circumstances. The French aristocracy, helped by Polish lords during the Revolution, did not later repay their kindness; the faubourg Saint-Germain is still indebted to Poland.

Was Comte Adam rich, poor, or an adventurer? The question remained. Diplomatic salons, following official caution, treated Polish exiles discreetly, leaving them in social solitude. In a city of pleasure like Paris, a Pole’s heedless gaiety found ample encouragement to dissipation.

Adam had two points against him—his appearance and his mental habit. There are two species of Pole as there are two of Englishwoman: when an Englishwoman is not handsome she seems horribly ugly. Comte Adam looked pinched. His small, sharp face seemed pressed in a vise; his short nose, fair hair, and reddish beard gave him a goatish look, and his tarnished yellow eyes were oblique. Yet he had exquisite manners and a distinguished air, due partly to his dress and partly to training by his mother, a Radziwill. His courage was daring; his mind suited to ephemeral Parisian conversation. Young men of fashion talked too much then; real conversation needs leisure and social inequalities. Consequently, the dandies did not see the great seigneur in the careless young fellow who flitted from subject to subject, intent on amusement and freed from family constraints.

But one day in 1834 Adam suddenly bought a house in the rue de la Pépinière. Six months later his style of living rivaled the best in Paris. About the time he took himself seriously he had seen Clémentine du Rouvre at the Opera and fallen in love. A year later they were married. The salon of Madame d’Espard first sounded his praises. Mothers of daughters learned too late that the Laginski family was among the most illustrious of the North. By an act of prudence very un-Polish, his mother had mortgaged her property during the insurrection for an immense sum lent by two Jewish bankers in Paris. Comte Adam was now in possession of eighty thousand francs a year. When this was discovered society ceased to be surprised at the imprudence laid to the charge of Clémentine’s guardians. People jumped from one extreme to the other.

During the winter of 1836 Comte Adam was the fashion, and Clémentine Laginska one of the queens of Paris. Madame Laginska joined that circle of young women—Mesdames de Lestorade, de Portenduere, Marie de Vandenesse, du Guénic, and de Maufrigneuse—flowers of Paris, distinct from parvenus and speculators of the new regime.

This preamble shows the sphere in which one of those noble actions, less rare than calumniators admit, occurred—actions like pearls hidden in rough shells and lost in the tossing waves of society.

If architecture expresses manners and morals, it was proved after the insurrection of 1830. As old fortunes diminish, majestic mansions are demolished and replaced by phalansteries; peers of July occupy upper floors above newly enriched empirics below. A confused mixture of styles is used. Lacking a court or real nobility to set a tone, art has no harmony; yet architects find economical ways to imitate the real and solid and display resources in distributing space. Propose to an architect to build in a garden and he will run up a little Louvre with a courtyard, stables, and, if desired, a garden. Inside are many small rooms and passages; the eye is deceived into thinking there is space. It is still a nest of small rooms where a ducal family must turn about in space that once was a bakehouse.

The hôtel of the Comtesse Laginska, rue de la Pépinière, is one of these creations, standing between court and garden. On the right are kitchens and offices; to the left the coachhouse and stables. The porter’s lodge is between two charming portes-cochères. The chief luxury is a greenhouse at the end of a ground-floor boudoir opening on an admirable suite of reception rooms. An English philanthropist had built this bijou, designed the garden, added the greenhouse, polished doors, bricked the courtyard, and realized a dream resembling George IV’s Pavilion at Brighton. Parisian workmen moulded the doors; ceilings imitated the middle ages or a Venetian palace; marble veneering clung to outer walls. Steinbock and François Souchet designed mantelpieces and panels; Schinner painted ceilings. The staircase, white as a woman’s arm, rivaled that of the hôtel Rothschild. On account of riots and unsettled times the cost of this folly was about eleven hundred thousand francs. All this luxury, called princely by those who do not know real princes, was built in the garden of a house that had belonged to a purveyor who made a fortune by the Revolution, fled to Brussels, and died bankrupt. The Englishman died in Paris; his widow, a Methodist, ordered the establishment sold. Comte Adam bought it at a bargain; how he came to do so will be made known, for bargains were not in his line as a grand seigneur.

Behind the house lay the velvety lawn shaded by exotic trees, among which stood a Chinese pagoda. The greenhouse concealed the northern wall; climbing plants covered the opposite wall on poles painted green. This lawn, flower world, gravelled paths, simulated forest, and palisades were contained in twenty-five square rods, now worth four hundred thousand francs. Here, in solitude in the middle of Paris, birds sang. The greenhouse was an immense jardinière filling the air with perfume. Hot-water or steam pipes were cleverly concealed. The boudoir was large. Modern Parisian Architecture’s miracle is to get many things out of a limited bit of ground.

The boudoir of the young countess suited the taste of the artist who decorated the house. It was too full of pretty nothings to be a place of repose; one scarcely knew where to sit among carved Chinese work-tables, inlaid ivories, yellow topaz cups, mosaics, Dutch pictures, angels, and statuettes. Superb sketches by the best artists, lids of chests made into panels, fluted draperies of Italian silk, portières hanging from rods of old oak, Persian rugs, et cetera. For a last graceful touch, these elegant things were subdued by half-light filtered through embroidered curtains. On a table, among curiosities, lay a whip with a handle designed by Mademoiselle de Fauveau, showing that the countess rode.

Such is a lady’s boudoir in 1837—an exhibition of shop contents meant to amuse the eye, as if ennui were the worst danger in a social world that fears nothing more. Why is there nothing of an inner life? Because no one then was sure of the future; we live like prodigal annuitants.

One morning Clémentine seemed to be thinking. She lay full length on a marvellous couch, the greenhouse doors open letting tropical perfume pervade the room. The young wife looked at her husband who smoked a narghile, the only pipe she would suffer there. The portières gave a vista through two elegant salons, one white and gold, the other in a Renaissance style. The dining-room, rivalled only by the Baron de Nucingen’s, was at the end of a gallery decorated in the middle-ages manner. This gallery opened on the courtyard antechamber, through which could be seen the beautiful staircase.

The count and countess had just finished breakfast; the sky was blue and April nearly over. Married two years, Clémentine had discovered a secret or mystery in her household. The Pole is usually helpless before a woman; he is full of tenderness and becomes her inferior; Polish women make admirable wives. A Pole is even more vanquished by a Parisian woman. Comte Adam, pressed by questions, did not try roguery; he merely stipulated that he should not answer until he had finished his narghile.

“If any difficulty occurred when we were traveling,” said Clémentine, “you always dismissed it by saying, ‘Paz will settle that.’ You never wrote but to Paz. At home everybody keeps saying, ‘the captain, the captain.’ If I want the carriage—‘the captain.’ Is there a bill to pay—‘the captain.’ If I want my horse bitted, they must speak to Captain Paz. In short, it is like a game of dominoes—Paz is everywhere. I hear of nothing but Paz, but I never see Paz. Who and what is Paz? Why don’t you bring forth your Paz?”

“Isn’t everything going on right?” asked the count, taking the bocchettino from his narghile.

“Everything is going on so right that other people with an income of two hundred thousand francs would ruin themselves by going at our pace, and we have only one hundred and ten thousand.”

She pulled the bell-cord. A footman entered, dressed like a minister.

“Tell Captain Paz that I wish to see him.”

“If you think you are going to find out anything that way—” said Adam, laughing.

Adam and Clémentine had married in December 1835 and soon after had traveled two years in Italy, Switzerland, and Germany. Returning to Paris in November 1837, the countess entered society as a married woman and then became aware of a useful, half-suppressed factotum named Paz—spelt thus but pronounced “Patz.”

“Monsieur le capitaine Paz begs Madame la comtesse to excuse him,” said the footman returning. “He is at the stables; as soon as he has changed his dress Comte Paz will present himself to Madame.”

“What was he doing at the stables?”

“He was showing them how to groom Madame’s horse,” said the man. “He was not pleased with the way Constantin did it.”

The countess looked at the footman. He was perfectly serious.

“Ah! he was grooming Cora.”

“Madame la comtesse intends to ride out this morning?” said the footman, leaving the room without further answer.

“Is Paz a Pole?” asked Clémentine, turning to her husband, who nodded.

The footstep echoed through the gallery.

The countess beheld a tall, handsome man, well-made, bearing on his face signs of inward strength and secret sorrow. He wore a frogged overcoat called a polonaise. Thick black hair, rather unkempt, covered his square head; his broad forehead shone like white marble; he held his visored cap in his hand. His hand was like that of the Infant Hercules. Robust health flushed his face, marked by a large Roman nose and an Italian fervor in his jet eyes. The pleated trousers revealed fidelity to his land’s fashions. The contrast between the captain and the count, the little Pole and the stalwart soldier, was striking to a romantic woman.

“Good morning, Adam,” he said familiarly. Then he bowed courteously to ask Clémentine what he could do for her.

“You are Laginski’s friend!” exclaimed the countess.

“For life and death,” answered Paz, to whom the count smiled as he drew a last puff from his pipe.

“Then why don’t you take your meals with us? why did you not accompany us to Italy and Switzerland? why do you hide yourself so that I cannot thank you for the constant services you do for us?” said the countess with vivacity but no feeling.

She thought she perceived in Paz a voluntary servitude, a social amphibian half-secretary, half-bailiff, an embarrassing friend.
